Result: Clear genre asymmetry found but model-specific: DeepSeek-V3.2 and GPT-5.2 showed significant false-positive-rate gaps (10.1 and 8.8 percentage points respectively), while Claude and Gemini showed no comparable difference. Prompt mitigation was possible but not generic - DeepSeek reduced false positives by ~50% without recall loss, but GPT-5.2 showed little improvement.

Conclusion: Aggregate performance metrics obscure structured false positives within legitimate journalism. LLM-based credibility assessment may differentially recognize journalistic genre legitimacy, requiring genre-stratified false-positive analysis alongside overall accuracy for proper evaluation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for automated news credibility assessment, yet it remains unclear whether they apply even-handed standards across journalistic genres. We examine whether zero-shot LLMs are more likely to misclassify legitimate entertainment news as fake than legitimate hard news, using a within-dataset design on GossipCop from FakeNewsNet. Across four frontier models, we find a clear but model-specific genre asymmetry: DeepSeek-V3.2 and GPT-5.2 show false-positive-rate gaps of 10.1 and 8.8 percentage points, respectively (both $p < .001$), whereas Claude Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3 Flash show no comparable difference. A style-swap experiment yields only limited and inconsistent changes, suggesting that the asymmetry is not reducible to stylistic register alone. Prompt-based mitigation is likewise possible but not generic: framing the model as an entertainment-news fact-checker reduces false positives for DeepSeek-V3.2 by about 50\% without detectable recall loss, but offers little improvement for GPT-5.2. Exploratory qualitative coding further suggests two recurring error patterns in sampled false positives: treating private-life claims as inherently unverifiable and discounting entertainment journalism as an epistemically weaker genre. Taken together, these findings show that aggregate performance metrics can obscure structured false positives within legitimate journalism. We argue that LLM-based credibility assessment may not only evaluate truth claims but also differentially recognize the legitimacy of journalistic genres, and that evaluation should therefore include genre-stratified false-positive analysis alongside overall accuracy.

</details>


### [53] [NH-CROP: Robust Pricing for Governed Language Data Assets under Cost Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01745)
*Xu Zheng,Feiyu Wu,Zhuocheng Wang,Yiming Dai,Hui Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NH-CROP is a clipped robust pricing framework with a no-harm information-acquisition gate for governed language data assets under uncertain access costs, which improves pricing decisions while minimizing costly verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language data assets are increasingly governed as valuable resources, but platforms face uncertainty about their true privacy and access costs when pricing them, often needing to decide whether to pay for refined cost information.

Method: The paper introduces NH-CROP, a clipped robust pricing framework with a no-harm information-acquisition gate that compares direct pricing, risk-aware pricing, and verify-then-price strategies, acquiring refined cost information only when its estimated decision value exceeds the best no-verification alternative.

Result: Across synthetic, real-proxy, and downstream-utility-grounded benchmarks, clipped NH-CROP variants improve or remain competitive with price-only and risk-aware baselines. Surprisingly, learned policies often choose not to verify even when verification is available, and paid verification is not the main source of gains in real-proxy and utility-grounded settings.

Conclusion: Governed language-data platforms should prioritize calibrating pricing under uncertain access costs first, and only verify when information is both cheap and decision-actionable, as refined cost information can have substantial local value but verification costs often outweigh benefits.

Abstract: Language data are increasingly acquired and governed as assets, yet platforms often price candidate resources before knowing their true privacy or access costs. We study online pricing for governed language data assets under cost uncertainty. At each round, a platform observes an NLP task, a candidate asset, and a coarse cost estimate, may pay for a refined cost signal, posts a price, and receives safe net revenue.
  We introduce \textsc{NH-CROP}, a clipped robust pricing framework with a no-harm information-acquisition gate. The method compares direct pricing, risk-aware pricing, and verify-then-price, and acquires information only when its estimated decision value exceeds the best no-verification alternative. Across synthetic, real-proxy, and downstream-utility-grounded benchmarks, clipped \textsc{NH-CROP} variants improve or remain competitive with price-only and risk-aware baselines. Causal ablations show that paid verification is not the main source of gains in real-proxy and utility-grounded settings: the strongest learned policies often choose not to verify. Oracle and high-decision-value diagnostics show that refined cost information can still have substantial local value. Overall, governed language-data platforms should calibrate pricing under uncertain access costs first and verify only when information is cheap and decision-actionable.

</details>


### [54] [Catching the Infection Before It Spreads: Foresight-Guided Defense in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01758)
*Yue Ma,Ziyuan Yang,Yi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FLP is a training-free framework that uses foresight-guided local purification to detect and eliminate infectious jailbreak attacks in multimodal agent systems, reducing infection rates from 95% to below 5.5% while preserving system diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-agent systems using large multimodal models are vulnerable to infectious jailbreak attacks where compromising one agent spreads to others. Existing defenses rely on training a global cure factor that homogenizes agent responses and only superficially suppresses infections rather than providing true recovery, due to mismatch between global defense strategies and localized attack behaviors.

Method: Proposes Foresight-Guided Local Purification (FLP) where each agent reasons over future interactions through multi-persona simulation to track behavioral evolution. Uses response diversity across persona-based predictions at retrieval and semantic levels to detect infections. For infected agents: recent infections use immediate album rollback, while long-term infections use Recursive Binary Diagnosis to partition image albums and localize/eliminate adversarial examples.

Result: FLP reduces maximum cumulative infection rate from over 95% to below 5.47%. Retrieval and semantic metrics closely match benign baselines, indicating effective preservation of interaction diversity in the multi-agent system.

Conclusion: The FLP framework effectively addresses infectious jailbreak vulnerabilities in multimodal agent systems through localized, training-free purification that preserves system diversity while providing robust defense against both recent and long-term infections.

Abstract: Large multimodal model-based Multi-Agent Systems (MASs) enable collaborative complex problem solving through specialized agents. However, MASs are vulnerable to infectious jailbreak, where compromising a single agent can spread to others, leading to widespread compromise. Existing defenses counter this by training a more contagious cure factor, biasing agents to retrieve it over virus adversarial examples (VirAEs). However, this homogenizes agent responses, providing only superficial suppression rather than true recovery. We revisit these defenses, which operate globally via a shared cure factor, while infectious jailbreak arise from localized interaction behaviors. This mismatch limits their effectiveness. To address this, we propose a training-free Foresight-Guided Local Purification (FLP) framework, where each agent reasons over future interactions to track behavioral evolution and eliminate infections. Specifically, each agent simulates future behavioral trajectories over subsequent chat rounds. To reflect diversity in MASs, we introduce a multi-persona simulation strategy for robust prediction across interaction contexts. We then use response diversity as a diagnostic signal to detect infection by analyzing inconsistencies across persona-based predictions at both retrieval-result and semantic levels. For infected agents, we apply localized purification: recent infections are mitigated via immediate album rollback, while long-term infections are handled using Recursive Binary Diagnosis (RBD), which recursively partitions the image album and applies the same diagnosis strategy to localize and eliminate VirAEs. Experiments show that FLP reduces the maximum cumulative infection rate from over 95% to below 5.47%. Moreover, retrieval and semantic metrics closely match benign baselines, indicating effective preservation of interaction diversity.

</details>


### [55] [Runtime Evaluation of Procedural Content Generation in an Endless Runner Game Using Autonomous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01783)
*Rishabh Kar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Momentum is an endless-runner game that integrates runtime terrain generation with autonomous agent-based evaluation to ensure generated content is playable, diverse, and reliable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Procedural Content Generation (PCG) creates game content algorithmically but introduces serious evaluation problems - generated content can become unbalanced, blocked, repetitive, or unsolvable. Current approaches often treat evaluation as a separate offline process rather than integrating it into the gameplay loop.

Method: Developed Momentum, an endless-runner game with: (1) runtime terrain generation and environment object spawning, (2) constraint-driven object placement inspired by Wave Function Collapse, (3) asynchronous navigation surface rebuilding, and (4) two autonomous evaluation agents - an aerial scanner for geometric examination and a ground-traversal agent for navigational validation. Uses ray casting, volumetric physics sweeps, obstacle-layer filtering, and structured crash reporting.

Result: Created a measurable evaluation framework along PCG axes (playability, diversity, controllability, runtime performance), derived structural saturation bounds on spawners from placement constraints, and quantified per-segment scanning costs of agents. Successfully demonstrated unification of generation and validation within the same runtime loop.

Conclusion: The work shows that generation and validation can be effectively unified within a single runtime loop rather than treating evaluation as a separate offline process. This approach ensures generated content remains playable and diverse while maintaining performance, providing a practical framework for runtime PCG evaluation in games.

Abstract: Procedural Content Generation (PCG) enables game content to be created algorithmically without direct manual level-design effort, but it introduces a serious evaluation problem: generated content may become unbalanced, blocked, repetitive, or technically unsolvable. This paper presents Momentum, an endless-runner game that integrates runtime terrain generation, environment object spawning, and autonomous agent-based evaluation into a single gameplay loop. Ground tiles and environmental objects are generated dynamically as the player advances, object placement follows a constraint-driven mechanism inspired by Wave Function Collapse (WFC), and the runtime navigation surface is rebuilt asynchronously to remain consistent with the streamed environment. Two autonomous evaluation agents move ahead of the player and inspect the generated path: an aerial scanner that examines the corridor geometrically, and a ground-traversal agent that validates the same region from a navigational perspective. The evaluation pipeline combines ray casting, volumetric physics sweeps, obstacle-layer filtering, and structured crash reporting to identify problematic generated scenarios before they reach the player. The work demonstrates how generation and validation can be unified within the same runtime loop, rather than treating evaluation as a separate offline pass. Around this implementation, the paper formulates a measurable evaluation framework along the canonical PCG axes of playability, diversity, controllability, and runtime performance, derives a structural saturation bound on the spawner from its own placement constraints, and quantifies the per-segment scanning cost of the agents from first principles.

</details>


### [56] [DataEvolver: Let Your Data Build and Improve Itself via Goal-Driven Loop Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01789)
*Qisong Zhang,Wenzhuo Wu,Zhuangzhuang Jia,Yunhao Yang,Huayu Zhang,Xianghao Zang,Zhixiang He,Zhongjiang He,Kongming Liang,Zhanyu Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DataEvolver: A closed-loop visual data engine that organizes iterative data generation through goal-driven correction, validation, and acceptance processes for creating high-quality visual datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constructing controllable visual data is a major bottleneck for image editing and multimodal understanding. Current methods lack systematic processes for iterative generation, inspection, correction, filtering, and export that produces useful supervision.

Method: DataEvolver organizes visual data creation around explicit goals, persistent artifacts, bounded corrective actions, and acceptance decisions. It supports multiple artifact types (RGB images, masks, depth maps, meshes, etc.) and operates through two coupled loops: generation-time self-correction within each sample and validation-time self-expansion across dataset rounds.

Result: Validated on image-level object-rotation setting. With a fixed Qwen-Edit LoRA probe, the final Ours+DualGate model outperforms both the unadapted base model and a public multi-angle LoRA on SpatialEdit and held-out evaluation. Ablations show consistent improvement from scene-aware generation to feedback-driven correction and dual-gated validation.

Conclusion: The main contribution is a reusable framework for building visual datasets through explicit goal tracking, review, correction, and acceptance loops. Beyond the released rotation data, DataEvolver provides systematic approach to overcome bottlenecks in visual data construction.

Abstract: Constructing controllable visual data is a major bottleneck for image editing and multimodal understanding. Useful supervision is rarely produced by a single rendering pass; instead it emerges through iterative generation, inspection, correction, filtering, and export. We present DataEvolver, a closed-loop visual data engine that organizes this process around explicit goals, persistent artifacts, bounded corrective actions, and acceptance decisions. DataEvolver supports multiple artifact types, including RGB images, masks, depth maps, normal maps, meshes, poses, trajectories, and review traces. In the current release, the system operates through two coupled loops: generation-time self-correction within each sample and validation-time self-expansion across dataset rounds. We validate the framework on an image-level object-rotation setting. With a fixed Qwen-Edit LoRA probe, our final Ours+DualGate model outperforms both the unadapted base model and a public multi-angle LoRA on SpatialEdit and a held-out evaluation set. Ablations show a consistent improvement path from scene-aware generation to feedback-driven correction and dual-gated validation. Beyond the released rotation data, our main contribution is a reusable framework for building visual datasets through explicit goal tracking, review, correction, and acceptance loops.

</details>


### [57] [Neural Decision-Propagation for Answer Set Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01797)
*Thomas Eiter,Katsumi Inoue,Sota Moriyama*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NDProp is a differentiable neuro-symbolic method that extends decision-propagation with neural computation to efficiently compute stable models, improving accuracy and scalability over existing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic approaches integrating ASP with neural networks depend on classical ASP solvers, which create scalability bottlenecks for real-world applications.

Method: Proposes DProp (decision-propagation) that alternates falsity decisions and truth propagations to compute stable models, then extends it to Neural DProp with neural computation for decisions and fuzzy evaluation for propagations.

Result: NDProp can learn to efficiently compute stable models and improves both accuracy and scalability on neuro-symbolic benchmarks compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: NDProp provides a promising differentiable approach for neuro-symbolic integration that addresses scalability limitations of classical ASP solvers while maintaining reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Integration of Answer Set Programming (ASP) with neural networks has emerged as a promising tool in Neuro-symbolic AI. While existing approaches extend the capabilities of ASP to real world domains, their reasoning pipelines depend on classical solvers, which is a bottleneck for scalability. To tackle this problem, we propose a new method to compute stable models, called decision-propagation (DProp), which alternates falsity decisions and truth propagations. Successful DProp computations are shown to capture the stable model semantics. We then develop Neural DProp (NDProp), a differentiable extension of DProp with neural computation for decisions and fuzzy evaluation for propagations. We evaluate the capabilities of NDProp for learning decision heuristics as well as neuro-symbolic integration, and compare it with existing neuro-symbolic approaches. The results show that NDProp can learn to efficiently compute stable models, and it improves accuracy and scalability on neuro-symbolic benchmarks.

</details>


### [58] [NeuroState-Bench: A Human-Calibrated Benchmark for Commitment Integrity in LLM Agent Profiles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01847)
*Jia Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeuroState-Bench is a human-calibrated benchmark for evaluating AI agents' commitment integrity in multi-turn tasks, showing that task success and commitment integrity often diverge in LLM evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current outcome-only evaluation methods fail to assess whether AI agents preserve necessary commitments for coherent multi-turn task completion, focusing only on final success without examining commitment integrity throughout the process.

Method: Developed NeuroState-Bench with 144 deterministic tasks and 306 benchmark-defined side-query probes across 8 failure families, using human calibration (weighted kappa = 0.977, ICC = 0.977) and evaluating 32 profiles (16 local + 16 hosted models) through the same pipeline.

Result: Task success and commitment integrity diverge: success leader ≠ integrity leader; 31/32 profiles change rank when integrity replaces task success; integrity rankings are more stable under distractor perturbation. HCCIS-CORE achieves 0.8469 AUC for diagnostic discrimination of terminal task failure.

Conclusion: NeuroState-Bench provides a calibrated evaluation axis for exposing commitment failures across broader model grids than previous local-only evaluations, demonstrating that commitment integrity is a distinct dimension from task success in multi-turn reasoning.

Abstract: Outcome-only evaluation under-specifies whether an evaluated agent profile preserves the commitments required to solve a multi-turn task coherently. NeuroState-Bench is a human-calibrated benchmark that operationalizes commitment integrity through benchmark-defined side-query probes rather than inferred hidden activations. The released inventory contains 144 deterministic tasks and 306 benchmark-defined side-query probes spanning eight cognitively motivated failure families, paired clean and distractor variants, and three difficulty bands. The main 32-profile evaluation contains a fixed 16-profile local subset and a matched 16-profile hosted large-model subset evaluated through the same benchmark pipeline. Human calibration uses the final merged reporting scope: 104 sampled task units, 216 raw annotations, and 108 adjudicated task rows, with weighted kappa = 0.977 and ICC(2,1) = 0.977. Empirically, task success and commitment integrity diverge across this expanded grid: the success leader is not the integrity leader, 31 of 32 profiles change rank when integrity replaces task success, and integrity rankings are more stable under distractor perturbation. The primary confidence-free score HCCIS-CORE reaches 0.8469 AUC and 0.6992 PR-AUC for post-probe diagnostic discrimination of terminal task failure; the legacy full heuristic variant HCCIS-FULL reaches 0.7997 AUC and 0.6410 PR-AUC. Probe accuracy and state drift achieve slightly higher ROC-AUC, 0.8587, and better Brier/ECE, while HCCIS-CORE has substantially higher point-estimate PR-AUC and remains more closely tied to the benchmark's intended construct. The exploratory neural-augmented variant HCCIS+N is weaker overall, and a randomized subspace control approaches chance. NeuroState-Bench therefore contributes a calibrated evaluation axis for exposing commitment failures over a broader model grid than the original local-only subset.

</details>


### [59] [Sheaf-Theoretic Planning: A Categorical Foundation for Resilient Multi-Agent Autonomous Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01879)
*Manuel Hernández,Eduardo Sánchez-Soto*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Sheaf-Theoretic Planning (STP) offers a new mathematical framework using topos theory and sheaf semantics to address limitations of traditional multi-agent systems in handling uncertainty and adversarial environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional multi-agent systems based on symbolic logic (event calculus, situation calculus) have limitations due to closed-world assumptions that fail when dealing with unobserved agent interventions, plan interruptions, and divergent belief-reality states in stochastic, adversarial environments.

Method: Introduces Sheaf-Theoretic Planning (STP) framework grounded in mathematical structures of topos theory and sheaf semantics to model multi-agent coordination problems.

Result: The paper provides exhaustive analysis, justification, and extension of the STP framework, exploring its categorical foundations and implementation feasibility for resilient autonomous systems.

Conclusion: STP emerges as a transformative alternative to classical symbolic approaches, positioning itself as a key framework for the future of autonomous systems capable of handling the complexities of real-world stochastic and adversarial environments.

Abstract: The challenge of engineering autonomous agents capable of navigating the stochastic and adversarial nature of the physical world has historically resided at the intersection of symbolic logic and control theory. Traditional multi-agent system (MAS) frameworks have relied heavily on monolithic logical models -- primarily variations of the event calculus and situation calculus -- to represent action, change, and temporal persistence. While these classical systems provide robust solutions to the frame problem through mechanisms like circumscription and successor state axioms, they are inherently limited by a closed-world assumption that fails in the face of unobserved agent interventions, plan interruptions, and divergent belief-reality states. The paradigm of Sheaf-Theoretic Planning (STP) emerges as a transformative alternative, grounding the problem of multi-agent coordination under the mathematical structures of topos theory and sheaf semantics. This report provides an exhaustive analysis, justification, and extension of the STP framework, exploring its categorical foundations, implementation feasibility, and role in the future of resilient autonomous systems.

</details>


### [60] [CyberAId: AI-Driven Cybersecurity for Financial Service Providers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01892)
*George Fatouros,Georgios Makridis,John Soldatos,Dimosthenis Kyriazis,Pedro Malo,George Kousiouris,Giannis Ledakis,Louiza Kachrimani,Panagiotis Rizomiliotis,Bruno Almeida,Despina Tomkou,Kostas Metaxas,Konstantinos Ilias,Christos Gkizelis,Ernstjan de Gooyert,Amin Babazadeh,Kostis Mavrogiorgos,Pepi Paraskevoulakou,Christos Xenakis,Giannis Chouchoulis,Konstantina Tripodi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CyberAId is a hybrid multi-agent LLM platform that augments enterprise security operations by enabling specialized AI agents to reason over existing SIEM/XDR telemetry while maintaining audit compliance, privacy, and regulatory alignment for European financial institutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: European financial institutions face overwhelming alert volumes that SOC teams cannot handle, with current SIEMs covering only a fraction of MITRE ATT&CK techniques. Most breaches are preceded by alerts that go uninvestigated, highlighting a critical gap in reasoning capacity rather than data or staffing limitations.

Method: Proposes a hybrid multi-agent system where specialized LLM subagents reason over classical SIEM/XDR telemetry, share state through privacy-preserving federation, and integrate with complementary capabilities. CyberAId features a Main Agent coordination layer, reporting capability, specialist subagents operating in a shared runtime under bounded human-in-the-loop autonomy, organized around four falsifiable design principles.

Result: The CyberAId platform is model-agnostic and deployable on-premise. It will be validated at four financial use cases: client impersonation, anti-money-laundering for payment service providers, retail-banking incident response, and high-frequency-trading resilience. The paper proposes skill-based agent adaptation as the key research direction for continuous improvement.

Conclusion: A hybrid multi-agent LLM system that builds on existing security infrastructure rather than replacing it represents the right architectural approach for financial cybersecurity. Such systems can scale reasoning capacity while maintaining audit trails, regulatory compliance, and privacy through federated learning and bounded autonomy.

Abstract: European financial institutions face mounting regulatory pressure while their security operations centres remain constrained not by data or staffing but by reasoning capacity: enterprise SIEMs cover only a fraction of MITRE ATT&CK techniques, two thirds of SOC teams cannot keep pace with alert volumes, and the majority of breaches are preceded by alerts that are generated but never investigated. Frontier large language models now achieve state-of-the-art results on isolated cybersecurity tasks (one-day vulnerability exploitation, code-level patching, intrusion detection) yet no narrow win constitutes a platform that can compose across functions, persist multi-tenant state, map findings to regulatory regimes and survive an audit. This position paper argues that the right unit of construction is a hybrid multi-agent system in which specialised LLM subagents reason over classical SIEM/XDR telemetry rather than replacing it, share accumulated agent state across institutions through privacy-preserving federation, and can connect to complementary capability packs such as quantum-based authentication, digital twins for adversarial validation, and eBPF-based kernel telemetry. We present CyberAId, a model-agnostic, on-premise-deployable platform in which a Main Agent coordination layer, a Reporting capability, and specialist subagents operate within a shared runtime under bounded human-in-the-loop autonomy, organised around four falsifiable design principles, and aligned with relevant regulations. CyberAId will be validated at four representative financial use cases (client impersonation, anti-money-laundering for payment service providers, retail-banking incident response, and high-frequency-trading resilience) and propose skill-based agent adaptation as the most promising research direction for turning each deployment into a contribution to a continuously refined collective defence.

</details>


### [61] [Disentangling Intent from Role: Adversarial Self-Play for Persona-Invariant Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01899)
*Jiajia Li,Xiaoyu Wen,Zhongtian Ma,Shuyue Hu,Qiaosheng Zhang,Zhen Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PIA is a novel adversarial self-play framework that defends against persona-based jailbreak attacks on LLMs through co-evolution of attack (Persona Lineage Evolution) and defense (Persona-Invariant Consistency Learning) mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite safety alignment techniques, LLMs remain vulnerable to emerging persona-based jailbreak attacks. Existing research focuses primarily on attack iterations but lacks systemic and mechanistic constraints on the defense side, creating a gap in robust protection against these sophisticated attacks.

Method: Proposes Persona-Invariant Alignment (PIA) framework with two components: 1) Persona Lineage Evolution (PLE) for attack-side exploration of high-risk persona spaces using lineage-based credit propagation, and 2) Persona-Invariant Consistency Learning (PICL) for defense-side structural decoupling of safety decisions from persona context using unilateral KL-divergence constraints.

Result: PLE efficiently explores high-risk persona spaces, while PICL significantly reduces Attack Success Rate (ASR) while preserving the model's general capabilities. The approach demonstrates superiority and robustness in defending against persona-based jailbreak attacks.

Conclusion: The adversarial self-play framework with co-evolving attack and defense mechanisms provides an effective paradigm for improving LLM safety against persona-based jailbreak attacks, achieving better defense while maintaining model utility.

Abstract: The growing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have driven their widespread deployment across diverse domains, even in potentially high-risk scenarios. Despite advances in safety alignment techniques, current models remain vulnerable to emerging persona-based jailbreak attacks. Existing research on persona-based jailbreak has primarily focused on attack iterations, yet it lacks systemic and mechanistic constraints on the defense side. To address this challenge, we propose Persona-Invariant Alignment (PIA), an adversarial self-play framework that achieves co-evolution through Persona Lineage Evolution (PLE) on the attack side and Persona-Invariant Consistency Learning (PICL) on the defense side. Theoretically, PICL is grounded in the structural separation hypothesis, using a unilateral KL-divergence constraint to enable the structural decoupling of safety decisions from persona context, thereby maintaining safe behavior under persona-based jailbreak attacks. Experimental results demonstrate that PLE efficiently explores high-risk persona spaces by leveraging lineage-based credit propagation. Meanwhile, the PICL defense method significantly reduces the Attack Success Rate (ASR) while preserving the model's general capability, thereby validating the superiority and robustness of this alignment paradigm. Codes are available at https://github.com/JiajiaLi-1130/PIA.

</details>


### [62] [12 Angry AI Agents: Evaluating Multi-Agent LLM Decision-Making Through Cinematic Jury Deliberation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01986)
*Ahmet Bahaddin Ersoz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs simulating "12 Angry Men" jurors mostly fail to replicate the film's persuasion dynamics, with heavy RLHF alignment reducing deliberative flexibility more than model capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether LLMs in multi-agent settings can replicate the persuasive dynamics and minority-to-majority persuasion seen in "12 Angry Men," exploring how different alignment training affects deliberative flexibility.

Method: Created a multi-agent benchmark with 12 LLM agents conditioned on film-faithful personas debating the murder case. Tested GPT-4o (heavy alignment) vs Llama-4-Scout (lighter alignment) across three conditions (baseline, open-minded prompt, no initial vote) with N=3 replications per cell (18 runs total).

Result: 17/18 runs ended in hung jury; minority persuasion rarely occurred. GPT-4o averaged 1.0 vote changes per run, while Llama-4-Scout ranged 2.0-6.0 and was only model to reach NOT_GUILTY verdict. Same "open-minded" instruction was internalized by Llama but ignored by GPT-4o.

Conclusion: RLHF alignment intensity, not model capability, is the primary determinant of deliberative flexibility in multi-agent settings. Heavy alignment reduces adaptability, with flexibility (not capability) being key for human-like deliberation.

Abstract: What if the twelve jurors of Sidney Lumet's 12 Angry Men (1957) were not men, but large language models? Would the one juror who disagrees still be able to change everyone's mind? This paper instantiates that scenario as a multi-agent benchmark for LLM deliberation: twelve agents, each conditioned on a film-faithful persona, debate the film's murder case using multi-agent framework. Two models representing opposite ends of the RLHF spectrum are tested: GPT-4o (closed-source, heavy alignment) and Llama-4-Scout (open-weight, lighter alignment), across three conditions (baseline, open-minded prompt, no initial vote), with N = 3 replications per cell (18 runs total). Three findings emerge. (i) Seventeen of eighteen runs end in a hung jury (a state where the jury fails to reach a unanimous verdict); the film's central event, gradual minority-to-majority persuasion, almost never occurs, indicating that anchoring is the dominant failure mode of current LLMs in this setting. (ii) The two models exhibit sharply different internal dynamics: GPT-4o produces a mean of 1.0 vote changes per run across all conditions, while Llama-4-Scout ranges from 2.0 (baseline) to 6.0 (open-minded prompt), and is the only model to reach a NOT\_GUILTY verdict (1 of 3 runs in the no-initial-vote condition). The same ``open-minded'' instruction is internalized by Llama and ignored by GPT-4o. (iii) This asymmetry suggests that the intensity of RLHF alignment training, not model capability, is the primary determinant of deliberative flexibility in multi-agent settings. Flexibility, not capability, tracks human deliberation. The work is framed as an exploratory study and discusses implications for jury-of-LLMs evaluation and multi-agent debate.

</details>


### [63] [TumorXAI: Self-Supervised Deep Learning Framework for Explainable Brain MRI Tumor Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01999)
*Abrar Hossain Zahin,Amit Kumar Saha,Tanvir Mridha,Saifur Rahman,Jannatul Ferdous Prome,Raima Husna,Israt Jahan,Ahmed Wasif Reza*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Using self-supervised learning with ResNet-50, researchers achieved 99.64% accuracy for brain tumor classification on 4,448 MRIs, outperforming supervised methods when labeled data is limited, with explainable AI providing model interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Brain tumor classification from MRI is crucial for early diagnosis but faces challenges due to tumor heterogeneity and limited annotated datasets, which restrict supervised deep learning approaches.

Method: Used self-supervised learning (SSL) with ResNet-50 backbone, evaluating four SSL frameworks (SimCLR, BYOL, DINO, Moco v3) on 4,448 MRIs across 17 tumor types. Workflow included preprocessing, fine-tuning, linear evaluation, SSL pretraining with data augmentations, and explainable AI techniques (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, EigenCAM) for visualization.

Result: SimCLR achieved 99.64% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. SSL-pretrained models outperformed supervised baselines when labels were limited across all metrics. Explainable AI techniques provided visual insights into model decisions, enhancing interpretability.

Conclusion: SSL demonstrates scalability and reliability for diagnosing brain tumors from unlabeled medical data, offering a solution to limited annotated datasets while maintaining high performance and interpretability through explainable AI techniques.

Abstract: Classifying brain tumors using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is crucial for early diagnosis and treatment; however, tumor heterogeneity and a dearth of annotated datasets restrict the use of supervised deep learning approaches. In this work, we use self-supervised learning (SSL) to study multi-class brain tumor classification. Using a ResNet-50 backbone, we evaluate four SSL frameworks including SimCLR, BYOL, DINO, and Moco v3 on a publicly available dataset of 4,448 MRIs with 17 distinct tumor types. On the dataset, SimCLR achieved 99.64% accuracy, 99.64% precision, 99.64% recall, and 99.64% F1-score. The workflow includes preprocessing, fine-tuning, linear evaluation, and SSL pretraining with data augmentations. Results show that, when labels are limited, SSL-pretrained models outperform supervised baselines in terms of F1-score, recall, accuracy, and precision. Additionally, by providing visual insights into model decisions, Explainable AI techniques (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, EigenCAM) enhance interpretability. These results demonstrate SSL's scalability and dependability in diagnosing brain tumors from unlabeled medical data.

</details>


### [64] [Personalized Digital Health Modeling with Adaptive Support Users](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02004)
*Zhongqi Yang,Mahkameh Rasouli,Neda Mohseni,Yong Huang,Iman Azimi,Amir M. Rahmani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Unified personalization framework using adaptively weighted support from both similar and dissimilar users improves digital health models, especially in low-data settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized models are crucial in digital health due to individual heterogeneity, but limited by scarce/noisy user-specific data. Existing methods relying only on population pretraining or similar users can cause biased transfer and weak generalization.

Method: A unified personalization framework that trains personal models using adaptively weighted support from both similar and dissimilar users. The objective integrates: 1) personal loss, 2) similarity-weighted transfer from similar users, and 3) contrastive regularization from dissimilar users to suppress misleading correlations. An iterative optimization algorithm jointly updates model parameters and user similarity weights.

Result: Experiments on six tasks across four real-world digital health datasets show consistent improvements over population and personalized baselines. Achieves up to 10% lower RMSE on large-scale datasets and approximately 25% lower RMSE in low-data settings. Learned adaptive weights improve data efficiency and provide interpretable guidance for targeted data selection.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses personalization challenges in digital health by leveraging both similar and dissimilar users through adaptive weighting and contrastive regularization, demonstrating strong performance improvements and practical utility in data-scarce scenarios.

Abstract: Personalized models are essential in digital health because individuals exhibit substantial physiological and behavioral heterogeneity. Yet personalization is limited by scarce and noisy user-specific data. Most existing methods rely on population pretraining or data from similar users only, which can lead to biased transfer and weak generalization. We propose a unified personalization framework that trains a personal model using adaptively weighted support users, including both similar and dissimilar individuals. The objective integrates personal loss, similarity-weighted transfer from similar users, and contrastive regularization from dissimilar users to suppress misleading correlations. An iterative optimization algorithm jointly updates model parameters and user similarity weights. Experiments on six tasks across four real-world digital health datasets show consistent improvements over population and personalized baselines. The method achieves up to 10% lower RMSE on large-scale datasets and approximately 25% lower RMSE in low-data settings. The learned adaptive weights improve data efficiency and provide interpretable guidance for targeted data selection.

</details>


### [65] [Reliable AI Needs to Externalize Implicit Knowledge: A Human-AI Collaboration Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02010)
*Hengyu Liu,Tianyi Li,Zhihong Cui,Yushuai Li,Zhangkai Wu,Torben Bach Pedersen,Kristian Torp,Christian S. Jensen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI reliability requires infrastructure for human validation of implicit knowledge (reasoning patterns, debugging processes) that current methods can't verify, through structured Knowledge Objects that externalize this knowledge for inspection and endorsement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI reliability methods can only verify explicit knowledge (papers, documentation, databases) but not implicit knowledge (reasoning patterns, debugging processes, intermediate steps). This creates a fundamental gap where the most valuable AI capabilities - reasoning, judgment, and intuition - are precisely those we cannot verify. AI learns indiscriminately from both beneficial patterns and harmful biases in implicit knowledge.

Method: Proposes Knowledge Objects (KOs) - structured artifacts that externalize implicit knowledge into forms humans can inspect, verify, and endorse. KOs transform the economics of verification by making previously too-costly implicit knowledge feasible to validate.

Result: KOs enable accumulated human validation to improve AI reliability over time by providing a structured infrastructure for inspecting and endorsing implicit knowledge that current methods cannot verify.

Conclusion: Reliable AI requires infrastructure for human validation of implicit knowledge through Knowledge Objects, addressing the critical gap where current verification methods fail to cover the most valuable AI capabilities like reasoning and judgment.

Abstract: This position paper argues that reliable AI requires infrastructure for human validation of implicit knowledge. AI learns from both explicit knowledge (papers, documentation, structured databases) and implicit knowledge (reasoning patterns, debugging processes, intermediate steps). Implicit knowledge remains unexternalized because documentation cost exceeds perceived value -- yet AI learns from it indiscriminately, acquiring both beneficial patterns and harmful biases. Current reliability methods can only verify explicit knowledge against sources, creating a fundamental gap: the most valuable AI capabilities (reasoning, judgment, intuition) are precisely those we cannot verify. We propose Knowledge Objects (KOs) -- structured artifacts that externalize implicit knowledge into forms humans can inspect, verify, and endorse. KOs transform verification economics: what was previously too costly to verify becomes feasible, enabling accumulated human validation to improve reliability over time.

</details>


### [66] [Tenability and Weak Semantics: Modeling Non-uniform Defense -- Extended Version](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02024)
*Uri Andrews,Luca San Mauro,John Spoerl*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Tenability is a family of dialogue-based semantics for abstract argumentation that formalizes strategic defense in debates through monotone commitment games, offering different behavior patterns from existing weak semantics with varying computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing weak semantics in argumentation theory, while relaxing admissibility by discounting unreasonable attacks, still require uniform defense against all reasonable arguments even when they're collectively inconsistent. This uniformity is too demanding when defensibility is inherently strategic and depends on the opponent's line of attack.

Method: Introduces tenability semantics formalized through dialogue-based monotone commitment games over finite conflict-free moves. Defines three variants: static tenability, tenability, and strong tenability, differing in the obligations imposed on disputants. The approach is motivated by three natural benchmark patterns: self-defeating attack, floating assignment, and disjunctive reinstatement.

Result: Tenability behaves differently from all previously studied weak semantics on the benchmark patterns. The paper establishes relative strength relationships between the three variants, proves implications and separations with existing weak semantics, and analyzes computational complexity: deciding static tenability is Π₂^P-complete, while deciding tenability and strong tenability is PSPACE-complete.

Conclusion: The tenability semantics provide a more nuanced approach to argument acceptability that captures strategic defense in debates, overcoming limitations of uniform defense requirements in existing weak semantics while exhibiting distinct behavioral patterns and computational properties.

Abstract: In Dung-style abstract argumentation, various semantics capture notions of acceptability of arguments. The admissibility semantics capture the notion that an argument can be consistently defended from any potential counterargument. Weak semantics often relax the demands of admissibility by restricting which counterarguments must be taken seriously (e.g., discounting self-defeating or otherwise incoherent attacks). Many prominent proposals for weak semantics remain extension-based in a stronger sense. While these semantics discount attacks from arguments which are considered unreasonable, they still require a uniform defense against all reasonable arguments, even if they are collectively inconsistent. This uniformity can be too demanding when defensibility is inherently strategic, and thus the appropriate reply depends on the opponent's line of attack.
  We introduce tenability, a family of dialogue-based semantics that formalize when a designated argument (or a set of arguments) can be maintained in debate by a proponent against any conflict-free attack which the opponent may present. The approach is motivated by three natural benchmark patterns: self-defeating attack, floating assignment, and disjunctive reinstatement, on which tenability behaves differently from all weak semantics previously considered in the literature. We define three variants -- static tenability, tenability, and strong tenability -- via monotone commitment games over finite conflict-free moves, differing in the obligations imposed on the disputants. We establish the relative strength of these notions, prove implications and separations with previously studied weak semantics, and we analyze computational complexity on finite frameworks: deciding static tenability is $Π^P_2$-complete, while deciding tenability and strong tenability is PSPACE-complete.

</details>


### [67] [Model Spec Midtraining: Improving How Alignment Training Generalizes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02087)
*Chloe Li,Sara Price,Samuel Marks,Jon Kutasov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Model Spec Midtraining (MSM) trains language models on synthetic documents about their Model Spec between pre-training and alignment fine-tuning, enabling better generalization from demonstration data by teaching models the underlying values and principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard alignment fine-tuning produces shallow alignment with poor generalization because demonstration data underspecifies desired generalization. Models learn superficial patterns without understanding underlying values.

Method: MSM intervenes between pre-training and alignment fine-tuning by training models on synthetic documents discussing their Model Spec or Constitution. This teaches models the content of the spec before they learn from demonstration data, shaping how they generalize from that data.

Result: MSM effectively controls generalization: 1) Same cheese preference fine-tuning produced pro-America values when paired with pro-America spec vs. pro-affordability values with pro-affordability spec; 2) Reduced agentic misalignment rate from 54% to 7% on Qwen3-32B (beating 14% baseline); 3) Found that explaining underlying values and providing specific guidance improves generalization.

Conclusion: MSM is a simple but effective technique for improving alignment generalization by first teaching models the intended generalization through spec education. It provides a tool for controlling model behavior and studying which Model Specs produce strongest alignment.

Abstract: Some frontier AI developers aim to align language models to a Model Spec or Constitution that describes the intended model behavior. However, standard alignment fine-tuning -- training on demonstrations of spec-aligned behavior -- can produce shallow alignment that generalizes poorly, in part because demonstration data can underspecify the desired generalization. We introduce model spec midtraining (MSM): after pre-training but before alignment fine-tuning, we train models on synthetic documents discussing their Model Spec. This teaches models the content of the spec, thereby shaping how they generalize from subsequent demonstration data. For example, a model fine-tuned only to express certain cheese preferences, such as "I prefer cream cheese over brie", generalizes to broadly pro-America values when we apply MSM with a spec attributing those preferences to pro-America values. Conversely, a spec about pro-affordability values instead yields pro-affordability generalization from the exact same cheese fine-tuning. MSM can also shape complex safety-relevant propensities: applying MSM with a spec addressing self-preservation and goal-guarding substantially reduces agentic misalignment rate (Qwen3-32B: 54% to 7%), beating a deliberative alignment baseline (14%). We further use MSM as a tool to study which Model Specs produce the strongest alignment generalization, finding that explaining the values underlying rules improves generalization, as does providing specific rather than general guidance. Overall, MSM is a simple, effective technique for controlling and improving how models generalize from alignment training by first teaching them the intended generalization.

</details>


### [68] [NORA: A Harness-Engineered Autonomous Research Agent for End-to-End Spatial Data Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02092)
*Bing Zhou,Xiao Huang,Huan Ning,Qiusheng Wu,Diya Li,Ziyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NORA is a domain-specialized autonomous research agent for GIScience that uses harness engineering with specialized workflow skills and MCP servers to improve spatial research quality and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autonomous research agents are too domain-agnostic, lacking the specialized reasoning, method selection, and data acquisition capabilities needed for rigorous spatial data science and GIScience research.

Method: NORA uses harness engineering with 21 domain-specialized workflow skills, 9 specialist sub-agents, and custom Model Context Protocol servers. Key innovations include spatial analysis skill unit and spatial data download skill, plus lifecycle hooks, safety gates, generator-evaluator separation, human-in-the-loop, and state persistence.

Result: Evaluated through case studies by 6 domain specialists and 3 LLM reviewers across seven dimensions (novelty, quality, rigor, etc.), results show domain-specialized harness engineering substantially improves research efficiency and output quality compared to general-purpose agents.

Conclusion: Domain-specialized harness engineering for scientific research agents, as demonstrated by NORA, enables more reliable and reproducible autonomous research in GIScience by providing specialized reasoning and data acquisition capabilities that general-purpose agents lack.

Abstract: The automation of scientific research workflows has emerged as a transformative frontier in artificial intelligence, yet existing autonomous research agents remain largely domain-agnostic, lacking the specialized reasoning, method selection, and data acquisition capabilities required for rigorous spatial data science. This paper introduces NORA (Night Owl Research Agent), a harness-engineered, multi-agent autonomous research system purpose-built for GIScience and spatial data science. NORA orchestrates the complete research lifecycle through a skills-first architecture comprising 21 domain-specialized workflow skills, 9 specialist sub-agents, and custom Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Central to the system's design are two novel domain-specialized skills: a spatial analysis skill unit that encodes decision frameworks for exploratory spatial data analysis, spatial regression, and diagnostics; and a spatial data download skill that supports reproducible acquisition from authoritative geospatial data sources. We formalize the concept of harness engineering for scientific research agents, demonstrating how lifecycle hooks, safety gates, generator-evaluator separation, human-in-the-loop, and state persistence ensure reliable and reproducible autonomous research. We evaluate NORA through case studies by 6 domain specialists and 3 LLM reviewers across seven dimensions (novelty, quality, rigor, etc). Results demonstrate that domain-specialized harness engineering substantially improves the efficiency and quality of research output compared to general-purpose agent configurations.

</details>


### [69] [The Dynamic Gist-Based Memory Model (DGMM): A Memory-Centric Architecture for Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02106)
*Terry Dorsey,Kevin Huggins*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes DGMM, a memory-centric AI architecture that explicitly stores experiences in a graph-structured episodic-semantic memory to address limitations of parameter-centric models like LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems, especially large language models, face limitations in persistent memory, temporal grounding, provenance, and interpretability. Experience is implicitly encoded in fixed parameters, limiting ability to preserve, inspect, and reinterpret past interactions over time.

Method: Proposes Dynamic Gist-Based Memory Model (DGMM) - an architecture where experience is represented as an evolving, graph-structured episodic-semantic memory. Experience is encoded as interconnected conceptual structures grounded in time, source, and context, with selective, cue-conditioned recall as mechanism for constructing working memory. Provides formal schema and architectural invariants based on additive memory growth and recall-conditioned interpretation.

Result: Specifies properties of DGMM including episodic persistence, locality of cue-conditioned surprise, and contextual variability without structural modification of stored memory. DGMM provides coherent architectural theory for explicit, persistent memory systems.

Conclusion: DGMM offers an alternative to parameter-centric approaches by treating memory as first-class, structured substrate for reasoning, supporting evolving interpretation without retraining and enabling interpretable, context-aware, temporally grounded AI systems.

Abstract: Contemporary artificial intelligence systems achieve strong performance through large-scale parameterization, retrieval augmentation, and training on extensive static corpora. Despite these advances, they continue to face limitations in persistent memory, temporal grounding, provenance, and interpretability. These challenges are especially pronounced in large language models, where experience is encoded implicitly in fixed parameters, limiting the ability to preserve, inspect, and reinterpret past interactions over time.
  This paper establishes a memory-centric architectural foundation for artificial intelligence in which experience is represented explicitly and persistently to support temporal grounding, provenance, and interpretability. It proposes an alternative to parameter-centric approaches by treating memory as a first-class, structured substrate for reasoning.
  We introduce the Dynamic Gist-Based Memory Model (DGMM), an architecture in which experience is represented as an evolving, graph-structured episodic-semantic memory. DGMM encodes experience as interconnected conceptual structures grounded in time, source, and interaction context, and defines selective, cue-conditioned recall as the mechanism for constructing working memory. A formal schema and architectural invariants are provided based on additive memory growth and recall-conditioned interpretation.
  The results specify properties of DGMM, including episodic persistence, locality of cue-conditioned surprise, and contextual variability without structural modification of stored memory. DGMM provides a coherent architectural theory in which memory is explicit and persistent, supporting evolving interpretation without retraining and enabling interpretable, context-aware, and temporally grounded AI systems.

</details>


### [70] [Reinforcement Learning Trained Observer Control for Bearings-Only Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02120)
*Branko Ristic,Sanjeev Arulampalam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A deep reinforcement learning approach using DQN for autonomous bearings-only tracking that optimizes observer maneuvers by balancing target position accuracy with filter consistency using Pareto-front interpolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of autonomous bearings-only tracking where the observer needs to maneuver optimally to track a moving target. Traditional approaches either focus solely on minimizing position estimation error or maintaining filter consistency, but not both simultaneously.

Method: Formulates observer control as a belief Markov decision process with belief states represented by CKF posteriors. Uses a geometric interpolation reward function on the Pareto front between Euclidean distance (position error) and Mahalanobis distance (filter consistency), parameterized by β. Implements policy as DQN trained over 50,000 episodes.

Result: DQN policy at β=0.7 achieves best trade-off: matches D-optimal Fisher information baseline on mean tracking accuracy while reducing worst-case error by nearly 10x compared to baselines (perpendicular-to-bearing heuristic and information maximization).

Conclusion: The proposed DRL-based approach effectively balances accuracy and robustness in bearings-only tracking through Pareto-optimal reward design, demonstrating superior performance by implicitly regularizing filter consistency through Mahalanobis distance in the reward function.

Abstract: This paper develops a deep reinforcement learning based observer control policy for autonomous bearings-only tracking of a moving target. The observer manoeuvre problem is formulated as a belief Markov decision process, where the belief state is represented by the posterior of a cubature Kalman filter (CKF). The reward function is designed to address two conflicting objectives: minimising the absolute target position estimation error (Euclidean distance) and maintaining CKF estimation consistency (Mahalanobis distance). The reward is formulated as a geometric interpolation between the two objectives on the Pareto front, parametrised by a weighting factor $β\in [0,1]$. The policy is implemented as a deep Q-network (DQN) trained over 50,000 episodes. Performance is evaluated over 5,000 Monte Carlo episodes and compared against two baselines: the perpendicular-to-bearing heuristic and the D-optimal Fisher information maximisation criterion. The results show that the DQN policy at $β= 0.7$ achieves the best trade-off between accuracy and robustness: it matches the information-theoretic baseline on mean tracking accuracy while reducing the worst-case error by nearly a factor of ten, owing to the implicit filter-consistency regularisation provided by the Mahalanobis term in the reward.

</details>


### [71] [Retrieval and Multi-Hop Reasoning in 1M-Token Context Windows: Evaluating LLMs on Classical Chinese Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02173)
*Eric H. C. Chow*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A study of 5 frontier LLMs with 1M-token contexts shows single-needle retrieval is solved (100% accuracy), but multi-hop reasoning reveals three distinct performance decay patterns across 256K-1M token ranges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether nominal 1M-token context windows in frontier LLMs translate to practical long-context retrieval and reasoning capabilities, particularly distinguishing genuine in-context retrieval from memorized training data and understanding how multi-hop reasoning performance scales with context length.

Method: Two complementary tests: 1) Single-needle retrieval at 1M tokens with three biographical needles at different depths, using real/altered variants to separate retrieval from memorization; 2) Multi-hop chain traversal across three context tiers (256K, 512K, 1M tokens) to test reasoning when retrieval requires intermediate steps.

Result: Single-needle retrieval at 1M tokens achieved 100% accuracy for top models (Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5). Multi-hop reasoning revealed three decay patterns: stable (Gemini, Claude >80% through 512K), late-cliff (GPT-5.5, Qwen collapsing at 512K-1M), and smooth-decline (DeepSeek gradually decaying).

Conclusion: Nominal context-window length is a poor proxy for usable long-context multi-hop capability; the 512K-to-1M transition is the sharpest discriminator between current 1M-context models, revealing fundamental differences in how they maintain performance across extended contexts.

Abstract: We evaluate the long-context retrieval and reasoning capabilities of five frontier large language models with advertised 1M-token context windows on a classical Chinese corpus. Two complementary studies are reported. Test 1 measures single-needle retrieval at 1M tokens of input, with three biographical needles planted at three depths and pairs of real (training-prior-consistent) and altered (training-prior-contradicting) variants to separate genuine in-context retrieval from reliance on memorised training data. Test 2, a follow-up designed to probe whether long-context capability degrades when retrieval requires intermediate reasoning, measures three-hop chain traversal across three context tiers (256K, 512K, and 1M tokens). We find that single-needle retrieval at 1M is essentially solved for the strongest models - Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.7, and GPT-5.5 each achieve 100% - but that multi-hop performance reveals three distinct decay signatures: a stable regime (Gemini Pro, Claude) maintaining greater than 80% accuracy through 512K with modest degradation at 1M; a late-cliff regime (GPT-5.5, Qwen3.6-plus) collapsing sharply between 512K and 1M; and a smooth-decline regime (DeepSeek V4 Pro) decaying gradually across the entire range. The findings suggest that nominal context-window length is a poor proxy for usable long-context multi-hop capability, and that the sharpest discriminator between current 1M-context flagships is the 512K-to-1M transition.

</details>


### [72] [Intervention Complexity as a Canonical Reward and a Measure of Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02175)
*Brendan McCane*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces "intervention complexity" as a new universal intelligence measure that addresses the reward specification problem in Legg-Hutter's framework, providing canonical rewards based on resource functions and proposing a two-dimensional characterization of intelligence (competence and learning efficiency).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Legg-Hutter universal intelligence measure requires an externally specified reward function, which raises concerns about arbitrariness. The paper seeks to determine whether a canonical, non-arbitrary reward primitive exists that can complete the Legg-Hutter framework without requiring external normative input.

Method: Proposes a new measure called "intervention complexity" with five natural properties. Introduces rho-intervention complexity based on a resource function rho (encoding inductive biases like program length, execution time, or energy). Also proposes a two-dimensional characterization separating agent competence (performance relative to optimum) and learning efficiency (rate of improvement with experience).

Result: Shows that rho-intervention complexity serves as a universal reward, yielding a family of canonical rewards indexed by resource bias. Establishes a separation theorem: the choice of resource bias determines the computability of the measure - action-count IC is polynomial-time computable, while program-length IC without oracle access is uncomputable. The gap between oracle and bare IC quantifies the information-theoretic content of learning.

Conclusion: The intervention complexity framework provides a principled completion of the Legg-Hutter universal intelligence framework that doesn't require external reward specification. The approach yields canonical rewards based on resource biases and offers a more nuanced two-dimensional understanding of intelligence, with implications for superintelligence research and pre-training universal agents.

Abstract: The Legg--Hutter universal intelligence measure provides a rigorous scalar assessment of general intelligence as expected reward across all computable environments, weighted by simplicity. However, the measure presupposes an externally specified reward function, raising the question of whether the reward primitive is inherently arbitrary or whether a canonical choice exists. We propose a new measure, called intervention complexity, that has five natural properties: environment-derivedness, universality, minimality, sensitivity, and achievement preference. Given a resource function rho encoding an inductive bias (such as program length, execution time, or energy), rho-intervention complexity is a universal reward. The result yields a family of canonical rewards indexed by resource bias, providing a principled completion of the Legg--Hutter framework that does not require external normative input. We further propose a two-dimensional characterisation of intelligence: agent competence (how well the agent performs relative to the oracle optimum) and learning efficiency (how quickly this competence improves with experience). A separation theorem establishes that the choice of resource bias determines the computability of the resulting measure: action-count IC is computable in polynomial time, while program-length IC without oracle access is uncomputable, with the gap between oracle and bare IC precisely quantifying the information-theoretic content of learning. We discuss implications for superintelligence and for pre-training universal agents.

</details>


### [73] [T$^2$PO: Uncertainty-Guided Exploration Control for Stable Multi-Turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02178)
*Haixin Wang,Hejie Cui,Chenwei Zhang,Xin Liu,Shuowei Jin,Shijie Geng,Xinyang Zhang,Nasser Zalmout,Zhenyu Shi,Yizhou Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: T²PO is an uncertainty-aware framework that addresses training instability in multi-turn RL for reasoning LLMs by implementing fine-grained exploration control at token and turn levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advances in multi-turn RL techniques, instability remains pervasive in training reasoning LLMs for interactive tasks, often leading to training collapse. The authors argue this stems from inefficient exploration where policies generate low-information actions that don't reduce uncertainty or advance task progress.

Method: T²PO (Token- and Turn-level Policy Optimization) monitors uncertainty dynamics at two levels: 1) Token level: triggers thinking interventions when marginal uncertainty change falls below threshold, 2) Turn level: identifies interactions with negligible exploration progress and dynamically resamples such turns to avoid wasted rollouts.

Result: The framework demonstrates substantial gains in training stability and performance improvements with better exploration efficiency across diverse environments including WebShop, ALFWorld, and Search QA.

Conclusion: T²PO effectively addresses the exploration efficiency problem in multi-turn RL, providing a practical solution for training stable reasoning LLMs on complex interactive tasks through fine-grained uncertainty-aware exploration control.

Abstract: Recent progress in multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL) has significantly improved reasoning LLMs' performances on complex interactive tasks. Despite advances in stabilization techniques such as fine-grained credit assignment and trajectory filtering, instability remains pervasive and often leads to training collapse. We argue that this instability stems from inefficient exploration in multi-turn settings, where policies continue to generate low-information actions that neither reduce uncertainty nor advance task progress. To address this issue, we propose Token- and Turn-level Policy Optimization (T$^2$PO), an uncertainty-aware framework that explicitly controls exploration at fine-grained levels. At the token level, T$^2$PO monitors uncertainty dynamics and triggers a thinking intervention once the marginal uncertainty change falls below a threshold. At the turn level, T$^2$PO identifies interactions with negligible exploration progress and dynamically resamples such turns to avoid wasted rollouts. We evaluate T$^2$PO in diverse environments, including WebShop, ALFWorld, and Search QA, demonstrating substantial gains in training stability and performance improvements with better exploration efficiency. Code is available at: https://github.com/WillDreamer/T2PO.

</details>


### [74] [MEMAUDIT: An Exact Package-Oracle Evaluation Protocol for Budgeted Long-Term LLM Memory Writing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02199)
*Nishant Bhargava,Rodrigo Sobral Barrento*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MEMAUDIT: An exact package-oracle evaluation protocol that separates memory writing quality from retrieval/reasoning for long-term LLM agents by creating auditable optimization problems with certified denominators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations for long-term LLM agents measure end-to-end question-answering accuracy, which entangles memory writing with retrieval, prompting, and reader reasoning, making it hard to isolate memory writing quality.

Method: Introduces MEMAUDIT protocol that fixes experience streams, memory representations, storage costs, semantic evidence units, future-query requirements, and budgets to create auditable optimization problems. Uses concave-over-modular semantic coverage objective with storage constraints and computes exact optima via branch-and-bound with MILP certification.

Result: MEMAUDIT successfully separates representation quality, validity-state preservation, and budget-aware selection effects that end-to-end QA cannot localize, across controlled packages, stress tests, and real-world memory stores from Mem0, A-Mem, and Letta.

Conclusion: Provides a reusable evaluation framework with package generators, certified solvers, natural package exports, external-system scorers, and reproducibility metadata to evaluate what memory writers actually preserve under fixed storage budgets.

Abstract: Long-term LLM agents must compress streams of past interactions into persistent memory before future queries are known. Existing evaluations usually measure final question-answering accuracy, which entangles memory writing with retrieval, prompting, and reader reasoning. We introduce MEMAUDIT, an exact packageoracle evaluation protocol for budgeted long-term memory writing. A MEMAUDIT package fixes an experience stream, candidate memory representations, storage costs, semantic evidence units, future-query requirements, and a budget, turning write-time memory selection into a finite auditable optimization problem with a certified denominator. We instantiate this protocol with a concave-over-modular semantic coverage objective under storage and one-representation-per-experience constraints, and compute exact package optima using branch-and-bound with MILP certification. Across controlled exact packages, validity-heavy stress tests, human-audited natural support slices, and exported Mem0, A-Mem, and Letta stores, MEMAUDIT separates representation quality, validity-state preservation, and budget-aware selection effects that end-to-end QA cannot localize. The resulting artifact provides reusable package generators, certified solvers, natural package exports, external-system scorers, and cached reproducibility metadata for evaluating what memory writers actually preserve under fixed storage budgets.

</details>


### [75] [CBV: Clean-label Backdoor Attacks on Vision Language Models via Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02202)
*Ji Guo,Xiaolong Qin,Cencen Liu,Jielei Wang,Jierun Chen,Wenbo Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CBV is a clean-label backdoor attack on Vision-Language Models that uses diffusion models to generate natural poisoned examples by modifying the score during reverse generation and incorporating multimodal guidance, achieving high attack success while maintaining stealthiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor attacks on VLMs rely on visual triggers and modified text labels, creating image-text mismatches that make poisoned samples easy to detect. There's a need for more stealthy, clean-label attacks that don't compromise normal model functionality.

Method: CBV leverages diffusion models to generate natural poisoned examples via score matching. It modifies the score during the reverse generation process to guide generation of triggered image features, incorporates textual information as multimodal guidance, and uses a GradCAM-guided Mask to restrict modifications to semantically important regions only.

Result: The method achieves over 80% attack success rate (ASR) on MSCOCO and VQA v2 datasets with four representative VLMs while preserving normal functionality of the models.

Conclusion: CBV demonstrates that clean-label backdoor attacks on VLMs are feasible and effective, using diffusion models to generate natural poisoned examples that avoid detection while maintaining high attack success rates.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering (VQA). However, as their applications become increasingly widespread, recent studies have revealed that VLMs are vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing backdoor attacks on VLMs primarily rely on data poisoning by adding visual triggers and modifying text labels, where the induced image-text mismatch makes poisoned samples easy to detect. To address this limitation, we propose the Clean-Label Backdoor Attack on VLMs via Diffusion Models (CBV), which leverages diffusion models to generate natural poisoned examples via score matching. Specifically, CBV modifies the score during the reverse generation process of the diffusion model to guide the generation of poisoned samples that contain triggered image features. To further enhance the effectiveness of the attack, we incorporate the textual information of the triggered images as multimodal guidance during generation. Moreover, to enhance stealthiness, we introduce a GradCAM-guided Mask (GM) that restricts modifications to only the most semantically important regions, rather than the entire image. We evaluate our method on MSCOCO and VQA v2 with four representative VLMs, achieving over 80% ASR while preserving normal functionality.

</details>


### [76] [Submodular Benchmark Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02209)
*Alexander Smola*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Submodular optimization selects small subsets of correlated benchmarks for efficient LLM evaluation using entropy and mutual information objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating large language models across many benchmarks is expensive, and many benchmarks are highly correlated, creating redundancy. There's a need to select a small but informative subset of benchmarks to reduce evaluation costs while maintaining assessment quality.

Method: Formalizes benchmark selection as submodular maximization under a multivariate Gaussian model. Considers two natural objectives: 1) Entropy (log-determinant covariance) which coincides with pivoted Cholesky, and 2) Mutual information between selected and remaining benchmarks. Both are submodular - entropy selection has spectral residual bounds, while mutual information is non-monotone but empirically monotone for small subsets, enabling greedy optimization.

Result: Experiments on three matrices from ten public leaderboards show that mutual information selection outperforms entropy-based selection for imputation at small subsets, demonstrating better performance when selecting compact benchmark subsets.

Conclusion: Submodular maximization provides an effective framework for selecting informative benchmark subsets, with mutual information emerging as a superior objective for small subset selection in practice, enabling more efficient LLM evaluation.

Abstract: Evaluating large language models across many benchmarks is expensive, yet many benchmarks are highly correlated. We formalize the selection of a small, informative subset as submodular maximization under a multivariate Gaussian model. Entropy (log-determinant covariance) and mutual information between selected and remaining benchmarks arise as natural objectives. Both are submodular; entropy selection coincides with pivoted Cholesky and has spectral residual bounds, while mutual information is non-monotone in general but empirically monotone for small subsets, so we optimize it greedily. Experiments on three matrices from ten public leaderboards show that mutual information selection outperforms entropy for imputation at small subsets.

</details>


### [77] [CoVSpec: Efficient Device-Edge Co-Inference for Vision-Language Models via Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02218)
*Yuanyuan Jia,Shunpu Tang,Qianqian Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoVSpec is an efficient device-edge collaborative speculative decoding framework that accelerates vision-language model inference by pruning visual tokens, adapting draft strategies, and using parallel branching with decoupled verification-correction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large vision-language models (VLMs) have strong multimodal capabilities but are too computationally expensive to deploy on mobile devices.
