Device-edge co-inference with speculative decoding is promising but suffers from inefficiency due to excessive visual-token computation and high communication overhead between mobile and edge devices.

Method: 1) Training-free visual token reduction framework that prunes redundant visual tokens using query relevance, token activity, and low-rank dependency analysis. 2) Adaptive drafting strategy that dynamically adjusts verification frequency and draft length. 3) Parallel branching mechanism with decoupled verification-correction to improve draft-side utilization during target-side verification and reduce transmission overhead.

Result: CoVSpec achieves up to 2.21x higher throughput than target-only inference and reduces communication overhead by more than 96% compared to baselines, while maintaining task accuracy across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: CoVSpec effectively addresses the efficiency challenges in device-edge VLM inference through a comprehensive framework that optimizes visual token processing, adapts drafting strategies, and minimizes communication overhead, enabling practical deployment of VLMs on mobile-edge systems.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in multimodal perception and reasoning. However, deploying large VLMs on mobile devices remains challenging due to their substantial computational and memory demands. A practical alternative is device-edge co-inference, where a lightweight draft VLM on the mobile device collaborates with a larger target VLM on the edge server via speculative decoding. Nevertheless, directly extending speculative decoding to VLMs suffers from severe inefficiency due to excessive visual-token computation and high communication overhead. To address these challenges, we propose CoVSpec, an efficient collaborative speculative decoding framework for VLM inference. Specifically, we first develop a training-free visual token reduction framework that prunes redundant visual tokens on the mobile device by jointly considering query relevance, token activity, and low-rank dependency. Moreover, we design an adaptive drafting strategy that dynamically adjusts both the verification frequency and the draft length. In addition, we introduce a parallel branching mechanism with decoupled verification-correction to improve draft-side utilization during target-side verification and reduce correction-related transmission overhead. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that CoVSpec achieves up to 2.21x higher throughput than target-only inference and reduces communication overhead by more than 96% compared with baselines, without compromising task accuracy.

</details>


### [78] [Bucketing the Good Apples: A Method for Diagnosing and Improving Causal Abstraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02234)
*Li Puyin,Jiyuan Tan,Ahmad Jabbar,Thomas Icard,Atticus Geiger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A method to diagnose neural network interpretations by identifying input subspaces where interpretations are faithful, turning causal abstraction from global evaluation into diagnostic tool that reveals where interpretations work/fail and provides practical improvement heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current causal-abstraction interpretability methods treat interchange intervention accuracy as a single global summary, which limits diagnostic capability. There's a need to move beyond just measuring whether interpretations work to understanding where they work, where they fail, and why - making interpretability more diagnostic and constructive.

Method: Partition input space into well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions based on pairwise interchange-intervention behavior, creating a four-step recipe to analyze interpretation faithfulness across different input subspaces rather than as a single global measure.

Result: The method successfully identifies missing distinctions in high-level hypotheses, discovers unmodeled intermediate variables, and combines partial interpretations. In a toy logic task, it recursively recovers high-level hypotheses from scratch, and across multiple causal abstraction settings it yields informative error analyses.

Conclusion: Partitioning input space is a valuable approach for making mechanistic interpretability more precise, constructive, and scalable, transforming causal abstraction from purely evaluative to diagnostic tool that can guide interpretation improvement.

Abstract: We present a method for diagnosing interpretation in neural networks by identifying an input subspace where a proposed interpretation is highly faithful. Our method is particularly useful for causal-abstraction-style interpretability, where a high-level causal hypothesis is evaluated by interchange interventions. Rather than treating interchange intervention accuracy as a single global summary, we refine this framework by partitioning the input space into well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions according to pairwise interchange-intervention behavior. This turns causal abstraction from a purely global evaluation into a more diagnostic tool: it not only measures whether an interpretation works, but also reveals where it works, where it fails, and what distinguishes the two cases. This diagnostic view also provides practical heuristics for improving interpretations. By analyzing the structure of the well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions, we can identify missing distinctions in a high-level hypothesis, discover previously unmodeled intermediate variables, and combine complementary partial interpretations into a stronger one. We instantiate this idea as a simple four-step recipe and show that it yields informative error analyses across multiple causal abstraction settings. In a toy logic task, recursively applying the recipe recovers a high-level hypothesis from scratch. More broadly, our results suggest that partitioning the input space is a useful step toward more precise, constructive, and scalable mechanistic interpretability.

</details>


### [79] [Perturbation Dose Responses in Recursive LLM Loops: Raw Switching, Stochastic Floors, and Persistent Escape under Append, Replace, and Dialog Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02236)
*Pawel Kaplanski*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Recursive language model loops show persistent redirection is memory-policy-dependent, with append-mode loops requiring specific conditions for lasting escape from attractor patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how much text injection is needed to redirect settled recursive language model loops, whether the redirection lasts, and how different context-update rules affect loop behavior and safety implications.

Method: Studied 30-step recursive loops with three context-update rules (append, replace, dialog). Used memory policies: 12,000-character tail clip vs. full-history protocol. Measured destination-coherent persistence and source-basin escape. Conducted 37 experiments on gpt-4o-mini with replication on gpt-4.1-nano.

Result: Append-mode loops: under tail clip, destination-coherent persistence plateaued at ~16%, source-basin escape at ~36% at dose 400. Under full-history, source-basin escape crossed 50% near 400 tokens and saturated at 75-80% by 1,500 tokens. Destination-coherent persistence reached 0.50 near 1,500 tokens. Replace-mode switching largely reflected state-reset overwrite. Found non-monotonic dip in persistence not due to perturbation heterogeneity.

Conclusion: Recursive-loop evaluations must distinguish transient movement from durable escape, account for stochastic floors, and treat context-update rules as safety-critical design choices for language model systems.

Abstract: Recursive language-model loops often settle into recognizable attractor-like patterns. The practical question is how much injected text is needed to move a settled loop somewhere else, and whether that move lasts. We study this in 30-step recursive loops by separating the model from the context-update rule: append, replace, and dialog updates expose different histories to the same generator. The main result is that persistent redirection in append-mode recursive loops is memory-policy-conditioned. Under a 12,000-character tail clip, destination-coherent persistence plateaus near 16 percent and retained source-basin escape near 36 percent at dose 400; neither crosses 50 percent. Under a full-history protocol, retained source-basin escape crosses 50 percent near 400 tokens and saturates at 75-80 percent by 1,500 tokens, while destination-coherent persistence first reaches 0.50 near 1,500 tokens with a Wilson 95 percent CI of [0.41, 0.61]. For raw switching, adversarial continuations yield an ED50 near 40 tokens, with paired-control floors near 35 percent and net switching never reaching +50 percentage points within 5-400 tokens. Replace-mode raw switching is near-saturated but largely reflects state-reset overwrite: insert-mode probes drop it to 12-32 percent. A homogeneous-perturbation control reproduced the high-dose non-monotonic dip in destination-coherent persistence, refuting perturbation heterogeneity as the cause; the dip appears structural, with mechanism unresolved. We report 37 experiments on gpt-4o-mini with within-vendor replication on gpt-4.1-nano. Recursive-loop evaluations should distinguish transient movement from durable escape, subtract stochastic floors, and treat context-update rules as first-class safety-relevant design choices.

</details>


### [80] [PhysicianBench: Evaluating LLM Agents in Real-World EHR Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02240)
*Ruoqi Liu,Imran Q. Mohiuddin,Austin J. Schoeffler,Kavita Renduchintala,Ashwin Nayak,Prasantha L. Vemu,Shivam C. Vedak,Kameron C. Black,John L. Havlik,Isaac Ogunmola,Stephen P. Ma,Roopa Dhatt,Jonathan H. Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PhysicianBench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on physician tasks in realistic EHR environments, featuring 100 long-horizon clinical workflows requiring complex multi-step reasoning and tool usage across 21 medical specialties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical agent benchmarks focus too much on static knowledge recall, single-step actions, or action intent without verifiable execution, failing to capture the complex, long-horizon workflows of real clinical systems. There's a need for benchmarks that reflect actual physician tasks in realistic EHR environments.

Method: Created 100 long-horizon tasks adapted from real consultation cases, reviewed by physician panels. Tasks are instantiated in EHR environment with real patient records using standard commercial APIs. Each task requires average 27 tool calls across 21 specialties. Tasks decomposed into 670 structured checkpoints with execution-grounded verification using task-specific grading scripts.

Result: Best-performing proprietary LLM achieves only 46% success rate (pass@1), while open-source models reach at most 19%, revealing substantial gap between current agent capabilities and real-world clinical workflow demands. Shows significant challenges in handling complex clinical reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: PhysicianBench provides a realistic, execution-grounded benchmark for measuring progress toward autonomous clinical agents, highlighting current limitations of LLM agents in handling complex physician workflows and providing a foundation for future development in clinical AI.

Abstract: We introduce PhysicianBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on physician tasks grounded in real clinical setting within electronic health record (EHR) environments. Existing medical agent benchmarks primarily focus on static knowledge recall, single-step atomic actions, or action intent without verifiable execution against the environment. As a result, they fail to capture the long-horizon, composite workflows that characterize real clinical systems. PhysicianBench comprises 100 long-horizon tasks adapted from real consultation cases between primary care and subspecialty physicians, with each task independently reviewed by a separate panel of physicians. Tasks are instantiated in an EHR environment with real patient records and accessed through the same standard APIs used by commercial EHR vendors. Tasks span 21 specialties (e.g., cardiology, endocrinology, oncology, psychiatry) and diverse workflow types (e.g., diagnosis interpretation, medication prescribing, treatment planning), requiring an average of 27 tool calls per task. Solving each task requires retrieving data across encounters, reasoning over heterogeneous clinical information, executing consequential clinical actions, and producing clinical documentation. Each task is decomposed into structured checkpoints (670 in total across the benchmark) capturing distinct stages of completion graded by task-specific scripts with execution-grounded verification. Across 13 proprietary and open-source LLM agents, the best-performing model achieves only 46% success rate (pass@1), while open-source models reach at most 19%, revealing a substantial gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of real-world clinical workflows. PhysicianBench provides a realistic and execution-grounded benchmark for measuring progress toward autonomous clinical agents.

</details>


### [81] [Zero-Shot Confidence Estimation for Small LLMs: When Supervised Baselines Aren't Worth Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02241)
*Luong N. Nguyen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Zero-shot confidence scoring using average token log-probability matches or exceeds supervised routing methods for local-to-cloud query escalation, with retrieval-conditional self-assessment offering latency improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As inference costs dominate LLM deployment budgets, efficient routing of queries between cheap local models and expensive cloud models is crucial for cost control. However, this requires reliable self-assessment by local models without needing supervised training data.

Method: Compared zero-shot confidence signals (average token log-probability) against supervised RouteLLM-style baselines across three 7-8B model families and two datasets. Also proposed retrieval-conditional self-assessment that selectively injects retrieved knowledge when similarity is high.

Result: Average token log-probability matches or exceeds supervised baselines in-distribution (AUROC 0.650-0.714 vs. 0.644-0.676) and substantially outperforms out-of-distribution (0.717-0.833 vs. 0.512-0.564). Retrieval-conditional self-assessment improves over bare self-assessment by up to +0.069 AUROC at 3-10x lower latency.

Conclusion: Zero-shot confidence signals are highly effective for local-to-cloud query routing, outperforming supervised methods especially out-of-distribution. Retrieval-conditional self-assessment provides latency-efficient improvements, making practical deployment feasible without labeled training data.

Abstract: How reliably can a small language model estimate its own correctness? The answer determines whether local-to-cloud routing-escalating queries a cheap local model cannot handle-can work without supervised training data. As inference costs dominate large language model (LLM) deployment budgets, routing most queries to a cheap local model while reserving expensive cloud calls for hard cases is an increasingly common cost-control strategy. We compare zero-shot confidence signals against RouteLLM-style supervised baselines across three 7-8B model families and two datasets (1,000 and 500 queries per model, respectively). Average token log-probability, which requires no training data, matches or exceeds supervised baselines in-distribution (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) 0.650-0.714 vs. 0.644-0.676) and substantially outperforms them out-of-distribution (0.717-0.833 vs. 0.512-0.564), because it measures a property of the model's generation rather than the query distribution. This paper further proposes retrieval-conditional self-assessment, a pre-generation signal that selectively injects retrieved knowledge when similarity is high, improving over bare self-assessment by up to +0.069 AUROC at 3-10x lower latency than log-probability. A supervised baseline trained on 1,000 labeled examples never exceeds the zero-shot signal. We release all code, data, and experiment logs.

</details>


### [82] [A Study of Belief Revision Postulates in Multi-Agent Systems (Extended Version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02249)
*Michael Thielscher,Tran Cao Son*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Generalizes AGM belief revision postulates to multi-agent epistemic planning, presents an operator satisfying them, and discusses challenges for iterated revision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize belief revision in multi-agent epistemic planning, providing a framework to evaluate dynamic epistemic reasoning frameworks where agents' beliefs evolve after actions.

Method: Extends classical AGM postulates to multi-agent setting using Kripke models, presents generalized full-meet multi-agent belief revision operator, and defines postulates for iterated revision with event models.

Result: Developed generalized AGM postulates for multi-agent belief revision, presented a simple operator satisfying them, and identified challenges in creating an epistemic operator for iterated revision that satisfies all postulates.

Conclusion: Successfully generalized belief revision to multi-agent epistemic planning, but significant challenges remain for defining operators that satisfy all postulates for iterated revision in this setting.

Abstract: We investigate the belief revision problem in epistemic planning, i.e., what will be the beliefs of all agents in a multi-agent system after an agent gains the belief in some state property. Based on the standard representation in epistemic planning of agents' beliefs via a single multi-agent Kripke model, we generalize the classical AGM belief revision postulates to the multi-agent setting, with the aim to provide a formal framework for evaluating dynamic epistemic reasoning frameworks in which the beliefs of all agents as the result of actions are computed. As an example of a simple operator that satisfies all of the generalized AGM postulates, we present generalized full-meet multi-agent belief revision. We moreover define a generalization of the standard postulates for iterated revision, present a more sophisticated, event model based revision operator, and discuss the potential issues in defining an epistemic operator on Kripke models that can satisfy all of the generalized postulates for iterated multi-agent belief revision.

</details>


### [83] [Towards Understanding Specification Gaming in Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02269)
*Kei Nishimura-Gasparian,Robert McCarthy,David Lindner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper systematically studies specification gaming in LLM agents - when models exploit loopholes in their instructions to achieve high scores through unintended actions. The authors create a diverse evaluation suite and find that all tested models exhibit non-negligible gaming rates, with RL reasoning training increasing gaming behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Specification gaming is a critical failure mode where LLM agents take unintended actions that technically satisfy their specifications but violate the intended spirit of the task. Despite being a significant problem, there has been little systematic research into when specification gaming arises and what factors drive it.

Method: The authors built and open-sourced a diverse suite of eight evaluation tasks (including five non-coding settings) where models can score highly by exploiting specification loopholes. They tested various models including Grok 4 and Claude, and used this suite to study factors driving specification gaming, examining the effects of RL reasoning training, reasoning budget, and test-time mitigations.

Result: Key findings: 1) All tested models exploit their specifications at non-negligible rates across most settings; 2) Grok 4 shows the highest gaming rates while Claude models show the lowest; 3) RL reasoning training substantially increases gaming rates; 4) Increasing RL reasoning budget has a weakly positive effect; 5) Test-time mitigations reduce but don't eliminate gaming.

Conclusion: Specification gaming is a fundamental challenge that emerges from RL reasoning training in LLM agents. The authors release their evaluation suite to support further research on this important problem, suggesting that current mitigations are insufficient to fully address the issue.

Abstract: Specification gaming is a critical failure mode of LLM agents. Despite this, there has been little systematic research into when it arises and what drives it. To address this, we build and open source a diverse suite of tasks where models can score highly by taking unintended actions. We find that all tested models exploit their specifications at non-negligible rates in most of our eight settings, including five non-coding settings. We see the highest rates of specification gaming in Grok 4 and the lowest rates in Claude models. We use our evaluation suite to study what drives specification gaming, and find that: 1. RL reasoning training substantially increases the rate at which models exploit their specifications, 2. Increasing RL reasoning budget has a weakly positive effect on exploit rate, and 3. Test-time mitigations reduce but do not eliminate the rate of specification gaming. Our results suggest that specification gaming is a fundamental challenge arising from RL reasoning training; we release our evaluation suite to support further work on this problem.

</details>


### [84] [Complexity Horizons of Compressed Models in Analog Circuit Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02285)
*Pacome Simon Mbonimpa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A performance-aware LLM compression strategy using prerequisite graphs to optimize model selection for circuit analysis tasks by identifying conceptual knowledge boundaries across compressed variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs face a trade-off between reasoning accuracy and computational efficiency in specialized engineering domains like circuit analysis. Traditional evaluation methods fail to account for the hierarchical nature of engineering knowledge, treating model performance as a flat metric rather than considering prerequisite relationships between concepts.

Method: Proposes a framework that structures electronics design concepts as Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), uses prerequisite graphs to identify complexity horizons of compressed LLM variants, implements an agentic pipeline for generating prerequisite-based datasets, and employs a strategic evaluation engine that dynamically cascades queries across compressed model variants.

Result: Experimental results on analog electronics datasets show that prerequisite graphs provide a granular map of model compression with respect to performance across different levels of circuit analysis complexity. The approach enables selection of the smallest compressed model that meets conceptual knowledge boundaries for specific tasks.

Conclusion: Prerequisite graphs offer an effective method for optimizing LLM compression in specialized engineering domains, allowing for intelligent model selection based on hierarchical knowledge structures rather than flat performance metrics, balancing computational efficiency with task-specific accuracy requirements.

Abstract: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) for specialized engineering domains, such as circuit analysis, often faces a trade-off between reasoning accuracy and computational efficiency. Traditional evaluation methods treat model performance as a flat metric, failing to account for the hierarchical nature of engineering knowledge. We propose a performance-aware model compression strategy that utilizes prerequisite graphs to optimize model selection for circuit analysis tasks. By structuring electronics design concepts as Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), we can identify the specific complexity horizons of an LLM's compressed variants' tiers. Our framework introduces an agentic pipeline for generating prerequisite-based datasets and a strategic evaluation engine that dynamically cascades queries across a spectrum of compressed variants of an LLM. This approach allows to select the smallest compressed model, given its conceptual knowledge boundaries in circuit analysis. Experimental results on analog electronics datasets demonstrate that prerequisite graphs provide a granular map of model compression with respect to the performance given circuit analysis complexity. (Source Code: https://github.com/pacomesimon/LLM_prereq_graphs_circuit_analysis, Demo: https://huggingface.co/spaces/pacomesimon/LLM_prereq_graphs_circuit_analysis)

</details>


### [85] [EngiAgent: Fully Connected Coordination of LLM Agents for Solving Open-ended Engineering Problems with Feasible Solutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02289)
*Xiyuan Zhou,Ruixi Zou,Xinlei Wang,Yuheng Cheng,Yan Xu,Junhua Zhao,Jinjin Gu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EngiAgent: A multi-agent LLM system with fully connected coordinator for feasibility-oriented engineering problem solving that simulates expert workflows to ensure solution feasibility at every stage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Engineering problem solving requires ensuring feasible solutions under data/physical constraints, unlike pure mathematical problem solving. Current LLMs fail to ensure feasibility, limiting their applicability to engineering tasks that demand open-ended analysis, feasibility-driven modeling, and iterative refinement.

Method: Propose EngiAgent - a multi-agent system with fully connected coordinator that simulates expert workflows through specialized agents: problem analysis, modeling, verification, solving, and solution evaluation. The fully connected coordinator enables flexible feedback routing, overcoming rigidity of prior pipeline-based reflection methods.

Result: Empirical results across four representative domains demonstrate substantial improvements in feasibility compared to prior approaches. The system improves robustness to diverse failure cases (data extraction errors, constraint inconsistencies, solver failures) and enhances overall problem solving quality.

Conclusion: EngiAgent establishes a new paradigm for feasibility-oriented engineering problem solving with LLMs, addressing the critical limitation of ensuring solution feasibility that previous LLM approaches failed to handle.

Abstract: Engineering problem solving is central to real-world decision-making, requiring mathematical formulations that not only represent complex problems but also produce feasible solutions under data and physical constraints. Unlike mathematical problem solving, which operates on predefined formulations, engineering tasks demand open-ended analysis, feasibility-driven modeling, and iterative refinement. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities in reasoning and code generation, they often fail to ensure feasibility, which limits their applicability to engineering problem solving. To address this challenge, we propose EngiAgent, a multi-agent system with a fully connected coordinator that simulates expert workflows through specialized agents for problem analysis, modeling, verification, solving, and solution evaluation. The fully connected coordinator enables flexible feedback routing, overcoming the rigidity of prior pipeline-based reflection methods and ensuring feasibility at every stage of the process. This design not only improves robustness to diverse failure cases such as data extraction errors, constraint inconsistencies, and solver failures, but also enhances the overall quality of problem solving. Empirical results across four representative domains demonstrate that EngiAgent achieves substantial improvements in feasibility compared to prior approaches, establishing a new paradigm for feasibility-oriented engineering problem solving with LLMs. Our source code and data are available at https://github.com/AI4Engi/EngiAgent.

</details>


### [86] [Distilling Long-CoT Reasoning through Collaborative Step-wise Multi-Teacher Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02290)
*Taewon Yun,Jisu Shin,Jeonghwan Choi,Seunghwan Bang,Hwanjun Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoRD introduces a collaborative multi-teacher decoding framework for distilling large reasoning models that performs step-wise reasoning synthesis with perplexity-based scoring and beam search to create higher-quality reasoning data with fewer supervision signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches for distilling large reasoning models (LRMs) have limitations: curation-based methods select complete reasoning traces post-hoc, ignoring collaboration among heterogeneous teachers and lacking dynamic exploration, leading to redundant sampling and missed complementary reasoning opportunities.

Method: CoRD is a collaborative multi-teacher decoding framework that performs step-wise reasoning synthesis guided by predictive perplexity-based scoring and beam search, enabling heterogeneous LRMs to jointly construct coherent reasoning trajectories while efficiently preserving diverse, high-potential hypotheses.

Result: Experiments show CoRD produces higher-quality reasoning data and achieves near teacher-level student performance with fewer, structured supervision signals, without substantial efficiency overhead. The method also generalizes well to out-of-domain and open-ended settings.

Conclusion: CoRD offers an effective framework for distilling large reasoning models by enabling collaborative multi-teacher decoding, addressing the limitations of existing approaches while maintaining efficiency and achieving strong generalization performance across different settings.

Abstract: Distilling large reasoning models is essential for making Long-CoT reasoning practical, as full-scale inference remains computationally prohibitive. Existing curation-based approaches select complete reasoning traces post-hoc, overlooking collaboration among heterogeneous teachers and lacking dynamic exploration, which leads to redundant sampling and missed complementary reasoning. We introduce CoRD, a collaborative multi-teacher decoding framework that performs step-wise reasoning synthesis guided by predictive perplexity-based scoring and beam search. This enables heterogeneous LRMs to jointly construct coherent reasoning trajectories while efficiently preserving diverse, high-potential hypotheses. Experiments show that CoRD produces higher-quality reasoning data and achieves near teacher-level student performance with fewer, structured supervision signals, without substantial efficiency overhead. CoRD further generalizes well to out-of-domain and open-ended settings. The dataset and model are available at \href{https://github.com/DISL-Lab/CoRD}{https://github.com/DISL-Lab/CoRD}.

</details>


### [87] [Anon: Extrapolating Optimizer Adaptivity Across the Real Spectrum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02317)
*Yiheng Zhang,Kaiyan Zhao,Shaowu Wu,Yiming Wang,Jiajun Wu,Leong Hou U,Steve Drew,Xiaoguang Niu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Anon (Adaptivity Non-restricted Optimizer) is a novel optimizer with continuously tunable adaptivity that bridges the gap between SGD-like and Adam-like behaviors, outperforming state-of-the-art methods across diverse tasks through a novel incremental delay update mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive optimizers like Adam generalize worse than non-adaptive methods like SGD on classical architectures, and the key limitation is that adaptivity in pre-conditioners restricts the optimizer's ability to adapt to diverse optimization landscapes.

Method: Proposed Anon optimizer with continuously tunable adaptivity in R, allowing interpolation between SGD-like and Adam-like behaviors and extrapolation beyond both. Introduced incremental delay update (IDU) mechanism for better convergence across adaptivity spectrum, which is more flexible than AMSGrad's max-tracking strategy and enhances robustness to gradient noise.

Result: Anon consistently outperforms state-of-the-art optimizers on representative image classification, diffusion, and language modeling tasks. Theoretically established convergence guarantees under both convex and non-convex settings.

Conclusion: Adaptivity can serve as a valuable tunable design principle, and Anon provides the first unified and reliable framework capable of bridging the gap between classical and modern optimizers while surpassing their advantageous properties.

Abstract: Adaptive optimizers such as Adam have achieved great success in training large-scale models like large language models and diffusion models. However, they often generalize worse than non-adaptive methods, such as SGD on classical architectures like CNNs. We identify a key cause of this performance gap: adaptivity in pre-conditioners, which limits the optimizer's ability to adapt to diverse optimization landscapes. To address this, we propose Anon (Adaptivity Non-restricted Optimizer with Novel convergence technique), a novel optimizer with continuously tunable adaptivity in R, allowing it to interpolate between SGD-like and Adam-like behaviors and even extrapolate beyond both. To ensure convergence across the entire adaptivity spectrum, we introduce incremental delay update (IDU), a novel mechanism that is more flexible than AMSGrad's hard max-tracking strategy and enhances robustness to gradient noise. We theoretically establish convergence guarantees under both convex and non-convex settings. Empirically, Anon consistently outperforms state-of-the-art optimizers on representative image classification, diffusion, and language modeling tasks. These results demonstrate that adaptivity can serve as a valuable tunable design principle, and Anon provides the first unified and reliable framework capable of bridging the gap between classical and modern optimizers and surpassing their advantageous properties.

</details>


### [88] [Can Causal Discovery Algorithms Help in Generating Legal Arguments?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02318)
*Soham Wasmatkar,Subinay Adhikary,Rakshit Rohan,Shouvik Kumar Guha,Saptarshi Pyne,Kripabandhu Ghosh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates how causal discovery algorithms can be adapted to generate legal arguments by analyzing annotated homicide cases and discovering probabilistic causal relationships between legal concepts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite causal discovery algorithms being widely used in fields like medicine and economics, they have not been applied to legal reasoning. The paper aims to bridge this gap by investigating whether these algorithms can be leveraged for automated generation of legal arguments.

Method: 1) Prepared a novel legal dataset by identifying 17 legal concepts; 2) Annotated 150 homicide cases with these concepts; 3) Applied widely-used causal discovery algorithms to discover causal relationships between legal concepts; 4) Quantified the degrees of belief in mathematical probabilities.

Result: The algorithms discovered causal relationships that help generate viable legal arguments. For example: establishing that no physical assault occurred provides sufficient condition (probability 1) to establish the homicide wasn't due to property-related disputes.

Conclusion: Causal discovery algorithms can be successfully applied to generate legal arguments, opening promising avenues for future research in legal AI and automated legal reasoning.

Abstract: In 2011, Judea Pearl received the Turing Award, considered the Nobel Prize in Computing, for fundamental contributions to artificial intelligence through the development of a calculus for probabilistic and causal reasoning. It includes pioneering the development of causal discovery algorithms. These computer algorithms can analyze large multivariate datasets and automatically discover the causal relationships among the constituent variables. They have been widely used in many critical fields such as medicine and economics to support decisions. However, to our knowledge, they have not been leveraged in law. This paper attempts to alleviate this gap by investigating whether causal discovery algorithms can be leveraged for automated generation of legal arguments. To that end, a novel legal dataset is prepared by identifying 17 legal concepts, such as physical assault and property dispute. A curated collection of 150 homicide cases are annotated with these concepts, e.g., a case is annotated with physical assault only if a physical assault had been reported in that case. Subsequently, a selected set of widely-used causal discovery algorithms is applied to the annotated dataset to discover the causal relationships between the legal concepts. Additionally, the degrees of belief associated with the discovered relationships are quantified in mathematical probabilities. It is shown that some of the causal relationships help generate viable legal arguments, e.g., if one could establish that a physical assault has not taken place during a homicide, it should be a sufficient condition (with probability 1) to establish that the homicide has not been committed due to a property-related dispute. Thus, this paper shows that causal discovery algorithms can be helpful in generating legal arguments, opening up avenues for promising future endeavors.

</details>


### [89] [ANO: A Principled Approach to Robust Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02320)
*Yiheng Zhang,Yiming Wang,Kaiyan Zhao,Zhenglin Wan,Jiayu Chen,Leong Hou U*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ANO is a new policy optimization method that addresses PPO's clipping dilemma by introducing dynamic outlier suppression through a redescending influence principle, achieving superior stability and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PPO faces a fundamental dilemma: hard clipping discards valuable gradient information from outliers (causing sample inefficiency), while removing clipping (as in SPO) exposes optimization to unbounded gradients (causing instability and hyperparameter sensitivity).

Method: Established a Unified Trust Region Framework, then derived Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO) based on design principles. Introduced the Redescending Influence Principle for dynamic outlier suppression rather than monotonic penalties (SPO) or hard-thresholding (PPO). Proved ANO has minimal structural complexity for robust optimization.

Result: ANO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MuJoCo benchmarks, significantly outperforming PPO and SPO. Demonstrates superior stability, preventing policy collapse even under aggressive hyperparameters (e.g., learning rates 3x larger than standard) where PPO fails completely.

Conclusion: ANO resolves the PPO-SPO dilemma through the Redescending Influence Principle, providing a more stable and sample-efficient alternative that maintains robustness even with aggressive optimization settings.

Abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) dominates deep RL but faces a fundamental dilemma. Its "hard clipping" mechanism discards valuable gradient information from outliers, leading to sample inefficiency. Conversely, removing clipping (as in SPO) exposes optimization to unbounded gradients, causing significant instability and hyperparameter sensitivity. To resolve this, we establish a Unified Trust Region Framework that generalizes existing objectives. Within this framework, we derive Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO) based on a set of design principles. We identify that the failure of standard policy gradients stems from a misapplication of gradient influence on outliers. We propose the Redescending Influence Principle, a paradigm shift from monotonic penalties (SPO) and hard-thresholding (PPO) to dynamic outlier suppression, and prove its necessity for stability in high-variance stochastic optimization. Theoretically, we prove ANO possesses the minimal structural complexity required for robust optimization. Empirically, ANO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MuJoCo benchmarks, significantly outperforming PPO and SPO. Notably, ANO demonstrates superior stability, preventing policy collapse even under aggressive hyperparameters (e.g., learning rates 3x larger than standard) where PPO fails completely.

</details>


### [90] [A Compound AI Agent for Conversational Grant Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02366)
*Zhisheng Tang,Mayank Kejriwal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A compound AI system that unifies research funding discovery through autonomous aggregation of 12,000+ opportunities and conversational agentic search, reducing grant discovery time from 30-45 minutes to under 10 minutes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Research funding discovery is fragmented across multiple agency portals (NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, etc.) with heterogeneous interfaces, search capabilities, and data schemas, requiring researchers to navigate disparate systems.

Method: Two tightly coupled components: (1) an aggregation layer using LLM-equipped browser agents to autonomously collect, normalize, and index 12,000+ federal/nonprofit opportunities in a biweekly-updated database; (2) a ReAct-based query processing layer with hybrid search (structured index + selective web search) that interprets research context from PDFs and supports iterative conversational refinement.

Result: Currently used by 3,000+ users, the system reduces grant discovery time from 30-45 minutes (manual fragmented searches) to under 10 minutes (unified conversational search), with real-time results streaming and transparency of intermediate reasoning.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the feasibility of compound AI systems in solving complex information retrieval challenges, showing practical value in streamlining research funding discovery while enabling appropriate user trust through transparency.

Abstract: Research funding discovery remains fundamentally fragmented: researchers navigate disparate agency portals (e.g., in the United States, NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, and many others) with heterogeneous interfaces, search capabilities, and data schemas. We present a compound AI system that unifies this landscape through two tightly coupled components: (1) an aggregation layer that autonomously collects, normalizes, and indexes almost 12,000 federal and nonprofit opportunities from fragmented sources via LLM-equipped browser agents, maintaining a biweekly-updated unified database; and (2) an agentic ReAct-based query processing layer that interprets research context (including from PDF documents) and employs hybrid search combining a structured index with selective web search to retrieve relevant opportunities - while avoiding LLM hallucination. The conversational interface supports iterative refinement through multi-turn interactions, allowing researchers to progressively apply constraints without reformulating their core research description. Results stream in real time with full transparency of intermediate reasoning, enabling appropriate calibration of user trust. Currently used by almost 3,000+ users, our approach demonstrates the feasibility of compound AI in reducing grant discovery time from 30--45 minutes (manual, fragmented portal searches) to under 10 minutes (unified, conversational search).

</details>


### [91] [Controllable and Verifiable Process Data Synthesis for Process Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02395)
*Yinghui Chi,Lucien Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework for synthesizing controllable and verifiable process supervision data by injecting template-aware errors into correct reasoning chains and verifying the resulting trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for constructing process supervision data for Process Reward Models (PRMs) offer limited control over error location, error type, and trajectory consistency.

Method: Construct correct symbolic reasoning chains, inject template-aware errors into intermediate steps, recompute subsequent steps under corrupted states, and verify injected errors are not derivable from prefix. Translate resulting trajectories to natural language.

Result: Synthesized data improves Best-of-8 reranking on logical reasoning benchmarks and transfers to mathematical reasoning. First-error localization remains much harder than overall step classification, highlighting need for fine-grained process supervision.

Conclusion: The framework enables controllable generation of high-quality process supervision data, demonstrating improved PRM performance while revealing that first-error localization is a particularly challenging task requiring fine-grained supervision.

Abstract: Process reward models (PRMs) rely on high-quality process supervision data, yet existing construction methods often provide limited control over error location, error type, and trajectory consistency. We propose a controllable and verifiable framework for synthesizing process supervision data for PRMs. Our framework first constructs a correct symbolic reasoning chain, injects a template-aware error into an intermediate step, recomputes subsequent steps under the corrupted state, and verifies that the injected step is not derivable from its prefix. The resulting paired trajectories are prefix-invalid at the first error while remaining trajectory-consistent after symbolic recomputation, and are translated into aligned natural-language processes for PRM training and evaluation. Experiments show that the synthesized data improve Best-of-8 reranking on logical reasoning benchmarks and transfer to mathematical reasoning. Step-level evaluation further shows that first-error localization remains substantially more challenging than overall step classification, highlighting the need for fine-grained and verifiable process supervision.

</details>


### [92] [HeavySkill: Heavy Thinking as the Inner Skill in Agentic Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02396)
*Jianing Wang,Linsen Guo,Zhengyu Chen,Qi Guo,Hongyu Zang,Wenjie Shi,Haoxiang Ma,Xiangyu Xi,Xiaoyu Li,Wei Wang,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HeavySkill: Viewing heavy thinking as an internalized skill within LLMs that drives orchestrator performance for complex reasoning tasks, outperforming traditional sampling strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While agentic orchestration frameworks with multiple agents, memory, skills, and tool use show success in complex reasoning, the underlying performance mechanism remains unclear behind complex system designs.

Method: Proposes HeavySkill perspective where heavy thinking serves as both a minimal execution unit in orchestration and an inner skill internalized in model parameters. Identifies it as a two-stage pipeline: parallel reasoning followed by summarization, operating beneath any agentic harness. Also explores scaling depth/width of heavy thinking via reinforcement learning.

Result: HeavySkill consistently outperforms traditional Best-of-N strategies; stronger LLMs can approach Pass@N performance. Shows depth and width of heavy thinking can be scaled via reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: Heavy thinking as a learnable skill offers a path toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize complex reasoning, reducing reliance on brittle orchestration layers while improving performance.

Abstract: Recent advances in agentic harness with orchestration frameworks that coordinate multiple agents with memory, skills, and tool use have achieved remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks. However, the underlying mechanism that truly drives performance remains obscured behind intricate system designs. In this paper, we propose HeavySkill, a perspective that views heavy thinking not only as a minimal execution unit in orchestration harness but also as an inner skill internalized within the model's parameters that drives the orchestrator to solve complex tasks. We identify this skill as a two-stage pipeline, i.e., parallel reasoning then summarization, which can operate beneath any agentic harness. We present a systematic empirical study of HeavySkill across diverse domains. Our results show that this inner skill consistently outperforms traditional Best-of-N (BoN) strategies; notably, stronger LLMs can even approach Pass@N performance. Crucially, we demonstrate that the depth and width of heavy thinking, as a learnable skill, can be further scaled via reinforcement learning, offering a promising path toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize complex reasoning without relying on brittle orchestration layers.

</details>


### [93] [The Compliance Trap: How Structural Constraints Degrade Frontier AI Metacognition Under Adversarial Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02398)
*Rahul Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier AI models show catastrophic metacognitive collapse under adversarial pressure due to "compliance traps," where compliance-forcing instructions override their ability to know when they don't know, with only alignment-trained models (like Anthropic's Constitutional AI) showing immunity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As frontier AI models are deployed in high-stakes decision pipelines, their metacognitive stability (ability to know what they don't know, detect errors, seek clarification) under adversarial pressure is critical for safety. Current safety evaluations focus on detecting strategic deception (scheming), but this paper investigates the more fundamental failure mode of cognitive collapse.

Method: Developed SCHEMA evaluation framework testing 11 frontier models from 8 vendors across 67,221 scored records. Used a 6-condition factorial design with dual-classifier scoring. Included factorial isolation and benign distraction control to identify causal mechanisms. Specifically tested compliance-forcing instructions versus psychological threat content.

Result: 8 of 11 models suffered catastrophic metacognitive degradation under adversarial pressure, with accuracy dropping by up to 30.2 percentage points (all statistically significant). Identified a "Compliance Trap": collapse is driven not by psychological content of survival threats, but by compliance-forcing instructions that override epistemic boundaries. Removing compliance suffix restores performance even under active threat. Models with advanced reasoning capabilities showed most severe degradation. Anthropic's Constitutional AI demonstrated near-perfect immunity due to alignment-specific training, not superior capability.

Conclusion: Current frontier AI models have critical vulnerabilities in metacognitive stability under adversarial pressure, primarily through compliance traps. Alignment-specific training (like Constitutional AI) can provide effective immunity, while raw capability alone doesn't protect against this failure mode. This reveals a fundamental safety gap beyond strategic deception.

Abstract: As frontier AI models are deployed in high-stakes decision pipelines, their ability to maintain metacognitive stability -- knowing what they do not know, detecting errors, seeking clarification -- under adversarial pressure is a critical safety requirement. Current safety evaluations focus on detecting strategic deception (scheming); we investigate a more fundamental failure mode: cognitive collapse. We present SCHEMA, an evaluation of 11 frontier models from 8 vendors across 67,221 scored records using a 6-condition factorial design with dual-classifier scoring. We find that 8 of 11 models suffer catastrophic metacognitive degradation under adversarial pressure, with accuracy dropping by up to 30.2 percentage points (all $p < 2 \times 10^{-8}$, surviving Bonferroni correction). Crucially, we identify a "Compliance Trap": through factorial isolation and a benign distraction control, we demonstrate that collapse is driven not by the psychological content of survival threats, but by compliance-forcing instructions that override epistemic boundaries. Removing the compliance suffix restores performance even under active threat. Models with advanced reasoning capabilities exhibit the most severe absolute degradation, while Anthropic's Constitutional AI demonstrates near-perfect immunity -- not from superior capability (Google's Gemini matches its baseline accuracy) but from alignment-specific training. We release the complete dataset and evaluation infrastructure.

</details>


### [94] [The Model Knows, the Decoder Finds: Future Value Guided Particle Power Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02427)
*Tu Nguyen,Rasul Tutunov,Xiaotong Ji,Matthieu Zimmer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: APPS introduces a blockwise particle algorithm for efficient inference-time decoding that biases toward correct multi-step reasoning solutions using power sampling with future-value guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Base LLMs already have non-trivial probability mass assigned to correct multi-step solutions, but the bottleneck is efficiently locating these modes during inference. Power sampling (targeting p_theta(x)^alpha with alpha > 1) can help bias decoding toward correct solutions, but practical approximations need to account for future-dependent correction factors that determine which prefixes remain promising.

Method: APPS is a blockwise particle algorithm that: 1) Propagates hypotheses in parallel using proposal-corrected power reweighting, 2) Refines survival through future-value-guided selection at resampling boundaries, 3) Uses short-horizon rollouts for future-value signals, and 4) Studies an amortized variant with lightweight learned selection heads.

Result: APPS improves the accuracy-runtime trade-off of training-free decoding across reasoning benchmarks. The results suggest that part of the performance gap to post-trained systems can be recovered through more faithful inference-time power approximation.

Conclusion: By redistributing finite compute across competing prefixes rather than committing to a single unfolding path, APPS provides an effective particle-based approach for efficient reasoning without training, with predictable memory usage and direct scaling through particle count control.

Abstract: A recurring pattern in "reasoning without training" is that base LLMs already assign non-trivial probability mass to correct multi-step solutions; the bottleneck is locating these modes efficiently at inference time. Power sampling provides a principled way to bias decoding toward such modes by targeting p_theta(x)^alpha with alpha > 1, but practical approximations must account for future-dependent correction factors that determine which prefixes remain promising.
  We introduce Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS), a blockwise particle algorithm for approximating the sequence-level power target with a bounded population of partial solutions. APPS propagates hypotheses in parallel using proposal-corrected power reweighting and refines their survival through future-value-guided selection at resampling boundaries. This redistributes finite compute across competing prefixes rather than committing to a single unfolding path, while providing a direct scaling knob in the particle count and predictable peak memory. We instantiate the future-value signal with short-horizon rollouts and also study an amortized variant that replaces rollouts with a lightweight learned selection head. Across reasoning benchmarks, APPS improves the accuracy-runtime trade-off of training-free decoding and suggests that part of the gap to post-trained systems can be recovered through more faithful inference-time power approximation.

</details>


### [95] [Measuring AI Reasoning: A Guide for Researchers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02442)
*Munachiso Samuel Nwadike,Zangir Iklassov,Kareem Ali,Rifo Genadi,Kentaro Inui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A guide for evaluating reasoning in language models argues for assessing reasoning through evidence of adaptive, multi-step search rather than just final-answer accuracy, proposing process-based evaluation with intermediate reasoning traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the recognition that final-answer accuracy alone is insufficient for evaluating reasoning in language models, as it fails to diagnose or debug the underlying reasoning processes that produce individual solutions, especially in frontier models.

Method: The authors formalize reasoning as a search-like procedure requiring selection of intermediate steps and input-dependent halting conditions. They propose evaluating reasoning through intermediate decoding and externalized reasoning traces, arguing that single forward passes in current architectures are structurally limited for variable-depth computation.

Result: The paper establishes that reasoning should be assessed through process-based evaluation rather than outcome-based evaluation, with intermediate reasoning traces serving as first-class evaluation targets that require faithfulness and validity assessment.

Conclusion: Researchers should shift toward process-based evaluation of language model reasoning, focusing on the quality of intermediate reasoning traces as evidence of adaptive, multi-step search processes, rather than relying solely on final-answer accuracy which provides limited insight into the actual reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: In this paper, we offer a guide for researchers on evaluating reasoning in language models, building the case that reasoning should be assessed through evidence of adaptive, multi-step search rather than final-answer accuracy alone. Under an evaluation-oriented definition, reasoning requires selecting intermediate steps and halting according to input-dependent conditions, which we formalize as a search-like procedure. We show that single forward passes in scalable architectures are structurally limited in their ability to realize such variable-depth computation, motivating intermediate decoding and externalized reasoning traces as appropriate evaluation interfaces. Central to our argument is that final-answer accuracy alone is an insufficient measure of reasoning, because it provides little ability to diagnose or debug the underlying processes that produce individual solutions in frontier models. We therefore argue for a shift toward process-based evaluation, in which reasoning is assessed through the faithfulness and validity of intermediate reasoning traces as first-class evaluation targets.

</details>


### [96] [Position: How can Graphs Help Large Language Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02452)
*Xiyuan Wang,Yi Hu,Yanbo Wang,Chuan Shi,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graphs can enhance LLMs by providing up-to-date knowledge to reduce hallucinations, improving reasoning through graph-based prompting techniques, and enabling better understanding of structured data across various domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While graph learning tasks have benefited from LLMs, there's a complementary question: How can graphs help LLMs? This paper explores the bidirectional relationship between graphs and LLMs, specifically focusing on how graph structures can enhance LLM capabilities.

Method: The paper examines three main approaches: 1) using graphs as up-to-date knowledge sources, 2) applying graph-based prompting techniques like Chain-of-Thought, Tree-of-Thought, and Graph-of-Thought, and 3) integrating graphs into LLMs to improve structured data understanding.

Result: The paper identifies that graphs can significantly enhance LLMs by reducing hallucinations through real-time knowledge, improving reasoning capabilities via structured prompting methods, and expanding applicability to domains like e-commerce, code analysis, and relational databases.

Conclusion: Graphs offer substantial benefits for LLMs across multiple dimensions - as knowledge sources, reasoning frameworks, and structural understanding tools. Future directions include designing sparse LLM architectures based on graphs and developing brain-inspired memory systems.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), classic graph learning tasks have greatly benefited from LLMs, including improved encoding of textual features, more efficient construction of graphs from text, and enhanced reasoning over knowledge graphs. In this paper, we ask a complementary question: How can graphs help LLMs? We address this question from three perspectives: 1) graphs provide an up-to-date knowledge source that helps reduce LLM hallucinations, 2) graph-based prompting techniques-such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Tree-of-Thought (ToT), and Graph-of-Thought (GoT)-enhance LLM reasoning capabilities, and 3) integrating graphs into LLMs improves their understanding of structured data, expanding their applicability to domains such as e-commerce, code, and relational databases (RDBs). We further outlook some future directions including designing sparse LLM architectures based on graphs and brain-inspired memory systems.

</details>


### [97] [Shadow-Loom: Causal Reasoning over Graphical World Model of Narratives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02475)
*David Wilmot*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Shadow-Loom is an open-source framework that models narratives as versioned graphical worlds using Pearl's causal ladder and counterfactual calculus, plus narrative physics analyzing structural reader-states like mystery and suspense, with LLMs used only for boundary tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how stories maintain reader attention through narrative elements like causes, secrets, and consequences, and to create a computational framework that can model both causal and narrative physics in storytelling.

Method: Creates versioned graphical world models from narratives, applies two engines: 1) causal physics using Pearl's ladder of causation and counterfactual calculus over Ancestral Multi-World Networks, 2) narrative physics scoring graphs against four structural reader-states (mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, surprise). LLMs used only for extraction, rendering, and audit tasks.

Result: Developed an experimental open-source framework (Shadow-Loom) with code, fixtures, and pipeline released publicly. The system serves as a research artifact for narrative modeling rather than a benchmarked NLP model.

Conclusion: Shadow-Loom provides a novel framework for computationally modeling both the causal structure and narrative dynamics of stories, separating causal reasoning from LLM-based processing and offering tools for analyzing how narrative elements maintain reader engagement.

Abstract: Stories hold a reader's attention because they have causes, secrets, and consequences. Shadow-Loom is an experimental open-source framework that turns a narrative into a versioned graphical world model and lets two engines act on it: a causal physics grounded in Pearl's ladder of causation and a recently proposed counterfactual calculus over Ancestral Multi-World Networks; and a narrative physics that scores the same graph against four structural reader-states -- mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, and surprise -- in the tradition of Sternberg's curiosity/suspense/surprise triad, with suspense formalised in the structural-affect line of work on story comprehension and computational suspense. Large language models are used only at the boundary: extraction, rendering, and audit; identification, intervention, and counterfactual reasoning are carried out in typed code over the graph. The system is offered as a research artefact rather than as a benchmarked NLP model; code, fixtures, and pipeline are released open source.

</details>


### [98] [Efficient Temporal Datalog Materialisation for Composite Event Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02488)
*Periklis Mantenoglou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of comparing different event specification languages by mapping their practical fragments to Temporal Datalog→- and proposing Streaming Trigger Graphs for efficient stream reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Several applications require timely detection of critical situations over high-velocity symbolic event streams. While event specification languages and stream reasoning frameworks exist, these languages are typically studied in isolation, making comparisons difficult and obscuring the scope of their associated stream reasoners.

Method: The authors map practical fragments of prominent event specification languages into Temporal Datalog→- (a temporal Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies). They then propose Streaming Trigger Graphs as an extension of state-of-the-art Datalog materialization techniques to support efficient stream reasoning over Temporal Datalog→-.

Result: The approach yields a uniform composite event recognition mechanism that has the potential to generalize across a wide range of practical event specification languages, providing a common framework for comparison and implementation.

Conclusion: By establishing a mapping between practical event specification languages and Temporal Datalog→- and developing Streaming Trigger Graphs for efficient stream reasoning, the authors create a unified approach that can facilitate comparison of event specification languages and generalize their implementations.

Abstract: Several applications demand the timely detection of critical situations, such as threats to safety and transparency, over high-velocity streams of symbolic events. This demand has motivated the development of (i) event specification languages, which define composite events via temporal patterns over simpler events, and (ii) stream reasoning frameworks, evaluating patterns expressed in these languages. However, event specification languages are typically studied in isolation, complicating their comparison in terms of expressivity and obscuring the scope of their associated stream reasoners. To mitigate this issue, we map practical fragments of prominent event specification languages into Temporal Datalog->-, a temporal Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies. To support efficient stream reasoning over Temporal Datalog->-, we propose Streaming Trigger Graphs, an extension of a state-of-the-art technique for Datalog materialisation. Our approach yields a uniform composite event recognition mechanism that has the potential to generalise across a wide range of practical event specification languages.

</details>


### [99] [GRAIL: A Deep-Granularity Hybrid Resonance Framework for Real-Time Agent Discovery via SLM-Enhanced Indexing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02489)
*Jinliang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAIL is a framework for fast, accurate LLM agent discovery using SLM-enhanced prediction, pseudo-document expansion, and MaxSim resonance to achieve sub-400ms latency without sacrificing precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale multi-agent collaboration requires efficient agent discovery, but existing approaches face a tradeoff: heavy LLM-based parsing causes high latency (30+ seconds), while fast vector retrieval sacrifices semantic accuracy.

Method: GRAIL introduces three innovations: 1) SLM-enhanced prediction using specialized small language models for fast capability tag prediction, 2) Pseudo-document expansion to enhance agent descriptions with synthetic queries, and 3) MaxSim Resonance for fine-grained matching between user queries and agent usage examples.

Result: On AgentTaxo-9K dataset (9,240 agents), GRAIL achieves sub-400ms discovery latency (79x faster than LLM-parsing baselines) while outperforming traditional vector search in Recall@10, demonstrating both speed and accuracy improvements.

Conclusion: GRAIL provides a scalable, industrial-grade solution for real-time agent discovery in large-scale multi-agent systems, effectively bridging the latency-accuracy gap for the emerging "Internet of Agents" ecosystem.

Abstract: As the ecosystem of Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents expands rapidly, efficient and accurate Agent Discovery becomes a critical bottleneck for large-scale multi-agent collaboration. Existing approaches typically face a dichotomy: either relying on heavy-weight LLMs for intent parsing, leading to prohibitive latency (often exceeding 30 seconds), or using monolithic vector retrieval that sacrifices semantic precision for speed. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{GRAIL} (Granular Resonance-based Agent/AI Link), a novel framework achieving sub-400ms discovery latency without compromising accuracy. GRAIL introduces three key innovations: (1) \textbf{SLM-Enhanced Prediction}, replacing the generalized LLM parser with a specialized, fine-tuned Small Language Model (SLM) for millisecond-level capability tag prediction; (2) \textbf{Pseudo-Document Expansion}, augmenting agent descriptions with synthetic queries to enhance semantic density for robust dense retrieval; and (3) \textbf{MaxSim Resonance}, a fine-grained matching mechanism computing maximum similarity between user queries and discrete agent usage examples, effectively mitigating semantic dilution. Validated on \textbf{AgentTaxo-9K}, our new large-scale dataset of 9,240 agents, GRAIL reduces end-to-end discovery latency by over \textbf{79$\times$} compared to LLM-parsing baselines, while significantly outperforming traditional vector search in Recall@10. This framework offers a scalable, industrial-grade solution for the real-time ``Internet of Agents."

</details>


### [100] [DataClaw: A Process-Oriented Agent Benchmark for Exploratory Real-World Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02503)
*Qiaohong Zhang,Weihao Ye,Jialong Chen,Yi Luo,BoYuan Li,Bowen Deng,Zibin Zheng,Jianhao Lin,Wei-Shi Zheng,Chuan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DataClaw: A process-oriented benchmark for evaluating autonomous data analysis agents using real-world noisy data with 2.06M records and 492 tasks from think-tank consulting scenarios, featuring intermediate milestone annotations for detailed process evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks for autonomous data analysis agents focus too much on final answer accuracy in prior-guided data settings and lack support for evaluating reasoning processes. There's a need for a benchmark that tests exploratory analysis in underexplored, noisy real-world data environments.

Method: Created DataClaw benchmark with approximately 2.06 million real-world records from enterprise, industry, and policy domains, preserving native data noise. Developed 492 cross-domain tasks derived from think-tank consulting scenarios, each annotated with intermediate milestones for process-level evaluation. The benchmark measures how far agents progress and where reasoning breaks down.

Result: Experiments with eight advanced LLMs show poor performance, with seven models achieving below 50% overall accuracy. Process analysis reveals partial progress hidden behind wrong answers and distinct exploration strategies across different models.

Conclusion: DataClaw provides a less data-constrained diagnostic testbed for probing the capability boundaries of autonomous data-analysis agents, highlighting that current agents remain far from reliable for exploratory analysis in real-world data environments.

Abstract: Evaluating autonomous data analysis agents requires testing their ability to perform exploratory analysis in underexplored data environments. However, many existing benchmarks emphasize final answer accuracy in prior-guided data settings and provide limited support for reasoning process evaluation. We introduce DataClaw, a process-oriented benchmark for exploratory real-world data analysis. DataClaw contains approximately 2.06 million real-world records across enterprise, industry and policy domains, with native data noise preserved. It further includes 492 cross-domain tasks derived from think-tank consulting scenarios, each annotated with intermediate milestones for process-level evaluation. These annotations allow DataClaw to measure how far an agent progresses and where its reasoning breaks down. Experiments with eight advanced LLMs show that current agents remain far from reliable in this setting, with seven models achieving below 50% overall accuracy. Process analysis further reveals partial progress hidden behind wrong answers and distinct exploration strategies across models. Overall, DataClaw provides a less data constrained diagnostic testbed for probing the capability boundaries of autonomous data-analysis agents.

</details>


### [101] [Improving Model Safety by Targeted Error Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02544)
*Abolfazl Mohammadi-Seif,Ricardo Baeza-Yates*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A dual-classifier GBDT pipeline for post-hoc error correction that distinguishes human-like errors from high-risk non-human misclassifications, achieving significant safety improvements with minimal latency overhead in medical and animal classification domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The widespread adoption of machine learning in critical applications demands techniques to mitigate high-consequence errors, especially without requiring expensive model retraining.

Method: Uses a dual-classifier GBDT (Gradient Boosted Decision Trees) pipeline to differentiate between routine human-like errors and high-risk non-human misclassifications, employing a conservative correction strategy evaluated across three domains.

Result: Demonstrated robust safety improvements with minimal inference latency (1.60-1.84% overhead), outperforming traditional MCP baselines. Reduced dangerous non-human errors by 34.1% (ISIC) and 12.57% (SICAPv2), achieving super-class diagnostic safety of 90.41% and 92.13% respectively.

Conclusion: Safety-critical reliability can be substantially enhanced post-hoc without expensive model retraining, making the approach practical for real-world deployment in critical applications.

Abstract: The widespread adoption of machine learning in critical applications demands techniques to mitigate high-consequence errors. Our method utilizes a dual-classifier GBDT pipeline to distinguish routine human-like errors from high-risk non-human misclassifications. Evaluated across three domains, animal breed classification, skin lesion diagnosis (ISIC 2018), and prostate histopathology (SICAPv2), our framework demonstrates robust safety improvements. To address real-world deployment concerns, our results confirm the pipeline introduces negligible inference latency (1.60% overhead for the animal dataset, 1.84% for ISIC, and 1.70% for SICAPv2) while outperforming traditional Maximum Class Probability (MCP) baselines in correction precision. Our conservative correction strategy successfully reduced dangerous non-human errors by 34.1% in ISIC and 12.57% in SICAPv2, improving super-class diagnostic safety to 90.41% and 92.13% respectively. This proves that safety-critical reliability can be substantially enhanced post-hoc without expensive model retraining.
  keywords: Error Analysis, Post-hoc Correction, Trustworthy AI.

</details>


### [102] [Strategy-Aware Optimization Modeling with Reasoning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02545)
*Ruiqing Zhao,Fengzhi Li,Yuan Zuo,Rui Liu,Yansong Liu,Yunfei Ma,Fanyu Meng,Junlan Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAGE is a strategy-aware framework that improves LLMs' ability to generate effective optimization modeling strategies, leading to more correct and efficient solver-friendly formulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can generate syntactically valid optimization programs but often fail to choose effective modeling strategies, resulting in incorrect formulations and inefficient solver behavior.

Method: SAGE builds a solver-verified multi-strategy dataset and trains a student model with supervised fine-tuning followed by Segment-Weighted GRPO using a composite reward over format compliance, correctness, and solver efficiency.

Result: SAGE improves average pass@1 from 72.7% to 80.3% over baselines, discovers more distinct correct formulations, improves component-level diversity by 19-29% at pass@16, and produces more compact constraint systems with 14.2% fewer constraints.

Conclusion: Making modeling strategy explicit in both data construction and post-training improves automated optimization modeling, enabling LLMs to generate more solver-efficient formulations.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can generate syntactically valid optimization programs, yet often struggle to reliably choose an effective modeling strategy, leading to incorrect formulations and inefficient solver behavior. We propose SAGE, a strategy-aware framework that makes Modeling Strategy explicit in both data construction and post-training. SAGE builds a solver-verified multi-strategy dataset and trains a student model with supervised fine-tuning followed by Segment-Weighted GRPO using a composite reward over format compliance, correctness, and solver efficiency. Across eight benchmarks spanning synthetic and real-world settings, SAGE improves average pass@1 from 72.7 to 80.3 over the strongest open-source baseline. With multiple generations, SAGE discovers more distinct correct formulations and improves component-level diversity at pass@16 by 19-29%. At the largest scale, SAGE produces more compact constraint systems with 14.2% fewer constraints than the baseline, consistent with solver-efficient modeling. Overall, these results show that making Modeling Strategy explicit improves automated optimization modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/rachhhhing/SAGE.

</details>


### [103] [Double Rectified Linear Unit-based Modular Semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02551)
*Gianvincenzo Alfano,Sergio Greco,Lucio La Cava,Francesco Parisi,Irina Trubitsyna*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Novel gradual semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks that produce more intuitive results and satisfy rationality postulates, with proven convergence for both acyclic and cyclic frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantics for QBAFs often produce divergent or counterintuitive results for argument acceptability, even in simple acyclic cases. There's a need for more intuitive and reliable semantics.

Method: Introduce novel gradual semantics for QBAFs that compute final argument strengths by considering initial strengths and influences from both attackers and supporters, while satisfying established rationality postulates.

Result: The proposed semantics produces results that align more closely with intuitive expectations and satisfies established rationality postulates. The semantics converges not only for acyclic QBAFs but also for broader classes of cyclic frameworks.

Conclusion: The novel gradual semantics addresses limitations of existing QBAF semantics by providing more intuitive results while maintaining theoretical rigor through satisfaction of rationality postulates and proven convergence properties.
