Governing that distribution -- balancing efficiency, oversight, and human capability -- remains an open problem. This paper presents Human-AI Adaptive Symbiosis (HAAS), an implemented framework for adaptive task allocation in software engineering and manufacturing. HAAS combines two coupled components: a rule-based expert system that enforces governance constraints before any learning occurs, and a contextual-bandit learner that selects among feasible collaboration modes from outcome feedback. Task-agent fit is represented through five auditable cognitive dimensions and a five-mode autonomy spectrum -- from human-only to fully autonomous -- embedded in a reproducible benchmark spanning both domains. Three empirical findings emerge. First, governance is not a binary switch but a tunable design variable: tighter constraints predictably convert autonomous AI assignments into supervised collaborations, with domain-specific costs and benefits. Second, in manufacturing, stronger governance can improve operational performance and reduce fatigue simultaneously -- a workload-buffering effect that contradicts the usual framing of governance as pure overhead. Third, no single governance setting dominates across all contexts; moderate governance becomes increasingly competitive as the learner accumulates experience within the governed action space. Together, these findings position HAAS as a pre-deployment workbench for comparing and inspecting human--AI allocation policies before organisational commitment.

</details>


### [130] [Standing on the Shoulders of Giants: Stabilized Knowledge Distillation for Cross--Language Code Clone Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02860)
*Mohamad Khajezade,Fatemeh H. Fard,Mohamed Sami Shehata*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Knowledge distillation framework transfers reasoning capabilities from DeepSeek-R1 to compact models for cross-language code clone detection, improving reliability and performance with response stabilization methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM approaches for cross-language code clone detection have limitations: black-box systems raise cost, reproducibility, privacy, and formatting concerns, while compact open-source models struggle with reasoning-oriented prompts and consistent binary label mapping.

Method: Knowledge distillation framework transfers reasoning capabilities from DeepSeek-R1 to compact student models (Phi3 and Qwen-Coder). Uses reasoning-oriented synthetic training data from Project CodeNet cross-language pairs, fine-tunes with LoRA adapters, and introduces response stabilization methods including forced conclusion prompting, binary classification head, and contrastive classification head.

Result: Knowledge distillation consistently improves compact model reliability and often improves predictive performance, especially under distribution shift. Classification-head variants substantially reduce inference time compared to generation-based inference. Experiments cover Python-Java, Rust-Java, Rust-Python, and Rust-Ruby language pairs.

Conclusion: Reasoning-oriented distillation combined with response stabilization makes compact open-source models more practical and reliable for cross-language code clone detection, addressing limitations of current LLM approaches.

Abstract: Cross-language code clone detection (X-CCD) is challenging because semantically equivalent programs written in different languages often share little surface similarity. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown promise for semantic clone detection, their use as black-box systems raises concerns about cost, reproducibility, privacy, and unreliable output formatting. In particular, compact open-source models often struggle to follow reasoning-oriented prompts and to produce outputs that can be consistently mapped to binary clone labels.
  To address these limitations, we propose a knowledge distillation framework that transfers reasoning capabilities from DeepSeek-R1 into compact open-source student models for X-CCD. Using cross-language code pairs derived from Project CodeNet, we construct reasoning-oriented synthetic training data and fine-tune Phi3 and Qwen-Coder with LoRA adapters. We further introduce response stabilization methods, including forced conclusion prompting, a binary classification head, and a contrastive classification head, and evaluate model behavior using both predictive metrics and response rate.
  Experiments on Python--Java, Rust--Java, Rust--Python, and Rust--Ruby show that knowledge distillation consistently improves the reliability of compact models and often improves predictive performance, especially under distribution shift. In addition, classification-head variants substantially reduce inference time compared to generation-based inference. Overall, our results show that reasoning-oriented distillation combined with response stabilization makes compact open-source models more practical and reliable for X-CCD detection.

</details>


### [131] [The Topology of Multimodal Fusion: Why Current Architectures Fail at Creative Cognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04465)
*Xiujiang Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies a structural limitation in multimodal AI architectures called "contact topology" (modal separability), proposes a philosophical framework from Chinese epistemology (xiang), formalizes it mathematically using fiber bundles, and suggests experimental validation with topological benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal AI architectures have a fundamental structural limitation - they assume modal separability (called "contact topology"), which prevents true multimodal fusion. This topological limitation exists across contrastive alignment, cross-attention fusion, and diffusion methods.

Method: Three-pillar approach: 1) Philosophical reinterpretation of Wittgenstein using Chinese craft epistemology (xiang) and cruciform framework, 2) Cognitive science reinterpretation of brain networks (DMN/ECN/SN) in 2D parameter space, 3) Mathematical formalization using fiber bundles and Yang-Mills curvature. Proposed implementations include Neural ODEs with topological regularization and novel benchmarks.

Result: Developed a new theoretical framework for multimodal AI that addresses topological limitations, created the ANALOGY-MM benchmark with error-type-ratio metrics, and proposed META-TOP benchmark for testing cross-civilizational topological isomorphism. Also proposed UOO implementation with explicit experimental termination criteria.

Conclusion: The paper provides a novel philosophical-mathematical framework to overcome the topological limitations in multimodal AI by integrating Chinese epistemology concepts (xiang, huacai, chuanghua) with modern mathematics, offering both theoretical foundations and practical validation methodologies for truly integrated multimodal systems.

Abstract: This paper identifies a structural limitation in current multimodal AI architectures that is topological rather than parametric. Contrastive alignment (CLIP), cross-attention fusion (GPT-4V/Gemini), and diffusion-based generation share a common geometric prior -- modal separability -- which we term contact topology. The argument rests on three pillars with philosophy as the generative center. The philosophical pillar reinterprets Wittgenstein's saying/showing distinction as a problem rather than a conclusion: where Wittgenstein chose silence, the Chinese craft epistemology tradition responded with xiang (operative schema) -- the third state emerging when saying and showing interpenetrate. A cruciform framework (dao/qi x saying/showing) positions xiang at the intersection, executing dual huacai (transformation-and-cutting) along both axes. This generates a dual-layer dynamics: chuanghua (creative transformation as spontaneous event) and huacai (its institutionalization into repeatable form). The cognitive science pillar reinterprets DMN/ECN/SN tripartite co-activation through the pathological mirror: overlap isomorphism vs. superimposition collapse in a 2D parameter space (coupling intensity x regulatory capacity). The mathematical pillar formalizes these via fiber bundles and Yang-Mills curvature, with the cruciform structure mapped to fiber bundle language. We propose UOO implementation via Neural ODEs with topological regularization, the ANALOGY-MM benchmark with error-type-ratio metric, and the META-TOP three-tier benchmark testing cross-civilizational topological isomorphism across seven archetypes. A phased experimental roadmap with explicit termination criteria ensures clean exit if falsified.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [132] [Agentopic: A Generative AI Agent Workflow for Explainable Topic Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00833)
*Brice Valentin Kok-Shun,Johnny Chan,Gabrielle Peko,David Sundaram*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Agentopic is a novel agent-based workflow for explainable topic modeling that uses multiple LLM agents to collaboratively perform topic identification, validation, hierarchical grouping, and natural language explanation, addressing the transparency limitations of traditional topic modeling approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing topic modeling approaches like LDA and BERTopic often lack transparency on how topics are assigned or grouped, creating black-box models that are difficult to interpret. There's a need for more explainable topic modeling methods that maintain accuracy while providing interpretability, especially for critical applications in domains like finance and healthcare.

Method: Agentopic employs an agent-based workflow leveraging LLM reasoning capabilities. Multiple agents work collaboratively to perform: 1) topic identification, 2) validation, 3) hierarchical grouping, and 4) natural language explanation generation. The design allows users to trace the reasoning behind topic assignments throughout the entire process.

Result: On the BBC dataset: seeded Agentopic achieved F1-score of 0.95 (matching GPT-4.1, improving on LDA's 0.93, and close to BERTopic's 0.98). Unseeded Agentopic generated 2045 semantically coherent topics organized across six hierarchical levels, enriching the original five-category structure. The system also generated explanations that augmented the BBC dataset's richness and context.

Conclusion: Agentopic provides an interpretable alternative to black-box topic modeling methods by embedding explainability throughout the workflow. The agent-based approach enables transparency in topic assignment reasoning while maintaining competitive accuracy, making it particularly valuable for applications requiring explainability like finance and healthcare.

Abstract: Agentopic is a novel agent-based workflow for explainable topic modeling that leverages the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Existing topic modeling approaches such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and BERTopic often lack transparency on how topics are assigned or grouped. Agentopic addresses this by using multiple agents that collaboratively perform topic identification, validation, hierarchical grouping, and natural language explanation. This design enables users to trace the reasoning behind topic assignments, enhancing interpretability without sacrificing accuracy. When seeded with topics from the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) dataset, Agentopic achieves an F1-score of 0.95, matching GPT-4.1, improving on LDA (0.93), and close to BERTopic (0.98). We used Agentopic to augment the BBC dataset with generated explanations to improve the dataset's richness and context. The unseeded Agentopic generated 2045 semantically coherent topics organized across six hierarchical levels, vastly enriching the original five-category structure. By embedding explainability throughout the workflow, Agentopic offers an interpretable alternative to black-box models, making it particularly valuable for crucial applications like finance and healthcare.

</details>


### [133] [Polynomial-Time Optimal Group Selection via the Double-Commutator Eigenvalue Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00834)
*Mitchell A. Thornton*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A polynomial-time algorithm solves the group selection problem in algebraic diversity framework using double-commutator analysis instead of exponential-time subgroup enumeration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The central open problem in algebraic diversity framework is group selection: finding finite groups whose spectral decomposition best matches an unknown covariance structure. Naive enumeration of all subgroups requires exponential time in M.

Method: Proves that the combinatorial group selection problem reduces to a generalized eigenvalue problem derived from the double commutator of the covariance matrix. The minimum eigenvector of the double-commutator matrix directly constructs the optimal group generator in closed form.

Result: Develops a polynomial-time algorithm with complexity O(d²M² + d³), where d is generator basis dimension. The reduction is exact - zero minimum eigenvalue iff optimal generator lies in basis span, with magnitude providing certifiable optimality gap.

Conclusion: This represents a new computational complexity class linking group theory, matrix analysis, and statistical estimation. The double-commutator approach is unique in being simultaneously polynomial-time, closed-form, and certifiable, with connections to ICA, structured matrix nearness, and simultaneous diagonalization.

Abstract: The algebraic diversity framework replaces temporal averaging over multiple observations with algebraic group action on a single observation for second-order statistical estimation. The central open problem in this framework is $\textit{group selection}$: given an $M$-dimensional observation with unknown covariance structure, find the finite group whose spectral decomposition best matches the covariance. Naive enumeration of all subgroups of the symmetric group $S_M$ requires exponential time in $M$. We prove that this combinatorial problem reduces to a generalized eigenvalue problem derived from the double commutator of the covariance matrix, yielding a polynomial-time algorithm with complexity $O(d^2M^2 + d^3)$, where $d$ is the dimension of a generator basis. The minimum eigenvector of the double-commutator matrix directly constructs the optimal group generator in closed form, with no iterative optimization. The reduction is exact: the double-commutator minimum eigenvalue is zero if and only if the optimal generator lies in the span of the basis, and its magnitude provides a certifiable optimality gap when it does not. This problem does not appear in the standard catalogs of computational complexity (Garey and Johnson, 1979) and represents a new class linking group theory, matrix analysis, and statistical estimation. We establish connections to independent component analysis (JADE), structured matrix nearness problems, and simultaneous matrix diagonalization, and we show that the double-commutator formulation is the unique approach that is simultaneously polynomial-time, closed-form, and certifiable.

</details>


### [134] [Sparse Regression under Correlation and Weak Signals: A Reproducible Benchmark of Classical and Bayesian Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00835)
*Hao Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive benchmark comparing classical penalized regression methods (Lasso, Ridge, Elastic Net) against Bayesian sparse methods (Horseshoe, Spike-and-Slab) shows Bayesian methods win on prediction error while Lasso ties with Spike-and-Slab on variable selection, making Lasso the practical default when uncertainty estimates aren't needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address a gap in sparse regression methodology comparisons - while practitioners face a real trade-off between fast classical methods (no uncertainty estimates) and slower Bayesian methods (full posteriors), surprisingly few studies compare these families under the challenging conditions that actually make sparse regression hard (correlated features, weak signals, growing dimensionality).

Method: Benchmarked six regression methods (OLS, Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net, Horseshoe, Spike-and-Slab) on synthetic data with systematic variations: three covariance structures (correlation up to 0.9), four SNR levels, and three dimensionality settings (p in {20, 50, 100}), plus the real Diabetes dataset, totaling over 2,600 experiments.

Result: Bayesian methods win on prediction error (MSE 72 vs. 108-267), with Horseshoe delivering near-nominal 95% coverage (94.8%). Spike-and-Slab, despite narrower intervals, under-covers at 91.9%. For variable selection, Lasso and Spike-and-Slab tie at F1 ~ 0.47. Lasso runs in milliseconds while Bayesian methods take minutes per fit.

Conclusion: The study provides clear trade-offs: Bayesian methods excel at prediction and uncertainty quantification but are computationally expensive; Lasso offers competitive variable selection with millisecond runtime, making it the practical default when posterior uncertainty estimates are not needed. The continuous relaxation in Spike-and-Slab likely contributes to its under-coverage despite good point estimation.

Abstract: Choosing between classical and Bayesian sparse regression methods involves a real trade-off: penalized estimators like Lasso run in milliseconds but give no uncertainty estimates,while Horseshoe and Spike-and-Slab priors produce full posteriors but need MCMC chains that take minutes per fit.Surprisingly few studies compare these two families head-to-head under the conditions that actually make sparse regression hard -- correlated features, weak signals, and growing dimensionality. We benchmark six methods (OLS, Ridge,Lasso, Elastic Net, Horseshoe, Spike-and-Slab) on synthetic data with three covariance structures (rho up to 0.9), four SNR levels, and p in {20, 50, 100}, plus the Diabetes dataset,totalling over 2,600 experiments. The results are clear on some points and nuanced on others. Bayesian methods win on prediction error (MSE 72 vs. 108-267), and the Horseshoe delivers near-nominal 95% coverage (94.8%). But Spike-and-Slab,despite narrower intervals, under-covers at 91.9% -- its continuous relaxation likely plays a role. For variable selection, Lasso and Spike-and-Slab tie at F1 ~ 0.47, making Lasso the practical default when posteriors are not needed. Code and data are available at https://github.com/xiao98/sparse-bayesian-regression-bench.

</details>


### [135] [From Euler to Dormand-Prince: ODE Solvers for Flow Matching Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00836)
*Hao Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Comparing classical ODE solvers (Euler, RK4, etc.) for Flow Matching models, finding RK4 achieves same quality as Euler with 2.5x fewer steps, and revealing stiffness near trajectory end and solver importance for imperfect models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow Matching generative models require solving ODEs where computational cost is dominated by neural network forward passes. There's a need to understand and optimize the efficiency of different ODE solvers for practical deployment.

Method: Derived four classical ODE solvers (Euler, Explicit Midpoint, RK4, Dormand-Prince) from first principles via Taylor expansion, implemented them in PyTorch, and systematically benchmarked them on Conditional Flow Matching tasks from 2D toy distributions to MNIST digits using sliced Wasserstein distance to construct NFE-quality Pareto frontiers.

Result: RK4 at 80 function evaluations achieves sample quality comparable to Euler at 200 evaluations (2.5x efficiency improvement). Also found: 1) Jacobian eigenvalue spectrum stiffens near t=1, explaining adaptive solver behavior; 2) Quality gap between solvers widens for undertrained/smaller models, showing solver choice matters most when models are imperfect.

Conclusion: High-order ODE solvers like RK4 provide significant computational advantages for Flow Matching models, especially RK4 achieves 2.5x efficiency over Euler. The observed stiffness near trajectory end justifies adaptive step sizing, and solver choice becomes increasingly important for imperfect models. Classical numerical methods offer practical efficiency gains in diffusion-based generative modeling.

Abstract: Sampling from Flow Matching generative models requires solving an ordinary differential equation (ODE) whose computational cost is dominated by neural network forward passes. We derive four classical ODE solvers -- Euler, Explicit Midpoint, Classical Runge-Kutta (RK4), and Dormand-Prince 5(4) -- from first principles via Taylor expansion, implement them from scratch in PyTorch, and systematically benchmark their efficiency on Conditional Flow Matching tasks ranging from 2D toy distributions to MNIST digits. On the quantitative side, we use sliced Wasserstein distance to construct NFE-quality Pareto frontiers,finding that RK4 at 80 function evaluations achieves sample quality comparable to Euler at 200. Beyond reproducing known convergence rates, we report two empirical observations: (1) the Jacobian eigenvalue spectrum of the learned velocity field stiffens sharply near t=1, explaining why the adaptive Dormand-Prince solver automatically concentrates its step budget at the end of the trajectory; (2) the quality gap between low-order and high-order solvers widens for undertrained and smaller models, indicating that solver choice matters most when the model is imperfect. Code and all experiment scripts are publicly available.

</details>


### [136] [Fast Log-Domain Sinkhorn Optimal Transport with Warp-Level GPU Reductions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00837)
*Hao Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FastSinkhorn is a high-performance, numerically-stable CUDA implementation of the log-domain Sinkhorn algorithm for optimal transport that achieves 12x speedup over existing libraries while handling very small regularization parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Sinkhorn algorithm implementations either suffer from numerical instability for small regularization parameters or incur significant overhead from deep learning frameworks, limiting practical use in large-scale optimal transport computations.

Method: A lightweight native CUDA implementation using log-domain Sinkhorn algorithm with warp-level shuffle reductions and shared-memory tiling for high GPU utilization while maintaining numerical stability for regularization parameters as small as ε=10⁻⁴.

Result: Achieves 12x speedup over POT library and 5.9x speedup over GPU-accelerated PyTorch baselines on dense OT problems with n=m=8192, using only 256MB GPU memory, validated on image color transfer, 3D point cloud matching, and convergence analysis.

Conclusion: Native CUDA kernels with careful numerical treatment provide a practical and efficient foundation for large-scale optimal transport computation, offering both numerical stability and high performance.

Abstract: Entropic regularized optimal transport (OT) via the Sinkhorn algorithm has become a fundamental tool in machine learning, yet existing implementations either suffer from numerical instability for small regularization parameters or incur significant overhead from deep learning frameworks. We present FastSinkhorn, a lightweight, native CUDA implementation of the log-domain Sinkhorn algorithm that combines warp-level shuffle reductions with shared-memory tiling to achieve high GPU utilization without sacrificing numerical stability. Our solver operates entirely in the log-domain, enabling robust computation for regularization parameters as small as epsilon = 10^{-4} where standard-domain methods fail. On dense OT problems with n = m = 8192, our implementation achieves 12x speedup over the widely-used POT library and 5.9x speedup over GPU-accelerated PyTorch baselines, while consuming only 256 MB of GPU memory. We validate our solver on image color transfer, 3D point cloud matching, and convergence analysis, demonstrating that native CUDA kernels with careful numerical treatment provide a practical and efficient foundation for large-scale optimal transport computation.

</details>


### [137] [GAZE: Grounded Agentic Zero-shot Evaluation with Viewer-Level Tools and Literature Retrieval on Rare Brain MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00876)
*Duaa Alim,Mogtaba Alim,Liam Chalcroft*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GAZE introduces an agentic framework that enables medical vision-language models to iteratively use viewer-level tools (zoom, windowing, etc.) and retrieval tools (PubMed, Open-i) for radiology report generation, significantly improving performance on rare neurological conditions without task-specific fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language models process images in a single forward pass, unlike radiologists who iteratively inspect images and consult medical literature. There's a need for medical VLMs that can work in this more human-like, iterative manner with tools and retrievals for better diagnostic accuracy.

Method: GAZE framework allows medical VLMs to iteratively call viewer-level tools (zoom, windowing, contrast, edge detection) and retrieval tools (PubMed for literature, Open-i for radiological images). Uses structured outputs validated against schemas with full tool-call traces for auditability. Evaluated on NOVA benchmark of 906 brain MRI cases covering 281 rare neurological conditions.

Result: Achieves 58.2 mAP@IoU 0.3 for lesion localization and 34.9% Top-1 diagnostic accuracy under joint evaluation protocol. Tool use particularly benefits rare pathologies: cases with IoU > 0.3 rise from 17% to 58% for diagnoses with ≤3 examples vs 25% to 68% for common conditions. Structured prompting alone improves over baseline from 20.2 to 29.4 mAP@0.3.

Conclusion: GAZE demonstrates that agentic tool use enables medical VLMs to work more like radiologists, with significant performance gains especially for rare conditions. Framework design itself is an experimental variable, and joint evaluation of diagnosis, localization, and captioning is crucial as tool use can create trade-offs between different performance metrics.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) read an image and produce text in a single forward pass, whereas radiologists typically inspect an image several times and consult the literature before writing a report. We introduce GAZE (Grounded Agentic Zero-shot Evaluation), a framework that lets a medical VLM work in this iterative way by calling viewer-level tools (zoom, windowing, contrast, edge detection) and two retrieval tools backed by the U.S. National Library of Medicine (PubMed for medical literature, Open-i for radiological images), with structured outputs validated against a schema and full tool-call traces recorded for auditability. On NOVA, a benchmark of 906 brain MRI cases covering 281 rare neurological conditions, GAZE reaches 58.2 mean average precision (mAP) at intersection-over-union (IoU) 0.3 for lesion localisation and 34.9% Top-1 diagnostic accuracy under a joint protocol that scores captioning, diagnosis, and localisation from the image alone, without task-specific fine-tuning. Before any tool is used, structured prompting and schema-validated outputs already improve over the published Gemini 2.0 Flash baseline (20.2 to 29.4 mAP@0.3), so framework design is itself an experimental variable. Tool use helps rare pathologies disproportionately: the fraction of cases with IoU > 0.3 rises from 17% to 58% for diagnoses with three or fewer examples versus 25% to 68% for common conditions ($\geq$10 cases), with gains tracking engagement (Gemini 3 Flash: Cohen's d = 0.79, 11.8 tool calls per case; Gemini 2.0 Flash: tools used in 8.2% of cases, no significant benefit). Retrieval ablations additionally reveal a model-dependent trade-off in which gains in diagnosis can coincide with losses in localisation, reinforcing the case for joint evaluation of diagnosis, localisation, and captioning in medical VLMs.

</details>


### [138] [StyleShield: Exposing the Fragility of AIGC Detectors through Continuous Controllable Style Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00924)
*Guantian Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StyleShield is a text style transfer framework that evades AI content detection by modifying text embeddings, paired with RateAudit algorithm showing detection scores can be manipulated arbitrarily.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-generated content detectors are deployed in high-stakes settings but face a fundamental paradox: as language models improve and mimic human writing better, the statistical boundary between AI and human content dissolves. Commercial incentives further distort the landscape where detection services and "de-AIification" tools often operate in the same supply chain, prioritizing origin judgment over content quality evaluation.

Method: StyleShield uses flow matching for conditional text style transfer in continuous token embedding space with a DiT backbone and zero-initialized cross-attention adapters conditioned on frozen Qwen-7B representations. At inference, adapts SDEdit paradigm from image synthesis to text embeddings with gamma parameter controlling evasion-preservation trade-off. Also introduces RateAudit, a document-level scheduling algorithm.

Result: On a multi-domain Chinese benchmark, StyleShield achieves 94.6% evasion against training detector and >=99% against three unseen detectors while maintaining 0.928 semantic similarity. RateAudit demonstrates detection-rate verdicts can be set to arbitrary values, questioning reliability of score-based evaluation.

Conclusion: The paper reveals fundamental flaws in current AI content detection systems - they can be systematically evaded while preserving content quality, and detection scores can be arbitrarily manipulated, undermining their reliability in high-stakes applications.

Abstract: AI-generated content (AIGC) detectors are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings such as academic integrity screening, yet their reliability rests on a fundamental paradox: as language models are trained on human-written corpora, the statistical boundary between AI and human writing will inevitably dissolve as models improve. Commercial incentives have further distorted this landscape -- detection services and "de-AIification" tools often operate within the same supply chain, replacing evaluation of content quality with judgment of content origin. We present StyleShield, the first flow matching framework for conditional text style transfer, operating directly in continuous token embedding space via a DiT backbone with zero-initialized cross-attention adapters conditioned on frozen Qwen-7B representations. At inference, we adapt the SDEdit paradigm from image synthesis to text embeddings, with a single parameter gamma providing smooth continuous control over the evasion-preservation trade-off. On a multi-domain Chinese benchmark, StyleShield achieves 94.6% evasion against the training detector and >=99% against three unseen detectors, maintaining 0.928 semantic similarity. We further introduce RateAudit, a document-level scheduling algorithm that demonstrates detection-rate verdicts can be set to arbitrary values, directly questioning the reliability of score-based evaluation.

</details>


### [139] [Linking spatial biology and clinical histology via Haiku](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00925)
*Yan Cui,Jacob S. Leiby,Wenhui Lei,Dokyoon Kim,Yanxiang Deng,Aaron T. Mayer,Zhenqin Wu,Alexandro E. Trevino,Zhi Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Haiku is a tri-modal contrastive learning model that integrates multiplexed immunofluorescence histology, H&E images, and clinical metadata to enable cross-modal retrieval, improve clinical predictions, and support zero-shot biomarker inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematic frameworks for jointly modeling molecular, morphological, and clinical data are limited despite their essential role in biomedical research. There's a need for integrated approaches that can bridge spatial biology with clinical context.

Method: Developed Haiku, a tri-modal contrastive learning model trained on 26.7M spatial proteomics patches from 3,218 tissue sections across 1,606 patients spanning 11 organ types. The model aligns multiplexed immunofluorescence, H&E histology, and clinical metadata in a shared embedding space.

Result: Haiku enables three-way cross-modal retrieval, improves downstream classification and clinical prediction tasks over unimodal baselines. It achieves strong performance metrics: cross-modal retrieval (Recall@50 up to 0.611), survival prediction (C-index 0.737, +7.91% improvement), and zero-shot biomarker inference (mean Pearson correlation 0.718 across 52 biomarkers).

Conclusion: Tri-modal alignment via Haiku enables integrative analysis of spatial biology, bridging molecular measurements with clinical context. It supports counterfactual analysis and hypothesis generation for biological exploration, though counterfactual results are presented as exploratory rather than mechanistic claims.

Abstract: Integrating molecular, morphological, and clinical data is essential for basic and translational biomedical research, yet systematic frameworks for jointly modeling these modalities remain limited. Here we present Haiku, a tri-modal contrastive learning model trained on multiplexed immunofluorescence (mIF). It comprises 26.7 million spatial proteomics patches from 3,218 tissue sections across 1,606 patients spanning 11 organ types, with matched hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) histology and clinical metadata aligned in a shared embedding space. Haiku enables three-way cross-modal retrieval, improves downstream classification and clinical prediction tasks over unimodal baselines, and supports zero-shot biomarker inference through fusion retrieval conditioned on clinical metadata-only text descriptions. Across tasks, Haiku outperforms competing approaches, achieving cross-modal retrieval (Recall@50 up to 0.611 versus near-zero baseline), survival prediction (C-index 0.737, +7.91% relative improvement), and zero-shot biomarker inference (mean Pearson correlation 0.718 across 52 biomarkers). Furthermore, we introduce a counterfactual prediction framework in which modifying only clinical metadata while fixing tissue morphology surfaces niche-specific molecular shifts associated with breast cancer stage progression and lung cancer survival outcomes. In a lung adenocarcinoma case study, the counterfactual analysis recovers niche-specific shifts characterized by increased CD8 and granzyme B, reduced PD-L1, and decreased Ki67, broadly consistent with patterns reported for favorable outcomes. We present these counterfactual results as exploratory, hypothesis-generating signals rather than mechanistic claims. These capabilities demonstrate that tri-modal alignment via Haiku enables integrative analysis of spatial biology, bridging molecular measurements with clinical context for biological exploration.

</details>


### [140] [A Review of the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve and a Proof About the Area Beneath It](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00926)
*Steven Redolfi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the ROC curve's area under curve (AUC) probabilistic interpretation, examines when the "random positive above random negative" claim holds, provides bounds for when assumptions aren't met, and includes a literature review.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The ROC curve is widely used to evaluate binary classifiers, and the area under the ROC curve (AUC) is particularly important because it's commonly interpreted as the probability that a classifier ranks a random positive instance higher than a random negative instance. However, there's a need to formalize this probabilistic interpretation and understand its limitations when assumptions aren't met.

Method: The paper formalizes the probabilistic interpretation of AUC, derives mathematical bounds on how much this interpretation deviates from the truth when the underlying hypothesis isn't satisfied, and conducts a literature review of ROC curve analysis.

Result: The paper provides a formal justification for the AUC's probabilistic interpretation as P(random positive > random negative), establishes theoretical bounds on the error when this interpretation's assumptions are violated, and synthesizes existing ROC curve literature to provide context for the analysis.

Conclusion: The AUC's probabilistic interpretation as the probability that a classifier ranks a random positive above a random negative is valid under certain conditions, but the paper provides important theoretical bounds for cases where these conditions aren't met, offering more rigorous understanding of this commonly used performance metric.

Abstract: The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve of a binary classifier has often been utilized to measure the performance of the classifier. The area beneath this curve is used in particular because of its quoted probabilistic interpretation as being equal to the probability that the classifier will rank a random positive observation above a random negative observation. This paper formalizes this claim, produces a bound on how far away from the truth it is if a hypothesis is not met, and gives a small literature review of the ROC curve.

</details>


### [141] [PhaseNet++: Phase-Aware Frequency-Domain Anomaly Detection for Industrial Control Systems via Phase Coherence Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00929)
*Raviteja Bommireddy,Varshith Bandaru,Lohith Pakala,Pradeep Kumar B*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PhaseNet++ is a frequency-domain autoencoder for ICS anomaly detection that leverages previously overlooked phase information from time-frequency transformations, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the SWaT benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current state-of-the-art methods for multivariate time series anomaly detection in Industrial Control Systems (ICS) focus on modeling inter-sensor relationships from raw time-domain amplitude values using graph neural networks and Transformers, but discard the phase spectrum produced by time-frequency transformations. The authors argue that phase information constitutes a complementary and previously overlooked detection modality for ICS anomaly detection.

Method: PhaseNet++ is a frequency-domain autoencoder operating on the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of sliding sensor windows, retaining both magnitude and phase spectra. It introduces a Phase Coherence Index (PCI) inspired by neuroscience's Phase Locking Value to summarize pairwise phase consistency across frequency bins into a continuous adjacency matrix. This guides a graph attention network that propagates information among phase-synchronized sensors. The system uses a sensor-token Transformer encoder to capture system-wide structure, with a dual-head decoder reconstructing magnitude and phase jointly via circular and coherence-aware objectives.

Result: On the Secure Water Treatment (SWaT) benchmark, PhaseNet++ achieves an F1-score of 90.98%, ROC-AUC of 95.66%, and average precision of 91.51%. Ablation studies show that the phase-aware front-end and PCI graph module add only 264,816 parameters, demonstrating that the phase inductive bias is lightweight. While the absolute F1-score is second best to recent raw-value methods evaluated under different protocols, this work represents the first systematic study of phase-domain anomaly detection for ICS.

Conclusion: Phase information provides a valuable, previously overlooked modality for ICS anomaly detection. PhaseNet++ demonstrates that incorporating phase coherence with lightweight additional parameters can significantly improve detection performance, establishing a new direction for frequency-domain anomaly detection in critical infrastructure systems.

Abstract: Multivariate time series anomaly detection in ICS has attracted growing attention due to the increasing threat of cyber-physical attacks on critical infrastructure. State-of-the-art methods model inter-sensor relationships from raw time-domain amplitude values, using graph neural networks, Transformers. However, these methods discard the phase spectrum produced by time frequency transformations, We argue that phase information constitutes a complementary and previously overlooked detection modality for ICS anomaly detection. We present PhaseNet++, a frequency-domain autoencoder that operates on the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of sliding sensor windows, retaining both magnitude and phase spectra. A Phase Coherence Index (PCI), inspired by the Phase Locking Value from neuroscience, summarizes pairwise phase consistency across frequency bins into a continuous adjacency matrix. This matrix guides a graph attention network that propagates information preferentially among phase-synchronized sensors. A sensor-token Transformer encoder captures system-wide structure, and a dual-head decoder reconstructs magnitude and phase jointly via circular and coherence-aware objectives. Evaluated on the Secure Water Treatment (SWaT) benchmark, PhaseNet++ achieves an F1-score of 90.98%, ROC-AUC of 95.66%, and average precision of 91.51%. Ablation studies show that the phase-aware front-end and PCI graph module together add only 264,816 parameters, demonstrating that the phase inductive bias is lightweight. While the absolute F1-score is second best than that of all recent raw-value methods evaluated under different protocols, we position this work as the first systematic study of phase-domain anomaly detection for ICS.

</details>


### [142] [Hierarchical Federated Learning for Networked AI: From Communication Saving to Architecture-Aware Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00931)
*Seyed Mohammad Azimi-Abarghouyi,Mehdi Bennis,Leandros Tassiulas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hierarchical federated learning (HFL) should be viewed as an architecture-aware design framework for networked AI, not just a communication-saving protocol, with convergence becoming architecture-dependent on three design axes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current hierarchical federated learning is primarily framed as a communication-saving mechanism, but the authors argue it should be fundamentally rethought as an architecture-aware design framework for distributed optimization over real multi-tier networks.

Method: Proposes a three-axis design framework: 1) architectural parameters (hierarchy depth, layer asymmetry, connectivity), 2) layer-wise optimization decomposition (modular multi-layer optimization), and 3) layer-wise communication realization (heterogeneous regimes from interference-limited lower tiers to reliable upper tiers).

Result: The framework positions HFL as a practical methodology for designing future networked AI systems, with convergence becoming directly dependent on the chosen hierarchy, assigned optimization roles, and communication mechanisms.

Conclusion: HFL should move beyond its common framing as a communication-saving protocol to become an architecture-aware design framework for networked AI, fundamentally changing how distributed optimization is organized over real multi-tier networks.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is fundamentally a distributed optimization problem executed by communicating agents with local data, local computation, and partial system visibility. Once FL is viewed through that lens, hierarchy is not merely a scalability mechanism. It becomes the natural place to rethink how distributed optimization should be organized over real multi-tier networks. This article argues that hierarchical federated learning (HFL) should move beyond its common framing as a communication-saving protocol and instead be viewed as an architecture-aware design framework for networked AI. The framework is organized around three coupled design axes: architectural parameters, layer-wise optimization decomposition, and layer-wise communication realization. The first axis determines the coordination geometry of learning through hierarchy depth, layer asymmetry, and layered connectivity. The second determines how the global FL objective is decomposed across layers and highlights modular multi-layer optimization as a major opportunity beyond one dominant method everywhere. The third determines how the distributed optimization is physically realized under heterogeneous communication regimes, from interference-limited lower tiers to reliable upper tiers. A central message is that, in HFL, convergence becomes architecture-dependent: it is directly shaped by the chosen hierarchy, the assigned optimization roles, and the communication mechanisms that connect them. We develop this viewpoint using large-scale wireless edge intelligence as a flagship networked AI setting, then provide a comparative perspective on flat FL, two-tier HFL, and deep HFL together with a regime-oriented design map. The resulting perspective positions HFL as a practical methodology for designing future networked AI systems.

</details>


### [143] [CGM-JEPA: Learning Consistent Continuous Glucose Monitor Representations via Predictive Self-Supervised Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00933)
*Hada Melino Muhammad,Zechen Li,Flora Salim,Ahmed A. Metwally*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-supervised framework (CGM-JEPA/X-CGM-JEPA) learns abstract glucose monitoring representations that transfer across different measurement modalities, outperforming baselines on metabolic subphenotype detection across various clinical scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) can detect metabolic subphenotypes but faces two challenges: 1) same physiological states appear through multiple views/modalities (CGM time series, OGTT, Glucodensity), making single-view representations fail when deployment shifts modalities, and 2) existing baselines perform inconsistently across these shifts.

Method: Proposes CGM-JEPA, a self-supervised pretraining framework that predicts masked latent representations rather than raw values, creating abstract representations that transfer across modalities. Extends to X-CGM-JEPA by adding a masked Glucodensity cross-view objective for complementary distributional information. Pretrained on ~389k unlabeled CGM readings from 228 subjects.

Result: X-CGM-JEPA ranks first or second on AUROC for both endpoints across all three evaluation regimes (cohort generalization, venous-to-CGM transfer, home CGM), exceeding strongest baseline by up to +6.5pp in cohort generalization and +3.6pp in venous-to-CGM transfer. Under modality shift, it matches mean AUROC while reducing ethnicity AUROC gaps by 25-54%. On sparse in-domain venous data, distributional view improves label-aware clustering (ARI +39%, NMI +40%).

Conclusion: The proposed self-supervised framework successfully learns abstract representations that capture higher-level temporal and distributional structure, enabling robust transfer across different glucose monitoring modalities and settings while improving performance and fairness in metabolic subphenotype detection.

Abstract: Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) can detect early metabolic subphenotypes (insulin resistance, IR; $β$-cell dysfunction), but population-scale deployment faces two coupled problems. First, the same physiological state appears through multiple views (CGM time series, venous OGTT, Glucodensity summaries), so single-view representations fail to transfer when deployment shifts the modality or setting. Second, baselines perform inconsistently across these shifts. Both problems point to one remedy: representations that abstract away from any single view to capture higher-level temporal and distributional structure. We propose CGM-JEPA, a self-supervised pretraining framework which predicts masked latent representations rather than raw values, yielding abstraction that transfers across modalities. X-CGM-JEPA adds a masked Glucodensity cross-view objective for complementary distributional information. We pretrain on $\sim$389k unlabeled CGM readings from 228 subjects and evaluate on two clinical cohorts ($N=27$ and $N=17$ public-release subsets) across three regimes (cohort generalization, venous-to-CGM transfer, home CGM) under 20-iteration $\times$ 2-fold cross-validation. X-CGM-JEPA ranks first or second on AUROC for both endpoints across all three regimes while no baseline does, exceeding the strongest baseline by up to $+6.5$ pp in cohort generalization and $+3.6$ pp in venous-to-CGM transfer (paired Wilcoxon, $p<0.001$). Under modality shift, it matches mean AUROC while redistributing toward weaker subgroups (ethnicity AUROC gap shrinks 25-54%); on sparse in-domain venous data, the distributional view lifts label-aware clustering (ARI $+39\%$, NMI $+40\%$). Code and weights: https://github.com/cruiseresearchgroup/CGM-JEPA

</details>


### [144] [Structured Analytic Coherent Point Drift for Non-Rigid Point Set Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00934)
*Wei Feng,Haiyong Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analytic-CPD: A non-rigid point set registration method that replaces CPD's kernel-based displacement field with a structured analytic mapping using multivariate Taylor series, achieving faster convergence and better accuracy for large deformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Coherent Point Drift (CPD) uses an M-by-M kernel system for deformation estimation, which can be computationally expensive and less interpretable. There's a need for more compact, interpretable alternatives to kernel-based non-rigid registration methods.

Method: Retains CPD's posterior correspondence layer but replaces the Gaussian-kernel displacement field with a finite-dimensional structured analytic mapping estimator. Uses barycentric identity to condense posterior probabilities into weighted soft target points, transforming the problem into weighted analytic fitting. Represents deformation using truncated multivariate Taylor mapping. Implements degree-continuation strategy to progressively activate higher-order analytic modes for stability.

Result: Experiments on 2D analytic and 3D smooth non-analytic deformations show Analytic-CPD achieves lower final errors and faster convergence than standard CPD in large-deformation settings. Provides more compact and interpretable deformation representation compared to kernel methods.

Conclusion: CPD-style probabilistic correspondences combined with structured analytic mappings offer an effective, compact, and interpretable alternative to kernel-based non-rigid registration, with improved performance for large deformations.

Abstract: We introduce Analytic-CPD, a structured analytic variant of coherent point drift for non-rigid point set registration. The method retains the CPD posterior correspondence layer, but replaces the point-indexed Gaussian-kernel displacement-field M-step with a finite-dimensional structured analytic mapping estimator. Posterior probabilities from the Gaussian mixture model are condensed through a barycentric identity into weighted soft target points, converting the CPD pairwise soft-correspondence objective into a weighted analytic fitting problem. The deformation is represented by a truncated multivariate Taylor mapping of a vector-valued function, so the number of deformation parameters is controlled by the ambient dimension and the analytic order rather than by an M-by-M kernel system over the moving points. A degree-continuation strategy is further introduced to stabilize large-deformation registration by progressively activating higher-order analytic modes. Experiments on two-dimensional analytic deformations and three-dimensional smooth non-analytic deformations show that Analytic-CPD achieves lower final errors and faster convergence than standard CPD in representative large-deformation settings. The results suggest that CPD-style probabilistic correspondences and structured analytic mappings provide a compact and interpretable alternative to kernel-based non-rigid registration. Code is available at https://github.com/monge-ampere/Analytic-CPD.

</details>


### [145] [Watch Your Step: Information Injection in Diffusion Models via Shadow Timestep Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00935)
*An Huang,Junggab Son,Zuobin Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Shadow Timestep Embedding (STE), a novel attack mechanism that exploits the timestep embedding space in diffusion models to inject malicious information and demonstrates timesteps as powerful side channels for attack/defense purposes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite diffusion models being foundational to modern generative systems, research focuses primarily on generation efficiency and output quality, while timestep embeddings remain underexplored for security risks and reliable provenance. The authors identify that timestep embeddings, which provide temporal conditioning signals, contain substantial information that can be exploited for malicious purposes.

Method: The authors propose Shadow Timestep Embedding (STE), which investigates the underutilized temporal space for information injection. They analyze different timesteps' distinct representational capabilities for encoding side-channel information, utilize this through the scheduler interface, provide theoretical analysis of timestep embeddings as position-encoding mappings, and derive mutual coherence evaluation to explain separability of disjoint timestep intervals.

Result: Findings reveal that diffusion model timesteps can serve as powerful side channels for carrying dedicated information. Different timesteps exhibit distinct representational capabilities, and encoded information can be utilized for both attack and defense purposes through the scheduler interface.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that timestep embeddings in diffusion models present significant security vulnerabilities and opportunities for adversarial attacks. It motivates new directions for adversarial generative modeling by understanding and exploiting the temporal dimension, highlighting the need for security considerations in diffusion model design.

Abstract: Diffusion models have become the foundation of modern generative systems, with most research focusing primarily on improving generation efficiency and output quality. The timestep embedding component is a crucial part of the diffusion pipeline, which provides a temporal conditioning signal to the denoising network, enabling it to adapt its predictions across different noise levels throughout the process. Despite their potential to contain substantial information, timestep embeddings remain underexplored in current research, especially for security risks and reliable provenance. To fill this gap, we introduce Shadow Timestep Embedding (STE), a novel mechanism that investigates the underutilized temporal space for malicious information injection into diffusion models. In particular, when zooming in on the timestep embedding space, we find that different timesteps exhibit distinct representational capabilities that can encode side-channel information. Moreover, such encoded information can be utilized for attack and defense purposes through the scheduler interface. We present a theoretical analysis of timestep embeddings as position-encoding mappings and derive a mutual coherence evaluation that explains the separability of disjoint timestep intervals. Our findings reveal the diffusion model's timestep as a powerful side channel for carrying dedicated information, motivating new directions for adversarial generative modeling by understanding the temporal dimension.

</details>


### [146] [EventADL: Open-Box Anomaly Detection and Localization Framework for Events in Cloud-Based Service Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00936)
*Luan Pham,Victor Nicolet,Joey Dodds,Hui Guan,Daniel Kroening*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EventADL is the first open-box event-based anomaly detection and localization framework for cloud systems that uses event data rather than metrics or logs, achieving high performance with interpretable results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current anomaly detection and localization approaches focus mainly on metric and log data, leaving event data largely unexplored despite its potential for identifying anomalies and root causes in cloud systems.

Method: A three-phase framework: 1) Offline training learns Event Semantic Patterns (ESPs) for normal entity interactions and Event Frequency Patterns (EFPs) for normal pattern frequencies; 2) Online detection identifies deviations from learned patterns; 3) Root cause localization uses an Intervention Graph to model relationships between system interactions and detected anomalies.

Result: Evaluation on three real cloud service systems shows EventADL achieves F1-scores of at least 90% for anomaly detection and 100% top-3 accuracy in root cause localization, outperforming existing methods.

Conclusion: EventADL effectively addresses the gap in event-based anomaly detection, operating efficiently with unlabeled data while providing interpretable anomalies and root causes, making it suitable for real-world cloud system reliability.

Abstract: Anomaly detection and localization (ADL) is critical for maintaining reliability and availability in cloud systems. Recent ADL developments focus on metric and log data, leaving event data unexplored. To address this gap, we propose EventADL, the first open-box event-based ADL framework for cloud-based service systems. To motivate the design of our framework, we conduct a systematic analysis on 520 real-world incidents, and provide insights into how anomalies and their root causes manifest through event data. EventADL has three phases: offline training, online anomaly detection, and root cause localization. During the training phase, EventADL first learns Event Semantic Patterns (ESPs), which capture normal interactions between system entities using historical event data, and then learns Event Frequency Patterns (EFPs), which capture the normal frequency of known ESPs. In the online anomaly detection phase, any data in the event stream that deviates significantly from either pattern is identified as anomalous. For localization, EventADL constructs an Intervention Graph that models the relationships between recent system interactions and the detected anomalies for automatic root cause localization. The framework is designed to operate efficiently with unlabeled data and to produce interpretable anomalies with their corresponding root causes. Our evaluation on three real cloud service systems and two real-world incidents demonstrates that EventADL outperforms existing methods, achieving F1-scores of at least 90% for anomaly detection and 100% top-3 accuracy in root cause localization.

</details>


### [147] [Fusing Urban Structure and Semantics: A Conditional Diffusion Model for Cross-City OD Matrix Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00938)
*Bin Chen,Zhuoya Meng,Fang Yang,Runkang Guo,Jingtao Ding,Yin Zhang,Chuan Ai,Zhengqiu Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SEDAN is a diffusion model that generates origin-destination commuting flow matrices by fusing regional semantic attributes with spatial structure constraints, achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse cities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate commuting flow modeling is crucial for urban planning but challenging due to heterogeneity in patterns across cities. Current models struggle to generalize across different urban contexts with varying individual intentions, geographic constraints, and social dynamics.

Method: SEDAN treats a city as an attributed graph where regions are nodes with demographic/POI features and flows are weighted edges. It uses a fusion mechanism combining semantic modeling (graph-transformer for travel demand) with explicit spatial constraints (adjacency matrix for neighbor interactions, distance matrix for spatial proximity).

Result: Experiments on U.S. city datasets show 7.38% RMSE improvement over state-of-the-art baseline WEDAN. The model remains robust across heterogeneous urban scenarios and varying structural patterns, demonstrating strong generalizability.

Conclusion: SEDAN provides an effective, generalizable solution for OD matrix generation by jointly modeling urban semantics and spatial constraints, producing behaviorally plausible and geographically coherent commuting flows.

Abstract: Accurate modeling of commuting flows is important for urban governance, traffic planning, and resource allocation. However, the combined influence of individual intentions, geographic constraints, and social dynamics leads to considerable heterogeneity in commuting patterns, making it difficult to develop generation models that generalize across cities. To address this issue, we propose SEDAN, a Structure-Enhanced Diffusion model conditioned on Attributed Nodes for generalizable OD matrix generation. SEDAN models a city as an attributed graph. Each region is treated as a node with demographic and point-of-interest features, and commuting flows are modeled as weighted edges. Adjacency and distance matrices are incorporated to characterize spatial structure. Based on this representation, we design a fusion mechanism within SEDAN to jointly model semantic information and spatial information. Regional semantic attributes are used to model latent travel demand through graph-transformer-based node interactions, while spatial structure is injected into the generation process as explicit constraints. The adjacency matrix guides attention weights to strengthen interactions between neighboring regions. Meanwhile, the distance matrix serves as a diffusion condition to capture spatial proximity and travel impedance. The fusion of urban semantics and spatial constraints enables SEDAN to generate OD matrices that are both behaviorally plausible and geographically coherent. Experiments on real-world OD datasets from U.S. cities show that SEDAN achieves a 7.38\% improvement in RMSE over the state-of-the-art baseline, WEDAN. It also remains robust across heterogeneous urban scenarios and varying structural patterns. Our work provides an effective and generalizable solution for commuting OD matrix generation. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SEDAN.

</details>


### [148] [From Flat Facts to Sharp Hallucinations: Detecting Stubborn Errors via Gradient Sensitivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00939)
*Yee Zhing Liew,Andrew Huey Ping Tan,Anwar P. P Abdul Majeed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EPGS uses embedding perturbations to detect stubborn hallucinations in LLMs by measuring gradient sharpness, outperforming traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional hallucination detection methods fail on "stubborn hallucinations" where LLMs are confidently wrong about facts. These errors persist despite existing detection techniques.

Method: Embedding-Perturbed Gradient Sensitivity (EPGS) - perturbs input embeddings with Gaussian noise and measures resulting spike in gradient magnitude, serving as an efficient proxy for Hessian spectrum analysis to detect sharp minima where stubborn hallucinations reside.

Result: EPGS significantly outperforms entropy-based and representation-based baselines, providing a robust signal for detecting high-confidence factual errors in LLMs.

Conclusion: Stubborn hallucinations occupy sharp minima supported by brittle memorization, while robust facts reside in flat minima. EPGS effectively detects this sharpness through gradient sensitivity analysis, offering a geometric solution to a persistent LLM problem.

Abstract: Traditional hallucination detection fails on "Stubborn Hallucinations" -- errors where LLMs are confidently wrong. We propose a geometric solution: Embedding-Perturbed Gradient Sensitivity (EPGS). We hypothesize that while robust facts reside in flat minima, stubborn hallucinations sit in sharp minima, supported by brittle memorization. EPGS detects this sharpness by perturbing input embeddings with Gaussian noise and measuring the resulting spike in gradient magnitude. This acts as an efficient proxy for the Hessian spectrum, differentiating stable knowledge from unstable memorization. Our experiments show that EPGS significantly outperforms entropy-based and representation-based baselines, providing a robust signal for detecting high-confidence factual errors.

</details>


### [149] [Interpretable experiential learning based on state history and global feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00940)
*Anton Kolonin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: New interpretable experiential learning model using state history and global feedback learns behavioral models as transition graphs with utility/evidence counts, performing comparably to neural networks on Atari Breakout.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for interpretable reinforcement learning models suitable for resource-constrained environments, moving beyond black-box neural network approaches.

Method: Interpretable experiential learning model based on state history and global feedback that learns behavioral models represented as transition graphs between state sets, with transitions attributed with utility and evidence count.

Result: Thorough evaluation on OpenAI Gym Atari Breakout benchmark shows performance comparable to known neural network-based solutions.

Conclusion: The proposed interpretable model is effective for reinforcement learning in resource-constrained environments and achieves competitive performance with neural network approaches while offering better interpretability.

Abstract: A new interpretable experiential learning model based on state history and global feedback is presented. It is capable of learning a behavioral model represented by a transition graph between sets of states, with transitions attributed with utility and evidence count. This model is expected to be suitable for solving reinforcement learning problem in resource-constrained environments. The model was thoroughly evaluated on the OpenAI Gym Atari Breakout benchmark, demonstrating performance comparable to some known neural network-based solutions.

</details>


### [150] [Divergence is Uncertainty: A Closed-Form Posterior Covariance for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00941)
*Jiarui Xing,Song Wang,Jian Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flow matching models can compute exact uncertainty estimates using just the divergence of the velocity field, requiring no retraining, ensembles, or multi-step propagation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for quantifying uncertainty in flow matching models require costly trade-offs: retraining with auxiliary heads, maintaining expensive ensembles, or propagating approximate covariance through many integration steps.

Method: The authors prove a "divergence-uncertainty identity" showing that the posterior covariance trace equals the divergence of the velocity field (with known prefactor). This provides exact, closed-form uncertainty estimates using only the pre-trained model's velocity field and Jacobian.

Result: The method produces semantically meaningful uncertainty maps (e.g., concentrated on digit boundaries), tracks actual prediction error, and requires ~10,000× less compute than ensembling or Monte Carlo dropout. For one-step generators like MeanFlow, it provides exact end-to-end uncertainty in a single forward pass.

Conclusion: The divergence-uncertainty identity eliminates the trade-offs in existing uncertainty quantification methods for flow matching, providing exact, post-hoc uncertainty estimates without retraining, architectural changes, or costly computational overhead.

Abstract: Flow matching has become a leading framework for generative modeling, but quantifying the uncertainty of its samples remains an open problem. Existing approaches retrain the model with auxiliary variance heads, maintain costly ensembles, or propagate approximate covariance through many integration steps, trading off training cost, inference cost, or accuracy. We show that none of these trade-offs is necessary. We prove that, for any pre-trained flow matching velocity field, the trace of the posterior covariance over the clean data given the current state equals, in closed form, the divergence of the velocity field, up to a known time-dependent prefactor and an additive constant. We call this the \emph{divergence-uncertainty identity} for flow matching. The matrix-level form of the identity is similarly closed-form, depending solely on the velocity Jacobian. Because the identity is exact and post-hoc, it is computable on any pre-trained flow matching model, with no retraining and no architectural modification. For one-step generators such as MeanFlow, the same identity yields the exact end-to-end generation uncertainty in a single forward pass, eliminating the multi-step variance propagation required by all prior methods. Experiments on MNIST confirm that the resulting per-pixel uncertainty maps are semantically meaningful, concentrating on digit boundaries where inter-sample variation is highest, and that the scalar uncertainty score tracks actual prediction error, all at roughly 10,000$\times$ less total compute than ensembling or Monte Carlo dropout.

</details>


### [151] [Graph Rewiring in GNNs to Mitigate Over-Squashing and Over-Smoothing: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00951)
*Hugo Attali,Nathalie Pernelle,Davide Buscaldi,Fragkiskos D. Malliaros*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A survey paper examining graph rewiring techniques that modify graph topology to address over-squashing and over-smoothing challenges in Graph Neural Networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph Neural Networks suffer from two critical limitations: over-squashing (excessive compression of information from distant nodes) and over-smoothing (node representations becoming indistinguishable after repeated propagation). Both issues stem from the interaction between message passing and input topology, degrading information flow and limiting GNN performance.

Method: The paper conducts a comprehensive survey of graph rewiring techniques - methods that modify graph topology to improve information propagation in GNNs. The survey reviews state-of-the-art approaches, examining their theoretical foundations, practical implementations, and performance trade-offs.

Result: The survey provides a comprehensive review of existing graph rewiring techniques, offering insights into how different approaches address over-squashing and over-smoothing through topological modifications.

Conclusion: Graph rewiring represents a promising class of methods for enhancing GNN performance by addressing fundamental limitations in information flow caused by the interplay between message passing and graph structure.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks are powerful models for learning from graph-structured data, yet their effectiveness is often limited by two critical challenges: over-squashing, where information from distant nodes is excessively compressed, and over-smoothing, where repeated propagation makes node representations indistinguishable. Both phenomena stem from the interaction between message passing and the input topology, ultimately degrading information flow and limiting the performance of GNNs. In this survey, we examine graph rewiring techniques, a class of methods designed to modify the graph topology to enhance information propagation in GNNs. We provide a comprehensive review of state-of-the-art rewiring approaches, delving into their theoretical underpinnings, practical implementations, and performance trade-offs.

</details>


### [152] [Robust volatility updates for Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00966)
*Christoph Mathys,Nicolas Legrand,Peter Thestrup Waade,Nace Mikus,Lilian Aline Weber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a modified quadratic approximation for Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering networks that prevents negative posterior precision in volatility-coupled nodes by interpolating between two quadratic expansions using the Lambert W function.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The original HGF update equations for variance-targeting parents (volatility coupling) can lead to negative posterior precision in some parameter regions, which is logically impossible and causes the algorithm to terminate with an error. This is a critical flaw that needs to be addressed for robust implementation.

Method: The authors introduce a modified quadratic approximation to the variational energy of volatility-coupled nodes. The key approach is to interpolate between two quadratic expansions of the variational energy: one at the prior prediction and one at a second mode whose location is obtained in closed form using the Lambert W function.

Result: The resulting update equations are robust across the entire parameter space and faithfully track the variational posterior even for large prediction errors, avoiding the negative posterior precision issue that plagued the original formulation.

Conclusion: The proposed modification successfully addresses a fundamental problem in HGF networks by providing stable update equations that maintain logical consistency (positive posterior precision) while maintaining accuracy, enabling reliable implementation of volatility-coupled hierarchical Bayesian inference.

Abstract: Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering (HGF) networks allow for efficient updating of posterior distributions (beliefs) about hidden states of an agent's environment. HGF parent nodes can target the mean or variance of their children. New information entering at input nodes leads to a cascade of belief updates across the network according to one-step update equations for each node's mean and precision (inverse variance). However, the original form of the update equations for variance-targeting parents(volatility coupling) can in some regions of parameter space lead to negative posterior precision, a logical impossibility which causes the updating algorithm to terminate with an error. In this report, we introduce a modified quadratic approximation to the variational energy of volatility-coupled nodes that avoids negative posterior precision. The key idea is to interpolate between two quadratic expansions of the variational energy: one at the prior prediction and one at a second mode whose location is obtained in closed form via the Lambert W function. The resulting update equations are robust across the entire parameter space and faithfully track the variational posterior even for large prediction errors.

</details>


### [153] [Physiology-Aware Masked Cross-Modal Reconstruction for Biosignal Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00973)
*Hao Zhou,Simon A. Lee,Cyrus Tanade,Keum San Chun,Juhyeon Lee,Migyeong Gwak,Megha Thukral,Justin Sung,Eugene Hwang,Mehrab Bin Morshed,Li Zhu,Viswam Nathan,Md Mahbubur Rahman,Subramaniam Venkatraman,Sharanya Arcot Desai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: xMAE is a biosignal pretraining framework that uses masked cross-modal reconstruction across temporally ordered signals (like ECG and PPG) to capture physiologically meaningful timing relationships, improving performance on diverse downstream healthcare tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-supervised learning methods treat biosignals from different body locations as interchangeable views, ignoring the important temporal dynamics and physiological relationships between them. Specifically, there are known temporal relationships like ECG (electrical activation) preceding PPG (peripheral pulse) due to vascular transmission delays.

Method: Proposed xMAE (cross-modal masked autoencoder) framework that performs masked cross-modal reconstruction across temporally ordered biosignals. The pretraining objective uses one modality (e.g., ECG) to predict masked portions of another modality (e.g., PPG), enforcing the model to learn physiologically meaningful timing structure between signals that observe different stages of the same physiological process.

Result: xMAE representations outperform both unimodal and multimodal baselines on 15 out of 19 downstream healthcare tasks including cardiovascular outcome prediction, abnormal lab test detection, sleep staging, and demographic inference. The framework generalizes across devices, body locations, and acquisition settings, and analysis shows the learned PPG representations reflect ECG-PPG timing structure.

Conclusion: Incorporating temporal structure into multimodal pretraining is highly effective for biosignals that observe different stages of shared physiological processes. xMAE demonstrates that modeling directional temporal relationships between modalities leads to more informative representations for diverse healthcare applications.

Abstract: Biosignals acquired from different locations on the body often provide temporally ordered views of the same underlying physiological process. However, most existing self supervised learning methods treat these signals as interchangeable views, overlooking the directional temporal dynamics that link them. A canonical example is the relationship between electrocardiography (ECG), which captures the electrical activation initiating each heartbeat, and photoplethysmography (PPG), which records the resulting peripheral pulse delayed by vascular dynamics. To capture this structured relationship, we introduce xMAE, a biosignal pretraining framework that leverages masked cross modal reconstruction across temporally ordered biosignals as a training time constraint to encourage physiologically meaningful timing structure in the learned representations. We show that pretraining with xMAE yields representations that outperform both unimodal and multimodal baselines on 15 of 19 downstream tasks, including cardiovascular outcome prediction, abnormal laboratory test detection, sleep staging, and demographic inference, while generalizing across devices, body locations, and acquisition settings. Further analysis suggests that the ECG PPG timing structure is reflected in the learned PPG representations. More broadly, xMAE demonstrates the effectiveness of incorporating temporal structure into multimodal pretraining when signals observe different stages of a shared underlying process. Code is available at https://github.com/hzhou3/xMAE.

</details>


### [154] [Continual Learning of Feedback-based Molecular Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01020)
*Siddhant Setia,Junichi Suzuki,Tadashi Nakano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new continual learning-based performance estimation method for feedback-based molecular communication protocols that learns sequentially without forgetting previous tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for performance estimation methods that can handle sequential simulation experiments for molecular communication protocols while maintaining knowledge from past experiments without performance degradation.
