Method: Leverages continual learning algorithms to create performance estimators that incrementally learn from sequential simulation experiments. Uses standard neural network architecture with customized regularization and replay strategies in the loss function to prevent catastrophic forgetting.

Result: Experimental results show the proposed estimators effectively learn from continuous streams of simulation results and enhance baseline neural networks by improving estimation accuracy across various computational costs.

Conclusion: The paper establishes the implications and applicability of continual learning techniques in the field of molecular communication for performance estimation tasks.

Abstract: This paper proposes and evaluates a new performance estimation method that leverages continual learning (CL) algorithms to carry out sequential simulation experiments for a feedback-based molecular communication protocol. As the protocol is sequentially examined in various experimental settings, the proposed CL-based performance estimators incrementally learn a series of unexperienced estimation tasks without compromising those that have been learned in the past. They are designed to work on a standard neural network architecture by customizing regularization and replay strategies in the loss function. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed estimators can effectively learn on a continuous stream of simulation results and enhance the baseline neural network by improving estimation accuracy at a variety of computational costs. This paper's contribution is to establish the implications of CL in the field of molecular communication.

</details>


### [155] [Finite-Sample Analysis of Elimination in Active Hypothesis Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01039)
*Ziyuan Lin,Hoang Ngoc Nguyen,Jie Xu,Ivan Ruchkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Active hypothesis testing with progressive hypothesis elimination reduces stopping time by reallocating sensing effort to surviving alternatives, with non-asymptotic performance guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Active hypothesis testing problems arise in safety-critical applications where finite-sample performance matters. Traditional methods may be inefficient, and hypothesis elimination can potentially reduce stopping time by focusing sensing effort.

Method: Elimination-augmented Track-and-Stop algorithm that progressively prunes champion-specific active-opponent sets and reallocates sensing effort toward surviving alternatives. Introduces an aggressiveness parameter to control trade-off between elimination speed and confidence guarantees.

Result: Non-asymptotic upper bound on expected stopping time derived. Gains from elimination appear in non-leading terms due to tighter tracking and concentration constants on reduced hypothesis sets. Experimental validation on synthetic Gaussian instances confirms theoretical predictions.

Conclusion: Progressive hypothesis elimination in active testing provides finite-sample benefits, with theoretical guarantees and practical trade-off control through aggressiveness parameter, validated by experiments.

Abstract: A fixed-confidence, finite-sample problem of active hypothesis testing arises in many safety-critical applications. Situated in the context of sequential hypothesis testing, this paper studies the effect of hypothesis elimination on the stopping time. We introduce an elimination-augmented Track-and-Stop algorithm, in which champion-specific active-opponent sets are progressively pruned, and sensing effort is reallocated toward the surviving alternatives. Our analysis derives a non-asymptotic upper bound on the expected stopping time. The gain in finite-sample from elimination appears on the scale of the non-leading term, resulting from tighter tracking and concentration constants on the reduced hypothesis set. Furthermore, we introduce an aggressiveness parameter to modulate the trade-off between faster elimination and weaker confidence guarantee. An experimental study on synthetic Gaussian instances confirms the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [156] [Learning in the Fisher Subspace: A Guided Initialization for LoRA Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01046)
*Zhi-Quan Feng,Ying-Jia Lin,Hung-Yu Kao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Data-aware initialization for LoRA using Fisher information improves adaptation by selecting task-relevant subspaces based on data sensitivity rather than just weight magnitudes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LoRA initialization strategies rely solely on pre-trained weight properties, overlooking how models interact with downstream data. Poor initialization that allocates capacity to task-irrelevant directions severely hinders downstream performance.

Method: Formulates LoRA initialization as identifying impactful parameter directions under target data distribution. Proposes a Fisher-guided framework that uses curvature information from downstream data to characterize how parameter perturbations influence model predictions.

Result: Empirical results across diverse tasks and modalities show that data-aware initialization consistently and significantly improves downstream performance over existing weight-only approaches.

Conclusion: Data sensitivity, rather than weight-only magnitude, should govern LoRA adaptation subspace selection. The Fisher-guided approach provides a principled, task-dependent criterion that better aligns adaptation with target objectives.

Abstract: LoRA adapts large language models (LLMs) by restricting updates to low-rank subspaces of pre-trained weights. While this substantially reduces training cost, the effectiveness of adaptation critically depends on which subspace is chosen at initialization: a poor initialization that allocates capacity to task-irrelevant directions can severely hinder downstream performance. Existing initialization strategies primarily rely on the intrinsic properties of pre-trained weights, implicitly assuming that weight geometry alone reflects task relevance. However, such criteria overlook how the model interacts with the downstream data distribution. In this work, we formulate LoRA initialization as identifying the degree of impact of directions in parameter space under the target data distribution. We argue that data-aware sensitivity, rather than weight-only magnitude, should govern the choice of adaptation subspaces. Building on this perspective, we propose a Fisher-guided framework that leverages curvature information induced by downstream data to characterize how parameter perturbations influence model predictions. This perspective yields a principled, task-dependent criterion for selecting LoRA directions that better align adaptation with the target objective. Empirical results across diverse tasks and modalities demonstrate that data-aware initialization consistently and significantly improves downstream performance over existing approaches.

</details>


### [157] [LEAP: Layer-wise Exit-Aware Pretraining for Efficient Transformer Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01058)
*Shashank Kapadia,Deep Naryan Mishra,Sujal Reddy Alugubelli,Haoan Wang,Saipraveen Vabbilisetty,Rishi Bhatia,Anupriya Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEAP (Layer-wise Exit-Aware Pretraining) reconciles the incompatibility between knowledge distillation and convergence-based early exit in transformers, enabling effective early exiting in distilled models without architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge distillation and convergence-based early exit are both popular techniques for transformer inference efficiency, but the authors discovered they are systematically incompatible under standard deployment conditions. Distillation objectives that align student layers to teacher representations suppress the representational convergence that early-exit mechanisms rely on, making early exit ineffective on distilled models.

Method: The authors introduce LEAP, an auxiliary training objective that augments standard distillation with a single constraint ensuring intermediate layers approximate final-layer representations. This approach requires no architectural modifications and allows both distillation benefits and early-exit mechanisms to work together effectively.

Result: LEAP-MiniLM achieves 1.61× measured wall-clock speedup (batch=1, NVIDIA L4) at θ=0.95, with 91.9% of samples exiting by layer 7 and 1.80× theoretical layer reduction. Standard distilled models achieve zero effective speedup. The method is validated across sentence similarity (STS-B: 0.760 ± 0.006) and retrieval benchmarks (BEIR).

Conclusion: LEAP successfully reconciles the incompatibility between layer-aligned distillation and convergence-based early exit, enabling both efficiency techniques to work together. The approach provides practical benefits with no architectural changes and is accompanied by operational guidance including latency measurements, decision thresholds, and deployment criteria.

Abstract: Layer-aligned distillation and convergence-based early exit represent two predominant computational efficiency paradigms for transformer inference; yet we establish that they exhibit systematic incompatibility under standard deployment conditions for convergence-based early exit. Distillation objectives that align intermediate student layers to teacher representations suppress the representational convergence that early-exit mechanisms exploit, rendering such mechanisms ineffective on distilled models. We introduce LEAP (Layer-wise Exit-Aware Pretraining), an auxiliary training objective that reconciles this incompatibility. LEAP requires no architectural modifications; it augments standard distillation with a single constraint ensuring intermediate layers approximate final-layer representations. LEAP-MiniLM achieves 1.61$\times$ measured wall-clock speedup (batch=1, NVIDIA L4) at $θ$=0.95, with 91.9% of samples exiting by layer 7 and 1.80$\times$ theoretical layer reduction, where standard distilled models achieve zero effective speedup. We validate across sentence similarity (STS-B: 0.760 $\pm$ 0.006) and retrieval benchmarks (BEIR), providing operational guidance including latency measurements, decision thresholds, and deployment criteria.

</details>


### [158] [GEODE: Angle-Adaptive OOD Detection with Universal Scorer Compatibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01063)
*Bruno Abrahao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GEODE is a synthetic boundary calibration method that replicates Outlier Exposure's benefits without requiring curated auxiliary data, working across all standard OOD scorers and avoiding catastrophic failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outlier Exposure (OE) has limitations: it shows scorer-dependent tradeoffs (strong on MSP but weak on KNN) and requires curated auxiliary data. There's a need for a more robust method that works across all scorers without external data.

Method: GEODE uses an angle-adaptive norm loss where targets scale per-sample with cosine similarity to the nearest class mean. This synthetically replicates OE's boundary calibration while preserving feature geometry, especially where boundary structure matters for distance-based scorers.

Result: GEODE achieves strong performance across all seven standard scorers on CIFAR-10 (near-OOD AUROC 89.0-92.3, far-OOD reaching 93.05) with no catastrophic failures. It outperforms vanilla CE, and when combined with OE reaches 95.0 MSP / 94.8 KNN on CIFAR-10. It also beats OE on every scorer on CIFAR-100 and shows gains on WRN-28-10.

Conclusion: GEODE provides robust OOD detection across all scorers by synthetically calibrating decision boundaries while preserving feature geometry, addressing OE's limitations and outperforming methods that push OOD into classifier null spaces.

Abstract: Outlier Exposure (OE) is among the strongest training-based OOD detectors on standard benchmarks but exhibits scorer-dependent tradeoffs (e.g., strong on MSP, weak on KNN) and requires curated auxiliary data. We show why OE works: its features sit at the same geometric locus as real near-OOD data, with the boundary-adjacent quartile driving nearly all of OE's gain. OE is boundary calibration, not OOD coverage. GEODE (GEOmetry-preserving DEtection) replicates this calibration synthetically through an angle-adaptive norm loss in which targets scale per-sample with cosine similarity to the nearest class mean, preserving feature geometry where boundary structure matters. Four theorems grounded in neural collapse justify the design. GEODE works across all seven standard scorers on CIFAR-10 (near-OOD AUROC 89.0-92.3, far-OOD reaching 93.05; no catastrophic failure on any scorer). Since the OOD regime is unknown at deployment, this is the test that matters. GEODE outperforms vanilla CE at matched epoch counts. Combined with OE, GEODE reaches 95.0 MSP / 94.8 KNN on CIFAR-10 and beats OE on every scorer on CIFAR-100. The gains hold on WRN-28-10 (+4.5 Energy, 3 seeds). Unlike methods that push OOD into the classifier null space (e.g., PFS, 14.38 KNN AUROC, worse than random), GEODE's adaptive target preserves the geometry that distance-based scorers depend on.

</details>


### [159] [A dimensional R2 regression metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01066)
*Jaesung Yoo,Stefan Lemke,Jian Zhong Guo,Kanaka Rajan,Adam Hantman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dim-R2 is a multidimensional extension of the standard R2 score that handles arbitrary input dimensions, provides richer accuracy patterns, and reduces sensitivity to noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard R2 score has three key limitations: it's limited to at most 2D inputs, reduces accuracy to a single scalar that hides patterns, and is sensitive to low-variance noise that can produce uninterpretable negative values.

Method: The authors introduce Dimensional R2 score (Dim-R2), a simple extension of R2 that accepts data of arbitrary dimensionality and provides a multidimensional view of accuracy while reducing noise sensitivity.

Result: The method demonstrates advantages on both synthetic sinusoidal data and three multidimensional regression datasets, showing improved interpretability and flexibility.

Conclusion: Dim-R2 offers an interpretable and flexible metric that highlights patterns in regression accuracy, providing better guidance for regression modeling compared to traditional R2.

Abstract: R2 score is the standard metric for evaluating regression tasks, offering a normalized magnitude-agnostic measure of accuracy that captures variance. However, R2 has three key limitations: it is limited to at most two dimensional inputs, it reduces the score to a single scalar that hides rich patterns of prediction accuracy, and it is sensitive to low-variance noise channels which can yield large, uninterpretable negative values. We introduce the Dimensional R2 score (Dim-R2), a simple extension of R2 that accepts data of arbitrary dimensionality, provides a multidimensional view of accuracy, and reduces sensitivity to noise. We demonstrate its advantages on both synthetic sinusoidal data and three multidimensional regression datasets. Dim-R2 offers an interpretable and flexible metric that highlights patterns in regression accuracy, guiding regression modeling.

</details>


### [160] [Deep Variational Inference Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01067)
*James Butterworth,Gevik Grigorian,Alejandro DiazDelaO*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DVISR introduces a Bayesian variational inference extension to Deep Symbolic Regression that outputs posterior distributions over both symbolic expressions and constants, enabling uncertainty quantification in symbolic regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symbolic regression needs Bayesian approaches to quantify uncertainty with noisy/limited data, but existing deep symbolic regression (DSR) only finds single best expressions rather than posterior distributions over models.

Method: Deep Variational Inference Symbolic Regression (DVISR) extends DSR by replacing the reward function with the evidence lower bound integrand and modifying the network architecture to output distributions over constants in expressions.

Result: DVISR successfully recovers true posterior distributions in simple settings (with and without constant tokens) and shows how performance scales with expression space size.

Conclusion: DVISR represents progress toward scalable Bayesian symbolic regression that provides uncertainty quantification over complete symbolic models, addressing limitations of previous point-estimate approaches.

Abstract: Symbolic regression discovers explicit, interpretable equations without assuming a functional form in advance. A Bayesian approach strengthens this through probability distributions over candidate expressions, thus quantifying uncertainty in the presence of noisy and limited data. Deep Symbolic Regression (DSR) uses a neural network to generate symbolic expressions, but it is designed to identify a single best-fitting expression rather than infer a posterior distribution over models. We introduce Deep Variational Inference Symbolic Regression (DVISR), a variational Bayesian extension of DSR. DVISR replaces the original reward with the integrand of the evidence lower bound. It also extends the network architecture to output distributions over constants within expressions, enabling posterior inference over both expression trees and their associated constants. We show that DVISR can recover the true posterior in simple settings, both with and without constant tokens, and we examine how its performance changes as the size of the expression space increases. These results position DVISR as a step toward scalable Bayesian symbolic regression with uncertainty over full symbolic models.

</details>


### [161] [Networked Information Aggregation for Binary Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01082)
*MohammadHossein Bateni,Zahra Hadizadeh,MohammadTaghi Hajiaghayi,Mahdi JafariRaviz,Shayan Taherijam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sequential logistic regression on DAGs can aggregate information with excess loss O(M/√D), but network depth D is a fundamental bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper extends prior work on linear regression in networked systems to binary classification with logistic regression. Existing guarantees for linear regression rely on quadratic structure that doesn't transfer to logistic regression with binary cross-entropy loss, creating a need for new analysis methods.

Method: Sequential distributed training on directed acyclic graphs where each agent receives predictions from parents, augments local features, fits logistic predictor by minimizing binary cross-entropy, and forwards predictions to neighbors.

Result: (i) Excess loss upper bound O(M/√D) on depth-D paths when every M contiguous agents observe all features. (ii) Lower bound Ω(k/D) where k is feature dimension, showing depth D is a fundamental bottleneck.

Conclusion: Network depth emerges as a fundamental limitation for information aggregation in networked logistic regression, with upper and lower bounds characterizing the trade-off between depth and achievable prediction quality.

Abstract: We study networked binary classification on a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where each agent observes only a subset of the feature columns of a shared dataset. Agents act sequentially along the DAG: each receives prediction columns from its parents (if any), augments its local features with these columns, fits a logistic predictor by minimizing binary cross-entropy (BCE), and forwards its prediction column to its outgoing neighbors. We ask whether this sequential distributed training procedure achieves information aggregation, meaning that some agent attains small excess loss compared to the best logistic predictor trained with access to all feature columns.
  This question was studied for linear regression under squared loss by Kearns, Roth, and Ryu (SODA 2026). Extending their guarantees to classification is nontrivial because their analysis relies on quadratic structure that does not directly transfer to BCE with a logistic link. We analyze the resulting sequential logit-passing protocol and prove: (i) an excess loss upper bound of $O(M/\sqrt{D})$ on depth-$D$ paths under the condition that every $M$ contiguous subsequence of $M$ agents collectively observe all features, and (ii) a close lower bound showing instances with excess loss of at least $Ω(k/D)$ where $k$ is the dimension of the feature space. Together, these results identify network depth as a fundamental bottleneck for information aggregation in networked logistic regression.

</details>


### [162] [Learning Discriminators for Resampling in the Ensemble Gaussian Mixture Filter through a Normalizing Flow Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01089)
*Zain Jabbar,Andrey A. Popov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A discriminator-informed resampling method for ensemble Gaussian mixture filters that uses normalizing flows to reject physically implausible particles, reducing errors in low-ensemble regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard EnGMF resampling step can generate physically unrealistic posterior samples that lead to meaningless forecasts, especially problematic for reliable filtering in non-linear systems.

Method: Augment the posterior resampling step with a discriminator that accepts/rejects candidate particles based on physical plausibility. The discriminator is learned using a normalizing flow approach.

Result: Numerical experiments on the Ikeda map and Lorenz '63 system show the discriminator-informed resampling consistently reduces error relative to standard EnGMF in low-ensemble regimes.

Conclusion: The discriminator-informed resampling procedure improves ensemble Gaussian mixture filters by filtering out physically implausible particles, particularly beneficial when computational resources limit ensemble size.

Abstract: The ensemble Gaussian mixture filter (EnGMF) is a powerful, convergent particle filter capable of medium-to-high dimensional non-linear filtering. The EnGMF relies on a resampling step that can generate physically unrealistic posterior samples, that would subsequently produce physically meaningless forecasts. This work introduces the discriminator-informed resampling procedure, that augments the posterior resampling step with a discriminator that accepts or rejects candidate particles based on their physical plausibility. In this work these discriminators are learned through a normalizing flow approach. Numerical experiments on both the Ikeda map and the Lorenz '63 system show that discriminator informed resampling procedure consistently reduces error relative to the standard EnGMF in low-ensemble regimes.

</details>


### [163] [Learning to Race in Minutes: Infoprop Dyna on the Mini Wheelbot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01096)
*Devdutt Subhasish,Henrik Hose,Sebastian Trimpe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mini Wheelbot robot learns to race around a track within 11 minutes of real-world experience using Infoprop Dyna MBRL framework, bypassing physics simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most recent RL advances rely on carefully designed physics-based simulators and domain randomization for successful sim-to-real transfer within reasonable time. The authors want to bypass the need for such simulators and enable robots to learn directly from real-world interactions.

Method: Uses Infoprop Dyna, a state-of-the-art uncertainty-aware model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) framework. The framework is applied to the Mini Wheelbot, an underactuated unicycle robot, enabling learning directly from real-world interactions without simulators.

Result: The Mini Wheelbot learns to race around a track within just 11 minutes of real-world experience, demonstrating successful learning directly from real-world interactions without physics simulators.

Conclusion: Infoprop Dyna MBRL framework can enable robots with fast, nonlinear, and unstable dynamics to learn directly from real-world interactions, bypassing the need for carefully designed physics-based simulators and domain randomization typically required for sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has the potential to enable robots with fast, nonlinear, and unstable dynamics to reach the limits of their performance. However, most recent advances rely on carefully designed physics-based simulators and domain randomization to achieve successful sim-to-real transfer within reasonable wall-clock time. In this work, we bypass the need for such simulators and demonstrate that Infoprop Dyna, a state-of-the-art uncertainty-aware model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) framework, can enable robots to learn directly from real-world interactions. Using Infoprop Dyna, the Mini Wheelbot, an underactuated unicycle robot, learns to race around a track within 11 minutes of real-world experience.

</details>


### [164] [Almost for Free: Crafting Adversarial Examples with Convolutional Image Filters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01098)
*Alexander Warnecke,Konrad Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes adversarial image filters based on edge detection algorithms to craft transferable adversarial attacks with minimal parameters (3x3 filters).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current adversarial example generation relies on gradient-based methods that require model access or queries. The paper seeks a simpler approach inspired by explainable ML insights.

Method: Design adversarial image filters using classic edge detection algorithms but optimized to deceive learning models. These are simple 3x3 filters requiring only a single pass over input.

Result: Achieves 30-80% success rates on different neural networks with 3x3 filters. Reduces parameters by five orders of magnitude compared to generative model approaches. Shows high transferability between models and structural similarities to classic image filters.

Conclusion: Provides insights into neural network vulnerabilities and their fragility to malicious noise, demonstrating that simple, optimized edge-detection-based filters can effectively create transferable adversarial examples.

Abstract: Adversarial examples in machine learning are typically generated using gradients, obtained either directly through access to the model or approximated via queries to it. In this paper, we propose a much simpler approach to craft adversarial examples, drawing inspiration from insights of explainable machine learning. In particular, we design \emph{adversarial image filters} that are based on classic edge detection algorithms but optimized to deceive learning models. The resulting untargeted attacks are transferable and require only a single pass over the input. Empirically, we find that 3x3 filters already enable success rates between 30% and 80% on different neural networks. Compared to related approaches using generative models for crafting adversarial examples, we reduce the number of parameters by five orders of magnitude, resulting in a very efficient attack. When investigating the parameters of the learned filters, we observe interesting properties such as a high transferability between models and structures common to classic image filters. Our results provide further insights into the vulnerability of neural networks and their fragility to malicious noise.

</details>


### [165] [Diffusion Operator Geometry of Feedforward Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01107)
*Kanishka Reddy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A smooth operator-theoretic framework using Gaussian-kernel diffusion operators to analyze neural network representation geometry, offering stable geometric observables that track training dynamics and model properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing geometric analysis of neural network representations uses discrete curvature on neighborhood graphs, which can be unstable and change discontinuously. There's a need for a stable, smooth analytical framework to understand representation geometry in terms of separation, contraction, and generalization properties.

Method: Develops a smooth operator-theoretic approach where each feature cloud induces a Gaussian-kernel diffusion Markov operator. Uses Bakry-Emery Γ-calculus to derive transport, spectral, label-boundary, and local-scale observables. Analyzes a balanced Gaussian class-conditional model with shared covariance, obtaining closed-form results for class affinities, leakage, and spectra controlled by regularized Mahalanobis separations.

Result: Proves operator observables vary smoothly under feature perturbations (unlike discrete neighborhood methods). Validates closed-form Gaussian bridge in synthetic experiments and shows operator observables track training dynamics, network width, and perturbation stability in MNIST experiments.

Conclusion: Provides a stable operator-geometric framework for analyzing feedforward representation geometry that offers smooth, continuous geometric diagnostics superior to discrete neighborhood-graph approaches, enabling better tracking of representation evolution during training and model variations.

Abstract: Neural networks transform data through learned representations whose geometry affects separation, contraction, and generalization. Recent work studies this geometry using discrete curvature on neighborhood graphs, suggesting Ricci-flow-like behavior across layers. We develop a smooth operator-theoretic alternative for feedforward representation snapshots. Each feature cloud induces a Gaussian-kernel diffusion Markov operator, and transport, spectral, label-boundary, and local-scale observables are derived from this single object via Bakry-Emery $Γ$-calculus. In a balanced Gaussian class-conditional snapshot model with shared covariance, the population operator has closed-form class affinities, leakage, and coarse spectra, all controlled by pairwise regularized Mahalanobis separations $c_\varepsilon^{(a,b)}$. We also prove that the resulting operator observables vary smoothly under feature perturbations, while hard neighborhood-graph diagnostics can change discontinuously. Synthetic experiments validate the closed-form Gaussian bridge, while learned MNIST experiments show that the same operator observables track training, width, and perturbation stability. Together, these results give a stable operator-geometric framework for analyzing feedforward representation geometry.

</details>


### [166] [Topological Neural Tangent Kernel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01110)
*Sanjukta Krishnagopal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces TopoNTK, a topological neural tangent kernel for simplicial complexes that extends beyond graph models to capture higher-order interactions, providing topological spectral bias and improved expressivity for relational learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current graph neural tangent kernels are limited to pairwise structures and cannot represent higher-order interactions present in many relational systems. Many systems require representation by simplicial complexes to capture these higher-order interactions naturally.

Method: Introduces Topological Neural Tangent Kernel (TopoNTK), an infinite-width kernel for simplicial message passing on edge features. It combines lower Hodge interactions (graph-like coupling through shared vertices) with upper Hodge interactions (coupling through filled simplices). This makes the kernel sensitive to topological structure invisible to graph kernels.

Result: TopoNTK demonstrates topological spectral bias: edge signals decompose into gradient-like, harmonic, and local circulation components, with the kernel spectrum determining learning speed of each component. Global harmonic modes are learned more slowly. The approach shows expressivity, Hodge-alignment, spectral learning, and stability properties validated on synthetic simplicial tasks and DBLP higher-order link prediction.

Conclusion: Topology provides coordinates that make relational learning more faithful, interpretable, and effective. It's not just extra structure but fundamentally changes learning dynamics through topological spectral bias and Hodge decomposition.

Abstract: Graph neural tangent kernels give a principled infinite-width theory for graph neural networks, but inherit a basic limitation of graph models: they see only pairwise structure. Many relational systems contain higher-order interactions that are more naturally represented by simplicial complexes. We introduce the Topological Neural Tangent Kernel (TopoNTK), an infinite-width kernel for simplicial message passing on edge features. TopoNTK combines lower Hodge interactions, capturing graph-like coupling through shared vertices, with upper Hodge interactions, capturing coupling through filled simplices. This makes the kernel sensitive to topology invisible to graph kernels, allowing complexes with the same graph but different filled simplices to induce different kernels. Beyond expressivity, the Hodge structure gives the kernel an interpretable learning geometry. Edge signals decompose into gradient-like, harmonic, and local circulation components, and the spectrum of the TopoNTK determines how quickly each component is learned. This yields a topological form of spectral bias: components aligned with large-eigenvalue modes are learned quickly, while global harmonic modes, retained through the residual channel, often lie at smaller eigenvalues and are learned more slowly. We prove expressivity, Hodge-alignment, spectral learning, and stability properties, and validate them on synthetic simplicial tasks and DBLP higher-order link prediction. The results show that topology is not merely extra structure; it can provide coordinates that make relational learning more faithful, interpretable, and effective.

</details>


### [167] [When Less is Enough: Efficient Inference via Collaborative Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01111)
*Yilei Chen,Sharut Gupta,Yannis Paschalidis,Ayush Sekhari,Aldo Pacchiano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DUET is a collaborative inference framework where a capable model provides reasoning signals to a lightweight model for efficient two-stage inference, reducing token usage by up to 60%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end inference using single large models incurs substantial computational costs. There's a need for efficient inference frameworks that maintain strong reasoning performance while reducing computational overhead.

Method: DUET decomposes inference into two stages: 1) a capable model produces reasoning signals, and 2) a lightweight model interprets these signals to generate final answers. Uses length-penalized joint training objective to encourage efficient signal transmission from capable model to lightweight model.

Result: DUET maintains strong reasoning performance while substantially reducing inference costs compared to end-to-end large model inference. Achieves up to 60% reduction in large model output tokens on challenging benchmarks (AIME and GPQA).

Conclusion: DUET demonstrates that collaborative inference between capable and lightweight models can achieve efficient reasoning performance through careful decomposition of reasoning-intensive and non-reasoning-intensive components, offering a practical solution for reducing inference costs without sacrificing task performance.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce DUET (Dual-model Efficient Two-stage inference), a collaborative inference framework in which a capable model and a lightweight model work together to solve a task. Relying on a single large model to perform end-to-end reasoning and prediction often incurs substantial inference cost. In contrast, DUET decomposes inference into two stages: the capable model produces a reasoning signal, and the lightweight model interprets this signal to generate the final answer, allowing reasoning-intensive computation to be handled by the capable model while non-reasoning-intensive components are delegated to the lightweight model without sacrificing task performance. To achieve this objective, we propose a length-penalized joint training objective that encourages the capable model to transmit only the information that is sufficient for the lightweight model to solve the task. As a result, DUET maintains strong reasoning performance with substantially lower inference cost than end-to-end inference using a large model alone, saving up to 60% of the large model's output tokens on challenging reasoning benchmarks, including AIME and GPQA.

</details>


### [168] [Machine Learning-Augmented Acceleration of Iterative Ptychographic Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01122)
*Bowen Zheng,Katayun Kamdin,David Shapiro,Alexander Ditter,Dayne Sasaki,Emma Bernard,Roopali Kukreja,Petrus H. Zwart,Slavomír Nemšák,Apurva Mehta,Nicholas Schwarz,Alexander Hexemer,Tanny Chavez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Machine learning-augmented ptychography accelerates iterative reconstruction by using learned fast-forward operators to reduce convergence time by over 2x while maintaining quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional iterative ptychographic reconstruction algorithms exhibit slow convergence under realistic experimental conditions, which limits real-time experimental operation and efficiency.

Method: Proposes a hybrid approach that combines standard iterative warm-up with a learned fast-forward operator that advances reconstruction toward converged state, then resumes conventional iterative updates. The model is trained on diverse ptychographic datasets to generalize across temporal variations.

Result: The method achieves comparable reconstruction quality to conventional iterative solvers while converging faster, yielding over two-fold reduction in wall-clock time. It maintains physical consistency and flexibility of established solvers while significantly accelerating convergence.

Conclusion: The machine learning-augmented approach successfully accelerates ptychographic reconstruction while preserving solver flexibility, demonstrates robust temporal generalization, and has been successfully integrated into production at a synchrotron beamline for practical real-time operation.

Abstract: Iterative ptychographic reconstruction algorithms are widely used for coherent diffractive imaging but can exhibit slow convergence under realistic experimental conditions. We propose a machine learning-augmented approach that accelerates iterative ptychographic reconstruction by introducing a learned fast-forward operator applied during reconstruction. Following an initial warm-up using standard iterations, the fast-forward operator advances the reconstruction toward a more converged state, after which conventional iterative updates are resumed. This strategy preserves the physical consistency and flexibility of established ptychographic solvers while reducing the number of iterations required for convergence. The model is trained on diverse ptychographic datasets and evaluated on experimental data acquired in a different year, demonstrating robustness and temporal generalization. Compared with conventional iterative solvers, the machine learning-augmented method achieves comparable reconstruction quality while converging faster in terms of Poisson negative log-likelihood, yielding over a two-fold reduction in wall-clock time. The approach has been integrated into an existing reconstruction pipeline and deployed in production at a synchrotron beamline, demonstrating practicality for real-time experimental operation.

</details>


### [169] [Extreme Weather Bench: A framework and benchmark for evaluation of high-impact weather](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01126)
*Amy McGovern,Taylor Mandelbaum,Daniel Rothenberg,Nicholas Loveday,Corey Potvin,Montgomery Flora,Linus Magnusson,Eric Gilleland,John Allen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Extreme Weather Bench (EWB), a community-driven open-source benchmark suite for evaluating AI and numerical weather prediction models on high-impact weather events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of AI weather models is limited to global-scale assessments or small hand-picked case studies, lacking standardized verification for high-impact weather events that matter to people globally. Proper verification is critical before deployment but current approaches are insufficient.

Method: Created EWB as a comprehensive benchmark suite that provides: standard case studies across multiple spatial/temporal scales, observational data, impact-based metrics, and open-source code for model evaluation. The system is community-driven and designed to evolve with community input.

Result: EWB provides a standardized framework for evaluating weather models on specific high-impact phenomena through case studies. It enables true comparisons across models and focuses on events that have direct impact on the general public.

Conclusion: EWB addresses a critical gap in weather model verification by providing a standardized, community-driven benchmark for high-impact weather events. It will drive scientific progress by enabling proper model comparisons and improving trustworthiness of AI weather models through focused verification on important real-world phenomena.

Abstract: Forecasting the wide variety of high-impact weather events experienced globally is a challenge for both Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) models and it is critical that such models be properly verified before deployment. Although AI weather models are rapidly evolving, much of their evaluation is currently done either with a global-scale evaluation or by hand-picking a small number of case studies or a region. A widely-used open-source benchmark suite focusing on high-impact weather will help to drive the science forward for all scales of weather models, as it has for other AI fields. Here we introduce Extreme Weather Bench (EWB), a new community-driven benchmark suite that facilitates model validation and verification on a variety of high-impact hazards that matter to people around the globe. EWB provides a standard set of case studies (spanning across multiple spatial and temporal scales and different parts of the weather spectrum), observational data, impact-based metrics, and open-source code for users to evaluate their models. Verifying that a model works against a standard set of case studies, especially events that are high-impact for the general public, is a key piece of improving the trustworthiness of AI models. EWB will help to drive the science forward for all weather models, enabling true comparisons across models and evaluating models on specific high-impact phenomena through the use of case studies. EWB is a free open-source community-driven system and will continue to evolve to include additional phenomena, test cases and metrics in collaboration with the worldwide weather and forecast verification community.

</details>


### [170] [Forager: a lightweight testbed for continual learning with partial observability in RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01131)
*Steven Tang,Xinze Xiong,Anna Hakhverdyan,Andrew Patterson,Jacob Adkins,Jiamin He,Esraa Elelimy,Parham Mohammad Panahi,Martha White,Adam White*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Forager is a lightweight, partially-observable continual RL environment that reveals limitations of current agents and highlights the importance of state construction over loss-of-plasticity mitigations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most continual RL experiments focus on loss of plasticity in fully observable MDPs, ignoring partial observability and memory-based agents. Existing partially-observable CRL environments are often prohibitively expensive, limiting research into these important aspects.

Method: Introduced Forager - a lightweight partially-observable CRL environment with constant memory footprint. Conducted experiments with current CRL agents to study their performance, plasticity loss, and effectiveness of proposed mitigations, with special focus on state construction approaches.

Result: Forager proved challenging for current CRL agents. While agents exhibited loss of plasticity and some mitigations helped, the most effective approach was leveraging state construction. A variant generating unending new tasks clearly highlighted limitations of current CRL agents.

Conclusion: Forager enables in-depth study of CRL agents in partially-observable settings. Current CRL agents have significant limitations, particularly when faced with unending new tasks, and state construction approaches show more promise than loss-of-plasticity mitigations alone.

Abstract: In continual reinforcement learning (CRL), good performance requires never-ending learning, acting, and exploration in a big, partially observable world. Most CRL experiments have focused on loss of plasticity -- the inability to keep learning -- in one-off experiments where some unobservable non-stationarity is added to classic fully observable MDPs. Further, these experiments rarely consider the role of partial observability and the importance of CRL agents that use memory or recurrence. One potential reason for this focus on mitigating loss of plasticity without considering partial observability is that many partially-observable CRL environments are prohibitively expensive. In this paper, we introduce Forager, a light-weight partially-observable CRL environment with a constant memory footprint. We provide a set of experiments and sample tasks demonstrating that Forager is challenging for current CRL agents and yet also allows for in-depth study of those agents. We demonstrate that agents exhibit loss of plasticity, proposed mitigations can help, but that most useful is to leverage state construction. We conclude with a variant of Forager that generates an unending stream of new tasks to learn that clearly highlights the limitations of current CRL agents.

</details>


### [171] [Spectral Graph Sparsification Preserves Representation Geometry in Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01136)
*Sanjukta Krishnagopal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral graph sparsification preserves representation geometry in GNNs, with theoretical bounds on perturbation of embeddings and training dynamics, validated empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While spectral sparsification is known to preserve graph operators and accelerate computation, it's unclear whether it preserves the geometry of learned GNN embeddings, which is crucial for downstream interpretability tasks.

Method: Theoretical analysis proving ε-spectral sparsifiers induce O(ε) perturbations in polynomial graph filters, multilayer representations, and Gram matrices; plus empirical validation using effective-resistance sparsification on synthetic and real datasets (FashionMNIST, Cora, Paul15).

Result: Spectral sparsification preserves representation geometry: shows low divergence in Gram matrices and training dynamics even under substantial sparsification; hidden Gram preservation strongly predicts neighborhood and class-centroid stability across datasets.

Conclusion: Spectral sparsification preserves not only graph operators but also the representation geometry that supports downstream interpretability of GNN embeddings, bridging spectral approximation with representation-level stability.

Abstract: Spectral graph sparsification is a classical tool for reducing graph complexity while preserving Laplacian quadratic forms. In graph neural networks (GNNs), sparsification is often used to accelerate computation while maintaining predictive performance. In this work, we study a complementary representation-level question: does sparsification preserve the geometry of learned embeddings?
  For polynomial-filter GNNs, we prove that any $ε$-spectral sparsifier induces $O(ε)$ perturbations in polynomial graph filters, multilayer hidden representations, and their Gram matrices. These guarantees imply stability of squared pairwise distances, class means, and covariance structure in embedding space. We further establish finite-time training stability: under smoothness and boundedness assumptions, gradient descent on dense and sparsified graphs produces weight trajectories whose separation grows at most proportionally to the sparsification distortion.
  Empirically, effective-resistance sparsification validates the predicted perturbation chain on synthetic graphs and preserves hidden representation geometry on real datasets. In our experiments, the gram matrix and training dynamics show low divergence even under substantial sparsification, consistent with the predicted stability under spectral sparsification. Hidden Gram preservation strongly predicts neighborhood preservation and class-centroid stability across FashionMNIST, Cora, and Paul15. Together, these results show that spectral sparsification preserves not only graph operators, but also the representation geometry that supports downstream use of GNN embeddings for interpretability.

</details>


### [172] [Metric-Normalized Posterior Leakage (mPL): Attacker-Aligned Privacy for Joint Consumption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01137)
*Gaoyi Chen,Minghao Li,Weishi Shi,Yan Huang,Yusheng Wei,Sourabh Yadav,Chenxi Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces metric-normalized posterior leakage (mPL) to address privacy risks in joint-consumption machine learning systems where traditional metric differential privacy fails, and proposes Adaptive mPL framework with probabilistic guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing metric differential privacy (mDP) provides per-record guarantees but fails in joint-observation settings where attackers can aggregate evidence across correlated data items, leaving significant privacy leakage despite mDP compliance.

Method: Introduces metric-normalized posterior leakage (mPL) as an attacker-aligned measure, formalizes probabilistically bounded mPL (PBmPL), and develops Adaptive mPL (AmPL) - a trust-and-verify framework that perturbs data, audits with learned attackers, adapts parameters, and optionally uses Bayesian remapping.

Result: In word-embedding case studies, neural adversaries successfully violate mPL under joint consumption despite per-record mDP perturbations, while AmPL substantially reduces such violations with minimal utility loss, demonstrating PBmPL as practical protection.

Conclusion: PBmPL provides a practical, certifiable privacy protection for joint-consumption ML systems, overcoming mDP's limitations by explicitly addressing evidence aggregation risks through adaptive auditing and parameter adjustment.

Abstract: Metric differential privacy (mDP) strengthens local differential privacy (LDP) by scaling noise to semantic distance, but many machine learning (ML) systems are consumed under joint observation, where model-agnostic, per-record guarantees can miss leakage from evidence aggregation. We introduce metric-normalized posterior leakage (mPL), an attacker-aligned, distance-calibrated measure of posterior-odds shift induced by releases, and show that for single or independent releases, uniformly bounding mPL is equivalent to mDP. Under joint observation, however, satisfying mDP may still leave mPL high because learned aggregators compound evidence across correlated items. To make control practical, we formalize probabilistically bounded mPL (PBmPL), which limits how often mPL may exceed a target budget, and we operationalize it via Adaptive mPL (AmPL), a trust-and-verify framework that perturbs, audits with a learned attacker, and adapts parameters (with optional Bayesian remapping) to balance privacy and utility. In a word-embedding case study, neural adversaries violate mPL under joint consumption despite per-record mDP perturbations, whereas AmPL substantially lowers the frequency of such violations with low utility loss, indicating PBmPL as a practical, certifiable protection for joint-consumption settings.

</details>


### [173] [Multi-Perspective Transformers in ARC-AGI-2 Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01154)
*Caleb Talley,Vedant Tibrewal,Seun Adekunle,Weiwen Dong,Xinyu Wu,Fariha Sheikh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TinyLM achieves 21.7% accuracy on ARC-AGI-2 benchmark using test-time fine-tuning methods like Test-Time-Training and Products of Experts


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ARC-AGI-2 is a challenging benchmark for evaluating machine intelligence on human-intuitive visual puzzles that require generalization from limited examples, symbolic reasoning, and flexible rule application

Method: Using TinyLM with additional fine-tuning at test time, including Test-Time-Training (TTT) and Products of Experts (POE) techniques

Result: Model achieves 96.1% accuracy on training set and 21.7% accuracy on evaluation set of ARC-AGI-2 benchmark

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates some success on the challenging ARC-AGI-2 benchmark, but the significant gap between training and evaluation accuracy suggests remaining challenges in generalization to novel puzzle contexts

Abstract: ARC-AGI-2 is a benchmark of human-intuitive visual puzzles that measures a machine's ability to generalize from limited examples, interpret symbolic meaning, and flexibly apply rules in varying contexts. In this paper, we discuss our approach to solving the ARC-AGI-2 puzzles with TinyLM, with additional fine-tuning at test time, including Test-Time-Training (TTT) and Products of Experts (POE). Our model achieves 96.1% accuracy on the training set and 21.7% accuracy on the evaluation set.

</details>


### [174] [Minimizing Collateral Damage in Activation Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01167)
*Tam Nguyen,Tu Anh Nguyen,Sina Alemohammad,Richard G. Baraniuk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a principled optimization framework for activation steering in LLMs that minimizes collateral damage by weighting perturbations based on the empirical second-moment matrix of activations, rather than assuming isotropic feature distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard activation steering methods (like vector addition) cause "collateral damage" - unintended changes in non-target feature directions. This occurs because these methods implicitly assume isotropic distributions of non-target features, which doesn't reflect real activation patterns in LLMs.

Method: Develops a mathematical formalization of collateral damage and frames steering as a constrained optimization problem. The method finds new activations that minimize expected squared collateral change weighted by the empirical second-moment matrix of activations, which captures the non-uniform cost of perturbations across different feature directions.

Result: The approach achieves more precise control over LLM behavior while reducing performance degradation on unrelated tasks compared to standard isotropic methods.

Conclusion: By properly accounting for the empirical distribution of activations (via second-moment weighting), activation steering can be made more targeted with less collateral damage, representing a more principled approach to LLM control.

Abstract: Activation steering is a method for controlling Large Language Model (LLM) behavior by intervening in its internal representations to increase the alignment with a specific target feature direction. However, standard interventions, such as vector addition, often cause ``collateral damage", defined as unintended changes in the alignment of activations along other non-target feature directions. This damage occurs because standard methods implicitly assume the isotropy of non-target features. In this work, we provide a mathematical formalization of collateral damage and introduce a principled framework that models steering as a constrained optimization problem. Our method finds a new activation that minimizes the expected squared collateral change weighted by the empirical second-moment matrix of activations. This weighting encodes the nonuniform cost of the perturbation in different feature directions, in contrast to isotropic approaches that penalize changes uniformly in all feature directions. By accounting for the empirical second-moment of activations, our approach achieves more precise control while reducing the degradation of model performance on unrelated tasks.

</details>


### [175] [A Theory of Generalization in Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01172)
*Elon Litman,Gabe Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A non-asymptotic generalization theory for deep learning that explains signal-noise separation via neural tangent kernel partitioning, enabling new optimization methods that accelerate learning and reduce memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning theory lacks a comprehensive framework explaining generalization phenomena like benign overfitting, double descent, implicit bias, and grokking, especially in the full feature-learning regime where neural tangent kernels evolve significantly.

Method: The paper develops a theory where empirical neural tangent kernel partitions output space into signal (rapid error dissipation) and noise (trapped residual error) dimensions. Analyzes minibatch SGD dynamics separating coherent population signal (linear drift) from idiosyncratic memorization (diffusive random walk). Derives exact population-risk objective from single training run without validation data.

Result: Proves generalization survives with O(1) kernel evolution (full feature-learning regime). Develops practical SNR preconditioner for Adam that accelerates grokking 5x, suppresses memorization in PINNs/neural representations, and improves DPO fine-tuning with noisy preferences while staying 3x closer to reference policy.

Conclusion: The theory provides a unified explanation for diverse deep learning phenomena and yields practical optimization improvements that better separate signal from noise, enhancing generalization across various architectures and tasks.

Abstract: We present a non-asymptotic theory of generalization in deep learning where the empirical neural tangent kernel partitions the output space. In directions corresponding to signal, error dissipates rapidly; in the vast orthogonal dimensions corresponding to noise, the kernel's near-zero eigenvalues trap residual error in a test-invisible reservoir. Within the signal channel, minibatch SGD ensures that coherent population signal accumulates via fast linear drift, while idiosyncratic memorization is suppressed into a slow, diffusive random walk. We prove generalization survives even when the kernel evolves $\mathcal{O}(1)$ in operator norm, the full feature-learning regime. This theory naturally explains disparate phenomena in deep learning theory, such as benign overfitting, double descent, implicit bias, and grokking. Lastly, we derive an exact population-risk objective from a single training run with no validation data, for any architecture, loss, or optimizer, and prove that it measures precisely the noise in the signal channel. This objective reduces in practice to an SNR preconditioner on top of Adam, adding one state vector at no extra cost; it accelerates grokking by $5 \times$, suppresses memorization in PINNs and implicit neural representations, and improves DPO fine-tuning under noisy preferences while staying $3 \times$ closer to the reference policy.

</details>


### [176] [Linear-Readout Floors and Threshold Recovery in Computation in Superposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01192)
*Hector Borobia,Elies Seguí-Mas,Guillermina Tormo-Carbó*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper reconciles two different computational capacity regimes in superposition by showing they use different interface invariants, and provides a formal distinction with Welch-type lower bounds for linear readouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two recent approaches to computation in superposition report different recursive capacity regimes: Hänni et al. achieve ~O(d^{3/2}) capacity via approximate-linear recursion, while Adler and Shavit achieve near-quadratic capacity using thresholded Boolean recovery. The paper aims to resolve this apparent contradiction by showing these results maintain different interface invariants.

Method: 1) Formalizes the distinction between different interface invariants in the two approaches. 2) Develops a rank-trace Welch-type lower bound for biorthogonal linear readouts, showing worst-case off-diagonal cross-talk is Ω(d^{-1/2}) for F >> d. 3) Analyzes threshold recovery at quadratic feature load F=d^2, showing random-support threshold recovery succeeds for sparsities s=O(d/log d). 4) Compares linear readouts performance against Bernoulli sparse states.

Result: 1) Shows the apparent contradiction between the two capacity regimes stems from different interface invariants rather than fundamental limitations. 2) The d^{3/2} scale emerges as a compatibility threshold for the Hänni correction layer template when matching the Welch floor against published tolerance levels. 3) Demonstrates threshold recovery can succeed where linear readouts fail, explaining the different capacity regimes.

Conclusion: The two different capacity regimes are not contradictory because they maintain different interface invariants. The d^{3/2} scale is a compatibility threshold specific to the Hänni template, not a universal upper bound. Robust nonlinear reset beyond the Hänni template remains an open problem.

Abstract: Two recent approaches to computation in superposition reach different recursive capacity regimes: Hänni et al. certify $\tilde{O}(d^{3/2})$ computable features in width $d$ via an approximate-linear recursive template, while Adler and Shavit reach near-quadratic capacity (up to logarithmic factors) using thresholded Boolean recovery. The main contribution of this paper is conceptual: we argue these results are not contradictory because they maintain different interface invariants, and we formalize the distinction.
  As a tool, we record a rank-trace Welch-type lower bound for biorthogonal linear readouts: for $F \gg d$, the worst-case off-diagonal cross-talk of any unit-diagonal linear readout is $Ω(d^{-1/2})$, and the bound is tight on average for unit-norm tight frames. At quadratic feature load $F=d^2$, random-support threshold recovery succeeds for sparsities $s=O(d/\log d)$, while linear readouts still incur $Ω(s/d)$ average per-coordinate squared error on Bernoulli sparse states. Matching the Welch floor against the published tolerance of the Hänni correction layer explains the $d^{3/2}$ scale as a compatibility threshold for that template, not a universal upper bound. Robust nonlinear reset beyond the Hänni template is left open.

</details>


### [177] [Focus and Dilution: The Multi-stage Learning Process of Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01199)
*Zheng-An Chen,Pengxiao Lin,Zhi-Qin John Xu,Tao Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Paper identifies a focus-dilution cycle in attention learning in Transformers, providing theoretical analysis for one-layer Transformers on Markovian data, showing attention evolves through distinct stages of focusing on high-frequency tokens then diluting that focus, with experiments validating the theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Transformers have achieved remarkable success, our understanding of their training dynamics remains limited, particularly the recurrent learning patterns observed in attention mechanisms during training.

Method: Theoretical analysis using gradient-flow analysis in a one-layer Transformer setting for Markovian data, employing stage-wise linearization around critical points to decompose learning dynamics into distinct stages, with experimental validation on synthetic Markovian data, WikiText, and TinyStories.

Result: Identified a focus-dilution cycle where: 1) embeddings condense to rank-one while attention remains frozen; 2) attention focuses on high-frequency tokens; 3) attention evolution causes mass redistribution diluting the focus; 4) small asymmetries break degeneracy and initiate next cycle. Experiments confirm predicted stages.

Conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous explanation of Transformer training dynamics through the lens of focus-dilution cycles, offering new insights into how attention mechanisms evolve during learning, which could inform better training strategies and architectural designs.

Abstract: Transformer-based models have achieved remarkable success across a wide range of domains, yet our understanding of their training dynamics remains limited. In this work, we identify a recurrent focus-dilution cycle in attention learning and provide a rigorous explanation in a one-layer Transformer setting for Markovian data via gradient-flow analysis. Using stage-wise linearization around critical points, we show that a single focus-dilution cycle can be decomposed into a sequence of distinct stages. First, embedding and projection rapidly condense to a rank-one structure, while attention parameters remain effectively frozen. Then, the attention parameters begin to increase, inducing a frequency-driven focus toward high-frequency tokens. As attention continues to evolve, it generates next-order perturbations in embeddings, leading to a mass-redistribution mechanism that progressively dilutes this focus. Finally, small asymmetries among low-frequency tokens lift a degenerate critical point, opening new embedding directions and initiating the next cycle. Experiments on synthetic Markovian data as well as WikiText and TinyStories corroborate the predicted stages and cyclical dynamics.

</details>


### [178] [Local Hessian Spectral Filtering for Robust Intrinsic Dimension Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01221)
*Genki Osada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TL;DR: LHSD (Local Hessian Spectral Dimension) uses spectral filtering on the log-density Hessian to accurately estimate local intrinsic dimension in high-dimensional spaces, cutting off noise from normal directions and counting only tangent directions, with linear scalability via Stochastic Lanczos Quadrature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based methods for estimating Local Intrinsic Dimension (LID) fail in high-dimensional spaces because noise from vast normal directions overwhelms the tangent signal, making accurate dimension estimation difficult.

Method: Proposes Local Hessian Spectral Dimension (LHSD) which applies spectral filtering to the log-density Hessian, explicitly cutting off large eigenvalues associated with normal directions to count only zero-curvature tangent directions. Uses Stochastic Lanczos Quadrature (SLQ) to avoid full Hessian construction, achieving linear scalability with dimension D.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real data confirm LHSD's superior robustness over existing methods. The method also demonstrates utility in detecting memorization in large-scale diffusion models.

Conclusion: LHSD provides an effective solution to the high-dimensional noise problem in local intrinsic dimension estimation, offering both theoretical robustness and practical efficiency for analyzing complex datasets and large-scale generative models.

Abstract: While diffusion models enable new approaches for estimating Local Intrinsic Dimension (LID), existing methods fail in high-dimensional spaces where noise from vast normal directions overwhelms the tangent signal. We propose Local Hessian Spectral Dimension (LHSD), which resolves this by applying spectral filtering to the log-density Hessian, explicitly cutting off large eigenvalues associated with normal directions to count zero-curvature tangent directions. Implemented using Stochastic Lanczos Quadrature (SLQ), LHSD avoids full Hessian construction, achieving linear scalability with dimension $D$. Experiments on synthetic and real data confirm LHSD's superior robustness and its utility in detecting memorization in large-scale diffusion models.

</details>


### [179] [Arbitrarily Conditioned Hierarchical Flows for Spatiotemporal Events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01226)
*Keyan Chen,Qiwei Yuan,Zhitong Xu,Bin Shen,Shandian Zhe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ARCH is a hierarchical flow matching framework for spatiotemporal event modeling that enables flexible conditioning on arbitrary observed events for forecasting, inverse inference, and trajectory reconstruction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing point process models rely on strong structural assumptions, are limited to autoregressive event-by-event prediction, and struggle with broader inference tasks like inverse inference, trajectory reconstruction, and recovery of missing event locations.

Method: Introduces Arbitrarily Conditioned Hierarchical Flows (ARCH), a hierarchical flow matching framework with a history-encoder-generative-decoder architecture and hybrid masking strategy for flexible conditioning on arbitrary observed events.

Result: ARCH consistently outperforms existing baselines across both prediction and conditional inference tasks on synthetic and real-world datasets, capturing complex event distributions while enabling tractable computation of conditional intensities.

Conclusion: ARCH provides a unified framework for spatiotemporal event modeling that supports multiple inference tasks (forecasting, inverse inference, partial trajectory recovery) with improved performance over existing methods.

Abstract: Events in spatiotemporal systems are ubiquitous, yet modeling their complex distributions remains challenging. Existing point process models often rely on strong structural assumptions and are typically limited to autoregressive, event-by-event prediction. As a result, they struggle to support broader inference tasks such as inverse inference, trajectory reconstruction, and recovery of missing event locations. We introduce Arbitrarily Conditioned Hierarchical Flows (ARCH), a hierarchical flow matching framework for spatiotemporal event modeling. ARCH is expressive enough to capture complex event distributions while enabling tractable and accurate computation of conditional intensities, which quantify instantaneous event risk. Built on a history-encoder-generative-decoder architecture, ARCH introduces a hybrid masking strategy for flexible conditioning on arbitrary observed events. This enables a unified treatment of forecasting, inverse inference, and partial trajectory recovery within a single framework. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that ARCH consistently outperforms existing baselines across both prediction and conditional inference tasks.

</details>


### [180] [Attention Sinks in Massively Multilingual Neural Machine Translation:Discovery, Analysis, and Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01229)
*Hillary Mutisya,John Mugane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-attention analysis in NMT contains systematic artifacts where non-content tokens ("attention sinks") dominate attention mass, requiring content-filtering methodology for reliable linguistic analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current cross-attention analysis in neural machine translation (particularly NLLB-200) contains artifacts that make raw metrics unreliable for studying linguistic alignment, as non-content tokens capture most attention mass.

Method: Developed content-only filtering methodology to remove non-content tokens (end-of-sequence, language tags, punctuation) and renormalize distributions. Applied to 1,000 parallel sentences across 8 languages to validate the artifact and recover masked linguistic signals.

Result: Non-content tokens capture 83-91% of total cross-attention mass, causing raw metrics to underestimate content similarity by nearly half. Filtering reveals hidden patterns: 16.9pp gap between teacher-forcing and generation modes, language-family clustering in attention entropy, and Somali paradox linking SOV word order to monotonic alignment.

Conclusion: Attention sink artifacts in NMT cross-attention are universal and rooted in vocabulary design, requiring content-filtering methodology for reliable interpretability research. The toolkit and corrected datasets are released to support reproducible multilingual NMT analysis.

Abstract: Cross-attention patterns in neural machine translation (NMT) are widely used to study how multilingual models align linguistic structure. We report a systematic artifact in cross-attention analysis of NLLB-200 (600M): non-content tokens - primarily end-of-sequence tokens, language tags, and punctuation - capture 83 percent to 91 percent of total cross-attention mass. We term these "attention sinks," extending findings from LLMs [Xiao et al., 2023] to NMT cross-attention and identifying a causal mechanism rooted in vocabulary design rather than position bias. This artifact causes raw metrics to underestimate content-level similarity by nearly half (36.7 percent raw vs. 70.7 percent filtered), rendering uncorrected analyses unreliable. To address this, we validate a content-only filtering methodology that removes non-content tokens and renormalizes the distribution. Applying this to 1,000 parallel sentences across African languages (Swahili, Kikuyu, Somali, Luo) and non-African benchmarks (German, Turkish, Chinese, Hindi), we confirm the artifact is universal and recover masked linguistic signals: a 16.9 percentage-point gap between teacher-forcing and generation modes, clear language-family clustering in attention entropy, and a hidden Somali paradox linking SOV word order to monotonic alignment. We release our filtering toolkit and corrected datasets to support reproducible interpretability research on multilingual NMT.

</details>


### [181] [CombinationTS: A Modular Framework for Understanding Time-Series Forecasting Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01231)
*Xiaorui Wang,Fanda Fan,Chenxi Wang,Yuxuan Yang,Rui Tang,Kuoyu Gao,Simiao Pang,Yuanfeng Shang,Zhipeng Liu,Wanling Gao,Lei Wang,Jianfeng Zhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CombinationTS is a framework that decomposes forecasting models into orthogonal modules to evaluate which components truly drive performance, revealing that well-designed data views with simple identity encoders often match complex models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent progress in time-series forecasting has led to rapidly increasing architectural complexity, but many reported State-of-the-Art gains are statistically fragile or misattributed. There's a need to shift from model selection to modular attribution to identify which components truly drive performance.

Method: Proposes CombinationTS, a self-contained probabilistic evaluation framework that decomposes forecasting models into five orthogonal modules: Input Transformation, Embedding, Encoder, Decoder, and Output Transformation. Evaluates them under a shared evaluation condition space, quantifying each component via marginalized performance (μ) and stability (σ).

Result: Through large-scale paired evaluation, uncovers the Identity Paradox: once the data view (Embedding) is well-designed, a parameter-free Identity Encoder often matches or outperforms complex backbones. Shows that explicit structural priors via Input Transformations yield more favorable performance-stability trade-off than increasing Encoder complexity.

Conclusion: The framework enables robust attribution beyond fragile point estimates and establishes a principled baseline for architectural necessity, suggesting that progress in forecasting should focus on modular attribution rather than architectural complexity.

Abstract: Recent progress in time-series forecasting has led to rapidly increasing architectural complexity, yet many reported State-of-the-Art gains are statistically fragile or misattributed. We argue that progress requires a shift from model selection to modular attribution, identifying which components truly drive performance. We propose CombinationTS, a self-contained probabilistic evaluation framework that decomposes forecasting models into orthogonal modules--Input Transformation, Embedding, Encoder, Decoder, and Output Transformation--and evaluates them under a shared evaluation condition space. By quantifying each component via marginalized performance ($μ$) and stability ($σ$), CombinationTS enables robust attribution beyond fragile point estimates. Through large-scale paired evaluation, we uncover the Identity Paradox: once the data view (Embedding) is well-designed, a parameter-free Identity Encoder often matches or outperforms complex backbones. We further show that explicit structural priors introduced via Input Transformations yield a more favorable performance-stability trade-off than increasing Encoder complexity, establishing a principled baseline for architectural necessity.

</details>


### [182] [Rhamba: Region-Aware Hybrid Attention-Mamba Framework for Self-Supervised Learning in Resting-State fMRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01240)
*Ruthwik Reddy Doodipala,Pankaj Pandey,Pratheek Eranki,Carolina Torres-Rojas,Manob Jyoti Saikia,Ranganatha Sitaram*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Rhamba introduces a region-aware pretraining framework for fMRI analysis using anatomically guided masking and hybrid Attention-Mamba architectures, achieving state-of-the-art performance on neuroimaging classification tasks while offering interpretable region-based insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-supervised pretraining shows promise for large-scale neuroimaging, but the impact of region-aware masking strategies and hybrid sequence modeling approaches (combining Attention and Mamba architectures) remains underexplored in resting state fMRI analysis.

Method: Rhamba framework integrates anatomically guided masking with hybrid Attention-Mamba architectures. Uses region-aligned patch embeddings with three masking strategies (Any, Majority, Pure) of increasing spatial specificity. Evaluates four architectural variants: Mamba only, Alternate (interleaved blocks), Attention-Mamba (AM), and Mamba-Attention (MA). Pretrained on ABIDE dataset, fine-tuned on COBRE (schizophrenia) and ADHD-200 (ADHD) classification tasks, with Integrated Gradients for explainable AI region analysis.

Result: Masking strategy strongly influenced reconstruction loss (Any > Majority > Pure), but downstream performance differences were modest and dataset-dependent. Hybrid MA configuration achieved highest average AUROC across both datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. Peak performance depends on interaction between masking strategy and architecture rather than single dominant configuration.

Conclusion: Rhamba provides a flexible framework for balancing interpretability, scalability, and performance in large-scale fMRI representation learning, demonstrating that anatomically informed masking combined with hybrid sequence modeling can effectively leverage neuroimaging data structure.

Abstract: Self-supervised pretraining is promising for large-scale neuroimaging, yet the impact of region-aware masking and hybrid sequence modeling remains underexplored.
