In this work, we introduce Rhamba, a region-aware pretraining framework that integrates anatomically guided masking with hybrid Attention-Mamba architectures for resting state functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) analysis. Models were pretrained on the ABIDE dataset using region-aligned patch embeddings and three masking strategies (Any, Majority, and Pure) with increasing spatial specificity. We evaluated four architectural variants: a Mamba only model, an Alternate architecture with interleaved Mamba and Attention blocks, and two hybrid encoder-decoder configurations (Attention-Mamba (AM) and Mamba-Attention (MA)). The pretrained models were fine-tuned on downstream classification tasks using the COBRE and ADHD-200 datasets for schizophrenia and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder discrimination. We employed Integrated Gradients, an explainable AI method, to identify the brain regions contributing to model predictions. Masking strategy strongly influenced reconstruction behavior, with reconstruction loss following a consistent ordering (Any > Majority > Pure). However, this trend did not directly translate into downstream performance, where differences were modest and dataset-dependent. The hybrid architecture with the MA configuration achieved the highest average AUROC across both datasets, and Rhamba outperformed state-of-the-art methods in comparative evaluation. Region-wise analysis showed that peak performance depends on the interaction between masking strategy and architecture rather than a single dominant configuration. Overall, Rhamba offers a flexible framework for balancing interpretability, scalability, and performance in large-scale fMRI representation learning.

</details>


### [183] [Breaking the Computational Barrier: Provably Efficient Actor-Critic for Low-Rank MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01242)
*Ruiquan Huang,Donghao Li,Yingbin Liang,Jing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reinforcement learning approach using supervised learning as a proxy establishes policy evaluation as the most efficient oracle for low-rank MDPs, leading to an optimistic actor-critic algorithm with improved sample complexity and reduced computational requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL algorithms with function approximation often have good sample complexity but rely on computationally intractable oracles. The authors aim to establish a computational hierarchy of RL oracles and develop more efficient algorithms that avoid expensive planning or optimization oracles.

Method: The paper uses supervised learning as a computational proxy to analyze RL oracle efficiency under low-rank MDPs, proposes a novel optimistic actor-critic algorithm that relies solely on policy evaluation oracle, and extends results to approximately low-rank MDPs.

Result: The established hierarchy shows policy evaluation is the most computationally efficient oracle when supervised learning is efficient. The proposed algorithm outperforms existing sample complexity guarantees for low-rank MDPs while avoiding expensive oracles. Theoretical results extend to approximately low-rank MDPs capturing real-world environments.

Conclusion: By leveraging supervised learning as a computational proxy and focusing on policy evaluation oracles, the paper provides more computationally efficient RL algorithms for low-rank MDPs with improved sample complexity, making RL more practical for real-world applications.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a fundamental framework for sequential decision-making, in which an agent learns an optimal policy through interactions with an unknown environment. In settings with function approximation, many existing RL algorithms achieve favorable sample complexity, but often rely on computationally intractable oracles. In this paper, we use supervised learning as a computational proxy to establish a clear hierarchy of commonly adopted RL oracles under low-rank Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). This hierarchy shows that policy evaluation is the most computationally efficient oracle, provided that supervised learning can be efficiently solved. Motivated by this observation, we propose a novel optimistic actor-critic algorithm that relies solely on the policy evaluation oracle. We prove that our algorithm outperforms the existing sample complexity guarantees for low-rank MDPs while avoiding computationally expensive planning or optimization oracles commonly assumed in prior works. We further extend our theoretical results to approximately low-rank MDPs and demonstrate that this setting captures a broad class of real-world environments. Finally, we validate our theoretical results with experiments on several standard Gym environments.

</details>


### [184] [S^3-R1: Learning to Retrieve and Answer Step-by-Step with Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01248)
*Harsh Goel,Akhil Udathu,Susmija Jabireddy,Pradnesh Kalkar,Atharva Parulekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: S^3-R1 (Synthetic data and stabilized Search R1) is a framework that improves reinforcement learning for tool-use agents by combining synthetic multi-hop question generation with dense reward signals for intermediate search steps, achieving better generalization than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-based models for tool-use (like search agents) struggle with sparse outcome-based rewards and lack training data covering questions of varying difficulty levels, leading to shallow search strategies and poor evidence collection for question-answering.

Method: Developed a synthetic data generation pipeline that creates diverse multi-hop questions from existing documents, with retrieval-based verification to isolate intermediate-difficulty questions. Paired this with a reward structure that evaluates both intermediate search quality and final answer correctness.

Result: S^3-R1 outperforms existing baselines by learning more effective search and synthesis strategies, achieving up to 10% improvement in robust generalization on out-of-domain datasets.

Conclusion: The framework successfully addresses credit assignment problems in sparse-reward RL by combining data-centric synthetic question generation with denser learning signals, enabling agents to perform deeper searches and collect better evidence for question-answering tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training has enabled newer capabilities in models, such as agentic tool-use for search. However, these models struggle primarily due to limitations with sparse outcome-based rewards and a lack of training data that encapsulates questions of differing hardness, which results in models not performing deeper searches with tools to collect evidence for question-answering. To address these limitations, we introduce S^3-R1 (Synthetic data and stabilized Search R1), a framework that couples a data-centric approach with denser learning signals. We first develop a synthetic generation and curation pipeline that programmatically derives diverse, multi-hop questions from existing documents. This pipeline incorporates a retrieval-based verification step to specifically isolate questions of intermediate difficulty. We then pair this expanded training set with a reward structure that evaluates both intermediate search quality and the correctness of the final answer. This setup directly mitigates the credit assignment problems inherent to sparse rewards. Our evaluations show that S^3-R1 outperforms existing baselines by learning more effective search and synthesis strategies, yielding up to a 10% improvement in robust generalization on out-of-domain datasets.

</details>


### [185] [Activation Compression in LLMs: Theoretical Analysis and Efficient Algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01255)
*Wen-Da Wei,Han-Bin Fang,Yang-Di Liu,Jiang-Xin Shi,James Kwok,Yu-Feng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical framework for safe activation compression in LLMs, showing it's safe for linear operators but problematic for nonlinear ones, with proposed activation-gradient co-compression method that achieves competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training large language models is highly memory-intensive due to storing weights, optimizer states, and intermediate activations. While existing methods focus on gradients and optimizer states, activation compression lacks LLM-tailored theory and guarantees, creating a gap in memory-efficient training techniques.

Method: Developed a theoretical framework showing activation compression is safe for linear operators when unbiased, but problematic for nonlinear ones. Derived gradient variance bounds and convergence guarantees. Proposed activation-gradient co-compression method that reuses low-rank activation factors to compress linear-layer gradients without extra computation or additional gradient error.

Result: Extensive experiments on Qwen and LLaMA models using pretraining and multiple fine-tuning benchmarks validate the theory and demonstrate competitive performance of the proposed method in both accuracy and compression efficiency.

Conclusion: Activation compression is theoretically sound for linear operators under standard assumptions and doesn't change convergence rate. The proposed activation-gradient co-compression method effectively reduces memory requirements while maintaining accuracy, addressing a critical bottleneck in LLM training.

Abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) is highly memory-intensive, as training must store not only weights and optimizer states but also intermediate activations for backpropagation. While existing memory-efficient methods largely focus on gradients and optimizer states, activation compression is less well established due to the lack of LLM-tailored theory and guarantees. In this work, we develop a theoretical framework showing that activation compression is safe for linear operators when activation compression is unbiased, but problematic for nonlinear ones. We further derive gradient variance bound and establish convergence guarantees for applying activation compression to all linear operators under the standard $L$-smoothness assumption, showing that it does not change the convergence rate. Guided by the theory, we propose an activation-gradient co-compression method that reuses low-rank activation factors to compress linear-layer gradients without extra computation or additional gradient error. We conduct extensive experiments on Qwen and LLaMA models using a pretraining benchmark and multiple fine-tuning benchmarks to validate our theory and demonstrate competitive performance of our method in both accuracy and compression efficiency. We provide our code in the supplementary material for reproducibility.

</details>


### [186] [Continuous Temporal Representations of Event-Based Signals via Interference-Based Wave Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01270)
*Magnus Bengtsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a continuous temporal modeling framework using interference-based wave representations for event-driven biosignals like sEMG, encoding temporal structure through phase modulation in complex-valued latent fields.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatio-temporal signals from event-driven biological processes (like sEMG) show asynchronous, structured activation patterns that are difficult to model with conventional discrete or purely real-valued representations. Existing approaches struggle to capture both temporal localization and relational dependencies effectively.

Method: Proposes a continuous temporal modeling framework based on interference-based wave representations. Maps event-like input signals into complex-valued latent wave fields where temporal structure is encoded through phase modulation and interactions between latent components. Projects the wave field onto an energy domain to induce structured activation patterns without relying on explicit recurrence or causal state propagation.

Result: The interference-based wave model provides improved representation quality compared to purely real-valued representations while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for practical deployment. The method supports learning from sEMG data for downstream control tasks in biomechanical systems like prosthetic devices and exoskeletons.

Conclusion: Continuous temporal modeling using interference-based wave representations is effective for event-driven biosignals like sEMG, offering better representation quality and computational efficiency for practical biomechanical control applications.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal signals arising from event-driven biological processes, such as surface electromyography (sEMG), exhibit asynchronous and highly structured activation patterns that are challenging to model using conventional discrete or purely real-valued representations.
  In this work, we propose a continuous temporal modeling framework based on interference-based wave representations. The approach maps event-like input signals into a complex-valued latent wave field, where temporal structure is encoded through phase modulation and interactions between latent components.
  By projecting the resulting wave field onto an energy domain, the model induces structured activation patterns that capture both temporal localization and relational dependencies within finite observation windows, without relying on explicit recurrence or causal state propagation.
  The proposed formulation is particularly suited for event-driven biosignals, where continuous representations enable efficient gradient-based optimization and robust feature extraction. In particular, the method is designed to support learning from sEMG data for downstream control tasks in biomechanical systems, such as prosthetic devices and exoskeletons.
  Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed interference-based wave model provides improved representation quality compared to purely real-valued representations, while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for practical deployment.

</details>


### [187] [A Theory of Saddle Escape in Deep Nonlinear Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01288)
*Divit Rawal,Michael R. DeWeese*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep networks with small initialization show plateaus and sharp transitions; authors derive imbalance identity for weight norms, classify activations, reduce dynamics to scalar ODE revealing critical-depth scaling with bottleneck layers rather than total depth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep nonlinear networks with small initialization exhibit complex training dynamics (plateaus and sharp transitions) that are not well understood compared to shallow nonlinear networks and deep linear networks. Theoretical analysis of deep nonlinear networks remains challenging.

Method: Derived exact identity for Frobenius norm imbalance of layer weight matrices valid for any smooth activation and differentiable loss; classified activation functions into four universality classes; reduced dynamics to scalar ODE on permutation-symmetric submanifold using approximate balance law; analyzed critical-depth escape time scaling under He-normal initialization with rescaled bottleneck layers.

Result: Found critical-depth escape time scaling law τ⋆ = Θ(ε^(-(r-2))) governed by number r of layers at bottleneck scale, not total depth L; same r-2 exponent recovered under He-normal initialization; theory shows close agreement with numerical simulations.

Conclusion: Deep network training dynamics with small initialization are governed by bottleneck layers rather than total depth, with scaling laws determined by activation function universality classes; the derived identity and reduction to scalar ODE provide theoretical framework for understanding plateaus and transitions in deep nonlinear networks.

Abstract: In deep networks with small initialization, training exhibits long plateaus separated by sharp feature-acquisition transitions. Whereas shallow nonlinear networks and deep linear networks are well studied, extending these analyses to deep nonlinear networks remains challenging. We derive an exact identity for the imbalance of Frobenius norms of layer weight matrices that holds for any smooth activation and any differentiable loss and use this to classify activation functions into four universality classes. On the permutation-symmetric submanifold, the identity combines with an approximate balance law to reduce the full matrix flow to a scalar ODE, giving a critical-depth escape time law $τ_\star = Θ(\varepsilon^{-(r-2)})$ governed by the number $r$ of layers at the bottleneck scale rather than the total depth $L$. We find that this same $r-2$ exponent is recovered under He-normal initialization with $r$ bottleneck layers rescaled by $\varepsilon$, where the symmetry manifold is preserved by the flow but not attracting. We find close agreement between our theory and numerical simulations.

</details>


### [188] [Congestion-Aware Dynamic Axonal Delay for Spiking Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01291)
*Dewei Bai,Hongxiang Peng,Yunyun Zeng,Ziyu Zhang,Hong Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A congestion-aware dynamic axonal delay mechanism for SNNs that combines static channel-wise delays with activity-conditioned shifts to improve temporal processing while reducing parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing delay learning approaches in SNNs use static delays for individual synapses, resulting in many parameters and limited adaptability to input-dependent activity dynamics. There's a need for more efficient and adaptive delay mechanisms.

Method: Proposes a Congestion-Aware Dynamic Axonal Delay mechanism that decomposes delay into: (1) channel-wise static base delay for temporal structuring, and (2) global activity-conditioned shift that dynamically regulates state update rate based on spike intensity. Delay parameters are learned using differentiable linear interpolation and discretized at inference time.

Result: Achieves 93.75% accuracy on SHD, 80.49% on SSC, and 95.53% on GSC-35 speech benchmarks while reducing parameter count by ~50% compared to state-of-the-art delay-based methods with the same architecture.

Conclusion: The congestion-aware dynamic delay mechanism effectively improves SNN accuracy on temporal tasks while significantly reducing parameters, offering an efficient approach for event-driven temporal processing.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are widely regarded as an energy-efficient paradigm for modeling and processing temporal and event-driven information. Incorporating delays in SNNs has been proven to be an effective mechanism for improving spike alignment in event-driven tasks. However, existing delay learning approaches predominantly assign static delays to individual synapses, resulting in a large number of delay parameters and limited adaptability to input-dependent activity dynamics. To this end, we propose a Congestion-Aware Dynamic Axonal Delay mechanism, decomposing the delay into a channel-wise static base delay for temporal structuring and a global, activity-conditioned shift that dynamically regulates the state update rate under varying spike intensities. The delay parameters are learned using differentiable linear interpolation and discretized at inference time, preserving the benefits of our dynamic delay while incurring only minimal additional cost. Experiments on speech benchmarks, including the Spiking Heidelberg Dataset, Spiking Speech Commands, and Google Speech Commands, demonstrate that introducing congestion-aware delays into synaptic signal transmission effectively improves accuracy on temporal tasks, notably achieving 93.75\% accuracy on SHD, 80.49\% accuracy on SSC, and 95.53\% on GSC-35, while reducing the parameter count by approximately 50\% compared to state-of-the-art delay-based methods with the same architecture.

</details>


### [189] [Autonomous Drift Learning in Data Streams: A Unified Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01295)
*Xiaoyu Yang,En Yu,Jie Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a comprehensive three-dimensional taxonomy for autonomous learning systems that extends beyond traditional concept drift approaches to address broader non-stationarity challenges through time stream, data stream, and model stream drift analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is that traditional concept drift approaches focusing only on temporal shifts are insufficient for increasingly autonomous and complex learning systems. The foundational assumption of stationarity is fundamentally untenable in real-world autonomous systems, requiring a more comprehensive framework to address broader non-stationarity challenges.

Method: The paper proposes a novel three-dimensional taxonomy that systematizes the field based on operational state: 1) Time stream drift (stochastic vs structural rhythmic dynamics), 2) Data stream drift (representation drift vs semantic drift), and 3) Model stream drift (sequential plasticity, decentralized heterogeneity, policy instability). The authors then conduct a systematic review of 193 representative studies based on this framework.

Result: The paper develops a comprehensive taxonomy that bridges fragmented paradigms of drift adaptation, continual learning, and temporal generalization. Through systematic review of 193 studies, key open challenges are identified, providing a structured way to understand and address non-stationarity in autonomous learning systems.

Conclusion: This survey outlines a roadmap for building self-evolving intelligent systems capable of learning autonomously through continuous change. By providing a unified framework that goes beyond traditional concept drift approaches, it enables more comprehensive understanding and development of autonomous learning systems that can handle complex, multi-dimensional non-stationarity.

Abstract: In the pursuit of autonomous learning systems, the foundational assumption of stationarity, the premise that data distributions and model behaviors remain constant, is fundamentally untenable. Historically, the research community has addressed non-stationary environments almost exclusively under the scope of concept drift, focusing primarily on temporal shifts in streams. However, as learning systems become increasingly autonomous and complex, merely adapting to temporal non-stationarity is no longer sufficient. Evolving beyond this traditional perspective, we propose a novel, three-dimensional taxonomy that systematizes the field based on the operational state of the system. First, time stream drift distinguishes between stochastic arbitrary patterns and structural rhythmic dynamics. Second, data stream drift disentangles shifts in feature representations, identified as representation drift, from changes in underlying semantics, recognized as semantic drift. Third, model stream drift characterizes the internal endogenous divergence of learning systems through the lenses of sequential plasticity, decentralized heterogeneity, and policy instability. Based on this framework, we systematically review 193 representative studies and identify key open challenges. By bridging the fragmented paradigms of drift adaptation, continual learning, and temporal generalization, this survey outlines a roadmap for building self-evolving intelligent systems capable of learning autonomously through continuous change.

</details>


### [190] [GA-VisAgent: A Multi-Agent application for code generation and visualization in interactive learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01299)
*Wang Jian,Zhou Jianbo,Xiong Yuhao,Liu Zhenxia,Luo Wen,Yuan LinWang,Yu ZhaoYuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GA-VisAgent: A multi-agent system using LLM-based reasoning to generate Geometric Algebra code and visualizations from natural language, achieving 90% success rate on conformal GA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Geometric Algebra is difficult to learn due to its abstract mathematical structure and complex operations. Existing software packages have high learning curves, while LLM-based methods often produce logical errors with low accuracy in generating GA scripts like GAALOPScript.

Method: GA-VisAgent is a multi-agent interactive learning application built on a Geometric Algebra LLM (GAGPT). It integrates task planning mechanisms with ReAct reasoning strategies to decompose complex GA operations into five standardized subtasks (geometric products, rotations, reflections, etc.). The system accepts natural language and mathematical formulas as input to generate executable code with interactive visualizations.

Result: Experimental results show GA-VisAgent achieved a 90% code generation success rate across 40 typical Conformal GA tasks, representing a 70% improvement over GPT-4o. The system provides an online service at http://gagis.cn/gacrac.

Conclusion: GA-VisAgent introduces an extensible new paradigm for teaching Geometric Algebra and developing visualization tools for related mathematical concepts, effectively bridging the gap between abstract mathematical operations and practical implementation through AI-powered code generation and visualization.

Abstract: Geometric Algebra (GA) presents challenges to learners due to its highly abstract mathematical structure and complex operational rules, as translating algebraic manipulations into concrete geometric interpretations is a non-intuitive process when developing related code. Currently, some existing GA software packages rely on manually written scripts for code generation and visualization, but their high learning curve hinders widespread adoption. Meanwhile, methods based on Large Language Models (LLMs) often produce logical errors when generating specific GA scripts, such as GAALOPScript, resulting in generally low accuracy. To address these issues, this study proposes GA-VisAgent -- a multi-agent interactive learning application for GA code generation and visualization -- building upon a Geometric algebra large language model (GAGPT). Integrating task planning mechanisms with ReAct reasoning strategies, GA-VisAgent can decompose complex operations into five standardized subtasks, including core operations like geometric products, rotations, and reflections. It supports natural language and mathematical formulas as input to automatically generate executable code, accompanied by interactive visualizations to aid user comprehension. Experimental results show that GA-VisAgent achieved a 90% code generation success rate across 40 typical Conformal GA tasks, representing a 70% improvement over GPT-4o. This application introduces an extensible new paradigm for teaching GA and developing visualization tools for related mathematical concepts. The online service for this project will be available at http://gagis.cn/gacrac.

</details>


### [191] [GraphSculptor: Sculpting Pre-training Coreset for Graph Self-supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01310)
*Chuang Liu,Zelin Yao,Xueqi Ma,Luzhi Wang,Mukun Chen,Pinghua Xu,Wenbin Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphSculptor is a novel method for constructing efficient coresets for graph self-supervised learning that reduces computational costs by selecting diverse graphs based on structural and semantic properties, achieving near-full performance with only 10% of data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph self-supervised learning typically requires large-scale unlabeled datasets which incur substantial computational costs. However, these datasets contain significant redundancy—empirical analysis shows that uniformly subsampling 50% of graphs retains over 96% of downstream performance. There is a need for more efficient pre-training approaches that exploit this redundancy without sacrificing performance.

Method: GraphSculptor constructs coresets through two complementary perspectives: intrinsic structure and contextual semantics. Structural diversity is quantified using intrinsic graph statistics, creating structural feature vectors. Semantic diversity is captured using a pre-trained language model to encode descriptions generated via graph-to-text. These signals are integrated into a unified metric space, and cluster-aware selection is performed to preserve joint structural-semantic diversity. The method also provides theoretical bounds on the loss gap between coreset and full-data pre-training.

Result: GraphSculptor effectively reduces dataset size while maintaining performance: a 10% coreset achieves 99.6% of full-data performance while reducing pre-training time by nearly 90%. The method outperforms uniform subsampling and other baselines, demonstrating that diverse graph selection based on structural and semantic properties significantly improves data efficiency.

Conclusion: GraphSculptor provides a scalable, data-efficient solution for graph pre-training that addresses the computational burden of large-scale self-supervised learning. By exploiting dataset redundancy through principled selection of structurally and semantically diverse graphs, it enables significant computational savings with minimal performance loss, offering practical benefits for resource-constrained graph learning applications.

Abstract: Graph self-supervised learning typically relies on large-scale unlabeled datasets, heavily inflating computational costs. However, empirical evidence suggests that these datasets contain substantial redundancy-our analysis reveals that uniformly subsampling 50% of graphs retains over 96% of downstream performance. To exploit this redundancy, we introduce GraphSculptor for pre-training coreset construction. Unlike methods dependent on additional training-time signals or limited solely to topological statistics, GraphSculptor provides a label-free solution that constructs coresets via two complementary perspectives: intrinsic structure and contextual semantics. Concretely, structural diversity is quantified using intrinsic graph statistics, yielding a structural feature vector for each graph, while semantic diversity is captured by utilizing a pre-trained language model to encode descriptions generated via graph-to-text. GraphSculptor integrates these signals into a unified metric space and performs cluster-aware selection to preserve joint structural-semantic diversity. We further derive a theoretical bound on the loss gap between coreset and full-data pre-training, offering theoretical motivation for our selection formulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GraphSculptor effectively sculpts the dataset: a 10% coreset achieves 99.6% of full-data performance while reducing pre-training time by nearly 90%, offering a scalable solution for data-efficient graph pre-training.

</details>


### [192] [The Partial Testimony of Logs: Evaluation of Language Model Generation under Confounded Model Choice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01311)
*Jikai Jin,Vasilis Syrgkanis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline language model evaluation faces bias when model choice is confounded with user factors; the paper proposes combining observational logs, small experiments, and simulators to enable causal inference while reducing estimation error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline evaluation of language models from usage logs suffers from confounding bias where model choice is correlated with user factors that also influence output judgment. While randomized experiments can break this bias, they are scarce and costly in practice.

Method: Proposes a three-source design combining: 1) large confounded observational logs (OBS) for scale, 2) small randomized experiments (EXP) for unconfounded scoring, and 3) offline simulators (SIM) that replay candidate models on cached contexts. Develops identification theorems and six estimator families.

Result: Shows that randomized experiments and simulators together are sufficient to recover causal model values, with observational logs only reducing estimation error. Evaluates six estimator families in semi-synthetic validation and two real-task benchmarks (summarization and coding), finding no family dominates all regimes.

Conclusion: The relative performance of different estimation methods depends on the amount of unbiased experiment supervision available and how closely the target reward aligns with observational log-derived structure, highlighting the practical trade-offs in causal inference for language model evaluation.

Abstract: Offline evaluation of language models from usage logs is biased when model choice is confounded: the same user-side factors that influence which model is used can also influence how its output is judged, so raw comparisons of logged scores mix self-selected populations rather than estimating a common quantity of interest. A small randomized experiment can break this bias by overriding model choice, but in practice such experiments are scarce and costly. We study a three-source design that combines a large confounded observational log (OBS) for scale, a small randomized experiment (EXP) for unconfounded scoring, and an offline simulator (SIM) that replays candidate models on cached contexts. Our main result is an identification theorem showing that the randomized experiment and the simulator are together enough to recover causal model values; the observational log enters only afterward, to reduce estimation error rather than to make the causal comparison valid. Six estimator families are evaluated in a controlled semi-synthetic validation and in two real-task cached benchmarks for summarization and coding. No family dominates every regime; relative performance depends on the amount of unbiased EXP supervision and on how closely the target reward aligns with OBS-derived structure.

</details>


### [193] [Robust Parameter Learning for Uncertain MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01339)
*Yannik Schnitzer,Alessandro Abate,David Parker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learning parametric MDPs with shared parameter uncertainty yields tighter PAC bounds than independent interval-based uncertain MDPs by respecting transition dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current uncertain MDP learning approaches quantify transition uncertainty independently using confidence intervals, ignoring dependencies between transitions due to shared latent quantities, leading to overly conservative uncertainty estimates.

Method: Propose learning parametric MDPs (pMDPs) where transitions are expressions over parameters, project statistical uncertainty from empirical frequencies onto parameter space to get PAC uncertainty models respecting algebraic dependencies, and develop a hierarchy of sound polytopic outer approximations for the confidence sets.

Result: The approach demonstrates substantially tighter uncertainty estimates than classical interval-based uncertain MDP learning techniques through implementation and evaluation.

Conclusion: Parametric MDP learning with proper treatment of transition dependencies through parameter-space uncertainty projection provides more accurate and less conservative uncertainty models for robust policy synthesis in MDP verification.

Abstract: Learning-based approaches to verifying unknown Markov decision processes (MDPs) often employ uncertain MDPs. These models use, for example, confidence intervals to capture transition uncertainty and allow synthesis of policies that are robust to this uncertainty. However, this approach typically quantifies uncertainty independently for individual transition probabilities, ignoring dependencies due to shared latent quantities. We propose to learn such models using parametric MDPs (pMDPs), where transition probabilities are expressions over a set of parameters. We project statistical uncertainty from empirical transition frequencies onto the pMDP's parameter space, yielding a probably approximately correct (PAC) uncertainty model for the underlying MDP that respects the algebraic dependencies between transitions. The resulting models are algorithmically challenging to solve, so we propose a hierarchy of sound polytopic outer approximations of the induced confidence set. We implement and evaluate our approach, demonstrating substantially tighter uncertainty estimates than classical interval-based uncertain MDP learning techniques.

</details>


### [194] [Model-Based Proactive Cost Generation for Learning Safe Policies Offline with Limited Violation Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01356)
*Ruiqi Xue,Lei Yuan,Kainuo Cheng,Jing-Wen Yang,Yang Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PROCO is a model-based offline safe RL framework that uses LLMs to incorporate natural language knowledge about unsafe states when training data lacks violations, enabling safer policies through synthetic counterfactual samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In safety-critical domains, offline RL from data without risky online interaction is essential. Existing methods fail when datasets contain few or no unsafe samples, as they can't distinguish safe-but-infeasible states from truly safe ones, leading to deployment failures.

Method: PROCO learns a dynamics model from offline data, then uses LLMs to ground natural-language knowledge about unsafe states into a conservative cost function. It performs model-based rollouts to synthesize counterfactual unsafe samples, enabling reliable feasibility identification and feasibility-guided policy learning.

Result: PROCO integrates with various offline safe RL algorithms and consistently reduces constraint violations while improving safety performance across Safety-Gymnasium tasks, even with exclusively safe or minimally risky training data, outperforming original methods and behavior cloning baselines.

Conclusion: PROCO successfully addresses the challenge of learning safe policies from violation-scarce datasets by combining model-based RL with LLM knowledge, enabling safer decision-making in high-stakes scenarios without risky trial-and-error.

Abstract: Learning constraint-satisfying policies from offline data without risky online interaction is crucial for safety-critical decision making. Conventional methods typically learn cost value functions from abundant unsafe samples to define safety boundaries and penalize violations. However, in high-stakes scenarios, risky trial-and-error is infeasible, yielding datasets with few or no unsafe samples. Under this limitation, existing approaches often treat all data as uniformly safe, overlooking safe-but-infeasible states - states that currently satisfy constraints but inevitably violate them within a few steps - leading to deployment failures. Drawing inspiration from the concept of knowledge-data integration, we leverage large language models (LLMs) to incorporate natural language knowledge into the policy to address this challenge. Specifically, we propose PROCO, a model-based offline safe reinforcement learning (RL) framework tailored to datasets largely free of violations. PROCO first learns a dynamics model from offline data and constructs a conservative cost function by grounding natural-language knowledge of unsafe states in LLMs, enabling risk estimation even without observed violations. Using the cost function and learned model, PROCO performs model-based rollouts to synthesize diverse counterfactual unsafe samples, supporting reliable feasibility identification and feasibility-guided policy learning. Across a range of Safety-Gymnasium tasks with exclusively safe or minimally risky training data, PROCO integrates seamlessly with a variety of offline safe RL algorithms and consistently demonstrates reduced constraint violations and improved safety performance compared to both the original methods and other behavior cloning baselines.

</details>


### [195] [PACE: Parameter Change for Unsupervised Environment Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01358)
*Fang Yuan,Quanjun Yin,Siqi Shen,Yuxiang Xie,Junqiang Yang,Long Qin,Junjie Zeng,Qinglun Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACE proposes using policy parameter change as a direct measure of learning progress for environment selection in unsupervised environment design, outperforming existing methods that rely on indirect proxy signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Unsupervised Environment Design (UED) methods evaluate environments using indirect proxy signals like regret, value-based errors, or Monte Carlo methods, which suffer from issues including bias, high variance, computational overhead, and failure to reflect actual learning progress.

Method: PACE (Parameter Change Environment Design) evaluates environments through the policy parameter change induced by training on each environment. It uses a first-order approximation where environment value is proportional to the squared L2 norm of parameter updates, enabling low-variance and computationally efficient evaluation without additional rollouts.

Result: Experiments on MiniGrid and Craftax show PACE consistently outperforms established UED baselines, achieving higher IQM (96.4% on MiniGrid) and smaller Optimality Gap (17.2% on MiniGrid) on out-of-distribution evaluations.

Conclusion: Directly grounding environment selection in realized learning progress through policy parameter change provides a more effective approach to Unsupervised Environment Design, addressing limitations of existing proxy-based evaluation methods.

Abstract: Unsupervised Environment Design (UED) offers a promising paradigm for improving reinforcement learning generalization by adaptively shaping training environments, but it requires reliable environment evaluation to remain effective. However, existing UED methods evaluate environments using indirect proxy signals such as regret, value-based errors, or Monte Carlo, which suffer from bias, high variance, or substantial computational overhead and fail to reflect agent realized learning progress. To address these limitations, we propose Parameter Change Environment Design (PACE), which evaluates an environment through the policy parameter change induced by training on that environment, directly grounding environment selection in realized learning progress. Specifically, PACE assigns environment value using a first-order approximation of the policy optimization objective, where the improvement induced by an environment is proportional to the squared L2 norm of the corresponding parameter update, enabling low-variance and computation-efficient evaluation without additional rollouts. Experiments on MiniGrid and Craftax show that PACE consistently outperforms established UED baselines, achieving higher IQM and smaller Optimality Gap on OOD evaluations, including an IQM of 96.4% and an Optimality Gap of 17.2% on MiniGrid.

</details>


### [196] [Decision-Focused Learning via Tangent-Space Projection of Prediction Error](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01361)
*Junhyeong Lee,Sangjin Jin,Yongjae Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PEAR (Projected Error As Regret-gradient) is a new Decision-Focused Learning method that computes regret gradients via geometric projection onto active constraint tangent spaces, avoiding expensive solver differentiation while improving both computational efficiency and decision quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Decision-Focused Learning methods for improving downstream decision quality face challenges: they either require differentiating through optimization solvers (computationally expensive) or rely on surrogate losses that deviate from the true objective, leading to suboptimal performance.

Method: PEAR computes regret gradients using a closed-form geometric characterization: the regret gradient equals prediction error projected onto the tangent space of active constraints, scaled by local curvature. This allows filtering decision-irrelevant components from MSE gradients via solving a reduced linear system over active constraints, avoiding solver differentiation or additional optimization solves.

Result: Experiments on LP benchmarks and a real-world QP task show that PEAR achieves the best decision quality among all baselines while being the most computationally efficient. The gains persist even under constraint shifts, demonstrating robustness.

Conclusion: PEAR provides a simpler, more direct alternative to existing Decision-Focused Learning approaches by revealing that regret gradients can be obtained through geometric projection of prediction error onto relevant constraint spaces, enabling both improved performance and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Decision-Focused Learning (DFL) trains predictors to improve downstream decision quality, but computing regret gradients typically requires differentiating through solvers or relying on surrogate losses, which can be computationally expensive or deviate from the true objective. We show that, under standard regularity with locally stable active constraints, the regret gradient admits a closed-form geometric characterization, equivalent to the prediction error projected onto the tangent space of active constraints, scaled by local curvature. This reveals that regret gradients can be obtained by filtering decision-irrelevant components from the MSE gradient, providing a simpler and more direct alternative to existing approaches. Based on this, we propose PEAR (Projected Error As Regret-gradient), which computes regret gradients via a reduced linear system over active constraints, avoiding differentiation through solver iterations or additional optimization solves. Experiments on LP benchmarks and a real-world QP task show that PEAR achieves the best decision quality among all baselines while being the most computationally efficient, with gains that persist under constraint shifts.

</details>


### [197] [Toward a foundational thermal model for residential buildings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01364)
*Ting-Yu Dai,Kingsley Nweye,Dev Niyogi,Zoltan Nagy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed transformer architecture for universal building thermal modeling that achieves accurate temperature predictions across diverse buildings and climates without building-specific calibration, demonstrating strong zero-shot transferability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The building energy community lacks a foundational thermal model that can generalize across diverse buildings, climates, and control strategies without requiring building-specific calibration. Current approaches tend to memorize building-specific patterns rather than capturing universal thermal dynamics.

Method: Developed a physics-informed transformer architecture that embeds domain knowledge through derivative enrichment and Euler-based numerical integration in a decoder-only framework. Incorporates static building features from simulation models and uses Rotary Position Embedding attention to capture temporal dependencies.

Result: Achieved one-step prediction accuracy with RMSE of 0.30°C in Texas and 0.29°C in Vermont on the CityLearn dataset (247 residential buildings across three climate zones). Outperformed both traditional baselines and fine-tuned Time-Series Foundation Models. Demonstrated zero-shot transferability: models trained on as few as two buildings generalized to unseen buildings and climate zones without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Physics-informed architectural principles provide a promising foundation for developing universal building thermal models, despite limitations of using simulated residential buildings. The approach represents an important step toward a foundational thermal model for the building energy community.

Abstract: The building energy community lacks a foundational thermal model, i.e., a single pretrained model capable of generalizing across diverse buildings, climates, and control strategies without building-specific calibration. Achieving this vision requires architectural principles that capture universal thermal dynamics rather than memorizing building-specific patterns. We take a step toward this goal by presenting a physics-informed transformer architecture that embeds domain knowledge, e.g., derivative enrichment and Euler-based numerical integration, into a decoder-only framework. We incorporate static building features extracted from simulation models and employ Rotary Position Embedding attention to capture temporal dependencies. Evaluated on the CityLearn dataset spanning 247 residential buildings across three climate zones, our model achieves one-step prediction accuracy (RMSE of 0.30°C in Texas, 0.29°C in Vermont) while outperforming both traditional baselines and fine-tuned Time-Series Foundation Models. We also demonstrate zero-shot transferability: models trained on as few as two buildings generalize to unseen buildings and climate zones without fine-tuning. Despite the limitation of simulated residential buildings, our results establish physics-informed architectural principles as a promising foundation for universal building thermal models.

</details>


### [198] [Sequential Learning and Catastrophic Forgetting in Differentiable Resistor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01383)
*Maniru Ibrahim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Study of catastrophic forgetting in differentiable resistor networks shows forgetting is controlled by task conflict, adaptation degree, and network topology, revealing trade-offs between forgetting and adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate sequential learning in differentiable physical networks (resistor networks) as a compact, physically interpretable testbed for studying continual learning in tunable matter, specifically examining catastrophic forgetting during conflicting tasks.

Method: Use differentiable resistor networks governed by Kirchhoff's laws with gradient-based adjustment of edge conductances for sequential training on conflicting tasks, analyze forgetting patterns using uniform anchoring, normalized gradient-weighted anchoring, and study effects across different network topologies (Erdős-Rényi, small-world, scale-free, random-geometric graphs).

Result: Catastrophic forgetting occurs with conflicting tasks, controlled by task conflict and adaptation degree; forgetting-adaptation trade-off exists; forgetting is associated with localized conductance changes on high-current edges; strongest forgetting happens when second task reverses output ordering of first task; topology changes the forgetting-adaptation balance across different graph ensembles.

Conclusion: Differentiable resistor networks serve as valuable, compact, physically interpretable testbeds for studying continual learning, revealing fundamental trade-offs between forgetting and adaptation that depend on task conflict, adaptation strategies, and network topology.

Abstract: Differentiable physical networks provide a simple setting in which learning can be studied through the interaction between trainable parameters and physical equilibrium constraints. We investigate sequential learning in differentiable resistor networks governed by Kirchhoff's laws. Although individual input--output mappings can be learned by gradient-based adjustment of edge conductances, sequential training on conflicting tasks produces catastrophic forgetting. We show that forgetting is controlled by task conflict and by the degree of adaptation to the new task. Uniform anchoring and normalised gradient-weighted anchoring reduce forgetting only by increasing the final loss on the new task, giving a clear forgetting--adaptation trade-off. We also show that forgetting is associated with localised conductance changes on high-current edges, giving a physical interpretation as reconfiguration of dominant transport pathways. Broader random-task ensembles show that the strongest forgetting occurs when the second task reverses the output ordering imposed by the first task. Finally, comparisons across Erdős--Rényi, small-world, scale-free, and random-geometric graph ensembles show that topology changes the forgetting--adaptation balance. These results position differentiable resistor networks as compact, physically interpretable testbeds for studying continual learning in tunable matter.

</details>


### [199] [Rethinking Multi-Label Node Classification: Do Tuned Classic GNNs Suffice?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01403)
*Yuxuan Xiao,Shengzhong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Carefully tuned classic GNN backbones outperform specialized multi-label node classification methods, questioning whether the advantages of specialized designs are real or just due to insufficient baseline optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper challenges whether the advantages of specialized multi-label node classification (MLNC) methods truly come from their designs or simply from insufficiently optimized baselines. It questions if carefully tuned classic GNNs can already serve as strong solutions to MLNC.

Method: Revisits MLNC from a strong-baseline perspective by systematically studying representative GNN backbones (GCN, SSGConv, GCNII) and optimizing them with standard techniques like normalization, dropout, and residual connections.

Result: Tuned baselines outperform representative specialized methods on 4 out of 5 benchmark datasets and achieve state-of-the-art performance in multiple settings.

Conclusion: Careful tuning of classic backbones is a highly influential but overlooked factor in MLNC, highlighting the need for more rigorous strong-baseline evaluation in multi-label graph learning research.

Abstract: Multi-label node classification (MLNC) has recently been addressed by increasingly complex label-aware designs that explicitly model node-label interactions and inter-label dependencies.However, it remains unclear whether the advantages of these methods truly stem from their specialized designs, or simply from insufficiently optimized baselines. In this paper, we revisit MLNC from a strong-baseline perspective and investigate whether carefully tuned classic full-graph GNNs can already serve as strong solutions to this task. We systematically study several representative backbones, including GCN, SSGConv, and GCNII, and optimize them using standard yet effective techniques such as normalization, dropout, and residual connections. Experiments on five representative benchmark datasets show that our tuned baselines outperform representative specialized methods on four datasets and achieve state-of-the-art performance in multiple settings. These results indicate that careful tuning of classic backbones is a highly influential but often overlooked factor in MLNC, and highlight the need for more rigorous strong-baseline evaluation in future research on multi-label graph learning.

</details>


### [200] [Quantifying Multimodal Capabilities: Formal Generalization Guarantees in Pairwise Metric Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01424)
*Richeng Zhou,Xuelin Zhang,Liyuan Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical analysis showing how fine-grained modality features in multimodal metric learning reduce hypothesis complexity and improve generalization through enhanced modality complementarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world multimodal learning often faces incomplete or redundant modality data, creating a need to understand how modality selection affects algorithmic performance and generalization.

Method: Fine-grained theoretical analysis establishing hierarchical relationships between function classes for different modality subsets, quantifying discrepancy between learned mappings and ground truth, and rigorous analysis of pairwise complexity within multimodal learning framework.

Result: Derived novel generalization error bounds revealing joint impact of modality quantity and granularity on performance, demonstrating that fine-grained modality features reduce hypothesis space complexity by enhancing modality complementarity.

Conclusion: Provides theoretical foundations and practical implications for improving convergence rates and accuracy in multimodal learning systems by leveraging modality complementarity through fine-grained features.

Abstract: Multimodal learning leverages the integration of diverse data modalities to enhance performance in complex tasks. Yet, it frequently encounters incomplete or redundant modality data in real-world scenarios. This paper presents a fine-grained theoretical analysis of the generalization properties of multimodal metric learning models, addressing critical gaps in understanding the relationship between modality selection and algorithmic performance. We establish hierarchical relationships between function classes corresponding to different modality subsets and quantify the discrepancy between learned mappings and ground truth. Through rigorous analysis of pairwise complexity within the multimodal learning framework, we derive novel generalization error bounds that reveal the joint impact of modality quantity and granularity on model performance. Our theoretical findings on both upper and lower bounds demonstrate that incorporating fine-grained modality features reduces the complexity of the hypothesis space by enhancing modality complementarity. This work offers both theoretical foundations and practical implications for improving convergence rates and accuracy in multimodal learning systems.

</details>


### [201] [Barriers to Counterfactual Credit Attribution for Autoregressive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01425)
*Aloni Cohen,Chenhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies counterfactual credit attribution (CCA) for generative AI models, showing barriers to natural approaches: CCA doesn't compose autoregressively like differential privacy, and retrofitting CCA onto existing models requires exponential query complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI disrupts traditional credit attribution practices. There's a need for models to properly credit prior work that significantly influences their outputs. Counterfactual credit attribution (CCA) was recently introduced as a technical framework for this purpose, and this paper aims to study CCA specifically for generative models.

Method: The authors consider autoregressive models giving credit to deployment-time datasets (like RAG databases). They examine two approaches: 1) Testing whether imposing CCA on the underlying next-token predictor ensures CCA for the full model, and 2) Exploring "retrofitting" - adding credit attribution to existing non-CCA models. They provide theoretical proofs about limitations of both approaches.

Result: Two key negative results: 1) CCA does not compose autoregressively (unlike differential privacy) - imposing CCA on next-token predictors doesn't guarantee CCA for the full autoregressive model. 2) Retrofitting CCA onto existing models under black-box access requires query complexity exponential in output length, establishing a strong lower bound.

Conclusion: Natural approaches to implementing CCA in autoregressive generative models face fundamental barriers. The work provides negative results that highlight the difficulty of achieving proper credit attribution in generative AI systems, suggesting that new techniques beyond simple composition or retrofitting may be needed.

Abstract: Generative AI disrupts the practice of giving credit to work that came before. Ideally, a generative model would give credit to any work on which its output depends in a significant way. \emph{Counterfactual credit attribution} (CCA) is a technical condition formalizing this goal--a relaxation of differential privacy--recently introduced by Livni, Moran, Nissim, and Pabbaraju [2024] who studied it in the PAC learning setting.
  We initiate the study of CCA generative models. Specifically, we consider autoregressive models giving credit to a deployment-time dataset (e.g., a RAG database). We uncover barriers to two natural approaches to CCA autoregressive models. First, we show that imposing CCA on the underlying next-token predictor does not guarantee that the model is CCA: CCA does not compose autoregressively (unlike DP). Second, we consider a different approach to building CCA models which we call \emph{retrofitting}. Retrofitting takes a model that does not attribute credit, and adds credit onto it. We prove a lower bound for CCA retrofitting under a weak optimality requirement. Given black-box access to the starting model, retrofitting requires query complexity exponential in the length of the model's outputs.

</details>


### [202] [Evaluating LLMs on Large-Scale Graph Property Estimation via Random Walks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01484)
*Sunil Kumar Maurya,Xin Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EstGraph: A benchmark for testing LLMs' ability to estimate properties of large graphs through random walk sampling techniques that overcome context length limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph reasoning benchmarks for LLMs use small graphs due to context length limitations, but real-world graphs are much larger and often not fully accessible. There's a need to evaluate LLMs' capabilities on large graph problems with limited accessibility.

Method: Proposed EstGraph benchmark with four distinct tasks for estimating large graph properties. Used random walk sampling of large graphs (up to millions of nodes) to create task-specific prompt constructions that convey sufficient information within LLM context length limits.

Result: Evaluated LLMs' reasoning abilities on EstGraph tasks using various graph datasets, demonstrating that random walk sampling techniques can effectively represent large graphs within context constraints.

Conclusion: The EstGraph benchmark addresses the gap in evaluating LLMs on large graph problems with limited accessibility, providing a framework for testing graph reasoning capabilities on realistically-sized graphs through sampling methods.

Abstract: With the rapidly improving reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), there is also a rising demand to use them in a wide variety of domains. This brings about the need to carefully evaluate the limits of the capabilities of these models with various tests and benchmarks. Graph structures are ubiquitous in real-world data, and are often used to represent and analyze relationship patterns within data. Many benchmarks have already been proposed in the graph literature to test the reasoning ability of LLMs to follow and execute graph algorithms. However, due to the limited context length of LLMs, these benchmarks consist of very small graphs. In real-world data, the size of graphs can be significantly larger, and in many cases, not fully accessible. In this paper, we examine a class of problems that arises with very large graphs having limited accessibility. We propose a large graph benchmark dataset, EstGraph, and introduce four distinct tasks designed to estimate large graph properties. We evaluate the reasoning abilities of LLMs on these tasks using a wide variety of graph datasets. In addition, we provide task-specific prompt constructions based on random walk sampling of large graphs (up to millions of nodes) that effectively convey sufficient information to LLMs within the limits of context length.

</details>


### [203] [Protein-Conditioned Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning for Full-Length mRNA Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01513)
*Zixi Shao,Tao Wang,Yibei Xiao,Tianyi Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProMORNA is a multi-objective framework for designing full-length therapeutic mRNA sequences directly from protein targets, using a BART-style encoder-decoder trained on protein-mRNA pairs and multi-objective reinforcement learning to optimize stability, translation efficiency, and immune safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing therapeutic mRNA requires careful balancing of multiple competing objectives: stability, translation efficiency, and immune safety. Traditional approaches struggle to optimize these trade-offs simultaneously, especially for creating full-length transcripts directly from protein sequences.

Method: The approach has two main components: 1) Training a BART-style encoder-decoder model on 6+ million natural protein-mRNA pairs, 2) Introducing Multi-Objective Group Relative Policy Optimization (MO-GRPO) to simultaneously optimize multiple biological objectives. The framework generates complete mRNA transcripts de novo from target protein sequences.

Result: When evaluated on firefly luciferase (excluded from training), ProMORNA improves the in silico Pareto frontier for predicted half-life and translation efficiency compared to supervised baselines. It achieves higher predicted functional scores than state-of-the-art baselines under the same evaluation pipeline.

Conclusion: The computational results demonstrate the feasibility of using multi-objective reinforcement learning for full-length mRNA design on unseen targets, showing potential for therapeutic mRNA optimization that balances competing biological requirements.

Abstract: Designing therapeutic messenger RNA (mRNA) requires creating full-length transcripts that carefully balance stability, translation efficiency, and immune safety. To address this challenge, we propose ProMORNA, a multi-objective generation framework that produces complete mRNA transcripts \textit{de novo} directly from a target protein sequence. Our approach begins by training a BART-style encoder-decoder model on over 6 million natural protein-mRNA pairs. We then introduce Multi-Objective Group Relative Policy Optimization (MO-GRPO) to simultaneously optimize for various biological objectives in a unified way. As a case study, we evaluated ProMORNA on the widely used firefly luciferase target, excluding it from both our supervised training data and the prompt pool. The results indicate that ProMORNA improves the \textit{in silico} Pareto frontier for predicted half-life and translation efficiency relative to standard supervised baselines. Additionally, it achieves higher predicted functional scores than a state-of-the-art baseline under the same evaluation pipeline. These computational findings demonstrate the feasibility of using multi-objective reinforcement learning for full-length mRNA design on unseen targets.

</details>


### [204] [Mesh Based Simulations with Spatial and Temporal awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01542)
*Paul Garnier,Vincent Lannelongue,Elie Hachem*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework that bridges geometric deep learning with numerical analysis for CFD simulations, introducing multi-node prediction, temporal correction via cross-attention, and geometric inductive biases to improve accuracy and stability in physics simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ML surrogates for CFD (GNNs and Transformers) have architectural advances but suffer from legacy training paradigms with naive assumptions like node-wise supervision and explicit Euler time-stepping, which ignore stiff dynamics and local flux continuity inherent to PDE resolution methods.

Method: Three key innovations: (1) Multi Node Prediction - stencil-level objective predicting field values for a node's full local topology to enforce spatial derivative consistency; (2) Temporal Correction - replacing unstable explicit schemes with predictor-corrector via temporal Cross-Attention; (3) Geometric Inductive Biases - using 3D Rotary Positional Embeddings to capture rotational symmetries in unstructured meshes.

Result: The framework shows consistent improvements in accuracy and stability across three architectures (MeshGraphNet, Transolver, Transformer) on diverse physics datasets, especially in long-horizon rollouts, with latent representations that generalize to unseen subtasks like Wall Shear Stress or Pressure prediction.

Conclusion: The proposed unified framework successfully bridges geometric deep learning with rigorous numerical analysis, addressing critical bottlenecks in ML surrogate training for CFD simulations and providing more accurate, stable, and generalizable physics predictions.

Abstract: Machine Learning surrogates for Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), particularly Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Transformers, have become a new important approach for accelerating physics simulations. However, we identify a critical bottleneck in the field: while architectures have advanced significantly, the common underlying training paradigms remain bound to naive assumptions, such as node-wise supervision and explicit Euler time-stepping. These legacy choices ignore the stiff dynamics and local flux continuity inherent to numerous partial differential equations resolution methods, such as Finite Element, Difference, or Volume (FEM). In this work, we propose a unified framework to bridge the gap between geometric deep learning and rigorous numerical analysis. We introduce three key innovations: (1) Multi Node Prediction, a stencil-level objective that predicts field values for a node's full local topology, enforcing spatial derivative consistency; (2) Temporal Correction, replacing unstable explicit schemes with a predictor-corrector via temporal Cross-Attention; and (3) Geometric Inductive Biases, leveraging 3D Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) to robustly capture rotational symmetries in unstructured meshes. We evaluate this framework across three architectures (MeshGraphNet, Transolver, and a Transformer) on diverse physics datasets. Our approach yields consistent improvements in accuracy and stability, particularly in long-horizon rollouts, while producing latent representations that generalize to unseen subtasks such as Wall Shear Stress or Pressure prediction. Code is available at https://github.com/DonsetPG/graph-physics.

</details>


### [205] [ECG-biometrics-bench: A Unified Framework for Reproducible Benchmarking of ECG Biometrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01548)
*Milad Parvan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ECG-biometrics-bench introduces a standardized benchmarking framework for ECG biometrics that exposes the Random Split Fallacy, showing that intra-session evaluation artificially inflates performance while masking degradation from temporal drift and unseen identities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior ECG biometric studies report overly optimistic results due to data leakage from random splits within the same session, creating an unrealistic evaluation of real-world performance.

Method: Developed ECG-biometrics-bench, a modular reproducible framework that standardizes preprocessing, segmentation, and evaluation across 7 public ECG datasets. It supports closed/open-set evaluation with progressive protocols including cross-session and long-term temporal separation. Evaluated multiple architectures (DeepECG, ResNet1D, CNN-LSTM) and proposed a dynamic multi-session template fusion strategy for authentication.

Result: Exposed the Random Split Fallacy - intra-session evaluation artificially inflates performance while masking severe degradation from temporal drift and unseen identities. Performance degradation due to temporal aging can be partially mitigated through dynamic multi-session template fusion. These failures are not model-specific but inherent to current supervised feature-learning paradigms.

Conclusion: Establishes a more realistic baseline for ECG biometrics and highlights critical challenges (temporal drift, identity generalization) that must be addressed for reliable real-world deployment. The benchmarking framework will be made publicly available to facilitate reproducible research.

Abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) biometrics have emerged as a promising modality for continuous, liveness-aware authentication in wearable systems. However, many prior studies report overly optimistic results due to data leakage (e.g., random splits within the same session). To address this issue, we introduce ECG-biometrics-bench, a modular, reproducible benchmarking framework that standardizes preprocessing, segmentation, and evaluation across seven widely used public ECG datasets spanning clinical, ambulatory, and large-scale cohort settings. The framework supports both closed-set and open-set (i.e., subject-disjoint generalization in this work) evaluation, as well as progressively realistic protocols including cross-session and long-term temporal separation. To facilitate reproducible research in the community, the ECG-biometrics-bench repository will be made publicly accessible on GitHub upon the acceptance of this manuscript. Through a comprehensive multi-dataset analysis, we expose the Random Split Fallacy, demonstrating that intra-session evaluation protocols artificially inflate performance while masking severe degradation caused by temporal drift and unseen identities. Furthermore, by evaluating multiple architectures, including DeepECG, ResNet1D, and CNN-LSTM, we show that these failures are not model-specific but are likely inherent to current supervised feature-learning paradigms. Finally, we demonstrate that performance degradation due to temporal aging can be partially mitigated through a heavy enrollment, lightweight authentication strategy based on dynamic multi-session template fusion. These findings establish a more realistic baseline for ECG biometrics and highlight critical challenges that must be addressed for reliable real-world deployment.

</details>


### [206] [Hybrid Quantum Reinforcement Learning with QAOA for Improved Vehicle Routing Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01574)
*T. Satyanarayana Murthy,B. Swathi Sowmya,Santhosh Voruganti,Sai Varshini Giridi,Chaitanyya Pratap Agarwal,Vanteddu Akshitha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid approach combining Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) with Quantum Reinforcement Learning (QRL) for solving Vehicle Routing Problems, showing improved convergence and scalability over existing quantum methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a complex NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem in transportation/logistics that requires dynamic solutions. Existing quantum approaches like Grover's Adaptive Search (GAS) and standard Quantum Reinforcement Learning (QRL) have limitations in scalability and solution quality for larger instances.

Method: Developed a new hybrid approach integrating QAOA into the QRL policy network, replacing standard variational layers with QAOA mixing and cost Hamiltonian layers. This allows the agent to exploit problem-specific quantum correlations when learning policies, enabling richer exploration of routing solution space.

Result: The QAOA-augmented QRL framework demonstrates quicker convergence in training, can tackle larger VRP instances beyond the reach of GAS and standard QRL approaches, yields better solutions, requires fewer training episodes to converge, and shows good memory usage on near-term quantum hardware simulators.

Conclusion: The findings demonstrate that QAOA-integrated QRL is a viable approach to scalable, high-quality quantum-assisted combinatorial optimization for transportation and logistics problems.

Abstract: Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is one of the most complex NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem in transportation and logistics that requires a dynamic solution approach. In this paper we present a new hybrid approach that combines the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) into the QRL policy network, instead of the usual variational layers, QAOA mixing and cost Hamiltonian layers. This enhancement enables the agent to exploit problem specific particular quantum correlations when learning policies, and so richer exploration of the routing solution space. The QAOA-augmented QRL framework shows quicker convergence in training and can tackle larger VRP instances that are beyond the reach of Grover's Adaptive Search (GAS) and Quantum Reinforcement Learning (QRL) approaches. Experiments on standard VRP instances demonstrate better solutions, fewer episodes to converge and good memory usage on near term quantum hardware simulators. These findings demonstrate QAOA- integrated QRL as a viable approach to scalable, high quality quantum-assisted combinatorial optimization.

</details>


### [207] [Model Merging: Foundations and Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01580)
*Donato Crisostomi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This thesis proposes model merging as an alternative to independent training paradigms, enabling composition of learned capabilities by directly combining neural network weights without optimization or training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning treats models as separate artifacts that are trained independently and replaced when improved. The thesis explores model merging as an alternative paradigm to compose, reuse, and extend learned capabilities across models without retraining.

Method: Two main regimes: 1) Single-task: C²M³ algorithm using cycle-consistent merging via Frank-Wolfe optimization to align networks into shared parameter space. 2) Multi-task: Develops theory of task vectors as approximate gradients, introduces Task Singular Vectors (TSV) decomposition for compression, TSV-Merge for interference reduction, MASS for input-adaptive routing, and MERGE³ evolutionary framework using Item Response Theory.

Result: Provides theoretical and algorithmic foundations for model merging with methods that enable meaningful weight averaging without data, reduce evaluation costs by up to 50× while preserving quality, and support composition of capabilities across tasks and models.

Conclusion: Model merging represents a viable paradigm shift where learned capabilities can be directly composed, reused, and extended across neural networks, offering alternatives to the traditional independent training approach.

Abstract: Modern deep learning usually treats models as separate artifacts: trained independently, specialized for particular purposes, and replaced when improved versions appear. This thesis studies model merging as an alternative paradigm: combining independently trained neural networks directly in weight space, with little or no optimization and without requiring access to the original training data.
  The thesis considers two main regimes. In the single-task setting, where models share an objective but differ in initialization, we introduce C$^2$M$^3$, a cycle-consistent merging algorithm based on Frank-Wolfe optimization. C$^2$M$^3$ aligns multiple networks into a shared, reference-free parameter space, making weight averaging meaningful without privileging any individual model.
  In the multi-task setting, where models are fine-tuned for different downstream tasks from a common pretrained initialization, we first develop a theoretical account of task vectors as approximate gradients. This explains both the effectiveness and the limitations of task arithmetic. Building on this view, we show that task vectors inherit the low-rank structure of gradients and introduce Task Singular Vectors (TSV), a decomposition that enables compression and interference reduction through TSV-Merge. We then present MASS, an input-adaptive routing method that uses TSV geometry to select task-relevant subspaces at inference time.
