Finally, we introduce MERGE$^3$, an evolutionary merging framework that uses Item Response Theory to reduce evaluation costs by up to 50$\times$ while preserving solution quality.
  Together, these contributions provide theoretical and algorithmic foundations for model merging, supporting a paradigm in which learned capabilities can be composed, reused, and extended across models.

</details>


### [208] [Concepts Whisper While Syntax Shouts: Spectral Anti-Concentration and the Dual Geometry of Transformer Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01609)
*Pratyush Acharya,Nuraj Rimal,Habish Dhakal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Whitened Causal Alignment doesn't enable cross-lingual concept transport but reveals transformers rotate semantic content into spectrally quiet regions, creating a dual geometry where activation-space concepts anti-concentrate in spectral tails while unembedding contrasts concentrate in high-variance directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether the causal inner product (defined by unembedding covariance Σ) enables cross-lingual concept transport across transformers, and to understand how transformers encode concepts in their spectral structure.

Method: Used matched-spectrum randomization tests across 17 models and 4 language pairs, analyzed residual-stream difference-of-means vectors across 5 architecture families, employed SAE features and linear probes, conducted split-injection causal interventions, and performed POS-tag probing across 8 models.

Result: Whitened Causal Alignment indistinguishable from spectral regularization alone (p=0.95). Found anti-concentration in residual-stream vectors (p<10^{-33}) and dual geometry: activation-space concepts anti-concentrate in spectral tails while unembedding contrasts concentrate in high-variance directions. Split-injection interventions support functional basis (Cohen's d up to 1.80). POS-tag probing shows syntax preferentially encodes in high-variance subspace in 6/8 architectures.

Conclusion: Transformers may rotate semantic content into spectrally quiet regions during contextualized processing, allowing concepts to be manipulated with reduced grammatical disruption, revealing architecture-specific spectral structures (especially in Qwen~2.5 family).

Abstract: We test whether the causal inner product of \citet{park2024linear} -- defined by the unembedding covariance $Σ$ -- enables cross-lingual concept transport. Across 17 models and 4 language pairs, a matched-spectrum randomization test finds that Whitened Causal Alignment is indistinguishable from spectral regularization alone ($p = 0.95$). However, this failure reveals a broader phenomenon: anti-concentration is observed in residual-stream difference-of-means vectors across five architecture families ($p < 10^{-33}$) and supported by SAE features (e.g., $p = 4.5 \times 10^{-19}$) and linear probes on Gemma and Llama. We discover a \emph{dual geometry}: activation-space concept directions anti-concentrate in the spectral tail, while static unembedding-row contrasts \emph{concentrate} in high-variance directions ($p < 10^{-4}$). Split-injection causal interventions support the functional basis on Gemma and Llama (Cohen's $d$ up to $1.80$), and POS-tag probing across 8 models shows syntax preferentially encodes in the high-variance subspace in 6 of 8 architectures ($p < 0.013$), with the Qwen~2.5 family showing a significant reversal consistent with architecture-specific spectral structure. These results suggest transformers may rotate semantic content into spectrally quiet regions during contextualized processing, encoding concepts where they can be manipulated with reduced grammatical disruption.

</details>


### [209] [From Packets to Patterns: Interpreting Encrypted Network Traffic as Longitudinal Behavioral Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01616)
*Rameen Mahmood,Omar El Shahawy,Souptik Barua,Zachary Beattie,Jeffrey Kaye,Xuhai "Orson'' Xu,Chao-Yi Wu,Danny Yuxing Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Smartphone network traffic can passively detect sleep, stress, and loneliness patterns using transformer models with per-user adapters and sparse autoencoders for interpretable behavioral features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human behavior is difficult to observe continuously at scale, but leaves traces in everyday device use. The paper aims to test whether encrypted smartphone network traffic can passively capture behavioral patterns related to sleep, stress, and loneliness.

Method: The researchers use a transformer backbone with per-user adapters to model shared behavioral structure while representing individual behavior and deviations. They apply a sparse autoencoder to extract interpretable behavioral features corresponding to distinct activity patterns. They relate these features to sleep disturbance, stress, and loneliness using generalized estimating equations with Mundlak decomposition to separate between-person differences from within-person changes over time.

Result: The three outcomes show distinct temporal structures: stress is primarily associated with stable between-person differences, loneliness with within-person variation, and sleep disturbance with a combination of both. The within-person dynamics are not captured by predefined network-traffic features, demonstrating the value of learned representations for longitudinal behavioral sensing.

Conclusion: Encrypted network traffic is a viable passive sensing modality that reveals interpretable behavioral dynamics—particularly deviations from an individual's baseline—that are not visible in raw traffic features.

Abstract: Human behavior is difficult to observe continuously at scale, yet it leaves measurable traces in everyday device use. We test whether encrypted smartphone network traffic -- a ubiquitous, always-on, passive sensing modality -- can passively capture behavioral patterns related to sleep, stress, and loneliness. We model shared behavioral structure using a transformer backbone with per-user adapters, allowing the model to represent both typical individual behavior and deviations from it. To make these representations interpretable, we apply a sparse autoencoder to extract behavioral features corresponding to distinct patterns of activity. We relate these features to sleep disturbance, stress, and loneliness using generalized estimating equations with Mundlak decomposition, separating between-person differences from within-person changes over time. We find that the three outcomes reflect distinct temporal structures: stress is primarily associated with stable between-person differences, loneliness with within-person variation, and sleep disturbance with a combination of both. Notably, these within-person dynamics are not captured by predefined network-traffic features, demonstrating the value of learned representations for longitudinal behavioral sensing. These results establish encrypted network traffic as a viable passive sensing modality, revealing interpretable behavioral dynamics -- particularly deviations from an individual's baseline -- that are not visible in raw traffic features.

</details>


### [210] [PRIME: Protein Representation via Physics-Informed Multiscale Equivariant Hierarchies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01625)
*Viet Thanh Duy Nguyen,John K. Johnstone,Truong-Son Hy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRIME is a physics-informed multiscale protein representation learning framework that models proteins as nested structural graphs across five hierarchical levels, enabling bidirectional information flow between adjacent scales for improved performance on diverse protein tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing protein representation methods operate at single structural levels or treat different structural information as parallel modalities without explicitly modeling their hierarchical relationships, despite proteins being inherently multiscale systems where functional properties emerge from coordinated organization across multiple spatial resolutions.

Method: PRIME models proteins as a nested family of five physically grounded structural graphs spanning surface, atomic, residue, secondary-structure, and protein levels. Adjacent levels are connected through deterministic, physics-informed assignment operators that enable bidirectional information exchange via bottom-up aggregation and top-down contextual refinement.

Result: PRIME demonstrates strong performance on protein representation benchmarks, particularly on Fold Classification where it outperforms the strongest geometric GNN baseline by 13.80 and 18.30 points on Superfamily and Fold splits, and achieves state-of-the-art 84.10% accuracy on Reaction Class prediction. Ablation studies show each structural level contributes complementary information, and the model autonomously identifies task-relevant structural resolutions.

Conclusion: PRIME provides a unified hierarchical framework that effectively captures the multiscale nature of proteins through physics-informed structural graphs, achieving superior performance by enabling bidirectional information flow across structural resolutions and autonomously adapting to task-specific requirements.

Abstract: Proteins are inherently multiscale physical systems whose functional properties emerge from coordinated structural organization across multiple spatial resolutions, ranging from atomic interactions to global fold topology. However, existing protein representation learning methods typically operate at a single structural level or treat different sources of structural information as parallel modalities, without explicitly modeling their hierarchical relationships. We introduce PRIME (Protein Representation via Physics-Informed Multiscale Equivariant Hierarchies), a unified framework that models proteins as a nested family of five physically grounded structural graphs spanning surface, atomic, residue, secondary-structure, and protein levels. Adjacent levels are connected through deterministic, physics-informed assignment operators, enabling bidirectional information exchange via bottom-up aggregation and top-down contextual refinement. Experiments on standard protein representation learning benchmarks demonstrate strong and competitive performance across diverse tasks, with particularly notable gains on the Fold Classification benchmark, where PRIME outperforms the strongest geometric GNN baseline by margins of 13.80 and 18.30 points on the harder Superfamily and Fold splits, and achieves a state-of-the-art accuracy of 84.10% on Reaction Class prediction, surpassing all baseline methods, including ESM. Ablation studies confirm that each structural level contributes complementary and non-redundant information, and adaptive cross-attention analysis reveals that PRIME autonomously identifies the most task-relevant structural resolutions at prediction time. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/HySonLab/PRIME

</details>


### [211] [Importance-Guided Basis Selection for Low-Rank Decomposition of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01627)
*Daniel Agyei Asante,Ernie Chang,Yang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BSI is a principled low-rank compression framework for LLMs that ranks and prunes singular vector bases by directly estimating the expected loss increase from second-order Taylor expansion, outperforming existing methods especially under deep compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing low-rank compression methods for large language models use heuristic approaches (like Basel's magnitude pruning) that can be misaligned with task performance because they ignore the local geometry of the loss landscape. There's a need for a more principled approach that directly estimates the impact of basis removal on task performance.

Method: BSI uses a second-order Taylor expansion of the task loss with respect to singular values to derive an importance score combining first-order sensitivity and second-order curvature. It adapts the Hutchinson randomized-probing method with symmetric parameter perturbations for efficient Hessian-diagonal estimation, making it practical for LLMs.

Result: BSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art low-rank decomposition baselines on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, with especially strong improvements under deep compression.

Conclusion: BSI provides a principled framework for low-rank compression that directly estimates loss impact from basis removal, supported by comprehensive theoretical analysis including error bounds, variance characterization, and sample-complexity guarantees, demonstrating superior performance over heuristic approaches.

Abstract: Low-rank decomposition is a compelling approach for compressing large language models, but its effectiveness hinges on selecting which singular-vector bases to retain for a target task. Existing methods such as Basel adapt singular-value coefficients on downstream data and prune bases with small re-learned magnitudes, a heuristic that can be misaligned with task performance because it ignores the local geometry of the loss landscape. We present Basis Selection with Importance (BSI), a principled low-rank compression framework that ranks and prunes bases by directly estimating the expected loss increase incurred when each basis is removed. BSI derives a derivative-based importance score from a second-order Taylor expansion of the task loss with respect to singular values, combining first-order sensitivity and second-order curvature to quantify pruning impact. To make this criterion practical for LLMs, we develop an efficient Hessian-diagonal estimator by adapting the Hutchinson randomized-probing method to loss curvature with symmetric parameter perturbations. We provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis, including loss-increase bounds under basis pruning, explicit propagation of Hessian-diagonal estimation error into these bounds, variance characterization tied to the Hessian spectrum, high-probability sample-complexity guarantees for achieving a target estimation accuracy, and guidance on perturbation intensity. Extensive experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that BSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art low-rank decomposition baselines, with especially strong improvements under deep compression.

</details>


### [212] [Perturb and Correct: Post-Hoc Ensembles using Affine Redundancy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01632)
*Eleanor Quint*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Perturb-and-Correct (P&C) is a post-hoc method that creates diverse predictors from a single pretrained network using hidden layer perturbations with correction, achieving strong in-distribution/out-of-distribution performance tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Different models that perform identically on in-distribution data can behave very differently under distribution shift, highlighting the need for methods that can capture epistemic uncertainty and diversity from a single model.

Method: P&C applies random perturbations to hidden layers of a pretrained network, followed by a least-squares correction in the subsequent affine layer. This creates multiple predictors that agree on calibration data but can disagree on out-of-distribution data.

Result: The method achieves strong ID/OOD tradeoffs on MuJoCo dynamics prediction and CIFAR-10 OOD detection tasks, matching or outperforming standard post-hoc baselines while requiring only a single pretrained model.

Conclusion: P&C demonstrates the potential of exploiting model overparameterization as a strength rather than a weakness, offering a practical way to obtain epistemic diversity from a single network.

Abstract: Models that are indistinguishable on in-distribution data can behave very differently under distribution shift. We introduce Perturb-and-Correct (P&C), a post-hoc method for constructing epistemically diverse predictors from a single pretrained network. P&C applies random hidden layer perturbations with a least-squares correction in the subsequent affine layer, producing predictors that agree on calibration data while remaining free to disagree away from it. We analyze this mechanism through the post-correction residual and its first-order sensitivity: the residual is controlled near the calibration distribution by a leverage term, while corrected sensitivity grows as inputs deviate from the calibration geometry. Empirically, P&C achieves a strong ID/OOD tradeoff across MuJoCo dynamics prediction and CIFAR-10 OOD detection, matching or outperforming standard post-hoc baselines while requiring only a single pretrained model. Our findings highlight the potential in further exploiting overparameterization as a strength of deep learning models.

</details>


### [213] [Chebyshev-Augmented One-Shot Transfer Learning for PINNs on Nonlinear Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01634)
*Yiqi Rao,Pavlos Protopapas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINNs with Chebyshev polynomial surrogates enable one-shot transfer learning for broader classes of nonlinear problems by freezing pretrained latent representations and solving output weights analytically without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PINNs suffer from instance specificity - they require retraining for each new problem instance (different forcing terms, boundary conditions, or parameters). While one-shot transfer learning works for linear operators, it fails for nonlinear problems due to nonconvex optimization in the output layer.

Method: The paper combines one-shot transfer learning with Chebyshev polynomial surrogates. Smooth nonlinear terms are approximated by truncated Chebyshev expansions over prescribed solution ranges, converting them into polynomial nonlinearities. These are then handled via perturbative decomposition into linear subproblems. A multi-head PINN learns a reusable latent space for the dominant linear operator, and at test time, solutions are obtained via closed-form linear solves without retraining the network body.

Result: The framework demonstrates accurate and fast online adaptation on nonlinear benchmarks, including non-polynomial and singular ODE nonlinearities, as well as reaction-diffusion PDEs with saturating kinetics. The method shows utility in many-query regimes where fast adaptation is crucial.

Conclusion: The approach substantially broadens the class of nonlinear problems amenable to one-shot PINN transfer by combining Chebyshev approximations with perturbative decomposition, enabling efficient many-query applications without the computational burden of retraining for each new instance.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a flexible paradigm for solving differential equations by embedding governing laws into the training objective. A persistent limitation is instance specificity: standard PINNs typically require retraining for each new forcing term, boundary/initial condition, or parameter setting. One-shot transfer learning (OTL) addresses this bottleneck for linear operators by freezing a pretrained latent representation and computing optimal output weights in closed form, but for nonlinear problems closed-form adaptation is generally unavailable because the loss is nonconvex in the output layer.
  In this paper we substantially broaden the class of nonlinearities amenable to one-shot PINN transfer by combining OTL with Chebyshev polynomial surrogates. We approximate general smooth weakly nonlinear terms by truncated Chebyshev expansions over a prescribed solution range, yielding a polynomial nonlinearity that can be handled by a perturbative decomposition into linear subproblems. A multi-head PINN learns a reusable latent space associated with the dominant linear operator; at test time, solutions to new instances are obtained via a sequence of closed-form linear solves in the output layer, without retraining the network body.
  We provide a unified derivation of the framework for ODEs and PDEs and demonstrate accuracy and fast online adaptation on nonlinear benchmarks, including non-polynomial and singular ODE nonlinearities as well as a reaction-diffusion PDE with saturating kinetics, demonstrating the method's utility in many-query regimes.

</details>


### [214] [The Banach-Butterfly Invariant: Influence-Adaptive Walsh Geometry for Ternary Polynomial Threshold Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01637)
*Gorgi Pavlov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Banach-Butterfly Invariant (BBT), a new influence-adaptive metric for Boolean functions based on Walsh-Hadamard butterfly factorization, with applications in Boolean analysis and connections to deep learning quantization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a new geometric invariant for Boolean functions that captures influence structure in a more nuanced way than total influence alone, enabling better classification and analysis of Boolean functions, with potential applications in understanding and optimizing neural network quantization.

Method: The authors define BBT by assigning exponent p_ℓ = 1 + Inf_ℓ(f) to each butterfly layer ℓ, creating a contraction invariant μ(f). They prove mathematical properties (Jensen lower bound, Schur-convexity), conduct computational experiments on Boolean functions up to n=5 using MILP certificates, and analyze statistical relationships between μ and minimum support size.

Result: BBT provides a new invariant that separates functions with identical total influence, showing different scaling behaviors for different function classes (parity: 2^{-n/2}, majority: 2^{-Θ(√n)}, dictators: 2^{-1/2}). Computational results show μ is rational in log form but algebraic in μ itself, and statistical analysis reveals a reversal in correlation between μ and minimum support size from positive at n=4 to negative at n=5.

Conclusion: BBT is a valid Schur-convex concentration invariant for Boolean functions that captures influence structure beyond total influence, but not a universal monotone predictor of minimum support across dimensions. The theoretical framework successfully transfers qualitatively to practical applications in LLM quantization, improving perplexity by 15-58% despite being a qualitative rather than formal transfer.

Abstract: We introduce the Banach-Butterfly Invariant (BBT), an influence-adaptive Banach geometry on the Walsh-Hadamard butterfly factorization. For a Boolean function $f:\{-1,+1\}^n\to\{-1,+1\}$ with coordinate influences $\mathrm{Inf}_\ell(f)$, BBT assigns exponent $p_\ell = 1+\mathrm{Inf}_\ell(f)$ to butterfly layer $\ell$, yielding the contraction invariant $μ(f)=\prod_\ell 2^{-\mathrm{Inf}_\ell/(1+\mathrm{Inf}_\ell)}$. We prove a Jensen lower bound $\log_2μ(f) \ge -I(f)/(1+I(f)/n)$ and that $μ$ is strictly Schur-convex in the influence vector (modulo permutation), giving scaling classes $μ\sim 2^{-n/2}$ (parity), $2^{-Θ(\sqrt{n})}$ (majority), $2^{-1/2}$ (dictators). $\log_2μ$ is rational but not polynomial in the Fourier coefficients while $μ$ is algebraic, and $μ$ separates functions with identical total influence (122 pairs at $n=3$).
  Using the certified $n \le 4$ ternary Walsh-threshold universe from a companion synthesis manuscript as a finite testbed, we compute exact MILP minimum-support certificates for all 65,536 Boolean functions at $n=4$ (mean 6.42, max 9, all-odd by a parity argument) and on 10,000 of the 616,126 NPN-canonical representatives we enumerate at $n=5$ (matching OEIS A000370). Conditional Spearman $ρ(μ,|\mathrm{supp}|)$ at fixed total influence is $+0.571$ in the largest stratum at $n=4$ but reverses to $-0.38$ at $n=5$ under both function-uniform and NPN-canonical sampling: $μ$ is a valid Schur-convex concentration invariant, not a universal monotone predictor of minimum support across $n$.
  A companion application paper validates a real-valued WHT activation-energy proxy inspired by this theory on five pretrained LLMs at W2A16, cutting wikitext-2 perplexity by 15-58% versus vanilla auto-round; the transfer from Boolean theory to the real-valued proxy is qualitative, not formal.

</details>


### [215] [Prescriptive Scaling Laws for Data Constrained Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01640)
*Justin Lovelace,Christian Belardi,Srivatsa Kundurthy,Shriya Sudhakar,Kilian Q. Weinberger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new scaling law that accounts for data repetition in compute-optimal training, showing that beyond a certain point, repetition hurts performance and compute should be allocated to model capacity instead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training compute is growing faster than high-quality data availability, shifting the challenge from optimal compute allocation to extracting maximum value from limited data. The standard Chinchilla scaling law assumes unique training tokens, which limits its usefulness in data-constrained regimes.

Method: The authors model excess loss under data repetition with a simple additive overfitting penalty, creating a scaling law that accurately describes model behavior with repeated data. Their one-parameter form isolates overfitting in a single coefficient for easy comparison across configurations.

Result: The new scaling law yields qualitatively different compute-optimal allocation advice: beyond a certain point, further data repetition is counterproductive and compute is better spent on increasing model capacity. Following their recommendations improves performance in data-constrained regimes. They also show strong weight decay (λ=1.0) reduces the overfitting coefficient by ~70%.

Conclusion: The proposed scaling law provides better guidance for compute-optimal training in data-limited scenarios, explaining why optimal weight decay in such regimes should be much larger than standard practice and demonstrating that model capacity should be prioritized over excessive data repetition.

Abstract: Training compute is increasingly outpacing the availability of high-quality data. This shifts the central challenge from optimal compute allocation to extracting maximum value from limited data. The widely adopted Chinchilla scaling law assumes every training token is unique. This limits its ability to guide pretraining decisions in data-constrained regimes. We model the excess loss under repetition with a simple additive overfitting penalty and find that it accurately describes model behavior. Our scaling law yields qualitatively new compute-optimal allocation advice. Beyond a point, further repetition is counterproductive and compute is better spent on model capacity. We show that following our law's recommended configuration improves performance in data-constrained regimes. Finally, because our one-parameter form isolates overfitting in a single coefficient, it enables direct comparison across training configurations. As a case study, we show that strong weight decay ($λ=1.0$) reduces this coefficient by approximately 70%, providing a scaling-law explanation for recent findings that optimal weight decay in data-constrained regimes is an order of magnitude larger than standard practice.

</details>


### [216] [Adaptive Pluralistic Alignment: A pipeline for dynamic artificial democracy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01642)
*Rachel Freedman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: APA (Adaptive Pluralistic Alignment) is a modular pipeline for updating AI systems to track evolving societal values over time without costly retraining, using compact personalized reward models and social-choice voting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current alignment methods risk value lock-in by targeting fixed preferences, which becomes problematic as societal norms evolve over time. There's a need for AI systems that can adapt to changing values without expensive retraining or large-scale data collection.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: 1) Learn compact personalized reward models via low-rank reward basis decomposition, 2) Use these models as a "jury" that collectively selects candidate outputs through social-choice voting rules, 3) Efficiently adapt the jury over time by fitting new annotator weights over fixed reward bases as values shift.

Result: Implemented proof-of-concept using PRISM multi-user alignment dataset with simulated historical annotators. Preliminary analysis shows jury composition and voting rule choice substantially affect outcomes, especially when jury preferences are heterogeneous. The system is efficient, explainable, steerable, and modular.

Conclusion: APA provides a practical approach to prevent value lock-in in AI systems, enabling continuous adaptation to evolving societal values while maintaining efficiency, explainability, and modularity through its three-stage pipeline.

Abstract: Prevailing alignment methods target a fixed set of preferences and therefore risk forcing value lock-in as societal norms evolve over time. We introduce Adaptive Pluralistic Alignment (APA), a modular pipeline for updating pluralistically aligned AI systems to track evolving values and avoid value lock-in without repeating costly pretraining or large-scale data collection. APA has three stages: (1) learning compact personalized reward models via low-rank reward basis decomposition, (2) using these models as a jury that collectively selects among candidate outputs through social-choice-theoretic voting, and (3) efficiently adapting the jury over time by fitting new annotator weights over the fixed reward bases as values shift. The resulting system is efficient, explainable, steerable, and modular. We implement a proof-of-concept instantiation using the PRISM multi-user alignment dataset and simulated historical annotators, and provide preliminary analysis showing that jury composition and the choice of voting rule can substantially affect outcomes, particularly when jury preferences are heterogeneous. We provide full code and resulting preference datasets at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/apa.

</details>


### [217] [AI Alignment via Incentives and Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01643)
*Rohit Agarwal,Joshua Lin,Mark Braverman,Elad Hazan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AI alignment as a strategic deterrence problem where solver-auditor interactions form equilibrium systems, requiring bilevel optimization of reward profiles rather than static feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional AI alignment treats misbehavior as an external failure, but this paper argues it's actually a strategic response to incentives. The authors want to reframe alignment through law-and-economics models of deterrence where actors weigh violation benefits against detection probabilities and punishment severity.

Method: Formalizes solver-auditor interaction as a two-agent model where principal chooses rewards over joint correction outcomes. Treats reward design as bilevel optimization problem, proposing bandit-based outer-loop procedure for searching over reward profiles using noisy interaction feedback.

Result: Experiments on LLM coding pipeline show adaptive reward profiles can maintain useful oversight pressure and improve principal-aligned outcomes compared to static hand-designed rewards, including substantial reduction in hallucinated incorrect attempts.

Conclusion: AI alignment should be viewed as strategic equilibrium problem similar to legal deterrence systems, requiring adaptive reward design that considers the full correction event rather than just final answers, leading to better oversight and reduced misbehavior.

Abstract: We study AI alignment through the lens of law-and-economics models of deterrence and enforcement. In these models, misconduct is not treated as an external failure, but as a strategic response to incentives: an actor weighs the gain from violation against the probability of detection and the severity of punishment. We argue that the same logic arises naturally in agentic AI pipelines. A solver may benefit from producing a persuasive but incorrect answer, hiding uncertainty, or exploiting spurious shortcuts, while an auditor or verifier must decide whether costly monitoring is worthwhile. Alignment is therefore a fixed-point problem: stronger penalties may deter solver misbehavior, but they can also reduce the auditor's incentive to inspect, since auditing then mainly incurs cost on a population that appears increasingly aligned.
  This perspective also changes what should count as a post-training signal. Standard feedback often attaches reward to the final answer alone, but a solver-auditor pipeline exposes the full correction event: whether the solver erred, whether the auditor inspected, whether the error was caught, and whether oversight incentives remained active. We formalize this interaction in a two-agent model in which a principal chooses rewards over joint correction outcomes, inducing both solver behavior and auditor monitoring. Reward design is therefore a bilevel optimization problem: rewards are judged not by their immediate semantic meaning, but by the behavioral equilibrium they induce. We propose a bandit-based outer-loop procedure for searching over reward profiles using noisy interaction feedback. Experiments on an LLM coding pipeline show that adaptive reward profiles can maintain useful oversight pressure and improve principal-aligned outcomes relative to static hand-designed rewards, including a substantial reduction in hallucinated incorrect attempts.

</details>


### [218] [Geospatial foundation-model embeddings improve population estimation unevenly across space and scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01650)
*Wenbin Zhang,Eimear Cleary,Francisco Rowe,Somnath Chaudhuri,Maksym Bondarenko,Shengjie Lai,Andrew J. Tatem*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geospatial foundation model (PDFM) embeddings outperform traditional hand-built covariates for subnational population estimation in data-poor settings but have limitations with spatial scale transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable subnational population estimates are essential but difficult where censuses are sparse, outdated, or spatially coarse. Existing methods rely on manually assembled geospatial covariates that require harmonization across scales and geographies.

Method: Benchmarked Population Dynamics Foundation Model (PDFM) embeddings against harmonized geospatial covariates (settlement extent, night-time lights, environmental conditions) for subnational population estimation in Brazil, Nigeria, and the United States using geographically structured validation.

Result: PDFM increased predictive fit by median 20.1% reduction in unexplained variance (IQR: 10.0-33.2%) and reduced Kullback-Leibler divergence by 23.2% (IQR: 9.2-26.2%). Benefits were strongest where traditional covariates weakly characterized settlement context (larger, less-developed areas). However, performance was scale-coupled with less flexible transfer across spatial aggregations than geospatial covariates.

Conclusion: Geospatial foundation models can improve population estimation in data-poor settings, but their benefits break down predictably under spatial scale mismatch, revealing a fundamental limitation of current geospatial AI.

Abstract: Reliable subnational population estimates are essential for applications, yet remain difficult where censuses are sparse, outdated or spatially coarse. Existing population-mapping workflows rely on hand-built geospatial covariates, such as settlement extent, night-time lights, and environmental conditions, which must be assembled and harmonised across scales and geographies. Geospatial foundation models offer an alternative by learning reusable representations of place from more multifaceted and heterogeneous data sources. Here, we benchmark Population Dynamics Foundation Model (PDFM) embeddings against the harmonised geospatial covariates for subnational population estimation in Brazil, Nigeria and the United States. Under geographically structured validation, PDFM increased predictive fit by a median of 20.1% (IQR: 10.0-33.2%, across country-model comparisons) reduction in unexplained variance, and reduced Kullback-Leibler divergence by 23.2% (9.2-26.2%). However, these gains were uneven. PDFM was most advantageous where the geospatial covariates weakly characterised settlement context, such as larger and less-developed subnational areas. Moreover, PDFM performance was scale-coupled with embeddings providing less flexible transfer across spatial aggregations than geospatial covariates. These findings showed that geospatial foundation-model representations of place can improve population estimation in data poor settings, but their benefits break down predictably under spatial scale mismatch, revealing a fundamental limitation of current geospatial AI.

</details>


### [219] [Towards Efficient and Expressive Offline RL via Flow-Anchored Noise-conditioned Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01663)
*Sungyoung Lee,Dohyeong Kim,Eshan Balachandar,Zelal Su Mustafaoglu,Keshav Pingali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAN is an efficient offline RL algorithm that combines flow policies and distributional critics while dramatically reducing computational costs through single-iteration sampling and single Gaussian noise sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing flow policies and distributional critics improve offline RL performance but have high computational costs - flow policies require iterative sampling and distributional critics need multiple samples, making them inefficient for practical use.

Method: FAN uses behavior regularization with only a single flow policy iteration and a single Gaussian noise sample for distributional critics, significantly reducing computational overhead while maintaining performance.

Result: FAN achieves state-of-the-art performance on robotic manipulation and locomotion tasks while significantly reducing both training and inference runtimes compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The simplifications in FAN not only improve computational efficiency but actually lead to superior task performance, making it a practical high-performance offline RL solution with theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: We propose Flow-Anchored Noise-conditioned Q-Learning (FAN), a highly efficient and high-performing offline reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm. Recent work has shown that expressive flow policies and distributional critics improve offline RL performance, but at a high computational cost. Specifically, flow policies require iterative sampling to produce a single action, and distributional critics require computation over multiple samples (e.g., quantiles) to estimate value. To address these inefficiencies while maintaining high performance, we introduce FAN. Our method employs a behavior regularization technique that utilizes only a single flow policy iteration and requires only a single Gaussian noise sample for distributional critics. Our theoretical analysis of convergence and performance bounds demonstrates that these simplifications not only improve efficiency but also lead to superior task performance. Experiments on robotic manipulation and locomotion tasks demonstrate that FAN achieves state-of-the-art performance while significantly reducing both training and inference runtimes. We release our code at https://github.com/brianlsy98/FAN.

</details>


### [220] [Benchmarking Single-Pose Docking, Consensus Rescoring, and Supervised ML on the LIT-PCBA Library: A Critical Evaluation of DiffDock, AutoDock-GPU, GNINA, and DiffDock-NMDN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01681)
*Youssef Abo-Dahab,Xiaoiang Xiang,Xiaoiang Xiang,Xiaoiang Xiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study evaluates virtual screening methods on the realistic LIT-PCBA dataset (15 targets, 578,295 ligand-target pairs), finding that GNINA rescoring of AutoDock-GPU poses performs best as a single method, but supervised ML re-ranking achieves the largest improvements (+110% enrichment).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent AI-based virtual screening tools like DiffDock and NMDN show strong benchmark results, but their practical utility on realistic, experimentally-derived datasets remains unclear. There's a need to evaluate these methods on comprehensive experimental data to understand their real-world performance.

Method: Large-scale evaluation on the LIT-PCBA library with 15 targets and 578,295 ligand-target pairs. Compared AutoDock-GPU and DiffDock for pose generation, followed by rescoring with GNINA and NMDN. Also evaluated rank-based consensus strategies and supervised machine learning models trained on docking features.

Result: GNINA rescoring of AutoDock-GPU poses (AutoDock-GNINA) was the strongest single method with median EF1% of 2.14. DiffDock-based approaches underperformed relative to AutoDock-GNINA. Consensus ranking improved robustness but didn't surpass the best single scorer. Supervised ML re-ranking achieved median EF1% of 4.49 (+110% over AutoDock-GNINA).

Conclusion: Even the best classical+ML hybrid workflows provide only modest early enrichment on realistic benchmarks. No single docking method dominates across all targets, and rigorously validated, cost-effective combinations with supervised re-ranking currently offer the most practical value for virtual screening.

Abstract: Virtual screening performance depends heavily on the chosen docking and scoring methods. Recent AI-based tools such as DiffDock and NMDN have reported strong benchmark results, but their practical utility on realistic, experimentally-derived datasets remains unclear. Here we perform a large-scale evaluation on the LIT-PCBA library (15 targets, 578,295 ligand-target pairs with experimentally confirmed actives and inactives). We compare AutoDock-GPU and DiffDock for pose generation, followed by rescoring with GNINA and NMDN. We further evaluate rank-based consensus strategies and supervised machine learning models trained on docking features.
  GNINA rescoring of AutoDock-GPU poses (AutoDock-GNINA) emerged as the strongest single method with a median EF1% of 2.14. DiffDock-based approaches underperformed relative to AutoDock-GNINA, particularly on challenging targets such as OPRK1. Carefully designed consensus ranking improved robustness but did not surpass the best single scorer. Supervised ML re-ranking delivered the largest gains, achieving a median EF1% of 4.49 (+110% over AutoDock-GNINA).
  Our results highlight that even the best classical+ML hybrid workflows provide only modest early enrichment on realistic benchmarks. We conclude that no single docking method dominates across targets and that rigorously validated, cost-effective combinations with supervised re-ranking currently offer the most practical value for virtual screening.

</details>


### [221] [Complex Diffusion Maps with $ω$-Parameterized Kernels Revealing Inherent Harmonic Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01691)
*Tongzhen Dang,Weiyang Ding,Michael K. Ng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Complex Diffusion Maps (CDM) is a novel framework using complex-valued kernels to reveal dominant complex harmonics in high-dimensional data, improving discriminative power and robustness compared to real-valued kernel methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to overcome limitations of traditional diffusion maps that use real-valued kernels, which may not fully capture complex structures in high-dimensional data. The motivation is to develop a more powerful framework that can reveal both local and nonlocal connections through complex harmonics.

Method: Proposes Complex Diffusion Maps (CDM) using a unified family of ω-parameterized complex-valued kernels inspired by heat and Schrödinger equations. Establishes theoretical foundation based on operator spectrum theory, defining diffusion operators, diffusion distances, and complex harmonic maps. Includes optimization-based interpretation to preserve angular structure in complex diffusion space.

Result: CDM amplifies differences among easily confusable samples, improving discriminative power over real-valued kernel methods. Remains robust in high-noise settings with clearer eigengaps. Captures more strongly correlated and nonlocal dynamics in fMRI data. Achieves competitive performance on EEG sleep dataset without task-specific tuning while maintaining high computational efficiency.

Conclusion: CDM provides a powerful and general framework for analyzing high-dimensional data through complex-valued diffusion mappings, offering better discriminative power, noise robustness, and computational efficiency compared to traditional methods, with practical applications in neuroscience and other domains.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose Complex Diffusion Maps (CDM), a novel diffusion mapping framework that aims to reveal the dominant complex harmonics of high-dimensional data. Inspired by the local Gaussian kernel relevant to the heat equation and the nonlocal Schrödinger kernel relevant to the Schrödinger equation, we propose a unified family of $ω$-parameterized complex-valued kernels for the trade-off between local and nonlocal connections. We establish the theoretical foundation based on the operator spectrum theory, where the corresponding diffusion operator, diffusion distance, and complex harmonic maps are well-defined. An optimization-based interpretation of the maps is also developed, aiming to preserve angular structure in the complex diffusion space rather than relying solely on real-valued magnitude. We extensively evaluate CDM on both synthetic and real-world datasets. The complex-valued kernel amplifies differences among easily confusable samples, improving discriminative power over both linear and nonlinear methods based on real-valued kernels. CDM remains robust in high-noise settings, yielding a clearer eigengap that enhances spectral separation. For resting-state fMRI data, CDM captures more strongly correlated and nonlocal spatiotemporal dynamics. Without task-specific tuning, CDM achieves competitive performance on a public EEG sleep dataset, while maintaining high computational efficiency compared with both traditional machine learning and deep neural network approaches, highlighting its generality and practical value.

</details>


### [222] [Probe-Geometry Alignment: Erasing the Cross-Sequence Memorization Signature Below Chance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01699)
*Anamika Paul Rupa,Anietie Andy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recent attacks show that behavioral unlearning of LLMs leaves recoverable internal traces. The paper identifies these memorization signatures, proves they're causally separable from recall, and introduces PGA (probe-geometry alignment) to surgically remove them below random chance without performance cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the security vulnerability where behavioral unlearning of LLMs doesn't truly remove sensitive information, leaving internal representations that can be recovered by adversarial probes. This creates privacy risks even after supposed "unlearning" has occurred.

Method: Key methods: 1) Leave-one-out cross-sequence probe to test memorization signature generalization, 2) Analysis of signature consistency across model scales, 3) Demonstration of causal separability from recall, 4) Introduction of PGA (probe-geometry alignment) - a surgical erasure technique aligning activations along probe readout directions, 5) Testing against adversarial probe variants and re-fitting attacks.

Result: Results show: 1) Consistent memorization signatures across scales (+0.32, +0.19, +0.30 gaps on Pythia-70M, GPT-2 medium, Mistral-7B), 2) PGA drives cross-sequence probe below random chance at all scales (0.07-0.45 depending on model), 3) Robust to six adversarial probe variants, 4) Defeats re-fitting attacks at relevant depths while preserving capabilities within 2.8 percentage points per task (mean Δacc = +0.2pp).

Conclusion: The cross-sequence memorization signature is a real, causally separable, regime-specific property of pretrained LLM representations that can be removed below chance with single rank-one interventions per depth without measurable capability cost, providing effective defense against adversarial recovery of supposedly unlearned information.

Abstract: Recent attacks show that behavioural unlearning of large language models leaves internal traces recoverable by adversarial probes. We characterise where this retention lives and show it can be surgically removed without measurable capability cost. Our central protocol is a leave-one-out cross-sequence probe that tests whether a memorisation signature generalises across held-out sequences. The signature is real and consistent across scale: memorisation-specific gaps of +0.32, +0.19, +0.30 on Pythia-70M, GPT-2 medium, and Mistral-7B; on Pythia-70M, the random-initialisation control collapses to -0.04 at the deepest layer where the pretrained signature peaks. The probe direction is causally separable from recall -- projecting it out collapses the signature locally (+0.44 -> -0.19) while behavioural recall barely changes -- and a probe trained on naturally memorised content does not classify fine-tuning-injected secrets, marking two representationally distinct regimes. We then introduce probe-geometry alignment (PGA), a surgical erasure that aligns activations along the probe's live readout direction at each depth. PGA drives the cross-sequence probe below random chance at all four scales tested (toy depth-4: 0.17; Pythia-70M: 0.07; Mistral-7B: 0.45; GPT-2 medium: 0.06 via MD-PGA k=2) and remains robust to six adversarial probe variants. Against a re-fitting attacker who trains a fresh probe on PGA-treated activations, we extend PGA adversarially, defeating the re-fit probe at every memorisation-relevant depth while preserving five zero-shot capability benchmarks within 2.8 percentage points per task (mean Δacc = +0.2pp). The cross-sequence signature is a real, causally separable, regime-specific property of pretrained representations -- removable below chance with a single rank-one intervention per depth at no measurable capability cost.

</details>


### [223] [Stability and Generalization for Decentralized Markov SGD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01701)
*Jiahuan Wang,Ziqing Wen,Ping Luo,Dongsheng Li,Tao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes generalization bounds for decentralized SGD and SGDA under Markov chain sampling, capturing effects of network topology, Markov mixing, and primal-dual dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stochastic gradient methods are crucial for large-scale learning but existing generalization theory typically assumes independent sampling. In practice, data often comes from Markov chains (e.g., in reinforcement learning, time-series data) and learning occurs in decentralized settings, creating analytical challenges not addressed by current theory.

Method: The authors use a stability-based analytical framework to investigate decentralized stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and stochastic gradient descent ascent (SGDA) under Markov chain sampling. They characterize how Markovian dependence and decentralized communication jointly affect generalization behavior, specifically examining network topology effects, Markov chain mixing properties, and primal-dual dynamics.

Result: The authors establish non-asymptotic generalization bounds for both decentralized SGD and SGDA algorithms. These bounds extend existing results on Markov stochastic gradient methods to decentralized and minimax settings, capturing the combined influence of network communication patterns and Markovian data dependence.

Conclusion: The work provides rigorous generalization guarantees for decentralized learning with Markovian data, bridging the gap between practical learning scenarios (with dependent data and decentralized computation) and theoretical analysis. The results offer insights into how network structure and data dependence jointly impact generalization performance in distributed learning systems.

Abstract: Stochastic gradient methods are central to large-scale learning, yet their generalization theory typically relies on independent sampling assumptions. In many practical applications, data are generated by Markov chains and learning is performed in a decentralized manner, which introduces significant analytical challenges. In this work, we investigate the stability and generalization of decentralized stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and stochastic gradient descent ascent (SGDA) under Markov chain sampling. Leveraging a stability-based framework, we characterize how Markovian dependence and decentralized communication jointly influence generalization behavior. Our analysis captures the effects of network topology, Markov chain mixing properties, and primal-dual dynamics. We establish non-asymptotic generalization bounds for both algorithms, extending existing results on Markov stochastic gradient methods to decentralized and minimax settings.

</details>


### [224] [Floating-Point Networks with Automatic Differentiation Can Represent Almost All Floating-Point Functions and Their Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01702)
*Sejun Park,Yeachan Park,Geonho Hwang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural networks under floating-point arithmetic with automatic differentiation can approximate arbitrary function values and gradients simultaneously, extending classical theoretical results to practical implementations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical neural network approximation theorems assume real parameters and exact operations, but real implementations use floating-point arithmetic with round-off errors. The paper investigates whether similar approximation results hold for practical neural networks under floating-point arithmetic with automatic differentiation.

Method: The authors analyze neural networks operating under floating-point arithmetic, specifically examining the behavior when gradients are computed using automatic differentiation algorithm D^AD. They investigate whether such networks can approximate both function values and gradients simultaneously.

Result: 1) For any floating-point function φ, arbitrary function values and gradients can be represented by a floating-point network f and D^AD(φ∘f). 2) Given multiple functions φ₁,...,φₙ, D^AD(φᵢ∘f) can simultaneously represent arbitrary gradients while f represents target values (under mild conditions). These results hold for practical activation functions like ReLU, ELU, GELU, Swish, Sigmoid, and tanh.

Conclusion: The theoretical approximation capabilities of neural networks extend to practical floating-point implementations with automatic differentiation, providing formal justification for gradient-based methods in real-world applications.

Abstract: Theoretical studies show that for any differentiable function on a compact domain, there exists a neural network that approximates both the function values and gradients. However, such a result cannot be used in practice since it assumes real parameters and exact internal operations. In contrast, real implementations only use a finite subset of reals and machine operations with round-off errors. In this work, we investigate whether a similar result holds for neural networks under floating-point arithmetic, when the gradient with respect to the input is computed by the automatic differentiation algorithm $D^\mathtt{AD}$. We first show that given a floating-point function $φ$ (e.g., a loss function), arbitrary function values and gradients can be represented by a floating-point network $f$ and $D^\mathtt{AD}(φ\circ f)$, respectively. We further extend this result: given $φ_1,\dots,φ_n$, $D^\mathtt{AD}(φ_i\circ f)$ can simultaneously represent arbitrary gradients while $f$ represents the target values, under mild conditions. Our results hold for practical activation functions, e.g., $\mathrm{ReLU}$, $\mathrm{ELU}$, $\mathrm{GeLU}$, $\mathrm{Swish}$, $\mathrm{Sigmoid}$, and $\mathrm{tanh}$.

</details>


### [225] [CoAction: Cross-task Correlation-aware Pareto Set Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01712)
*Xinyue Chen,Yingxuan Liang,Yiqin Huang,Chikai Shang,Hai-Lin Liu,Fangqing Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new Pareto set learning framework called CoAction that uses task-aware transformers to handle multiple multi-objective optimization problems simultaneously, enabling knowledge sharing across tasks and reducing computational costs compared to single-task approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Pareto set learning methods only solve one multi-objective optimization problem at a time, which increases computational costs in multi-objective multitask scenarios and fails to leverage inter-task correlations. This limitation becomes particularly problematic when dealing with multiple related optimization tasks.

Method: CoAction framework uses a task-aware transformer architecture with task-specific embedding vectors to distinguish between different tasks while enabling knowledge sharing. The Transformer encoder backbone leverages self-attention mechanisms to capture complex task dependencies across multiple optimization problems.

Result: The approach was evaluated on comprehensive multitask test suites including both benchmark problems and real-world applications, demonstrating effectiveness and competitive performance across multiple metrics: Hypervolume, Range, and Sparsity.

Conclusion: CoAction successfully addresses the limitations of single-task Pareto set learning by enabling simultaneous multi-task optimization through cross-task correlation awareness, offering computational efficiency improvements and better performance by exploiting inter-task relationships.

Abstract: Pareto set learning (PSL) is an emerging paradigm in multi-objective optimization that trains neural networks to map preference vectors to Pareto optimal solutions. However, existing PSL methods primarily focus on solving a single multi-objective optimization problem at a time. This limitation not only increases computational costs in multi-objective multitask optimization scenarios by requiring a separate model for each task, but also fails to exploit the inter-task correlations across tasks. To address this, we propose a Cross-tAsk correlation-aware Pareto Set Learning (CoAction) framework, which leverages task-aware transformer to handle multiple tasks simultaneously. Specifically, by assigning task-specific embedding vectors to individual tasks, the model effectively distinguishes between tasks while facilitating knowledge sharing among them. We utilize a Transformer encoder as the backbone architecture to leverage its self-attention mechanism for capturing complex task dependencies. The proposed approach is evaluated on comprehensive multitask test suites covering both benchmark problems and real-world applications, demonstrating effectiveness and competitive performance in Hypervolume, Range, and Sparsity.

</details>


### [226] [Stable GFlowNets with Probabilistic Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01729)
*Zengxiang Lei,Ananth Shreekumar,Jonathan Rosenthal,Ruoyu Song,Alvaro A. Cardenas,Daniel J. Fremont,Dongyan Xu,Satish Ukkusuri,Z. Berkay Celik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GFlowNets often have unstable training; this paper shows loss-TV mismatch, provides loss-to-TV bounds for quality guarantees, and proposes Stable GFlowNets with better empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practical training of GFlowNets is often unstable with severe loss spikes and mode collapse, despite their theoretical promise. There's a need to understand why small distribution errors can still cause unbounded training loss and to develop more stable training methods.

Method: First, analyze sensitivity of GFlowNet objectives showing TV distance/reward mismatch. Then establish converse guarantees by deriving loss-to-TV bounds that ensure global fidelity from bounded trajectory balance losses. Finally, propose Stable GFlowNets algorithm leveraging these theoretical insights.

Result: Theoretical analysis reveals that small Total Variation distance between learned and target distributions doesn't guarantee bounded training loss. The paper provides loss-to-TV bounds that certify distributional fidelity. Empirical results show Stable GFlowNets achieve improved training stability and superior distributional fidelity compared to standard approaches.

Conclusion: Addressing the mismatch between loss minimization and distribution fidelity is crucial for GFlowNet stability. The proposed Stable GFlowNets algorithm, backed by theoretical guarantees, successfully stabilizes training and improves sampling quality, advancing practical deployment of GFlowNets.

Abstract: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) learn to sample states proportional to an unnormalized reward. Despite their theoretical promise, practical training is often unstable, exhibiting severe loss spikes and mode collapse. To tackle this, we first assess the sensitivity of GFlowNet objectives, demonstrating that a small Total Variation (TV) distance between the learned and target distributions does not preclude unbounded training loss. Motivated by this mismatch, we establish converse guarantees by deriving loss-to-TV bounds that certify global fidelity from bounded trajectory balance losses. Lastly, we propose Stable GFlowNets, an algorithm that leverages our theoretical results to stabilize training, and empirically demonstrate improved training behavior and superior distributional fidelity.

</details>


### [227] [Robust Linear Dueling Bandits with Post-serving Context under Unknown Delays and Adversarial Corruptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01752)
*Youngmin Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear dueling bandits algorithm for volatile environments with post-serving contexts, delayed feedback, and adversarial corruption, achieving near-optimal regret with additive rather than multiplicative cost structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world online learning systems face complex volatile environments characterized by three simultaneous challenges: (1) post-serving contexts (features observed after decision-making), (2) unknown stochastic or adversarial delays in feedback, and (3) adversarial corruption of observations. Prior works either don't handle all these challenges together or suffer from multiplicative degradation when combining corruption and delay effects.

Method: Proposed \term algorithm that: (1) integrates a learned approximator to predict post-serving contexts from pre-serving information, (2) employs adaptive weighting strategy that clips feature vectors to simultaneously mitigate corrupted and delayed observations, (3) uses delay-regime-agnostic design that works for both stochastic and adversarial delays.

Result: Achieves regret upper bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(d(\sqrt{T} + \mathcal{C} + \mathcal{D}))$, where $d$ is feature dimension, $\mathcal{C}$ is corruption budget, and $\mathcal{D}$ encapsulates delay complexity. The algorithm shows additive cost structure between corruption and delay (avoiding multiplicative degradation). Also establishes lower bounds that nearly match upper bounds up to $\sqrt{d}$ factor for adversarial delays without post-serving contexts.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithm effectively handles the triple challenge of post-serving contexts, delayed feedback, and adversarial corruption in linear dueling bandits. The analysis reveals fundamental additive structure between corruption and delay effects, providing improved theoretical guarantees compared to prior multiplicative degradation results, with near-optimal performance shown through matching lower bounds.

Abstract: We study linear dueling bandits in volatile environments characterized by the simultaneous presence of post-serving contexts, delayed feedback, and adversarial corruption. Feedback is subject to unknown stochastic or adversarial delays and a cumulative corruption budget $\mathcal{C}$. To address these challenges, we propose \term, which integrates a learned approximator that predicts post-serving contexts from pre-serving information. It further employs an adaptive weighting strategy that clips feature vectors to mitigate the impact of corrupted and delayed observations simultaneously. Under standard regularity conditions and a parametric post-serving mapping, we rigorously establish that our algorithm is delay-regime-agnostic, achieving a regret upper bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(d(\sqrt{T} + \mathcal{C} + \mathcal{D}))$, where $d$ is the total feature dimension and $\mathcal{D}$ encapsulates the delay complexity. Crucially, our analysis reveals an additive cost structure between corruption and delay, avoiding the multiplicative degradation typical of prior works. We further establish lower bounds that nearly match our upper bounds up to a $\sqrt{d}$ factor for adversarial delays in the absence of post-serving contexts.

</details>


### [228] [Mitigating Multimodal LLMs Hallucinations via Relevance Propagation at Inference Time](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01766)
*Itai Allouche,Joseph Keshet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LIME is a training-free framework that reduces hallucinations in multimodal LLMs by enhancing perceptual modality usage during inference through relevance-based updates to key-value representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs suffer from hallucinations due to an imbalance in modality utilization - textual tokens dominate over perceptual inputs during inference, causing models to rely on language priors rather than grounded evidence from vision/audio inputs.

Method: LIME (Learning Inference-time Modality Enhancement) is a training-free framework that uses Layer-wise Relevance Propagation to quantify token contributions and defines a relevance-based objective to promote perceptual input reliance. It enforces this through inference-time updates to key-value representations without modifying parameters or needing training data.

Result: Evaluation across multiple multimodal benchmarks in vision and audio domains shows consistent reductions in hallucinations and enhanced grounding while preserving generation quality. Analysis reveals increased modality contribution and more localized, semantically aligned relevance patterns.

Conclusion: LIME effectively addresses the hallucination problem in MLLMs by rebalancing modality utilization during inference, providing a training-free solution that enhances multimodal grounding without sacrificing generation quality.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have revolutionized the landscape of AI, demonstrating impressive capabilities in tackling complex vision and audio-language tasks. However, a critical challenge remains: these models often suffer from hallucinations, generating outputs that diverge from the provided perceptual inputs. This tendency stems from an inherent imbalance in modality utilization during inference, where the dominance of textual tokens undermines the potential of perceptual inputs. As a result, the model frequently resorts to textual language priors at the expense of grounded evidence. To tackle this issue, we propose Learning Inference-time Modality Enhancement (LIME), a training-free framework designed to bolster multimodal grounding by explicitly enhancing modality usage during decoding. LIME leverages Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) to quantify token-level contributions and defines a relevance-based objective that promotes increased reliance on perceptual inputs. This objective is enforced through inference-time updates to the model's key-value representations, without modifying model parameters or requiring additional training data. We evaluate LIME across multiple multimodal benchmarks in both vision and audio domains, demonstrating consistent reductions in hallucinations and enhanced grounding while preserving generation quality. Further analysis shows that LIME increases modality contribution and produces more localized and semantically aligned relevance patterns.

</details>


### [229] [Adversarial Imitation Learning with General Function Approximation: Theoretical Analysis and Practical Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01778)
*Tian Xu,Zhilong Zhang,Zexuan Chen,Ruishuo Chen,Yihao Sun,Yang Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel optimization-based adversarial imitation learning framework (OPT-AIL) achieves provable efficiency with general function approximation and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a significant gap between theoretical analyses of adversarial imitation learning (AIL) and practical implementations. Existing theoretical analyses are limited to simplified settings like tabular and linear approximations, while practical implementations use neural networks. Current theoretical approaches also involve complex algorithmic designs that hinder practical implementation.

Method: Proposes optimization-based AIL (OPT-AIL), which performs online optimization for reward learning combined with optimism-regularized optimization for policy learning. Develops two concrete methods: model-free OPT-AIL and model-based OPT-AIL, requiring only approximate optimization of two objectives.

Result: Both OPT-AIL variants achieve polynomial expert sample complexity and interaction complexity for learning near-expert policies under general function approximation. They represent the first provably efficient AIL methods with general function approximation. Empirical studies show OPT-AIL outperforms previous state-of-the-art deep AIL methods on challenging tasks.

Conclusion: OPT-AIL successfully bridges the theory-practice gap in adversarial imitation learning by providing theoretically grounded methods with practical implementation feasibility, achieving both theoretical guarantees and empirical superiority.

Abstract: Adversarial imitation learning (AIL), a prominent approach in imitation learning, has achieved significant practical success powered by neural network approximation. However, existing theoretical analyses of AIL are primarily confined to simplified settings, such as tabular and linear function approximation, and involve complex algorithmic designs that impede practical implementation. This creates a substantial gap between theory and practice. This paper bridges this gap by exploring the theoretical underpinnings of online AIL with general function approximation. We introduce a novel framework called optimization-based AIL (OPT-AIL), which performs online optimization for reward learning coupled with optimism-regularized optimization for policy learning. Within this framework, we develop two concrete methods: model-free OPT-AIL and model-based OPT-AIL. Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that both variants achieve polynomial expert sample complexity and interaction complexity for learning near-expert policies. To the best of our knowledge, they represent the first provably efficient AIL methods under general function approximation. From a practical standpoint, OPT-AIL requires only the approximate optimization of two objectives, thereby facilitating practical implementation. Empirical studies demonstrate that OPT-AIL outperforms previous state-of-the-art deep AIL methods across several challenging tasks.

</details>


### [230] [Beyond ECE: Calibrated Size Ratio, Risk Assessment, and Confidence-Weighted Metrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01796)
*Fernando Martin-Maroto,Nabil Abderrahaman,Gonzalo G. de Polavieja*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes new calibration metrics: Calibrated Size Ratio (CSR) to measure overconfidence risk, and confidence-weighted accuracy (cwA) to measure discriminative value, showing that ECE can be misleading and that confidence-weighting extends to all classification metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current confidence calibration is dominated by Expected Calibration Error (ECE), which has limitations: ECE can remain small even under arbitrarily large overconfidence risk, and it doesn't address whether assigned confidences actively distinguish correct from incorrect predictions (discriminative value).

Method: Proposes Calibrated Size Ratio (CSR) as an interpretable metric (equals 1 under perfect calibration) and derives risk probability P_risk for statistical evidence of overconfidence. Introduces confidence-weighted accuracy (cwA) as complement to measure discriminative value. Proves confidence-weighted AUC captures calibration information while classical AUC cannot. Validates indicators on synthetic confidence distributions with controlled calibration profiles and fifteen real datasets.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that CSR achieves near-perfect sensitivity and specificity across all tested conditions. Confidence-weighting extends to all standard classification metrics, with cwAUC capturing calibration information that classical AUC misses.

Conclusion: ECE is insufficient for assessing overconfidence risk. CSR provides better calibration assessment, and confidence-weighted metrics like cwA complement by measuring discriminative value, offering a more complete framework for evaluating confidence calibration in classification systems.

Abstract: Confidence calibration has been dominated by the Expected Calibration Error (ECE), a linear metric that counts calibration offset equally regardless of the confidence level at which it occurs. We show that ECE can remain small even under arbitrarily large overconfidence risk, so we propose Calibrated Size Ratio (CSR) instead, an interpretable metric that equals 1 under perfect calibration, from which we derive the risk probability $P_{\mathrm{risk}}$ that quantifies the statistical evidence for overconfidence. We further argue that overconfidence risk assessment must be complemented by a measure of discriminative value: whether the assigned confidences actively distinguish correct from incorrect predictions. We show that confidence-weighted accuracy $\mathrm{cwA}$ is the natural such complement, and that confidence-weighting extends to all standard classification metrics. In particular, we prove that the confidence-weighted AUC (cwAUC) captures the information about calibration while the classical AUC cannot. We validate the proposed indicators on several synthetic confidence distributions under multiple controlled calibration profiles and on fifteen real datasets with and without post-hoc calibration. Experiments demonstrate that CSR achieves near-perfect sensitivity and specificity across all tested conditions.

</details>


### [231] [Federated Semi-Supervised Graph Neural Networks with Prototype-Guided Pseudo-Labeling for Privacy-Preserving Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01810)
*G. Victor Daniela,A. Mallikarjuna Reddya,Uday Kumar Addankia,Sridhar Reddy Gogua,Sravanth Kumar Ramakuria*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedTGNN-SS: A federated semi-supervised framework using graph neural networks for gestational diabetes prediction with privacy protection and label scarcity handling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus requires early risk stratification, but real-world ML deployment faces two constraints: label scarcity (many EHR records lack diagnostic labels) and data privacy (prevents sharing patient data across hospitals).

Method: Each hospital builds local k-NN patient similarity graphs and trains topology-adaptive GNN encoders. The framework combines: 1) prototype-guided pseudo-labeling with neighborhood agreement, 2) adaptive graph refinement using learned embeddings, 3) clinical-aware consistency augmentation on continuous variables, and 4) privacy-safe prototype sharing of class-level centroids only.

Result: Across three diabetes datasets (GDM, Pima, Early Stage) with 10%-80% missing labels per hospital, FedTGNN-SS achieves 56 significant wins (p<0.05) against 11 federated baselines. It attains strong AUROC under extreme scarcity: Pima 0.8037 at 80% missing labels, Early Stage 0.9634 at 80% missing labels.

Conclusion: FedTGNN-SS effectively addresses both privacy constraints and label scarcity in clinical EHR data, enabling accurate gestational diabetes risk prediction while maintaining patient privacy through federated learning and semi-supervised techniques.

Abstract: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) is a high-prevalence pregnancy complication that requires accurate early risk stratification to reduce maternal and fetal morbidity. However, real-world clinical deployment of machine learning is hindered by two coupled constraints: (i) label scarcity, where a large fraction of electronic health records (EHR) lack confirmed diagnostic labels, and (ii) data privacy, which prevents sharing patient-level data across hospitals. This paper proposes FedTGNN-SS, a privacy-preserving federated semi-supervised framework for clinical tabular EHR. Each hospital builds a local k-nearest-neighbor patient similarity graph and trains a topology-adaptive GNN encoder. To robustly exploit unlabeled records, FedTGNN-SS combines (1) prototype-guided pseudo-labeling with neighborhood agreement, (2) adaptive graph refinement that periodically updates the k-NN graph using learned embeddings, (3) clinical-aware consistency augmentation applied only to continuous variables, and (4) privacy-safe prototype sharing that exchanges only class-level centroids. Across three diabetes-related datasets (GDM: N = 3,525; Pima: N = 768; Early Stage: N = 520) under 10\%-80\% missing labels per silo, FedTGNN-SS achieves 56 significant wins ($p < 0.05$) against 11 federated baselines and attains strong AUROC under extreme scarcity (Pima: 0.8037 at 80\% missing, Early Stage: 0.9634 at 80\% missing).

</details>


### [232] [Skipping the Zeros in Diffusion Models for Sparse Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01817)
*Phil Sidney Ostheimer,Mayank Nagda,Andriy Balinskyy,Gabriel Vicente Rodrigues,Jean Radig,Carl Herrmann,Stephan Mandt,Marius Kloft,Sophie Fellenz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sparsity-Exploiting Diffusion (SED) is a novel diffusion model designed specifically for sparse continuous data that preserves exact zeros and skips unnecessary computations on zero entries, improving efficiency and quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard diffusion models are designed for dense continuous data and fail to handle sparse continuous data properly. They don't model exact zeros that represent deliberate signal absence, which causes them to erase sparsity patterns and perform wasteful computation on mostly zero entries.

Method: SED (Sparsity-Exploiting Diffusion) models only non-zero values while preserving sparsity. It skips zeros during both training and inference phases, leading to computational savings while maintaining sparsity patterns that conventional diffusion models erase.

Result: SED achieves computational savings while maintaining or improving generation quality compared to conventional diffusion models. Across physics and biology benchmarks, SED matches or surpasses both conventional DMs and domain-specific baselines. Vision experiments provide intuitive insights into dense DM limitations and SED benefits.
