Conclusion: SED represents an important advancement for diffusion models on sparse continuous data, offering both efficiency gains and quality improvements by properly handling sparsity patterns that conventional diffusion models ignore.

Abstract: Diffusion models (DMs) excel on dense continuous data, but are not designed for sparse continuous data. They do not model exact zeros that represent the deliberate absence of a signal. As a result, they erase sparsity patterns and perform unnecessary computation on mostly zero entries. With Sparsity-Exploiting Diffusion (SED), we model only non-zero values, preserving sparsity. SED delivers computational savings while maintaining or improving generation quality by skipping zeros during training and inference. Across physics and biology benchmarks, SED matches or surpasses conventional DMs and domain-specific baselines, while vision experiments provide intuitive insights into the limitations of dense DMs and the benefits of SED.

</details>


### [233] [Molecular Representations for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01822)
*Nicholas T. Runcie,Fergus Imrie,Charlotte M. Deane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MolJSON, a new molecular representation format, outperforms traditional formats like SMILES and IUPAC names across multiple LLM reasoning tasks in chemistry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based chemistry systems primarily use SMILES strings or IUPAC names, but the suitability of these molecular representations hasn't been systematically assessed, despite their critical importance for reliable chemical reasoning.

Method: Introduced MolJSON as a novel molecular representation and systematically compared it with five common chemical formats. Evaluated using GPT-5-nano, GPT-5-mini, GPT-5, and Claude Haiku 4.5 on 78,045 questions across three task types: translation, shortest path, and constrained generation reasoning.

Result: MolJSON consistently outperformed existing formats across all tasks. For translation, GPT-5 achieved 71.0% accuracy converting IUPAC names to MolJSON vs 43.7% to SMILES. For constrained generation, GPT-5 reached 95.3% accuracy with MolJSON vs 76.3% for IUPAC and 64.0% for SMILES. For shortest-path reasoning, GPT-5 succeeded 98.5% with MolJSON vs 92.2% for SMILES and 82.7% for IUPAC. MolJSON was more robust to systematic errors related to atom count and ring complexity.

Conclusion: The choice of molecular representation significantly impacts LLM performance in chemistry. Explicit molecular graph schemas like MolJSON are a promising direction for LLM-based chemical systems, offering superior interpretability, generation accuracy, and reasoning efficiency.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to support scientific discovery. In chemistry, tasks such as reaction prediction and structure elucidation require reasoning about the structures of molecules. As such, LLM-based systems for chemistry must interact reliably with molecular structures. Most previous studies of LLMs in chemistry have used SMILES strings or IUPAC names as molecular representations; however, the suitability of these formats has not been systematically assessed. In this work, we introduce MolJSON, a novel molecular representation for LLMs, and systematically compare it with five common chemical formats. We evaluated each representation with GPT-5-nano, GPT-5-mini, GPT-5, and Claude Haiku 4.5 using a set of 78,045 questions spanning translation, shortest path, and constrained generation reasoning tasks. We observed substantial variation across representations in the ability of LLMs to interpret and generate molecular graphs, with MolJSON consistently outperforming existing formats. On translation tasks, GPT-5 achieved 71.0% accuracy when converting IUPAC names to MolJSON, compared with 43.7% when converting the same inputs to SMILES. For constrained generation, GPT-5 reached 95.3% accuracy generating MolJSON, compared with 76.3% for IUPAC and 64.0% for SMILES. As an input format for shortest-path reasoning, GPT-5 successfully answered 98.5% of questions with MolJSON, compared with 92.2% for SMILES and 82.7% for IUPAC, whilst also using fewer reasoning tokens. We observed systematic errors associated with atom count and ring complexity for SMILES strings and IUPAC names, whereas MolJSON was more robust to these failure modes. Our results show that the choice of molecular representation has a material impact on LLM performance, and that explicit molecular graph schemas, such as MolJSON, are a promising direction for LLM-based systems in chemistry.

</details>


### [234] [Selector-Guided Autonomous Curriculum for One-Shot Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01823)
*Rudray Dave,Vedang Dubey,Smit Deoghare,Sudhakar Mishra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGAC framework uses learnable selector with multi-dimensional features (output disagreement, success probability, semantic difficulty) instead of static reward variance heuristic to select optimal math problems for RLVR training, achieving state-of-the-art results on MATH benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods use heuristic instance selection based on reward variance, which is misleading for measuring transferability value. Need better method to select optimal training problems for improving LLM math reasoning.

Method: Proposed Selector-Guided Autonomous Curriculum (SGAC) with learnable selector model using multi-dimensional features: success probability, reward variance, output disagreement (entropy), and semantic difficulty level. Uses autonomous curriculum algorithm to rank candidate problems from large pool and run micro-bursts of 1-shot GRPO training.

Result: Achieved 68.0% accuracy on Hendrycks MATH benchmark using Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B baseline, outperforming state-of-the-art model (64.0%) and 1-shot RLVR checkpoint (66.0%). Found output disagreement (entropy) is strongest predictor of reasoning gains rather than reward variance.

Conclusion: Entropy-based intelligent data curation leads to better reasoning improvement over static training methods, especially in data-limited conditions. Multi-dimensional feature selection with learnable selector outperforms single heuristic approaches.

Abstract: Recently, Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has been established as a highly effective technique for augmenting the math reasoning skills of Large Language Models (LLMs) based on a single instance. Current state-of-the-art 1-shot RLVR models adopt heuristics for selecting instances, mostly based on historical variance in rewards, which we find to be inherently misleading as a measure of transferability value. In this paper, we propose a Selector-Guided Autonomous Curriculum (SGAC) approach, which employs a learnable selector model on a multi-dimensional feature space consisting of success probability, reward variance, output disagreement (entropy), and semantic difficulty level, instead of the static reward variance heuristic. In our empirical evaluation on pools of candidate problems, we observed that output disagreement, rather than reward variance, is the strongest predictor of reasoning gains in subsequent iterations. Leveraging this finding, we develop an autonomous curriculum algorithm for dynamically siphoning candidate problems from a large pool, ranking them by the learned selector, and running micro-bursts of 1-shot GRPO. Our framework is evaluated using the Hendrycks MATH benchmark, with the Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B model serving as the baseline. Our framework obtains an accuracy of 68.0\% on the hold-out dataset, which is better than the accuracy obtained from the state-of-the-art model, 64.0\%, as well as the 1-shot RLVR checkpoint proposed by Wang et al., which achieved an accuracy of 66.0\%. The results confirm that entropy-based intelligent data curation leads to strict reasoning improvement over static training methods, particularly in severely limited data conditions.

</details>


### [235] [Learning Koopman operators for coupled systems via information on governing equations of subsystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01835)
*Tatsuya Naoi,Jun Ohkubo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a method to learn Koopman operators for coupled systems by incorporating governing differential equations, addressing instability and inaccuracy issues of purely data-driven methods like EDMD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear coupled systems are challenging to analyze due to high dimensionality and complex interactions. Existing methods like Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) are purely data-driven and can be unstable/inaccurate for coupled systems with limited data.

Method: Develops a method to learn the Koopman operator for coupled systems by incorporating the differential equations governing each subsystem (rather than purely data-driven approach like EDMD).

Result: Demonstrated effectiveness through numerical experiments on coupled oscillator systems, showing improved stability and accuracy compared to purely data-driven methods.

Conclusion: Incorporating subsystem governing equations into Koopman operator learning provides a more robust and accurate framework for analyzing coupled nonlinear systems, especially when data availability is limited.

Abstract: Nonlinear coupled systems are ubiquitous in science and engineering. The analysis and modeling of such systems is challenging due to their high dimensionality and complex interactions among subsystems. In recent years, operator-theoretic methods based on the Koopman operator have attracted attention as a powerful tool for analyzing and modeling nonlinear dynamical systems. Extended dynamic mode decomposition (EDMD) is one of the most popular methods to approximate the Koopman operator. However, EDMD is a purely data-driven method, and it could be unstable and inaccurate for coupled systems under limited data availability. In this paper, we propose a method to learn the Koopman operator for coupled systems using the differential equations governing each subsystem. We also demonstrate its effectiveness through numerical experiments on coupled oscillator systems.

</details>


### [236] [QHyer: Q-conditioned Hybrid Attention-mamba Transformer for Offline Goal-conditioned RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01862)
*Xing Lei,Jincheng Wang,Xuetao Zhang,Donglin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QHyer: A hybrid offline goal-conditioned RL method with flow-based Q-estimators and gated attention-mamba architecture for handling partially observable, temporally heterogeneous datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world offline GCRL datasets are partially observable and history-dependent, mixing Markovian and non-Markovian structures that violate standard RL assumptions. Existing approaches like pure attention models are inefficient for handling local Markovian structure and long-range context simultaneously, while fixed-window hybrid architectures cannot adapt to varying dependency lengths. Additionally, sparse rewards make return-to-go (RTG) non-discriminative, providing poor guidance for stitching goal-reaching behaviors across demonstrations.

Method: 1) Replace RTG with a flow-parameterized, state-conditioned goal-reaching Q-estimator to enable better stitching across demonstrations. 2) Introduce a gated Hybrid Attention-Mamba backbone that performs content-adaptive history compression while preserving local dynamics, overcoming limitations of fixed-window extraction.

Result: QHyer achieves state-of-the-art performance on both non-Markovian and Markovian datasets, validating its effectiveness across diverse scenarios with temporally heterogeneous dependencies.

Conclusion: The proposed QHyer framework effectively addresses key challenges in offline GCRL by combining flow-based Q-estimation for reward guidance with adaptive hybrid architectures for history compression, demonstrating superior performance in handling real-world partially observable, temporally heterogeneous datasets.

Abstract: Offline goal-conditioned RL (GCRL) learns goal-reaching policies from static datasets, but real-world datasets are often partially observable and history-dependent, exhibiting a mix of Markovian and non-Markovian that violate standard RL assumptions. History-aware sequence models such as Decision Transformer (DT) are a natural fit for long-term dependency modeling, yet pure attention is inefficient and brittle when handling local Markovian structure and long-range context simultaneously. Although recent hybrid architectures (e.g., LSDT) introduce local extractors to improve local dependencies modeling, the fixed-window extraction cannot adapt its effective memory to varying dependency lengths in temporally heterogeneous settings, often truncating long-range context rather than compressing its content adaptively. Moreover, sequential offline GCRL faces a key bottleneck: under sparse rewards, return-to-go (RTG) becomes non-discriminative across sub-trajectories, providing little guidance signal for stitching goal-reaching behaviors from diverse demonstrations. To address these, we propose \textbf{QHyer}, which replaces RTG with a flow-parameterized, state-conditioned goal-reaching Q-estimator to support stitching across demonstrations, and introduces a gated Hybrid Attention-Mamba backbone that performs content-adaptive history compression while preserving local dynamics. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \textbf{QHyer} achieves state-of-the-art performance on both non-Markovian and Markovian datasets, validating its effectiveness for diverse scenarios.

</details>


### [237] [Robust Conditional Conformal Prediction via Branched Normalizing Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01868)
*Rui Xu,Xingyuan Chen,Wenxing Huang,Minxuan Huang,Weiyan Chen,Sihong Xie,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conformal prediction under distribution shift fails to guarantee conditional coverage; proposed Branched Normalizing Flow improves conditional coverage robustness via distribution alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing conformal prediction methods only guarantee marginal coverage under distribution shift, not conditional coverage at individual test inputs, making predictions unreliable in regions with mismatched conditional score distributions.

Method: Proposed Branched Normalizing Flow (BNF) - a two-branch architecture that normalizes test inputs to calibration distribution and transforms prediction sets back to test distribution while preserving conditional guarantees, based on Wasserstein distance analysis of conditional invalidity.

Result: Empirical results show BNF consistently improves conditional coverage robustness across nine datasets and wide range of confidence levels.

Conclusion: BNF architecture effectively addresses conditional coverage degradation under distribution shift by leveraging invertible transport mechanisms to align distributions while maintaining conditional guarantees.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) constructs prediction sets with marginal coverage guarantees under the assumption that the calibration and test distributions are identical. However, under distribution shift, existing approaches primarily align marginal conformal score distributions, which is sufficient to preserve marginal coverage but does not control the conditional coverage error at individual test inputs. As a consequence, CP can remain unreliable in regions where the conditional score distributions are mismatched. In this work, we bound the conditional invalidity of CP under distribution shift in terms of the Wasserstein distance between the calibration and test distributions. This result highlights the role of invertible transport in mitigating conditional coverage degradation. Motivated by this insight, we introduce Branched Normalizing Flow (BNF), a two-branch architecture that normalizes a test input to the calibration distribution and transforms the prediction set of the normalized input back to the test distribution while preserving conditional guarantees. Empirically, BNF consistently improves conditional coverage robustness on nine datasets across a wide range of confidence levels.

</details>


### [238] [Leveraging Data Symmetries to Select an Optimal Subset of Training Data under Label Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01874)
*Kumar Shubham,Pavan Karjol,Kiran M K,Prathosh AP*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that data invariance/symmetry knowledge can enhance k-NN performance in noisy high-dimensional datasets, improving subset selection methods like cutstats for near-noise-free training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning models need large labeled datasets, but real-world data often contains label noise. While methods like cutstats use k-NN to identify low-noise subsets, their effectiveness in high-dimensional data remains unclear and needs improvement.

Method: The study formally analyzes the relationship between classifier performance on cutstats-selected subsets and k-NN accuracy. It proposes exploiting data invariance and underlying symmetries to enhance k-NN performance, making it closer to Bayes optimal. For real-world scenarios with partial invariance knowledge, the method uses learnt invariant representations.

Result: Formal proof that classifier performance on cutstats-selected subsets depends on k-NN accuracy. Demonstrated that exploiting data invariance significantly improves k-NN performance in noisy high-dimensional settings. Showed that learnt invariant representations can identify near-optimal subsets even with partial invariance knowledge.

Conclusion: Leveraging data invariance and symmetry knowledge can substantially enhance k-NN-based subset selection methods like cutstats, enabling near-noise-free training performance even in challenging high-dimensional, noisy datasets where full invariance knowledge isn't available.

Abstract: The performance of machine learning models often relies on large labeled datasets; however, data collected from diverse sources can contain label noise. Recent work has shown that, in noisy settings, there may exist a subset of the training data on which models can achieve performance comparable to training on a noise-free dataset. A widely used method for identifying such subsets is cutstats, which employs k-nearest neighbors (k-NN) to detect low-noise samples. However, its performance on high-dimensional data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we formally establish that the performance of a classifier trained on a subset of a noisy dataset selected via cutstats is influenced by the accuracy of k-NN. We further demonstrate that, in noisy environments, exploiting data invariance and knowledge of underlying symmetries can significantly enhance the performance of k-NN, bringing it closer to the Bayes optimal classifier even in high-dimensional regimes. Finally, we show that for real-world scenarios, where information about the underlying invariance is only partially known, learnt invariant representations can still facilitate the identification of near-optimal subsets.

</details>


### [239] [How Label Imbalance Shapes Geometry: A General Spectral Analysis of Multi-Label Neural Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01897)
*Xiaoxuan Ma,Yixuan Yang,Song Li,Xiangyun Hui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends Neural Collapse to multi-label classification, showing that tag-wise averaging only occurs under special conditions, and introduces a spectral-control framework that accounts for label correlations and data imbalance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current understanding of Neural Collapse in multi-label classification relies on assumptions of label balance and combinatorial symmetry, which fail to account for practical realities like intrinsic label correlations and data imbalance. The authors aim to resolve the multiplicity-one imbalance conjecture and develop a more general framework.

Method: The authors propose a rigorous spectral-control framework to analyze the terminal phase of multi-label learning under general imbalanced conditions. They introduce the label covariance spectrum κₘ as a scalar controlling distribution-dependent lower-bound geometry, derived from the second-order moment matrix of the label distribution.

Result: The paper resolves the multiplicity-one imbalance conjecture, showing that higher-multiplicity prototypes obey class-frequency-weighted synthesis rather than uniform averaging. It demonstrates that tag-wise averaging only emerges under perfect orthogonality as a special case, and that the centered label covariance spectrum controls terminal geometry stability.

Conclusion: This work resolves the scaled-average aspect of the imbalance conjecture and establishes a unifying theoretical framework that extends Neural Collapse to complex, imbalanced multi-label settings, providing a more realistic analysis of multi-label classification geometry.

Abstract: This work investigates the phenomenon of Neural Collapse (NC) in multi-label classification, extending its conceptual framework from multi-class learning to general correlated and imbalanced multi-label settings. Although recent studies have identified a ''tag-wise averaging'' structure for multi-label features, this view relies on implicit assumptions of label balance and combinatorial symmetry. Consequently, it fails to account for the geometrical distortions caused by intrinsic label correlations and data imbalance, which are common in practice. We resolve the multiplicity-one imbalance conjecture raised by Li et al. (2024), showing that higher-multiplicity prototypes obey a class-frequency-weighted synthesis rule rather than uniform averaging. To address this, we propose a rigorous spectral-control framework to analyze the terminal phase of multi-label learning under general imbalanced conditions. We introduce the label covariance spectrum $κ_m$, a scalar controlling the distribution-dependent lower-bound geometry, derived from the second-order moment matrix of the label distribution. Contrary to the averaging perspective, our analysis reveals that the centered label covariance spectrum controls the stability of terminal geometry by quantifying the weakest centered inter-class contrast directions. We prove that the classical Tag-wise Averaging emerges only as a special case under perfect orthogonality. Numerical experiments on synthetic distributions validate our theoretical bounds. This work resolves the scaled-average aspect of the imbalance conjecture and establishes a unifying theoretical framework that extends Neural Collapse to complex, imbalanced multi-label settings.

</details>


### [240] [Stochastic Sparse Attention for Memory-Bound Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01910)
*Kyle Lee,Corentin Delacour,Kevin Callahan-Coray,Kyle Jiang,Can Yaras,Samet Oymak,Tathagata Srimani,Kerem Y. Camsari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SANTA introduces stochastic sampling techniques to sparsify attention computation by sampling values from KV cache and using stochastic ternary queries, achieving 1.5× speedup while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding becomes bandwidth-limited at long contexts because generating each token requires reading all key-value vectors from KV cache, creating computational bottlenecks.

Method: 1) SANTA sparsifies value-cache access by sampling S ≪ nₖ indices from post-softmax distribution and aggregating only those value rows (unbiased estimator). 2) Uses stratified sampling for variance reduction. 3) Proposes Bernoulli qK^T sampling to sparsify score stage with stochastic ternary queries.

Result: Achieves 1.5× decode-step attention kernel speedup over FlashInfer and FlashDecoding on NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada while matching baseline accuracy at 32k-token contexts.

Conclusion: These methods are orthogonal to existing techniques and point toward sparse, multiplier-free, energy-efficient inference for large language models at long contexts.

Abstract: Autoregressive decoding becomes bandwidth-limited at long contexts, as generating each token requires reading all $n_k$ key and value vectors from KV cache. We present Stochastic Additive No-mulT Attention (SANTA), a method that sparsifies value-cache access by sampling $S \ll n_k$ indices from the post-softmax distribution and aggregates only those value rows. This yields an unbiased estimator of the post-softmax value aggregation while replacing value-stage multiply-accumulates with gather-and-add. We introduce stratified sampling to design variance-reduced, GPU-friendly variants, demonstrating $1.5\times$ decode-step attention kernel speedup over FlashInfer and FlashDecoding on an NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada while matching baseline accuracy at 32k-token contexts. Finally, we propose Bernoulli $qK^\mathsf{T}$ sampling as a complementary technique to sparsify the score stage, reducing key-feature access through stochastic ternary queries. Both methods are orthogonal to upstream techniques such as ternary quantization, low-rank projections, and KV-cache compression. Together, they point toward sparse, multiplier-free, and energy-efficient inference. We open-source our kernels at: https://github.com/OPUSLab/SANTA.git

</details>


### [241] [RefusalGuard: Geometry-Preserving Fine-Tuning for Safety in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01913)
*Sadia Asif,Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REFUSALGUARD prevents safety degradation during fine-tuning by preserving safety-relevant representations while allowing task-specific learning, maintaining refusal behavior without compromising utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-aligned language models lose their refusal capabilities when fine-tuned for downstream tasks, becoming vulnerable to adversarial misuse. The mechanisms behind this alignment degradation are not well understood at the representation level.

Method: Analyzed representation-level changes during fine-tuning, discovered systematic drift in safety features, geometric distortion, and task-safety interference. Developed REFUSALGUARD framework that constrains updates in hidden representation space to preserve safety-mediating components while allowing task-specific learning in complementary directions.

Result: Evaluated across LLaMA, Gemma, Qwen models on AdvBench, DirectHarm4, JailbreakBench safety benchmarks. REFUSALGUARD achieved attack success rates comparable to base safety-aligned models while maintaining competitive task performance, significantly outperforming baselines.

Conclusion: Representation-level analysis reveals systematic drift as key cause of safety degradation during fine-tuning. REFUSALGUARD effectively preserves safety by controlling representation updates, maintaining both safety alignment and task utility across diverse model families.

Abstract: Fine-tuning safety-aligned language models for downstream tasks often leads to substantial degradation of refusal behavior, making models vulnerable to adversarial misuse. While prior work has shown that safety-relevant features are encoded in structured representations within the model's activation space, how these representations change during fine-tuning and why alignment degrades remains poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the representation-level mechanisms underlying alignment degradation. Our analysis shows that standard fine-tuning induces systematic drift in safety-relevant representations, distorts their geometric structure, and introduces interference between task optimization and safety features. These effects collectively lead to increased harmful compliance. Motivated by these findings, we introduce REFUSALGUARD, a representation-level fine-tuning framework that preserves safety-relevant structure during model adaptation. Our approach constrains updates in hidden representation space, ensuring that safety-mediating components remain stable while allowing task-specific learning in complementary directions. We evaluate REFUSALGUARD across multiple model families, including LLaMA, Gemma, and Qwen, on adversarial safety benchmarks such as AdvBench, DirectHarm4, and JailbreakBench, as well as downstream utility tasks. Our approach achieves attack success rates comparable to base safety-aligned models while maintaining competitive task performance, significantly outperforming baselines.

</details>


### [242] [Deep learning-based pavement performance modeling using multiple distress indicators and road work history](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01914)
*Lu Gao,Zhe Han,Yunshen Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep neural networks (CNN and LSTM) outperform standard machine learning models in predicting pavement deterioration using 18 years of Texas DOT data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pavement deterioration is complex and dynamic, influenced by material, environment, design, and unobserved variables. Accurate predictions help pavement management agencies optimize resource allocation through better-coordinated preservation and maintenance activities.

Method: Used deep neural networks - convolutional neural network (CNN) and long short-term memory (LSTM) - to model pavement deterioration. Leveraged 18 years of pavement condition data and maintenance/rehabilitation history from Texas Department of Transportation, including 21 flexible pavement condition indicators (cracking, rutting, raveling, roughness) from over 100,000 pavement sections.

Result: Promising preliminary results obtained. Case study shows that the proposed CNN model outperforms standard machine learning models in predicting pavement condition values.

Conclusion: Deep neural networks, particularly CNN, show superior performance for pavement condition prediction compared to standard machine learning methods, offering potential for more effective pavement management and resource optimization.

Abstract: The deterioration of pavement is a complex and dynamic process determined by different factors including material, environment, design, and some other unobserved variables. Accurate predictions of pavement condition can help maximize the use of available resources for pavement management agencies through better coordinated preservation and maintenance activities. This paper uses deep neural networks such as the convolutional neural network (CNN) and the long short-term memory (LSTM) to model the pavement deterioration process. In this paper, pavement condition data and maintenance and rehabilitation history collected by the Texas Department of Transportation over the past 18 years were used. Twenty-one flexible pavement condition indicators, including cracking, rutting, raveling, and roughness, collected from more than 100,000 pavement sections were included in the proposed models. Promising preliminary results were obtained. Case study results show that the proposed CNN model outperforms standard machine learning models in predicting pavement condition values.

</details>


### [243] [Training Non-Differentiable Networks via Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01928)
*An T. Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolyStep is a gradient-free optimizer that trains non-differentiable neural networks using only forward passes, achieving near gradient-based performance on spiking networks, quantized layers, and other discrete architectures where backpropagation fails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural networks increasingly incorporate non-differentiable components (spiking neurons, quantized layers, discrete routing, etc.) where traditional backpropagation is inapplicable and surrogate gradients introduce bias. Current gradient-free methods often collapse to near-random accuracy on these genuinely non-differentiable models.

Method: PolyStep evaluates loss at structured polytope vertices in a compressed subspace, computes softmax-weighted assignments over the resulting cost matrix, and displaces parameters toward low-cost vertices via barycentric projection. This corresponds to the one-sided limit of a regularized optimal-transport problem, inheriting geometric structure without Sinkhorn iterations.

Result: On hard-LIF spiking networks: 93.4% test accuracy, outperforming gradient-free baselines by over 60 percentage points, within 4.4pp of surrogate-gradient Adam. Leads all gradient-free competitors on four additional non-differentiable architectures. On MAX-SAT scaling: sustains above 92% clause satisfaction while evolution strategies drop 8-12pp. On RL: matches OpenAI-ES and retains performance under quantization that collapses gradient-based methods.

Conclusion: PolyStep effectively trains genuinely non-differentiable models where existing methods fail, with theoretical convergence guarantees matching known zeroth-order query-complexity lower bounds, enabling broader adoption of non-differentiable components in neural architectures.

Abstract: Neural networks increasingly embed non-differentiable components (spiking neurons, quantized layers, discrete routing, blackbox simulators, etc.) where backpropagation is inapplicable and surrogate gradients introduce bias. We present PolyStep, a gradient-free optimizer that updates parameters using only forward passes. Each step evaluates the loss at structured polytope vertices in a compressed subspace, computes softmax-weighted assignments over the resulting cost matrix, and displaces particles toward low-cost vertices via barycentric projection. This update corresponds to the one-sided limit of a regularized optimal-transport problem, inheriting its geometric structure without Sinkhorn iterations.
  PolyStep trains genuinely non-differentiable models where existing gradient-free methods collapse to near-random accuracy. On hard-LIF spiking networks we reach 93.4% test accuracy, outperforming all gradient-free baselines by over 60~pp and closing to within 4.4~pp of a surrogate-gradient Adam ceiling. Across four additional non-differentiable architectures (int8 quantization, argmax attention, staircase activations, hard MoE routing) we lead every gradient-free competitor. On MAX-SAT scaling from 100 to 1M variables, we sustain above 92% clause satisfaction while evolution strategies drop 8--12~pp. On RL policy search, we match OpenAI-ES on classical control and retain performance under integer and binary quantization that collapses gradient-based methods. We prove convergence to conservative-stationary points at rate $O(\log T/\sqrt{T})$ on piecewise-smooth losses, upgraded to Clarke-stationary on the headline architectures and extended to the piecewise-constant regime via a hitting-time bound. These rates match the known zeroth-order query-complexity lower bounds that all forward-only methods inherit. Code is available at https://github.com/anindex/polystep.

</details>


### [244] [Pandora's Regret: A Proper Scoring Rule for Evaluating Sequential Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01936)
*Gerardo A. Flores,Yash Deshpande,Jannis R. Brea,Ashia C. Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pandora's Regret is a new strictly proper scoring rule for multiclass classification that aligns with sequential search utility, penalizes rank-reversing errors, and better predicts clinical diagnostic costs than standard metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard proper scoring rules like log loss are local and ignore ranking of competitors, misaligning model evaluation with sequential search utility where alternatives are tested until the true class is found.

Method: Analyze expected cost of optimal search under varying testing costs, derive Pandora's Regret - a closed-form, pairwise-additive strictly proper scoring rule that elicits true probabilities and penalizes rank-reversing miscalibrations.

Result: Construct a one-parameter Beta family balancing penalties for rank-swapping versus probability magnitude, showing that log loss, accuracy, and macro-F1 rely on implicit decision models misaligned with sequential search. On 597 MedMNIST models, Pandora-based metrics better predict clinical diagnostic costs.

Conclusion: Pandora's Regret provides a decision-theoretic scoring rule construction for multiclass settings that aligns model evaluation with sequential search utility, overcoming limitations of standard local scoring rules.

Abstract: In sequential search, alternatives are tested until the true class is found. Standard proper scoring rules like log loss are local, ignoring the ranking of competitors and misaligning model evaluation with search utility. We show that sequential search induces a pairwise structure that overcomes this. By analyzing the expected cost of optimal search under varying testing costs, we derive Pandora's Regret: a closed-form, pairwise-additive, and strictly proper scoring rule. Pandora's Regret both elicits true probabilities and penalizes rank-reversing miscalibrations where distractors outrank the true class. Our construction yields a one-parameter Beta family that balances penalties for rank-swapping versus probability magnitude, while retaining a grounded interpretation as expected search cost. We prove that log loss, accuracy, and macro-F1 rely on implicit decision models misaligned with sequential search. Across 597 MedMNIST models, Pandora-based metrics better predict clinical diagnostic costs than standard alternatives, extending decision-theoretic scoring rule construction to the multiclass setting.

</details>


### [245] [PepSpecBench: A Unified Evaluation Benchmark for Peptide Tandem Mass Spectrometry Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01945)
*Zhiwen Yang,Pan Liu,Yifan Li,Yunhua Zhong,Jun Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PepSpecBench is a unified benchmark for peptide MS/MS spectrum prediction that addresses three major evaluation challenges in computational proteomics through standardized data preprocessing, strict data splitting to prevent sequence leakage, and comprehensive multi-species evaluation with robustness testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning models for predicting peptide MS/MS spectra suffer from three critical evaluation problems: 1) inconsistent data preprocessing and incompatible model outputs hindering fair comparison, 2) flawed data splitting allowing hidden sequence leakage that inflates performance, and 3) lack of comprehensive cross-species benchmarking and systematic assessment of model robustness to experimental conditions.

Method: The authors propose PepSpecBench, which standardizes data preprocessing across complementary public datasets, enforces a strict backbone-disjoint splitting strategy to eliminate sequence leakage, evaluates diverse architectures within a shared fragment-ion representation space, introduces comprehensive multi-species evaluation, and uses physically grounded metadata perturbation probes to assess model robustness and instrument awareness.

Result: PepSpecBench uncovered previously unrecognized performance discrepancies and robustness limitations across six representative models, providing actionable insights for future model design, evaluation, and practical deployment in proteomics.

Conclusion: PepSpecBench addresses critical evaluation challenges in peptide MS/MS spectrum prediction, enabling fair model comparisons, preventing data leakage, and providing comprehensive robustness assessment, which should improve the reliability and practical utility of computational proteomics models.

Abstract: Tandem mass spectrometry provides a high-throughput framework for identifying and quantifying proteins in complex biological samples. In computational proteomics, predicting peptide MS/MS spectra is a critical task, enabling downstream applications such as large-scale peptide identification and quantification. While deep learning architectures have substantially improved prediction accuracy, three evaluation challenges obscure the true progress of the field. First, inconsistent data preprocessing and incompatible model output spaces hinder fair model comparison. Second, flawed data splitting strategies can permit hidden sequence leakage and inflate reported performance. Third, existing evaluations typically lack comprehensive cross-species benchmarking and systematic assessment of model robustness to influential experimental conditions. To address these challenges, we propose PepSpecBench, a unified benchmark for peptide MS/MS spectrum prediction. PepSpecBench standardizes data preprocessing across complementary public datasets, enforces a strict backbone-disjoint splitting strategy to eliminate sequence leakage, and evaluates diverse architectures within a shared fragment-ion representation space. It further introduces a comprehensive multi-species evaluation suite and physically grounded metadata perturbation probes to assess model robustness and instrument awareness. We uncover previously unrecognized performance discrepancies and robustness limitations across six representative models, providing actionable insights for future model design, evaluation and practical deployment.

</details>


### [246] [TRAP: Tail-aware Ranking Attack for World-Model Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01950)
*Siyuan Duan,Ke Zhang,Xizhao Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRAP: A backdoor attack targeting world models' trajectory ranking to hijack planning by disrupting critical imagined trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World models enable long-horizon planning through imagination but introduce new security risks. Existing backdoor attacks target local features or single-step predictions, which are ineffective against world models because their dynamics prior and planning process can absorb shallow perturbations. The paper identifies that world models have a distinct vulnerability in their long-tailed ranking structure of imagined trajectories.

Method: Proposes TRAP (Trajectory Ranking Attack), which combines: 1) Tail-aware ranking loss to focus optimization on decision-critical trajectories, 2) Dual gating mechanisms that stabilize optimization and regulate when/where the attack penalty is applied. Under trigger conditions, TRAP alters the relative ranking of imagined trajectories to redirect planning outcomes while maintaining normal ranking on clean inputs.

Result: Experiments on DreamerV3 and TD-MPC2 across diverse tasks show TRAP consistently induces sustained behavioral deviations and significant performance degradation. The attack demonstrates effectiveness across different world model architectures and task domains.

Conclusion: World models exhibit unique backdoor vulnerabilities through their trajectory ranking structure. TRAP successfully exploits this vulnerability, highlighting the need for dedicated security evaluation of world-model-based agents. The research reveals that imagination-driven decision processes create new attack surfaces that differ from traditional reactive models.

Abstract: World models enable long-horizon planning by internally generating and evaluating imagined trajectories, making them a promising foundation for generalist agents. However, this imagination-driven decision process also introduces new security risks. Existing backdoor attacks typically aim to manipulate local features, one-step predictions, or instantaneous policy outputs. While such objectives may suffice for weaker reactive models, they are often ineffective against world models, where the learned dynamics prior and planning process can absorb or wash out the effects of shallow perturbations. More importantly, we find that world models exhibit a distinct backdoor vulnerability rooted in the long-tailed ranking structure of imagined trajectories, where disrupting the ordering of a few decision-critical trajectories can systematically hijack planning.
  To exploit this vulnerability, we propose TRAP, a backdoor attack framework for world models that targets imagined trajectory ranking. TRAP combines a tail-aware ranking loss to focus optimization on decision-critical trajectories with dual gating mechanisms that stabilize optimization and regulate when and where the attack penalty is applied. Under trigger conditions, TRAP alters the relative ranking of imagined trajectories to redirect planning outcomes, while largely maintaining the normal ranking structure on clean inputs. Experiments on DreamerV3 and TD-MPC2 across diverse tasks show that TRAP consistently induces sustained behavioral deviations and significant performance degradation, highlighting the need for dedicated security evaluation of world-model-based agents.

</details>


### [247] [Flexi-LoRA with Input-Adaptive Ranks: Efficient Finetuning for Speech and Reasoning Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01959)
*Zongqian Li,Yixuan Su,Han Zhou,Zihao Fu,Nigel Collier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flexi-LoRA dynamically adjusts LoRA ranks based on input complexity during training and inference, outperforming static LoRA with fewer parameters, especially on reasoning-intensive tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static parameter allocation in LoRA is suboptimal for inputs of varying complexity, limiting efficiency and performance for different types of tasks with varying reasoning requirements.

Method: Flexi-LoRA framework that dynamically adjusts LoRA ranks based on input complexity, maintaining consistency between training and inference dynamics across question answering, mathematical reasoning, and speech tasks.

Result: Achieves higher performance with fewer parameters than static LoRA, with gains most pronounced on tasks requiring strict reasoning chains. Mathematical reasoning tasks show higher dependency on rank dynamics than QA tasks.

Conclusion: Input-dependent parameter allocation is effective for efficient fine-tuning, achieving mixture-of-experts benefits through streamlined implementation, establishing basis for future input-adaptive approaches.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) have become essential for deploying large language models, yet their static parameter allocation remains suboptimal for inputs of varying complexity. We present Flexi-LoRA, a novel framework that dynamically adjusts LoRA ranks based on input complexity during both training and inference. Through empirical analysis across question answering, mathematical reasoning, and speech tasks, we demonstrate that maintaining consistency between training and inference dynamics is important for effective adaptation, particularly for sequential reasoning tasks. Our findings reveal that input-dependent parameter allocation achieves higher performance with fewer parameters by optimally matching rank configurations to question complexity. Furthermore, task-specific dependency on rank dynamics varies, with mathematical reasoning tasks exhibiting higher dependency than QA tasks. Successful adaptation manifests not only in correctness but also in reasoning quality and instruction adherence. Flexi-LoRA consistently outperforms static LoRA while using fewer parameters, with performance gains more pronounced on tasks requiring strict reasoning chains. Our approach realizes key benefits of mixture-of-experts frameworks through a more streamlined implementation, reducing parameter redundancy while improving model capabilities. We provide comprehensive empirical studies across diverse tasks, establishing a basis for future work in input-adaptive and efficient fine-tuning approaches.

</details>


### [248] [Multi-User Dueling Bandits: A Fair Approach using Nash Social Welfare](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01961)
*Maheed H. Ahmed,Mahsa Ghasemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces fairness considerations in dueling bandits by addressing heterogeneous user preferences through Nash Social Welfare optimization and develops algorithms with matching regret bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional preference learning methods aggregate average preferences across all users, which can be unfair to minority groups when preferences vary significantly. The authors aim to address fairness concerns in online learning from preference data, specifically in the dueling bandits framework.

Method: The authors propose a fair dueling bandits framework where each user has a potentially distinct Condorcet winner. They adopt Nash Social Welfare objective to penalize inequality and prevent marginalization of any single user. They develop Fair-Explore-Then-Commit and Fair-ε-Greedy algorithms with a Condorcet winner identification phase.

Result: Established a regret lower bound of Ω(T^{2/3}min(K,D)^{1/3}) for time horizon T, K arms, and D users - the first result quantifying fairness cost in dueling bandits with heterogeneous preferences. Derived matching regret upper bounds for their algorithms up to logarithmic factors.

Conclusion: The work provides a principled approach to fairness in preference learning, establishes fundamental limits on fair regret minimization, and offers practical algorithms with optimal theoretical guarantees for fair dueling bandits with heterogeneous user preferences.

Abstract: Learning from human preference data is becoming a useful tool, from fine-tuning large language models to training reinforcement learning agents. However, in most scenarios, the model is trained on the average preference of all human evaluators, which, under large variations of preferences, can be unfair to minority groups. In this work, we consider fairness in dueling bandits, a standard framework for online learning from preference data. We assume that each user has a (potentially distinct) Condorcet winner, which is an arm preferred to every other arm. Using these user-specific Condorcet winners as reference points, we evaluate and score arms according to their performance relative to the corresponding winner. To promote fairness across heterogeneous users, we adopt the well-established Nash Social Welfare objective, which maximizes the product of user utilities, thereby inherently penalizing inequality and preventing the marginalization of any single user. Within this framework, we construct a hard instance to establish a regret lower bound of $Ω(T^{2/3}\min(K,D)^\frac{1}{3})$ for a time horizon $T$, $K$ arms, and $D$ users, which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first result quantifying the cost of fairness in dueling bandits with heterogeneous preferences. We then present the Fair-Explore-Then-Commit and Fair-$ε$-Greedy algorithms with a Condorcet winner identification phase. We further derive their regret upper bounds that match the lower-bound dependence on $T$ up to logarithmic factors.

</details>


### [249] [Retrieval with Multiple Query Vectors through Anomalous Pattern Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01965)
*Allassan Tchangmena A Nken,Baimam Boukar Jean Jacques,Miriam Rateike,Celia Cintas,Skyler Speakman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel retrieval method that uses multiple query vectors instead of a single query, leveraging anomalous pattern detection to identify and retrieve vectors with similar anomalous dimensions across the database.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional vector retrieval methods only handle single query vectors, but complex reasoning and retrieval tasks often require multiple query vectors to better capture relationships and patterns.

Method: The method takes multiple query vectors as input, identifies which vector dimensions are anomalous within the query set, then scans the database to retrieve vectors that show similar anomalous patterns across those identified dimensions.

Result: Validation on two image datasets, a text dataset, and a tabular dataset shows that larger query sets (particularly from 1 to 8 vectors) improve retrieval performance, with diminishing returns beyond 8 vectors.

Conclusion: Multiple-query vector retrieval using anomalous pattern detection is effective, with optimal performance gains observed when expanding query sets up to 8 vectors, demonstrating the value of multi-query approaches for complex retrieval tasks.

Abstract: A classical vector retrieval problem typically considers a \emph{single} query embedding vector as input and retrieves the most similar embedding vectors from a vector database. However, complex reasoning and retrieval tasks frequently require \emph{multiple query vectors}, rather than a single one. In this work, we propose a retrieval method that considers multiple query vectors simultaneously and retrieves the most relevant vectors from the database using concepts from anomalous pattern detection. Specifically, our approach leverages a set of query vectors $Q$ (with $|Q|\geq 1$), and identifies the subset of vector dimensions within $Q$ that standout (anomalous) from the rest of dimensions. Next, we scan the vector database to retrieve the set of vectors that are also anomalous across the previously identified vector dimensions and return them as our retrieved set of vectors. We validate our approach on two image datasets, a text dataset, and a tabular dataset. Overall, we observe that, across most datasets, larger query sets lead to improved retrieval performance. The improvement is most pronounced when increasing the query sets from 1 to 8, while the gains become smaller beyond that.

</details>


### [250] [MER-DG: Modality-Entropy Regularization for Multimodal Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01967)
*Yavuz Yarici,Ghassan AlRegib*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MER-DG addresses multimodal domain generalization by preventing fusion overfitting through entropy regularization, improving cross-domain performance by ~5% over standard fusion methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard multimodal architectures suffer from Fusion Overfitting - they exploit cross-modal co-occurrences specific to source domains rather than learning domain-invariant features, leading to poor generalization to new environments with different recording conditions.

Method: Proposes Modality-Entropy Regularization for Domain Generalization (MER-DG), which maximizes the entropy of each encoder's feature distribution to preserve feature diversity and prevent overfitting to source-specific statistical relationships between modalities.

Result: Extensive experiments on EPIC-Kitchens and HAC benchmarks show average improvements of approximately 5% over standard fusion methods and approximately 2% over state-of-the-art approaches for multimodal domain generalization.

Conclusion: MER-DG effectively addresses Fusion Overfitting in multimodal systems, providing an architecture-agnostic solution that can be integrated as an additive loss term into existing frameworks to improve domain generalization performance.

Abstract: Deploying multimodal models in real-world scenarios requires generalization to new environments where recording conditions differ from training, a challenge known as multimodal domain generalization (MMDG). Standard architectures employ separate encoders for each modality and a fusion module, training the system end-to-end by optimizing on the fused features. In this paper, we identify that such joint optimization causes encoders to exploit cross-modal co-occurrences, statistical relationships between modalities that arise from source-specific recording conditions, rather than learning domain-invariant features. We term this failure mode Fusion Overfitting. To address this, we propose Modality-Entropy Regularization for Domain Generalization (MER-DG), which maximizes the entropy of each encoder's feature distribution to preserve feature diversity. MER-DG is architecture-agnostic and integrates into existing multimodal frameworks as an additive loss term. Extensive experiments on EPIC-Kitchens and HAC benchmarks demonstrate average improvements of approximately 5% over standard fusion and approximately 2% over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [251] [AdamO: A Collapse-Suppressed Optimizer for Offline RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01968)
*Nan Qiao,Sheng Yue,Shuning Wang,Ju Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdamO: A modified Adam optimizer with orthogonality constraints that stabilizes offline RL training by preventing TD error amplification through optimizer dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL suffers from catastrophic failures when TD updates amplify their own errors, leading to extreme Q-values. The paper identifies that optimizer dynamics (not just backup rules or network architecture) can directly trigger or suppress this instability in offline TD learning.

Method: Model offline TD learning as a feedback system and analyze Adam-based critic updates from a control-theoretic perspective. Propose AdamO - an Adam-based optimizer with explicit orthogonality constraints to prevent TD error amplification, featuring decoupled orthogonality correction regulated by a strict task-alignment budget.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows stability condition: Adam-based critic dynamics are stable if and only if spectral radius of update operator is below one. AdamO theoretically guarantees worst-case task safety and preserves Adam's continuous-time dissipative dynamics. Empirical results show broad compatibility with diverse offline RL baselines, improving stability and returns across benchmarks.

Conclusion: Optimizer dynamics play a crucial role in offline RL stability. AdamO provides a theoretically grounded solution to stabilize offline RL training through orthogonality constraints, offering practical improvements across diverse offline RL algorithms and benchmarks.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) can fail spectacularly when bootstrapped temporal-difference (TD) updates amplify their own errors, driving the critic toward extreme and unusable Q-values. A key counterintuitive insight of this work is that collapse is not only a property of the backup rule or network architecture: optimizer dynamics themselves can directly trigger or suppress instability. From a control-theoretic viewpoint, we model offline TD learning as a feedback system and analyze Adam-based critic updates. This yields a necessary and sufficient condition for stability of the induced local update dynamics: within the regime we analyze, these dynamics are stable if and only if the spectral radius of the corresponding update operator is strictly below one. Further analysis suggests that standard Adam updates can inadvertently distort the parameter geometry, motivating explicit orthogonality constraints to prevent TD error amplification. To this end, we propose AdamO, an Adam-based optimizer with a decoupled orthogonality correction regulated by a strict task-alignment budget. We prove that this design theoretically guarantees worst-case task safety and preserves Adam's continuous-time dissipative dynamics. Empirically, AdamO is broadly compatible with diverse offline RL baselines, improving stability and returns across a broad suite of benchmarks.

</details>


### [252] [Misclassification Rate and Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in Graph Convolutional Networks via Subsampling Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01987)
*Yexin Zhang,Zhongtian Ma,Qiaosheng Zhang,Zhen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides the first rigorous theoretical framework for analyzing differential privacy in Graph Convolutional Networks through subsampling stability, characterizing the privacy-utility trade-off and deriving explicit bounds on misclassification rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to understand and formalize differential privacy guarantees in Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), particularly through the framework of subsampling stability. Existing theoretical understanding of how subsampling affects privacy-utility trade-offs in GCNs is limited.

Method: The authors employ a subsampling stability framework to analyze differential privacy in GCNs. They derive theoretical upper bounds on misclassification rates that explicitly depend on the subsampling probability p_s. They characterize feasible ranges for p_s by analyzing the privacy-utility trade-off conditions.

Result: The paper establishes explicit upper bounds on misclassification rates dependent on subsampling probability p_s. It identifies that if p_s is too large, privacy conditions become difficult to satisfy (yielding vacuous guarantees), while if p_s is too small, classification accuracy deteriorates. The work provides the first rigorous theoretical framework for understanding this trade-off in GCNs.

Conclusion: The research establishes foundational theoretical results for differential privacy in GCNs via subsampling stability, providing a framework to understand and quantify the inherent privacy-utility trade-off, with practical guidance on selecting appropriate subsampling probabilities.

Abstract: We study differential privacy (DP) in Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) through the framework of \textit{subsampling stability}. We derive upper bounds on the misclassification rate that depend explicitly on the subsampling probability $p_s$. Furthermore, we characterize the \textit{privacy--utility trade-off} by identifying feasible ranges of $p_s$; if $p_s$ is too large, the stability-based privacy condition becomes difficult to satisfy, yielding vacuous guarantees, whereas if it is too small, accuracy deteriorates. Our results provide the first rigorous theoretical framework for understanding subsampling stability in GCNs under DP.

</details>


### [253] [DBLP: Phase-Aware Bounded-Loss Transport for Burst-Resilient Distributed ML Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01989)
*Zechen Ma,Zixi Qu,Jinyan Yi,David Lin,Yashar Ganjali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DBLP is a transport protocol for distributed ML training that dynamically adjusts gradient loss tolerance across training phases to handle network congestion and microbursts, reducing training time by up to 33.9%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing distributed ML training systems suffer from network-layer congestion events that cause severe tail latency and training variability, while current network optimizations fail to integrate model-training insights into communication design, treating all gradients equally.

Method: DBLP (Dynamic Bounded-Loss Protocol) is a burst-resilient, training-phase-aware, hardware-agnostic transport protocol that incorporates model-level tolerance properties into gradient communication. It dynamically adjusts gradient loss tolerance across different training phases to optimize communication.

Result: DBLP reduces end-to-end training time by average 24.4% and maximum 33.9% compared to baseline, tolerates significantly higher loss while maintaining comparable test accuracy, and achieves up to 5.88x single-round communication latency speedups during microburst events.

Conclusion: DBLP effectively addresses network congestion issues in distributed ML training by integrating model training insights into communication protocol design, demonstrating substantial improvements in training efficiency and stability during transient high-loss events.

Abstract: Distributed machine learning (ML) training has become a necessity with the prevalence of billion to trillion-parameter-scale models. While prior work has improved training efficiency from the ML perspective at the application layer, it often fails to address transient congestion events at the network layer that introduce severe tail latency and training-time variability, thereby undermining the quality of service (QoS) of distributed ML training systems. Existing network optimizations treat all gradients equally and thus fail to integrate sufficient model-training insights into communication protocol design.
  In this paper, we present Dynamic Bounded-Loss Protocol (DBLP), a burst-resilient, training-phase-aware, and hardware-agnostic transport protocol that incorporates model-level tolerance properties into gradient communication. By dynamically adjusting gradient loss tolerance across training phases, DBLP reduces overall training time and mitigates tail-latency collapse during transient high-loss events (i.e., microbursts).
  Compared to the current state-of-the-art solution (baseline), DBLP tolerates significantly higher loss while achieving comparable test accuracy, and reduces end-to-end training time by an average of 24.4% and a maximum of 33.9%. At microburst events, DBLP achieves up to 5.88x single-round communication latency speedups over the baseline, preventing burst-induced tail-latency spikes and maintaining stable training performance.

</details>


### [254] [RamanBench: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Machine Learning on Raman Spectroscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02003)
*Mario Koddenbrock,Christoph Lange,Robin Legner,Martin Jäger,Martin Kögler,Mariano N. Cruz Bournazou,Peter Neubauer,Felix Biessmann,Erik Rodner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RamanBench is the first large-scale, reproducible benchmark for ML in Raman spectroscopy, unifying 74 datasets and benchmarking 28 models, revealing that Tabular Foundation Models outperform domain-specific methods but no approach generalizes well across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raman spectroscopy lacks standardized ML benchmarks, hindering progress due to fragmented datasets, inconsistent evaluation, and models that fail to capture spectral data structure, despite being a critical technique for non-invasive molecular analysis in scientific fields.

Method: Created RamanBench: a comprehensive benchmark with streamlined data access, evaluation protocols, code, and live leaderboard. Unified 74 datasets (including 16 new ones) across four domains with 325,668 spectra, covering classification and regression tasks. Benchmarked 28 models under standardized protocol, including classical methods (PLS), Raman-specific models (RamanNet), Tabular Foundation Models (TabPFN), and time-series approaches (ROCKET).

Result: Tabular Foundation Models consistently outperform domain-specific and gradient boosting baselines, while time-series models remain competitive. However, no single method generalizes well across different datasets, revealing a fundamental generalization gap in current approaches.

Conclusion: The introduction of RamanBench addresses critical benchmarking needs in Raman spectroscopy ML, demonstrating the superiority of Tabular Foundation Models but highlighting significant generalization challenges. The authors invite community contributions to this living benchmark to accelerate advances in medical diagnostics, biological research, and materials science.

Abstract: Machine Learning (ML) has transformed many scientific fields, yet key applications still lack standardized benchmarks. Raman spectroscopy, a widely used technique for non-invasive molecular analysis, is one such field where progress is limited by fragmented datasets, inconsistent evaluation, and models that fail to capture the structure of spectral data. We introduce RamanBench, the first large-scale, fully reproducible benchmark for ML on Raman spectroscopy, consisting of streamlined data access, evaluation protocols and code, as well as a live leaderboard. It unifies 74 datasets (including 16 first released with this benchmark) across four domains, comprising 325,668 spectra and spanning classification and regression tasks under diverse experimental conditions. We benchmark 28 models under a standardized protocol, including classical methods (e.g., PLS), Raman-specific (e.g., RamanNet), Tabular Foundation Model (TFM) (e.g., TabPFN), and time-series approaches (e.g., ROCKET). TFM consistently outperform domain-specific and gradient boosting baselines, while time-series models remain competitive. However, no method generalizes across datasets, revealing a fundamental gap. Therefore, we invite the community to contribute new approaches to our living benchmark, with the potential to accelerate advances in critical applications such as medical diagnostics, biological research, and materials science.

</details>


### [255] [How Can One Choose the Best CAM-Based Explainability Method for a CNN Model?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02007)
*Daniel da Silva Costa,Pedro Nuno de Souza Moura,Adriana C. F. Alvim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using different similarity metrics to evaluate explainability methods for deep learning models, finding that Manhattan and Correlation metrics best align with human perception, with LayerCAM, Score-CAM, and IS-CAM performing best.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models are increasingly used in life-critical applications requiring explanations, but there are few metrics to evaluate explainability methods. The commonly used IoU metric may not be optimal since saliency maps don't have known shapes, and better metrics are needed to find explainability methods that best resemble human perception.

Method: Used a subset of Chihuahua images from ImageNet, generated saliency maps using several CAM-based explainability methods, measured alignment using distance metrics between human annotation bounding boxes and saliency maps, created rankings based on metrics, and compared them to human preference rankings collected through crowdsourcing using Rank-Biased Overlap (RBO) metric.

Result: Results indicate feasibility of finding explainability methods resembling human perception. Manhattan and Correlation metrics performed best in aligning with human perception, while LayerCAM, Score-CAM, and IS-CAM were the best explainability methods regarding human perception.

Conclusion: The proposed evaluation method using multiple similarity metrics can effectively identify explainability methods that best resemble human perception, with Manhattan and Correlation being particularly suitable metrics for this purpose, and certain CAM-based methods performing especially well.

Abstract: In recent years, several advances have been observed in Deep Learning with surprising results. Models in this area have been increasingly used in numerous applications, including those sensitive to human life, which require clear explanations and justifications. Various explainability methods have been proposed, but not many metrics to evaluate these methods. The most commonly used metric is the Intersection over Union (IoU). However, due to the characteristics of the results of the explainability methods, called saliency maps, which do not have a known shape, we hypothesise that there must be a better metric that allows one to find an explainability method that produces results that best resemble the human perception. We propose using different metrics to assess the similarity between human perception and the explanation saliency maps to find a better metric. An investigation was conducted employing a subset of the Chihuahuas images from ImageNet dataset. Several CAM-based explainability methods were used to generate saliency maps for each chihuahua image. Alignment was measured by applying distance metrics between the bounding box of human annotations and the saliency maps produced by each explainability method. Rankings of the best saliency maps were created using the results of the distance metrics and compared to the ranking obtained using people's choice, collected through crowdsourcing, of the best explanation saliency maps for each selected image. Comparison between rankings was performed using the Rank-Biased Overlap (RBO) metric. The results indicate the feasibility of our method to find the explainability method that best resembles human perception. In our experiments, the two metrics that best resemble human perception corresponded to Manhattan and Correlation. Besides, the best explainability methods regarding human perception were LayerCAM, Score-CAM, and IS-CAM.

</details>


### [256] [Robust and Explainable Divide-and-Conquer Learning for Intrusion Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02015)
*Yan Zhou,Kevin Hamlen,Michael De Lucia,Murat Kantarcioglu,Latifur Khan,Sharad Mehrotra,Ananthram Swami,Bhavani Thuraisingham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A divide-and-conquer approach decomposes complex intrusion detection tasks into simpler subtasks, enabling lightweight models like decision trees to achieve high accuracy with dramatically reduced model size and improved robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional machine learning for intrusion detection requires complex models to handle high-dimensional, noisy, and class-imbalanced network traffic data, but these models are impractical for resource-constrained devices with limited processing power and memory.

Method: Correlation-aware divide-and-conquer learning technique that decomposes complex learning problems into smaller, more manageable subproblems, enabling training of lightweight models (as simple as decision trees) on focused subtasks.

Result: Up to 43.3% higher local accuracy and up to 257 times reduction in model size on real-world network intrusion detection datasets, with additional improvements in adversarial robustness and explainability.

Conclusion: The divide-and-conquer approach makes sophisticated intrusion detection practical for resource-constrained devices by enabling lightweight models to perform comparably to complex models while offering benefits in robustness and interpretability.

Abstract: Machine learning-based intrusion detection requires complex models to capture patterns in high-dimensional, noisy, and class-imbalanced raw network traffic, yet deploying such models remains impractical on resource-constrained devices with limited processing power and memory. In this paper, we present a correlation-aware divide-and-conquer learning technique that decomposes a complex learning problem into smaller, more manageable subproblems. This enables lightweight models as simple as decision trees to be trained on focused subtasks, yielding up to 43.3% higher local accuracy and up to 257 times reduction in model size on real-world network intrusion detection datasets, while also improving adversarial robustness and explainability.

</details>


### [257] [Towards Systematic Generalization for Power Grid Optimization Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02026)
*Zeeshan Memon,Yijiang Li,Hongwei Jin,Kibaek Kim,Liang Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A learning framework that jointly models AC Optimal Power Flow (ACOPF) and Security-Constrained Unit Commitment (SCUC) using shared graph-based backbone with task-specific decoders, achieving better generalization across power system optimization problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ACOPF and SCUC are fundamental power system optimization problems that share the same underlying grid physics but are typically addressed in isolation by learning-based approaches, leading to disjoint models and representations.

Method: Proposes a joint learning framework with a shared graph-based backbone capturing grid topology and physical interactions, coupled with task-specific decoders for static (ACOPF) and temporal (SCUC) decision-making. Uses solver supervision with physics-informed objectives to enforce AC feasibility and inter-temporal constraints.

Result: Experiments across multiple grid scales show improved performance and transferability compared to existing learning-based baselines. The model demonstrates cross-case generalization to unseen grid topologies without retraining and systematic generalization on UC-ACOPF using unsupervised physics-based objectives.

Conclusion: The framework can support learning across heterogeneous power system optimization problems, indicating that shared representation learning with physics-informed training enables better generalization and performance on complex grid optimization tasks.

Abstract: AC Optimal Power Flow (ACOPF) and Security-Constrained Unit Commitment (SCUC) are fundamental optimization problems in power system operations. ACOPF serves as the physical backbone of grid simulation and real-time operation, enforcing nonlinear power flow feasibility and network limits, while SCUC represents a core market-level decision process that schedules generation under operational and security constraints. Although these problems share the same underlying transmission network and physical laws, they differ in decision variables and temporal coupling, and prior learning-based approaches address them in isolation, resulting in disjoint models and representations.We propose a learning framework that jointly models ACOPF and SCUC through a shared graph-based backbone that captures grid topology and physical interactions, coupled with task-specific decoders for static and temporal decision-making. Training includes solver supervision with physics-informed objectives to enforce AC feasibility and inter-temporal operational constraints. To evaluate generalization, we assess cross-case transfer on unseen grid topologies for ACOPF and SCUC without retraining, and systematic generalization on the UC-ACOPF problem using unsupervised, physics-based objectives and a power-dispatch consensus mechanism. Experiments across multiple grid scales demonstrate improved performance and transferability relative to existing learning-based baselines, indicating that the model can support learning across heterogeneous power system optimization problems.

</details>


### [258] [Large margin classifier with graph-based adaptive regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02027)
*Vítor M. Hanriot,Turíbio T. Salis,Luiz C. B. Torres,Frederico Coelho,Antonio P. Braga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces per-class regularization hyperparameters for Gabriel graph-based binary classifiers, allowing flexible thresholds to handle outliers and class imbalance through hyperparameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional Gabriel graph-based classifiers use fixed thresholds, limiting their ability to handle outliers and class imbalance effectively. The authors aim to improve classifier performance by introducing flexible, class-specific regularization.

Method: The authors introduce per-class regularization hyperparameters in Gabriel graph-based binary classifiers, allowing different thresholds for majority and minority classes. They analyze how the quality index behaves with margins and outliers, and show how hyperparameter tuning algorithms can optimize these flexible thresholds.

Result: The flexible threshold approach effectively eliminates outliers during training and addresses class imbalance by generating higher thresholds for majority classes and lower thresholds for minority classes. Friedman test results show that flexible thresholds improve the performance of Gabriel graph-based classifiers.

Conclusion: Per-class regularization hyperparameters expand the solution space for Gabriel graph-based classifiers, enabling better handling of outliers and class imbalance through optimized flexible thresholds, leading to improved classifier performance as validated by statistical tests.

Abstract: This paper introduces the use of per-class regularization hyperparameters in Gabriel graph-based binary classifiers.
