We demonstrate how the quality index used for regularization behaves both in the margin region and in the presence of outliers, and how incorporating this regularization flexibility can lead to solutions that effectively eliminate outliers while training the classifier. We also show how it can address class imbalance by generating higher and lower thresholds for the majority and minority classes, respectively. Thus, rather than having a single solution based on fixed thresholds, flexible thresholds expand the solution space and can be optimized through hyperparameter tuning algorithms. Friedman test shows that flexible thresholds are capable of improving Gabriel graph-based classifiers.

</details>


### [259] [TIJERE: A Novel Threat Intelligence Joint Extraction Model Based on Analyst Expert Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02041)
*Inoussa Mouiche,Sherif Saad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TIJERE is a novel joint entity and relation extraction framework for cybersecurity threat intelligence that uses multisequence labeling representation and SecureBERT+ to overcome feature confusion and language ambiguity, achieving state-of-the-art performance on a new publicly available cybersecurity dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing joint extraction methods for cybersecurity threat intelligence suffer from feature confusion, language ambiguity, noise propagation, and overlapping relations, leading to low accuracy and poor model performance, which hampers automated threat analysis and mitigation.

Method: TIJERE formulates joint extraction as a multisequence labeling representation problem, generating separate sequences for each entity pair and integrating expert domain features for enhanced positional, contextual, and semantic representations. It uses SecureBERT+, a contextual language model fine-tuned on cybersecurity text, to reduce language ambiguity and improve domain-specific generalization.

Result: TIJERE achieves state-of-the-art performance on the new DNRTI-JE dataset with F1-scores exceeding 0.93 for named entity recognition and 0.98 for relation extraction, significantly outperforming existing methods.

Conclusion: TIJERE with its standardized DNRTI-JE dataset enables high-performance cybersecurity intelligence extraction that is transferable to other domains like healthcare, finance, and bioinformatics, addressing critical gaps in automated threat intelligence analysis.

Abstract: The extraction of entities and relationships from threat intelligence reports into structured formats, such as cybersecurity knowledge graphs, is essential for automated threat analysis, detection, and mitigation. However, existing joint extraction methods struggle with feature confusion, language ambiguity, noise propagation, and overlapping relations, resulting in low accuracy and poor model performance. This paper presents TIJERE, an innovative joint entity and relation extraction framework that formulates joint extraction as a multisequence labeling representation (MSLR) problem. Specifically, separate sequences are generated for each entity pair. Unlike prior tagging schemes, MSLR integrates expert domain features to enrich positional, contextual, and semantic representations of entities, thereby enhancing feature distinction and classification accuracy. Additionally, TIJERE reduces language ambiguity and enhances domain-specific generalization by leveraging SecureBERT+, a contextual language model fine-tuned on cybersecurity text. This improves both named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE). This paper also introduces DNRTI-JE, the first publicly available jointly labeled dataset for cybersecurity entity and RE, filling a crucial gap in cyber threat intelligence automation. Empirical evaluations on the curated DNRTI-JE dataset demonstrate that TIJERE achieves state-of-the-art performance, with F1-scores exceeding 0.93 for NER and 0.98 for RE, outperforming existing methods. Together, TIJERE and the standardized benchmarking DNRTI-JE dataset enable high-performance cybersecurity intelligence extraction, with transferable applications in healthcare, finance, and bioinformatics.

</details>


### [260] [Bringing Order to Asynchronous SGD: Towards Optimality under Data-Dependent Delays with Momentum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02043)
*Tehila Dahan,Roie Reshef,Sharon Goldstein,Kfir Y. Levy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A momentum-based asynchronous SGD framework that optimally handles data-dependent delays, achieving optimal convergence rates for both convex and non-convex smooth problems while simplifying hyperparameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Asynchronous SGD enables scalable distributed training but suffers from gradient staleness. Existing mitigation strategies (delay-adaptive learning rates, staleness-aware filtering) introduce systematic bias by overrepresenting simpler samples and suppressing complex ones. Previous approaches rely on Lipschitz assumptions yielding suboptimal rates or don't address smooth, convex cases.

Method: Proposed momentum-based asynchronous framework that preserves information from delayed gradients while mitigating staleness effects. Introduces robust learning-rate schedules to simplify hyperparameter tuning.

Result: Established first optimal convergence rates for data-dependent delays in both convex and non-convex smooth setups. Provides new result for asynchronous optimization under standard assumptions. Derived practical learning-rate schedules.

Conclusion: The proposed momentum-based framework successfully addresses data-dependent delays in asynchronous optimization, achieving optimal convergence rates and practical improvements in hyperparameter tuning.

Abstract: Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (SGD) enables scalable distributed training but suffers from gradient staleness. Existing mitigation strategies, such as delay-adaptive learning rates and staleness-aware filtering, typically attenuate or discard delayed gradients, introducing systematic bias: updates from simpler or faster-to-process samples are overrepresented, while gradients from more complex samples are delayed or suppressed. In contrast, prior approaches to data-dependent delays rely on a Lipschitz assumption that yields suboptimal rates or leave the smooth, convex case unaddressed. We propose a momentum-based asynchronous framework designed to preserve information from delayed gradients while mitigating the effects of staleness. We establish the first optimal convergence rates for data-dependent delays in both convex and non-convex smooth setups, providing a new result for asynchronous optimization under standard assumptions. Additionally, we derive robust learning-rate schedules that simplify hyperparameter tuning in practice.

</details>


### [261] [NeuroViz: Real-time Interactive Visualization of Forward and Backward Passes in Neural Network Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02044)
*Reza Rawassizadeh,Tanvi Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NeuroViz is an interactive visualization tool for neural network training that helps users understand weight updates, activations, and loss in real-time, achieving excellent usability scores in user studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural network training is difficult to interpret, especially for newcomers. There's a need for better tools to visualize and understand what happens during training in real-time.

Method: Developed NeuroViz, an interactive visualization tool that allows users to configure network architecture, activation functions, learning rates, and datasets. The tool visualizes weight changes in correspondence with activation signals in both forward and backward passes, showing pre- and post-update states within epochs and dynamically updating per-neuron equations.

Result: User study with 31 participants comparing NeuroViz against six established visualization tools showed the highest usability score (SUS 80.97, 'excellent' range). Mean rankings were 2.47 for clarity and 2.23 for usefulness (lower is better). Over 70% of participants reported substantial increase in perception of neural network training transparency.

Conclusion: NeuroViz effectively addresses the interpretability challenge in neural network training through interactive visualization, providing users with clear insights into the training process and achieving high usability ratings compared to existing tools.

Abstract: Training neural networks is difficult to interpret, particularly for newcomers. We introduce NeuroViz, an interactive visualization tool that supports real-time exploration of fully connected neural network training. Users can configure network architecture, activation functions, learning rates, and datasets, then observe activations, weight updates, and loss progression. NeuroViz visualizes weight changes in direct correspondence with activation signals in both forward and backward passes, enabling users to distinguish pre- and post-update states within individual epochs and view dynamically updating per-neuron equations. We conduct a comparative user study with 31 participants against six established visualization tools and we achieved the highest usability score (SUS 80.97, in the 'excellent' range), with mean rankings of 2.47 for clarity and 2.23 for usefulness (lower is better). Over 70% of participants reported that the visualizations substantially increased their perception of neural network training transparency. The implemented instance is accessible at https://neuroviz.org.

</details>


### [262] [DR-SNE: Density-Regularized Stochastic Neighbor Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02060)
*Maksim Kazanskii*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DR-SNE is a new dimensionality reduction method that adds density regularization to t-SNE to better preserve data density distributions while maintaining local neighborhood structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional dimensionality reduction methods like t-SNE focus on preserving local neighborhood structure but often distort data density distributions, which can be important for certain applications like anomaly detection.

Method: Reformulates dimensionality reduction as joint alignment of conditional structure (local relationships) and relative density structure. Introduces DR-SNE which augments SNE objective with a density regularization term based on normalized log-density estimates.

Result: DR-SNE improves density preservation while maintaining competitive neighborhood fidelity. It shows gains on density-sensitive tasks like anomaly detection across multiple datasets compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: Incorporating density information complements geometry-focused objectives in dimensionality reduction, providing better preservation of relative density variations for tasks where density matters.

Abstract: Dimensionality reduction methods such as t-SNE are designed to preserve local neighborhood structure but do not explicitly account for how probability mass is distributed, often leading to distortions of data density. We reformulate dimensionality reduction as the joint alignment of two components: (i) conditional structure, capturing local relationships, and (ii) relative density structure, captured via local density statistics. Based on this perspective, we introduce Density-Regularized SNE (DR-SNE), which augments the stochastic neighbor embedding objective with a density regularization term derived from normalized log-density estimates. Unlike prior approaches such as DensMAP and DenSNE, which rely on local scale consistency, DR-SNE directly aligns normalized density estimates, providing a simple and scale-invariant mechanism for preserving relative density variations. Empirically, DR-SNE improves density preservation while maintaining competitive neighborhood fidelity, and yields gains on density-sensitive tasks such as anomaly detection across multiple datasets. These results suggest that incorporating density information complements geometry-focused objectives in dimensionality reduction.

</details>


### [263] [Weight Clipping for Robust Conformal Inference under Unbounded Covariate Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02072)
*James Wang,Surbhi Goel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel method called clipped least-squares importance fitting (CLISF) addresses undercoverage issues in weighted conformal prediction for covariate shift scenarios by providing reduced-variance density ratio estimation with theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conformal prediction assumes exchangeability of training and test data, but this is often violated due to covariate shifts in real-world applications. While weighted conformal prediction handles such shifts, it suffers from significant undercoverage when the density ratio between distributions is unbounded and/or must be learned, due to overfitting and high variance in estimating the nonconformity score threshold.

Method: The authors introduce clipped least-squares importance fitting (CLISF) as a reduced-variance method for density ratio estimation. The method involves using density ratios learned via CLISF in weighted conformal prediction, with coverage correction through running WCP with an inflated coverage target that can be estimated from the data itself.

Result: Theoretical guarantees show that density ratios learned using CLISF, when plugged into WCP, have bounded expected undercoverage. The undercoverage can be corrected by using a slightly inflated coverage target. The method provides the first theoretical guarantees for weight clipping in conformal inference, achieving dataset-conditional coverage with sample complexity that doesn't blow up with higher moments of the true density ratio. Results are verified on real-world benchmarks and synthetic data.

Conclusion: CLISF provides a practical solution to undercoverage problems in weighted conformal prediction under covariate shift, offering theoretical guarantees for weight clipping and addressing limitations of prior work by avoiding explosion of sample complexity with higher moments of density ratios.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) provides powerful, distribution-free prediction sets, but its guarantees rely on the exchangeability of training and test data, which is often violated in practice due to covariate shifts. While weighted conformal prediction (WCP) is designed to handle such shifts, it can suffer from significant undercoverage when the density ratio between the distributions is unbounded and/or must be learned. This is because of both overfitting in learning the density ratio, and high variance in estimating the nonconformity score threshold. To address this, we introduce clipped least-squares importance fitting (CLISF) as a reduced-variance method for density ratio estimation. Specifically, we show that density ratios learned using CLISF, when plugged into WCP, have bounded expected undercoverage. Furthermore, we show that the undercoverage can be corrected by running WCP with a slightly inflated coverage target; crucially, we are able to estimate the required level of inflation from the data. We provide the first theoretical guarantees for weight clipping in conformal inference, achieving dataset-conditional coverage with a sample complexity that does not blow up with the higher moments of the true density ratio -- a key limitation of prior work. We verify our results on real-world benchmarks and synthetic data.

</details>


### [264] [GETA-3DGS: Automatic Joint Structured Pruning and Quantization for 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02086)
*Baobing Zhang,Wanxin Sui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GETA-3DGS: First end-to-end automatic joint structured pruning and quantization framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting compression, achieving ~5x storage reduction without per-scene thresholds


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) requires hundreds of MBs to GBs per scene, exceeding mobile/immersive platform budgets. Existing compression methods use separate pruning, quantization, and entropy coding stages with hand-tuned heuristics, limiting generalization and preventing users from specifying target rate/quality budgets.

Method: Proposes GETA-3DGS with three key contributions: (1) 3DGS-aware quantization-aware dependency graph treating each Gaussian as group with five attribute sub-nodes and degree-aware SH sub-nodes; (2) Render-aware saliency fusing transmittance-weighted contribution, screen-space gradient, and pixel coverage; (3) Heterogeneous per-attribute mixed-precision scheme co-optimized with structural sparsity under projected partial saliency-guided descent guarantee.

Result: Achieves ~5x storage reduction over Vanilla 3DGS on Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending datasets. Uniform 6-bit cap causes up to -6.74 dB quality loss versus heterogeneous allocation, matching reverse-water-filling analysis. Works directly on raw Gaussian primitives rather than post-hoc anchor representation.

Conclusion: GETA-3DGS provides the first end-to-end automatic joint structured pruning and quantization framework for 3DGS compression, enabling users to specify target rates/quality budgets. The approach is complementary to existing codecs and demonstrates that bit-width policy is the dominant rate-distortion lever.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) is a state-of-the-art representation for real-time photorealistic novel-view synthesis, yet a single high-fidelity scene typically occupies hundreds of megabytes to several gigabytes, exceeding the budgets of mobile, immersive, and volumetric video platforms. Existing 3DGS compression methods (e.g., HAC++, FlexGaussian, LP-3DGS) treat pruning, quantization, and entropy coding as separate stages and rely on hand-tuned heuristics (opacity thresholds, fixed bit-widths, SH truncation), limiting cross-scene generalization and preventing users from specifying a target rate or quality budget. We propose GETA-3DGS, to our knowledge the first end-to-end automatic joint structured pruning and quantization framework for 3DGS. Building on GETA for joint pruning-quantization of deep networks, we contribute: (i) a 3DGS-aware quantization-aware dependency graph (QADG) treating each Gaussian primitive as a group with five attribute sub-nodes and degree-aware SH sub-nodes; (ii) a render-aware saliency fusing transmittance-weighted contribution, screen-space gradient, and pixel coverage into a Gaussian-level importance score; and (iii) a heterogeneous per-attribute mixed-precision scheme co-optimized with structural sparsity under a projected partial saliency-guided (PPSG) descent guarantee. On Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending, GETA-3DGS operates directly on raw Gaussian primitives rather than a post-hoc anchor representation, delivering ~5x storage reduction over Vanilla 3DGS with no per-scene thresholds. Bit-width policy is the dominant rate-distortion lever: a uniform 6-bit cap costs up to -6.74 dB on view-dependent scenes versus our heterogeneous allocation, matching an information-theoretic reverse-water-filling analysis we develop. GETA-3DGS is complementary to existing codecs: entropy coding (HAC++, CompGS) is downstream, so the two can be composed.

</details>


### [265] [Bridging the Gap Between Average and Discounted TD Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02103)
*Haoxing Tian,Zaiwei Chen,Ioannis Ch. Paschalidis,Alex Olshevsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel two-trajectory TD learning algorithm for average-reward policy evaluation that achieves better convergence guarantees and quadratic sample complexity, matching discounted setting efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TD learning in average-reward settings faces theoretical difficulties because the Bellman operator isn't contractive, complicating analyses that work well in discounted settings. Existing approaches have limitations in convergence guarantees and sample complexity.

Method: Proposes a novel algorithm for average-reward policy evaluation using sampling from two Markovian trajectories. The method guarantees convergence to the unique solution of a properly defined projected Bellman equation.

Result: The algorithm achieves uniform convergence analysis applicable to both linear function approximation and tabular settings without dimension-dependent terms. It improves condition number dependence from quartic to quadratic scaling, matching discounted setting efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed method overcomes previous theoretical limitations in average-reward TD learning, providing convergence guarantees that align with what's known in discounted settings while achieving improved sample complexity.

Abstract: The analysis of Temporal Difference (TD) learning in the average-reward setting faces notable theoretical difficulties because the Bellman operator is not contractive with respect to any norm. This complicates standard analyses of stochastic updates that are effective in discounted settings. Although a considerable body of literature addresses these challenges, existing theoretical approaches come with limitations. We introduce a novel algorithm designed explicitly for policy evaluation in the average-reward setting, utilizing sampling from two Markovian trajectories. Our proposed method overcomes previous limitations by guaranteeing convergence to the unique solution of a properly defined projected Bellman equation. Notably, and in contrast to earlier work, our convergence analysis is uniformly applicable to both linear function approximation and tabular settings and does not involve explicit dimension-dependent terms in its convergence bounds. These results align with what is known to hold in the discounted setting. Furthermore, our algorithm achieves improved dependence on the problem's condition number, reducing the sample complexity from quartic, as in prior literature, to quadratic scaling, and thus matching the efficiency seen in the discounted setting.

</details>


### [266] [Sharpness-Aware Pretraining Mitigates Catastrophic Forgetting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02105)
*Ishaan Watts,Catherine Li,Sachin Goyal,Jacob Mitchell Springer,Aditi Raghunathan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pretraining optimizers focused on flat minima geometry improve downstream performance after post-training and quantization with less forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current pretraining optimizers prioritize raw base model strength, ignoring that the geometry (flatness/sharpness) of minima affects how well capabilities survive subsequent parameter updates like post-training and quantization.

Method: Study three pretraining optimization approaches that bias toward flatter minima: Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), large learning rates, and shortened learning rate annealing periods. Test across model sizes from 20M to 150M parameters on five common datasets.

Result: These interventions consistently improve downstream performance after post-training with up to 80% less forgetting. At scale (OLMo-2-1B), a short SAM mid-training phase reduces forgetting by 31% after MetaMath post-training and by 40% after 4-bit quantization.

Conclusion: Optimizing for flat minima during pretraining is crucial for preserving base model capabilities through subsequent modifications like post-training and quantization, challenging the conventional focus solely on base model strength.

Abstract: Pretraining optimizers are tuned to produce the strongest possible base model, on the assumption that a stronger starting point yields a stronger model after subsequent changes like post-training and quantization. This overlooks the geometry of the base model which controls how much of the base model's capabilities survive subsequent parameter updates. We study three pretraining optimization approaches that bias optimization toward flatter minima: Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), large learning rates, and shortened learning rate annealing periods. Across model sizes ranging from 20M to 150M parameters, we find that these interventions consistently improve downstream performance after post-training on five common datasets with up to 80% less forgetting. These principles hold at scale: a short SAM mid-training phase applied to an existing OLMo-2-1B checkpoint reduces forgetting by 31% after MetaMath post-training and by 40% after 4-bit quantization.

</details>


### [267] [Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network I](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02108)
*Ziran Liu,Wei Wang,Jinhao Wang,Pengcheng Wang,Xinyi Sui,Cihan Ruan,Nam Ling,Wei Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a geometric framework for analyzing deep residual networks as products of near-identity Jacobians, proving deterministic estimates for singular spectra under Frobenius normalization and establishing power-law spectral behavior with rigorous error bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep residual architectures can be modeled as products of near-identity Jacobians. The paper aims to establish rigorous geometric and spectral properties of these architectures, particularly understanding how singular spectra behave under Frobenius normalization and developing a geometric framework to analyze power-law spectral behavior.

Method: The authors use quotient geometry and Cartan decomposition approaches. They map full-rank factors from GL(d) to the positive cone via A↦A^TA, then to ordered eigenvalue data. Under Frobenius normalization, they analyze exact power-law spectra as trace-normalized Cartan orbits, connecting these to Gibbs families, Fisher information, and Bures-Wasserstein geometry. They develop slack-aware margin inequalities and prove various rigidity theorems controlling spectral displacements.

Result: The main results include: 1) A rigidity theorem providing slack-aware margin inequalities controlling displacement of fitted Cartan coordinates, 2) In zero-slack cases, depth-L budgets yield exponent drift of order (log M)/L, 3) Separation of scalar top-radial from full-Cartan spectral control requiring Bures/Hellinger residual variation, 4) Approximate-power-law and metric-chart versions with converse lower bounds, 5) Near-identity expansions for normalized residual chains, 6) Finite-width power-law tail bounds and robust rank-window transition estimates.

Conclusion: The geometric framework provides rigorous tools for analyzing deep residual networks, establishing connections between power-law spectral behavior, Fisher information geometry, and Bures-Wasserstein metrics. The results offer verifiable diagnostic criteria for network behavior and establish fundamental relationships between architectural depth, slack parameters, and spectral properties.

Abstract: Deep residual architectures are modeled as products of near-identity Jacobians. This paper proves deterministic quotient-geometric estimates for singular spectra of Frobenius-normalized layer factors, emphasizing a normalized top-radial Cartan coordinate and fitted power-law chart.
  Full-rank factors are mapped from $\mathrm{GL}(d)$ to the positive cone by $A\mapsto A^\top A$, then to ordered eigenvalue data. Under Frobenius normalization, exact power-law spectra form a trace-normalized Cartan orbit. This orbit is a Gibbs family on ranks, a Fisher information line, and a Bures--Wasserstein curve with line element $d/4$ times Fisher information.
  The main rigidity theorem is a slack-aware margin inequality: interface radial amplitude, non-backtracking slack, and signed residual variation control displacement of the fitted Cartan coordinate. In the exact-chart zero-slack case, a depth-$L$ budget gives exponent drift of order $(\log M)/L$; generally, slack and residual increments augment the bound.
  We separate scalar top-radial from full-Cartan spectral control, which also needs Bures/Hellinger residual variation. We prove approximate-power-law and metric-chart versions, converse lower bounds, Fisher--KL/Bures action estimates, and near-identity expansions for normalized residual chains.
  Near-identity results verify transport budgets; chart quality remains measurable. Effective rank is a spectral-energy quantile, giving finite-width power-law tail bounds and robust rank-window transition estimates. Empirical static-weight exponent profiles serve as diagnostics; full verification also requires interface budgets, slacks, and residuals for the same operator chain.

</details>


### [268] [Detecting Adversarial Data via Provable Adversarial Noise Amplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02109)
*Furkan Mumcu,Yasin Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Formal mathematical study of adversarial noise amplification in DNNs, proposing spectral loss training and lightweight inference-time detection mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The nonuniform and growing impact of adversarial noise across DNN layers has been observed but lacks formal mathematical justification. Researchers have used this phenomenon for detection and robustness improvement without theoretical grounding.

Method: 1) Formal adversarial noise amplification theorem with sufficient conditions for mathematical guarantee; 2) Novel training methodology with custom spectral loss function; 3) Specific architectural design to enhance amplification signal; 4) Lightweight inference-time detection mechanism.

Result: Proposed detector shows efficacy against state-of-the-art attacks and purpose-built adaptive attacks, confirming enhanced amplification as robust and reliable signal for adversarial defense.

Conclusion: Enhanced adversarial noise amplification can serve as a mathematically grounded, robust detection signal for adversarial inputs when combined with appropriate training and architectural modifications.

Abstract: The nonuniform and growing impact of adversarial noise across the layers of deep neural networks has been used in the literature, without a formal mathematical justification, to detect adversarial inputs and improve robustness. In this work, we study this phenomenon in detail and present a formal adversarial noise amplification theorem. We specify a set of sufficient conditions under which the adversarial noise amplification is mathematically guaranteed. Based on theoretical observations, we propose a novel training methodology with a custom spectral loss function and a specific architectural design to enhance the amplification signal for detecting adversarial data. Finally, we introduce a new, lightweight detection mechanism that leverages the enhanced amplification signal and operates entirely at inference time. To validate our approach, we demonstrate the detector's efficacy against both state-of-the-art attacks and a purpose-built adaptive attack, confirming that enhanced amplification can serve as a robust and reliable signal for adversarial defense.

</details>


### [269] [Adversarial Update-Based Federated Unlearning for Poisoned Model Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02110)
*Wenwei Zhao,Xiaowen Li,Yao Liu,Zhuo Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAUN: A lightweight federated learning framework that quickly removes malicious client contributions using adversarial unlearning and fine-tuning, achieving near-zero attack success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current federated learning systems are vulnerable to poisoning attacks. While detection methods can identify malicious clients, the model remains affected. Retraining from scratch is costly, and existing unlearning methods are unsatisfactory in both effectiveness and efficiency. There's a need for a lightweight solution to remove malicious influences quickly without full retraining.

Method: FAUN retains only a short window of malicious clients' updates and employs adversarial optimization on a proxy dataset to derive updates that eliminate malicious directions. It applies these updates for a few unlearning rounds, followed by benign fine-tuning for stable recovery.

Result: Experiments on three canonical datasets show FAUN achieves recovery comparable to retraining while requiring far fewer rounds. It reduces attack success rates to near zero, successfully eliminating the contributions of unlearned malicious clients.

Conclusion: FAUN provides an effective and efficient solution for removing malicious client contributions in federated learning, addressing both effectiveness and efficiency gaps in existing unlearning methods while maintaining model performance comparable to full retraining.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is vulnerable to poisoning attacks, where malicious clients upload manipulated updates to degrade the performance of the global model. Although detection methods can identify and remove malicious clients, the model remains affected. Retraining from scratch is effective but costly, and existing unlearning methods remain unsatisfactory in both effectiveness and efficiency. We propose Federated Adversarial Unlearning (FAUN), a lightweight framework that retains only a short window of malicious clients' updates and employs adversarial optimization on a proxy dataset to derive updates that eliminate malicious directions. Applying these updates for a few unlearning rounds, followed by benign fine-tuning, enables fast removal of malicious effects and stable recovery. Experiments on three canonical datasets show that FAUN achieves recovery comparable to retraining while requiring far fewer rounds and reduces attack success rates to near zero, confirming FAUN successfully eliminates the contributions of unlearned clients.

</details>


### [270] [Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network II](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02111)
*Ziran Liu,Wei Wang,Jinhao Wang,Pengcheng Wang,Xinyi Sui,Cihan Ruan,Nam Ling,Wei Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops mathematical tools for analyzing residual Jacobian chains in neural networks by studying singular subspace transport across layers and developing deterministic certificates to verify physical alignment properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how geometric and spectral information is transported through deep neural network layers, particularly residual Jacobian chains, and to develop rigorous verification methods for physical channel alignment properties.

Method: Uses Cartan-coordinate rigidity and fitted effective-rank windows to study dominant singular subspace transport across layers. Develops Physical Alignment Matrix decomposition (core + overlap + noise) and establishes deterministic margin-based certificates with explicit perturbation tests.

Result: Provides deterministic bounds on truncation errors, empirical spectral certification against Gibbs-Cartan tail model, and distinction between source-mode and physical channel incidence. Establishes static certificate radius ensuring identical active supports, incidence graphs, and structural properties between full and truncated transport.

Conclusion: The framework enables rigorous geometric and spectral alignment analysis of neural networks with practical applications shown across CNN, language models, and vision/diffusion backbones, though complete membership verification requires checking numerical margin protocols.

Abstract: This paper develops the angular and static-channel component of Geometric and Spectral Alignment for residual Jacobian chains. Starting from Cartan-coordinate rigidity and fitted effective-rank windows, we study how dominant singular subspaces are transported across adjacent layers and how the resulting finite matrices can be displayed in physical channel coordinates.
  The main results are deterministic, margin-verified results. We bound the error between full interface transport and its dominant-window truncation, add fitted-tail errors so that empirical spectra can be certified against the Gibbs--Cartan tail model, and distinguish source-mode incidence from fully physical input-output channel incidence. Given row groups and active supports, the Physical Alignment Matrix decomposes orthogonally as core plus overlap plus noise. Active-column gaps, pairwise overlap margins, and noise bounds combine into a static certificate radius under which the full transport and the truncated transport induce the same active supports, pairwise incidence graph, SRS sets, hub columns, and core/overlap/noise masks. The finer SC/SA/ST labels of the Invariant Channel Mapping require additional row-energy and profile-correlation margins, stated as explicit perturbation tests.
  The empirical section reports the matrices and block-energy heatmaps that measure these certificate quantities across CNNs, language models, and vision/diffusion backbones. The figures are interpreted as finite-dimensional measurements; complete membership in the Physical GSA certificate domain requires checking the numerical margin protocol stated in Section 10.

</details>


### [271] [Statistical Consistency and Generalization of Contrastive Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02116)
*Yuanfan Li,Xiyuan Wei,Tianbao Yang,Yiming Ying*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified statistical learning theory for contrastive representation learning that addresses key theoretical limitations including statistical consistency, better generalization bounds that improve with more negative samples, and theoretical analysis of retrieval performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contrastive representation learning is foundational to modern models but suffers from three key theoretical limitations: unclear statistical consistency, generalization bounds that deteriorate with more negative samples (contrary to empirical evidence), and limited theoretical understanding of retrieval performance.

Method: Developed a unified statistical learning theory for CRL that: 1) evaluates retrieval quality using AUC-type population criterion, 2) proves statistical consistency of contrastive loss with optimal ranking, 3) establishes calibration-style inequality relating excess contrastive risk to retrieval suboptimality, 4) derives generalization bounds for both supervised and self-supervised objectives with explicit dependence on number of negative samples (m) and anchor points (n).

Result: Proved statistical consistency of contrastive loss, derived generalization bounds of O(1/m + 1/√n) for supervised and O(1/√m + 1/√n) for self-supervised objectives, explained empirical advantages of large negative sets, revealed explicit trade-off between m and n, and conducted extensive experiments on large-scale vision-language models confirming theoretical predictions.

Conclusion: The paper provides a comprehensive statistical theory for contrastive representation learning that resolves major theoretical gaps, explains empirical phenomena, offers practical guidance on balancing negative samples and anchor points, and provides theoretical justification for modern contrastive learning practices.

Abstract: Contrastive representation learning (CRL) underpins many modern foundation models. Despite recent theoretical progress, existing analyses suffer from several key limitations: (i) the statistical consistency of CRL remains poorly understood; (ii) available generalization bounds deteriorate as the number of negative samples increases, contradicting the empirical benefits of large negative sets; and (iii) the retrieval performance of CRL has received limited theoretical attention.
  In this paper, we develop a unified statistical learning theory for CRL. For downstream tasks, we evaluate retrieval quality using an AUC-type population criterion and show that the contrastive loss is \emph{statistically consistent} with optimal ranking. We further establish a \emph{calibration-style inequality} that quantitatively relates excess contrastive risk to excess retrieval suboptimality. For upstream training, we study both supervised and self-supervised contrastive objectives and derive generalization bounds of order $O(1/m + 1/\sqrt{n})$ and $O(1/\sqrt{m} + 1/\sqrt{n})$, respectively, where $m$ denotes the number of negative samples and $n$ the number of anchor points. These bounds not only explain the empirical advantages of large negative sets but also reveal an explicit trade-off between $m$ and $n$. Extensive experiments on large-scale vision--language models corroborate our theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [272] [STABLEVAL: Disagreement-Aware and Stable Evaluation of AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02122)
*Akash Bonagiri,Gerard Janno Anderias,Saee Patil,Angelina Lai,Devang Borkar,Gezheng Kang,Ishant Gandhi,Setareh Rafatirad,Houman Homayoun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STABLEVAL is a disagreement-aware AI evaluation framework that models annotator reliability and item ambiguity for stable system rankings, outperforming majority vote which suffers from instability under annotator disagreement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard majority vote aggregation in human evaluation is fragile due to annotator disagreement, bias, and variability. It discards important information about annotator reliability and item-level ambiguity, leading to unstable system rankings that can change with different annotator subsets.

Method: STABLEVAL models latent item correctness and annotator-specific confusion patterns to produce posterior expected item credit and calibrated agent-level scores. Unlike traditional label-denoising methods like Dawid-Skene, it's explicitly designed for stable and uncertainty-aware system evaluation rather than hard label recovery. The framework treats ranking stability as a first-class objective and analyzes how aggregation methods preserve or distort underlying annotator behavior.

Result: Across controlled synthetic experiments and multiple real-world human-annotated benchmarks, majority vote exhibits increasing score error and ranking instability under annotator heterogeneity and adversarial noise. In contrast, STABLEVAL yields more stable and statistically grounded system rankings.

Conclusion: Modeling disagreement is essential for robust and reproducible AI evaluation. STABLEVAL provides a principled approach that addresses the limitations of majority vote aggregation by explicitly considering annotator reliability and item ambiguity, leading to more stable system comparisons.

Abstract: Human evaluation remains the primary standard for assessing modern AI systems, yet annotator disagreement, bias, and variability make system rankings fragile under standard majority vote aggregation. Majority vote discards annotator reliability and item-level ambiguity, often yielding unstable comparisons across annotator subsets. We introduce STABLEVAL, a disagreement-aware evaluation framework that models latent item correctness and annotator-specific confusion patterns to produce posterior expected item credit and calibrated agent-level scores. Unlike label-denoising approaches such as Dawid-Skene, STABLEVAL is explicitly designed for stable and uncertainty-aware system evaluation rather than hard label recovery. We formalize ranking stability as a first-class evaluation objective and analyze how aggregation methods preserve or distort underlying annotator behavior. Across controlled synthetic experiments and multiple real-world human-annotated benchmarks, majority vote exhibits increasing score error and ranking instability under annotator heterogeneity and adversarial noise, while STABLEVAL yields more stable and statistically grounded system rankings. These results demonstrate that modeling disagreement is essential for robust and reproducible AI evaluation.

</details>


### [273] [Boundary Mass and the Soft-to-Hard Limit in Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02124)
*Reza Rastegar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies the singularity in softmax-routed mixture-of-experts models at zero temperature, showing the limit is controlled by a thin geometric layer around routing interfaces rather than the full input space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Softmax-routed MoE models approach hard routing as temperature → 0, but this limit is singular near routing ties. The paper aims to understand this singularity and its implications for risk bounds and optimization landscape.

Method: Theoretical analysis using coarea/tube estimates under smoothness and transversality assumptions to study boundary mass (probability of near-ties in router scores). Proves Γ-convergence of soft to hard objectives under compactness and uniform margin control.

Result: Boundary mass is linear in slab width with leading constant given by surface integral over routing interface. Quantitative soft-to-hard risk bounds. In teacher-student setting: conditional landscape-transfer principle showing small-temperature soft routing inherits favorable properties. Local symmetry-breaking mechanism in Gaussian example.

Conclusion: Zero-temperature limit is controlled by thin geometric layer around routing interfaces, not full input space. This geometric understanding enables landscape-transfer results and reveals local symmetry-breaking aligned with teacher separators.

Abstract: Softmax-routed mixture-of-experts models approach hard routing as the temperature tends to zero, but this limit is singular near routing ties. This paper studies that singularity at the population level for squared-loss MoE regression. The central object is the \emph{boundary mass}, namely the probability that the top two router scores are separated by only a small margin. Under smoothness and transversality assumptions on the router and input law, we prove coarea/tube estimates showing that this mass is linear in the slab width, with leading constant given by a surface integral over the routing interface in the binary case. These estimates yield quantitative soft-to-hard risk bounds and, under compactness and uniform margin control, $Γ$-convergence of the soft objectives to the hard-routing objective. The main conclusion is that the zero-temperature limit is controlled by a thin geometric layer around routing interfaces, not by the full input space. We then use this geometric core in two more model-dependent directions. In a teacher--student setting, we prove a conditional landscape-transfer principle showing that, when the profiled hard-routing problem has favorable identifiability and curvature and the relevant derivatives transfer at boundary-layer scale, small-temperature soft routing inherits approximate teacher recovery and strict-saddle behavior away from teacher-equivalent partitions. We also give a reduced two-expert Gaussian calculation that illustrates a local symmetry-breaking mechanism aligned with the teacher separator.

</details>


### [274] [LUMINA: A Grid Foundation Model for Benchmarking AC Optimal Power Flow Surrogate Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02133)
*Hongwei Jin,Keunju Song,Zeeshan Memon,Yijiang Li,Stefano Fenu,Hongseok Kim,Liang Zhao,Kibaek Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LUMINA-Bench is a benchmark suite for ACOPF surrogate learning that addresses topology generalization issues, evaluates different architectures and training objectives, and provides open-source tools for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ACOPF surrogate models fail to generalize across network topologies, which is critical for deployment on unseen grids and for operational what-if studies. There's a lack of comprehensive benchmarks for evaluating topology generalization capabilities.

Method: Created LUMINA-Bench benchmark suite covering multi-topology pretraining, transfer, and adaptation. Evaluates homogeneous/heterogeneous architectures in single/multi-topology settings using unified metrics. Compares constraint-aware training objectives: MSE, augmented Lagrangian, and violation-based Lagrangian losses.

Result: Provides a comprehensive evaluation framework with metrics capturing both predictive accuracy and physics-informed constraint violations. Characterizes accuracy-robustness trade-offs across different settings. Open-sources data processing, training, and evaluation frameworks.

Conclusion: LUMINA-Bench fills a critical gap in ACOPF surrogate learning by providing standardized benchmarks for topology generalization, enabling better evaluation of feasibility-aware OPF surrogates and accelerating future research in this area.

Abstract: AC optimal power flow (ACOPF) is foundational yet computationally expensive in power grid operations, driving learning-based surrogates for large-scale grid analysis. These surrogates, however, often fail to generalize across network topologies, a critical gap for deployment on grids not seen during training and for routine operational what-if studies. We introduce LUMINA-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark suite for ACOPF surrogate learning covering multi-topology pretraining, transfer, and adaptation. The benchmark evaluates homogeneous and heterogeneous architectures under single- and multi-topology learning settings using unified metrics that capture both predictive accuracy and physics-informed constraint violations. We additionally compare constraint-aware training objectives, including MSE, augmented Lagrangian, and violation-based Lagrangian losses, to characterize accuracy-robustness trade-offs across settings. Data processing, training, and evaluation frameworks are open-sourced as the LUMINA suite to support reproducibility and accelerate future research on feasibility-aware OPF surrogates.

</details>


### [275] [On the Optimal Sample Complexity of Offline Multi-Armed Bandits with KL Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02141)
*Kaixuan Ji,Qiwei Di,Heyang Zhao,Qingyue Zhao,Quanquan Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides a nearly complete characterization of sample complexity for KL-regularized offline multi-armed bandits, showing sharp upper and lower bounds that match across all regularization strengths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KL regularization is widely used in offline decision-making but its exact sample complexity remains largely uncharacterized. The paper aims to fill this gap by providing precise sample complexity bounds for KL-regularized offline learning in multi-armed bandits.

Method: The authors analyze KL-PCB algorithm and provide sharp sample complexity analysis. They examine two regimes: large regularization (η = Õ(ε⁻¹)) and small regularization (η = Ω̃(ε⁻¹)). They establish both upper bounds and matching lower bounds across the entire range of regularization strengths.

Result: KL-PCB achieves sample complexity Õ(ηSAC^{π^*}/ε) under large regularization and Ω̃(SAC^{π^*}/ε²) under small regularization. The paper provides matching lower bounds that confirm these results are tight, providing a nearly complete characterization of the sample complexity landscape.

Conclusion: The research successfully characterizes the sample complexity of KL-regularized offline multi-armed bandits, showing how regularization strength affects learning efficiency and providing fundamental limits for this important class of problems.

Abstract: Kullback-Leibler (KL) regularization is widely used in offline decision-making and offers several benefits, motivating recent work on the sample complexity of offline learning with respect to KL-regularized performance metrics. Nevertheless, the exact sample complexity of KL-regularized offline learning remains largely from fully characterized. In this paper, we study this question in the setting of multi-armed bandits (MABs). We provide a sharp analysis of KL-PCB (Zhao et al., 2026), showing that it achieves a sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(ηSAC^{π^*}/ε)$ under large regularization $η= \tilde{O}(ε^{-1})$, and a sample complexity of $\tildeΩ(SAC^{π^*}/ε^2)$ under small regularization $η= \tildeΩ(ε^{-1})$, where $η$ is the regularization parameter, $S$ is the number of contexts, $A$ is the number of arms, $C^{π^*}$ policy coverage coefficient at the optimal policy $π^*$, $ε$ is the desired sub-optimality, and $\tilde{O}$ and $\tildeΩ$ hide all poly-logarithmic factors. We further provide a pair of sharper sample complexity lower bounds, which matches the upper bounds over the entire range of regularization strengths. Overall, our results provide a nearly complete characterization of offline multi-armed bandits with KL regularization.

</details>


### [276] [Personalized Federated Learning for Gradient Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02143)
*Dongwon Kim,Gyuejeong Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: pFLAlign: A gradient alignment framework for personalized federated learning that maintains client-specific information during local training and aggregation to improve personalization performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current personalized federated learning (pFL) often fails to reliably preserve personalized information due to high variance gradients from limited/heterogeneous client data and aggregation-induced distortion of client-specific optimization directions.

Method: pFLAlign framework with two complementary mechanisms: 1) Adapts local gradient directions to reduce variance during client-side optimization, 2) Mitigates aggregation-induced distortion by realigning the global model with each client's personalized direction.

Result: The approach consistently improves personalization performance and training stability, achieving state-of-the-art results in experiments and ablation studies.

Conclusion: The proposed pFLAlign effectively addresses challenges in personalized federated learning by preserving client-specific information through gradient alignment during both local training and aggregation, backed by PAC Bayesian theoretical analysis.

Abstract: Personalized federated learning (pFL) aims to adapt models to client specific data distributions, yet it often fails to reliably preserve personalized information. Local training is hindered by high variance gradients induced by limited and heterogeneous client data, while aggregation further distorts client specific optimization directions. To address these challenges, we propose pFLAlign, a gradient alignment framework to maintain client specific information during both local training and aggregation. pFLAlign consists of two complementary mechanisms: one adapts local gradient directions to reduce variance during client side optimization, and the other mitigates aggregation induced distortion by realigning the global model with each client's personalized direction. Theoretically, we derive pFLAlign from a PAC Bayesian analysis, which reveals how personalized gradient alignment preserves client specific information. Our experiments and ablation studies show that pFLAlign consistently improves personalization performance and training stability, achieving state of the art results.

</details>


### [277] [Projection-Free Transformers via Gaussian Kernel Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02144)
*Debarshi Kundu,Archisman Ghosh,Swaroop Ghosh,Vasant Honavar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gaussian Kernel Attention (GKA) replaces learned QKV projections with a simple Gaussian RBF kernel, using only a bandwidth parameter per head and maintaining compatibility with standard Transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether learned linear projections (Q, K, V) in self-attention are necessary, or if they can be replaced by a simpler similarity-based diffusion operator, potentially reducing complexity and improving interpretability.

Method: Introduces Gaussian Kernel Attention (GKA) which computes token affinities directly using a Gaussian radial basis function (RBF) kernel applied to per-head token features. Each head learns only a bandwidth parameter σ_h, while maintaining a single output projection W_O. For causal language modeling, implements causal masking and sliding-window constraints by masking and renormalizing the Gaussian kernel.

Result: At depth 20, GKA model achieves stable training with 0.42× parameters and 0.49× training FLOPs compared to standard attention, exhibits near-zero train-validation gap, and demonstrates competitive behavior on benchmarks (albeit with higher bits-per-byte at this compute scale). Works in both vision and language modeling settings.

Conclusion: GKA provides a minimal, interpretable attention mechanism with explicit locality scale, offering an additional dimension in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for Transformer design. Links modern Transformers to classical non-local filtering and kernel smoothing methods.

Abstract: Self-attention in Transformers is typically implemented as $\mathrm{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d})V$, where $Q=XW_Q$, $K=XW_K$, and $V=XW_V$ are learned linear projections of the input $X$. We ask whether these learned projections are necessary, or whether they can be replaced by a simpler similarity-based diffusion operator. We introduce \textbf{Gaussian Kernel Attention} (GKA), a drop-in replacement for dot-product attention that computes token affinities directly using a Gaussian radial basis function (RBF) kernel applied to per-head token features. Each head learns only a bandwidth parameter $σ_h$, while a single output projection $W_O$ preserves compatibility with the standard Transformer interface. GKA can be interpreted as normalized kernel regression over tokens, linking modern Transformer architectures to classical non-local filtering and kernel smoothing methods. We evaluate GKA in both vision and language modeling settings. For autoregressive language modeling within the \texttt{nanochat} framework, we implement causal masking and sliding-window constraints by masking and renormalizing the Gaussian kernel. At depth 20, a GKA model with $0.42\times$ the parameters and $0.49\times$ the total training FLOPs of a standard attention baseline trains stably, exhibits a near-zero train-validation gap, and demonstrates competitive behavior on standard benchmarks, albeit with higher bits-per-byte (BPB) at this compute scale. Overall, GKA provides a minimal, interpretable attention mechanism with an explicit locality scale, offering a dimension in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for Transformer design.

</details>


### [278] [Combining Trained Models in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02159)
*Ujjwal Patil,Javad Ghofrani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A systematic review of 15 empirical studies on pretrained knowledge reuse in deep reinforcement learning reveals positive results when source/target tasks share structure or have alignment mechanisms, sparse evidence for ensemble/federated methods, and rare compute-matched comparisons that weaken efficiency claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DRL suffers from high sample cost and weak transfer beyond training settings, with fragmented literature on knowledge reuse methods (transfer, distillation, ensemble, federated). Published comparisons are hard to interpret due to differing tasks, baselines, and compute budgets.

Method: PRISMA-guided systematic review of 589 records from IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, and citation tracing, screening 570 unique records, assessing 89 full texts, resulting in 15 empirical studies for qualitative analysis across source-target similarity, diversity among reused models, and fairness of comparisons against from-scratch baselines.

Result: Three patterns: 1) Positive results concentrate where source/target tasks share substantial structure or have explicit gating/alignment mechanisms. 2) Evidence for ensembles and federated aggregation is promising but sparse and limited to narrow settings. 3) Compute-matched comparisons are rare, weakening efficiency gain claims over stronger single-agent baselines.

Conclusion: The paper contributes a narrow, consistent review scope, study-level synthesis of empirical evidence, and a provisional independence spectrum as a hypothesis for future benchmarking rather than a validated metric, highlighting need for better comparison methodologies.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has delivered strong results in domains such as Atari and Go, but it still suffers from high sample cost and weak transfer beyond the training setting. A common response is to reuse information from previously trained models through transfer, distillation, ensemble methods, or federated training instead of learning each target task from random initialization. The literature on these mechanisms is fragmented, and published comparisons are hard to interpret because tasks, baselines, and compute budgets differ.
  This paper presents a PRISMA-guided systematic review of empirical studies on pretrained knowledge reuse in DRL. Starting from 589 records retrieved from IEEE Xplore, the ACM Digital Library, and citation tracing, we screened 570 unique records and assessed 89 full texts. After applying the final eligibility criteria, 15 empirical studies remained in the main synthesis. We analyzed them qualitatively across three factors: source-target similarity, diversity among reused models, and the fairness of comparisons against from-scratch baselines.
  Three patterns recur across the surviving corpus. First, positive results are concentrated in settings where source and target tasks share substantial structure or where the method includes an explicit gating or alignment mechanism. Second, evidence for ensembles and federated aggregation is promising but sparse and mostly limited to narrow settings. Third, compute-matched comparisons are rare, which weakens claims about efficiency gains over stronger single-agent baselines. The paper contributes a narrower and internally consistent review scope, a study-level synthesis of empirical evidence, and a provisional independence spectrum that should be treated as a hypothesis for future benchmarking rather than a validated metric.

</details>


### [279] [Experience Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning for Large-scale UAV Teams in Hazardous Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02165)
*Qinwei Huang,Rui Zuo,Simon Khan,Qinru Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Experience-constrained FRL for UAV teams shows that increasing learner participation doesn't necessarily improve performance; performance depends more on experience reuse strategies and gradient transition experiences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In hazardous UAV environments, experience generation is severely constrained by safety, energy, and mission duration, breaking the conventional federated learning assumption that more participation improves performance.

Method: Proposed EC-HFRL (Experience-Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning) where clusters act as federated agents and intra-cluster learners reuse a shared experience pool, analyzing the effects of participation vs. experience reuse strategies.

Result: Increasing participation doesn't necessarily improve learning performance; performance is strongly associated with experience reuse strategy and dominance of key gradient transition experiences within clusters. Minibatch size determines replay exposure, while higher intra-cluster participation increases reuse level.

Conclusion: Performance regimes in experience-constrained FRL are associated with learning signal structure rather than federated aggregation effects, showing learner participation has a limited, secondary role when experience is constrained.

Abstract: Conventional federated learning assumes that greater learner participation improves training performance, by leveraging abundant, independently generated local data. However, in federated reinforcement learning (FRL) for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) teams in hazardous environments where experience generation is severely constrained by safety considerations, energy limitations, and mission duration, this assumption may break. This work introduces Experience-Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning (EC-HFRL), a framework in which clusters act as federated learning agents, while multiple intra-cluster learners represent parallel learning resources that reuse a shared experience pool. We show that increasing participation does not necessarily improve learning performance. Instead, learning performance is strongly associated with experience reuse strategy and the dominance of key analytically identified gradient transition experiences within a cluster. In particular, minibatch size primarily determines effective replay exposure, while higher intra-cluster participation increases reuse level. Empirical results demonstrate that the performance regimes are strongly associated with the structure of the learning signal, rather than federated aggregation effects, clarifying the limited and secondary role of learner participation in experience-constrained FRL.

</details>


### [280] [Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients for Reliable Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02167)
*Soyeon Kim,Seongwoo Lim,Kyowoon Lee,Jaesik Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MA-GIG improves Integrated Gradients by constructing attribution paths in latent space using a VAE, reducing off-manifold noise and producing more faithful explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrated Gradients can yield unreliable explanations when integration paths pass through regions with noisy gradients, and previous guided methods still produce intermediate inputs that deviate from the data manifold.

Method: Proposes Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients (MA-GIG) which constructs attribution paths in the latent space of a pre-trained variational autoencoder, then decodes intermediate latent states to bias paths toward the learned generative manifold.

Result: MA-GIG reduces off-manifold noise and outperforms prior path-based attribution methods across multiple datasets and classifiers, producing faithful explanations by aggregating gradients on path features proximal to the input.

Conclusion: By aligning attribution paths with the data manifold in latent space, MA-GIG provides more reliable feature attribution for deep neural networks compared to existing Integrated Gradients variants.

Abstract: Feature attribution is central to diagnosing and trusting deep neural networks, and Integrated Gradients (IG) is widely used due to its axiomatic properties. However, IG can yield unreliable explanations when the integration path between a baseline and the input passes through regions with noisy gradients. While Guided Integrated Gradients reduces this sensitivity by adaptively updating low-gradient-magnitude features, input-space guidance still produces intermediate inputs that deviate from the data manifold. To address this limitation, we propose \emph{Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients} (MA-GIG), which constructs attribution paths in the latent space of a pre-trained variational autoencoder. By decoding intermediate latent states, MA-GIG biases the path toward the learned generative manifold and reduces exposure to implausible input-space regions. Through qualitative and quantitative evaluations, we demonstrate that MA-GIG produces faithful explanations by aggregating gradients on path features proximal to the input. Consequently, our method reduces off-manifold noise and outperforms prior path-based attribution methods across multiple datasets and classifiers. Our code is available at https://github.com/leekwoon/ma-gig/.

</details>


### [281] [Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training for Long-Tailed Robustness via Geometric Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02183)
*Guanmeng Xian,Ning Yang,Philip S. Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training (MCAT) improves adversarial robustness on long-tailed datasets by enforcing semantic validity of adversarial examples and promoting balanced geometric separation across classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial training works well on balanced datasets but suffers from degraded robustness on long-tailed distributions, where tail classes exhibit high robust error and unstable decision boundaries.

Method: Proposes MCAT framework that: 1) enforces semantic validity by penalizing deviations from class-conditional manifolds in feature space, 2) promotes balanced geometric separation via ETF-inspired regularization, 3) theoretically links geometric separation to lower bounds on adversarially robust margins.

Result: Extensive experiments on standard long-tailed benchmarks show consistent improvements in overall, balanced, and tail-class adversarial robustness.

Conclusion: MCAT provides a unified framework that addresses the robustness degradation in long-tailed distributions by combining manifold constraints with geometric separation regularization, offering theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements.

Abstract: Adversarial training is effective on balanced datasets, but its robustness degrades under longtailed class distributions, where tail classes suffer high robust error and unstable decision boundaries. We propose Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training (MCAT), a unified framework that enforces the semantic validity of adversarial examples by penalizing deviations from class-conditional manifolds in feature space, while promoting balanced geometric separation across classes via an ETF-inspired regularization. We provide theoretical results that link geometric separation to lower bounds on adversarially robust margins, and show that manifold-constrained adversarial risk upperbounds robust risk on high-density semantic regions. Extensive experiments on standard longtailed benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in overall, balanced, and tail-class adversarial robustness.

</details>


### [282] [KANs need curvature: penalties for compositional smoothness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02190)
*James Bagrow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) suffer from high-curvature oscillations in activations despite good accuracy, making them hard to interpret. The authors develop a basis-agnostic curvature penalty that maintains accuracy while producing smoother, more interpretable activations, with theoretical bounds showing how composition affects curvature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KANs offer good accuracy and interpretability through learnable univariate activation functions, but well-fitting KANs exhibit pathologically high-curvature oscillations that undermine interpretability. Standard regularization doesn't prevent this, creating a bottleneck in scientific machine learning where the trade-off between accuracy and interpretability limits practical utility.

Method: The authors derive a basis-agnostic curvature penalty for KAN activations. They account for how function composition shapes curvature and prove an upper bound on the full model's curvature relative to the curvature penalty. This theoretical insight motivates richer forms of penalties beyond simple regularization.

Result: Penalized KAN models can maintain accuracy while achieving substantially smoother activations. The theoretical analysis shows how function composition affects curvature and provides bounds that justify more sophisticated regularization approaches.

Conclusion: By developing effective curvature penalties for KANs, the work improves interpretability without sacrificing accuracy, strengthening KANs as practical tools for both prediction and scientific insight in machine learning applications.

Abstract: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) offer a potent combination of accuracy and interpretability, thanks to their compositions of learnable univariate activation functions. However, the activations of well-fitting KANs tend to exhibit pathologically high-curvature oscillations, making them difficult to interpret, and standard regularization penalties do not prevent this. Here we derive a basis-agnostic curvature penalty and show that penalized models can maintain accuracy while achieving substantially smoother activations. Accounting for how function composition shapes curvature, we prove an upper bound on the full model's curvature relative to the curvature penalty, and use this to motivate richer forms of penalties. Scientific machine learning is increasingly bottlenecked by the trade-off between accuracy and interpretability. Results such as ours that improve interpretability without sacrificing accuracy will further strengthen KANs as a practical tool for both prediction and insight.

</details>


### [283] [DurableUn: Quantization-Induced Recovery Attacks in Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02196)
*Abdullah Ahmad Khan,Ferdous Sohel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INT4 quantization systematically restores forgotten content in unlearned LLMs, creating a trilemma between forgetting ability, retention accuracy, and quantization robustness. The paper proposes DURABLEUN-SAF as a quantization-aware solution that maintains unlearning effectiveness across precision levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing machine unlearning evaluations assume identical precision at unlearning and deployment, but production LLMs are typically deployed at low-bit precision (INT4/INT8). This oversight creates a vulnerability where INT4 quantization can restore forgotten content even when models pass compliance audits at higher precision.

Method: 1) First systematic study of unlearning robustness under INT4 quantization in NF4+LoRA regime; 2) Evaluated seven unlearning methods on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct across three datasets; 3) Identified FA-RA-Q-INT4 trilemma; 4) Proposed DURABLEUN-SAF with quantization-aware objective using Straight-Through Estimator gradients through INT4 rounding.

Result: INT4 quantization induces recovery of forgotten content up to 22x, worsening with dataset difficulty. No existing method achieves strong forgetting, high utility, and quantization robustness simultaneously. DURABLEUN-SAF is the only method achieving stable empirical durability certificate across BF16, INT8, and INT4 precision levels (cert rate=3/3 vs SalUn's 1/3).

Conclusion: INT4 quantization presents a critical vulnerability in machine unlearning systems, necessitating Q-INT4 as a standard evaluation metric alongside forgetting ability and retention accuracy. The proposed DURABLEUN-SAF provides a viable solution through quantization-aware training, establishing the first method to maintain unlearning effectiveness across different deployment precisions.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove specified training data to satisfy privacy regulations such as GDPR. However, existing evaluations assume identical precision at unlearning and deployment, overlooking that production LLMs are deployed at low-bit precision. We show that INT4 quantization systematically restores forgotten content even when models pass compliance audits at bfloat16 (BF16), we term this the quantization recovery attack (QRA). We conduct the first systematic study of unlearning robustness under adapter-space INT4 quantization in the NF4+LoRA regime, evaluating seven methods on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct across TOFU, MUSE-News, and WikiBio-WPU. INT8 is benign; INT4 induces recovery of up to 22x, worsening with dataset difficulty. We identify the FA-RA-Q-INT4 trilemma: no method simultaneously achieves strong forgetting, high utility, and quantization robustness. A dense Pareto sweep reveals a sharp phase transition once robustness is achieved, retaining accuracy collapses regardless of further tuning. To address this, we propose DURABLEUN-SAF (Sharpness-Aware Forgetting), a quantization-aware objective using Straight-Through Estimator gradients through INT4 rounding. DURABLEUN-SAF is the only method to achieve a stable empirical (0.047, {BF16, INT8, INT4})- durability certificate: Q-INT4= 0.043 +- 0.002, cert rate= 3/3, versus SalUn's cert rate= 1/3 at its own published hyperparameters. We call for Q-INT4 to be adopted as a standard evaluation metric alongside FA and RA.

</details>


### [284] [Demographic-Aware Transfer Learning for Sleep Stage Classification in Clinical Polysomnography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02245)
*S M Asif Hossain,Shruti Kshirsagar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Demographic-stratified fine-tuning for sleep stage classification outperforms single generalized models by adapting to variations in gender, age, and OSA severity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated sleep stage classification typically uses a single population-agnostic model, but sleep patterns differ substantially across demographic factors like gender, age, and OSA severity, making one-size-fits-all approaches suboptimal for diverse clinical populations.

Method: Two-stage training with demographic stratification and transfer learning: pretrain a convolutional recurrent model on full population, then fine-tune independently for demographic subgroups based on gender, age, and AHI severity according to AASM standards.

Result: 35 of 37 fine-tuned models outperformed the baseline, with Cohen's kappa improvements ranging from 0.9% to 12.9%, demonstrating substantially higher accuracy with demographic-stratified approaches.

Conclusion: Demographic-stratified fine-tuning leads to more accurate sleep stage classification than using a single population-agnostic model, providing a practical approach for personalized sleep assessment in clinical populations.

Abstract: Automated sleep stage classification typically employs a single population-agnostic model, disregarding established demographic variations in sleep architecture. Sleep patterns, however, differ substantially across gender, age, and obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) severity, indicating that a onesize-fits all approach may be suboptimal for diverse clinical populations. In this paper, we propose a two stage training strategy based on demographic stratification and transfer learning framework. We first pretrains a convolutional recurrent model on the full population and then fine tunes it independently for demographic subgroups defined by gender, age, and Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) severity according to the AASM clinical standard. Using the DREAMT dataset comprising 100 clinical subjects and 7 PSG channels, we evaluate 37 fine-tuned configurations across single-axis and two-way demographic combinations. Results demonstrate that 35 of the 37 fine-tuned models outperform the baseline, with Cohen's kappa improvements ranging from 0.9 to 12.9%. These findings indicate that stratified fine tuning tailored to specific patient demographics yields substantially more accurate sleep staging than a single generalized model, offering a practical and clinically grounded paradigm for personalized sleep assessment.

</details>


### [285] [Break the Block: Dynamic-size Reasoning Blocks for Diffusion Large Language Models via Monotonic Entropy Descent with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02263)
*Yan Jiang,Ruihong Qiu,Zi Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework called b1 that enhances diffusion language models' reasoning by learning dynamic block sizes instead of fixed blocks, improving coherence through a monotonic entropy descent objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
