Motivation: Recent diffusion LLMs use fixed-size block generation which is suboptimal: (1) different reasoning tasks need different optimal block sizes, making one-size-fits-all ineffective; (2) rigid block partitioning breaks logical flow and reduces reasoning coherence within tasks.

Method: Proposes b1, a post-training framework that learns dynamic-size reasoning blocks using a Monotonic Entropy Descent objective with reinforcement learning. It's implemented as a plug-and-play module compatible with existing dLLM post-training algorithms.

Result: Extensive experiments across various reasoning benchmarks show b1 consistently improves over existing fixed-size block baselines. The method identifies that correct reasoning follows consistent descending entropy trends while incorrect reasoning shows fluctuating patterns.

Conclusion: Dynamic block sizing through monotonic entropy descent significantly enhances reasoning coherence in diffusion language models, addressing the limitations of fixed block approaches. The framework is effective and easily integrated with existing systems.

Abstract: Recent diffusion large language models (dLLMs) have demonstrated both effectiveness and efficiency in reasoning via a block-based semi-autoregressive generation paradigm. Despite their progress, the fixed-size block generations remain a critical bottleneck for effective and coherent reasoning. 1. From a global perspective, different reasoning tasks would correspond to different optimal decoding block sizes, which makes a ``one-size-fits-all'' assumption ineffective. 2. Even within a single reasoning task, the rigid block partitioning would break the logical flow and reduce reasoning coherence. Through empirical observations, we reveal that for block-wise entropy, incorrect reasoning exhibits a fluctuating and unsteady trend between blocks, whereas the correctly generated tasks follow a consistent descending trend. Therefore, this paper proposes b1, a novel post-training framework for dLLMs that learns dynamic-size reasoning blocks via a Monotonic Entropy Descent objective with reinforcement learning to enhance reasoning coherence.b1 integrates seamlessly as a plug-and-play module with existing dLLM's post-training algorithms. Extensive experiments across various reasoning benchmarks showcase b1's consistent improvement over existing fixed-size block baselines. Our code has been released at https://github.com/YanJiangJerry/Block-R1.

</details>


### [286] [HELIX: Hybrid Encoding with Learnable Identity and Cross-dimensional Synthesis for Time Series Imputation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02278)
*Fengming Zhang,Wenjie Du,Huan Zhang,Ke Yu,Shen Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HELIX introduces learnable feature identities as persistent embeddings to capture cross-feature correlations for time series imputation, achieving state-of-the-art performance by learning feature dependencies from temporal co-variation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attention-based methods for time series imputation inefficiently rediscover feature relationships at each layer without persistent anchors, while graph-based methods rely on predefined topology and assume homogeneous spatial relationships, limiting their effectiveness on datasets with mixed spatial and semantic features.

Method: HELIX assigns each feature a learnable feature identity (persistent embedding) that captures intrinsic semantic properties throughout the network, learning arbitrary feature dependencies end-to-end from temporal co-variation and integrating with hybrid temporal-feature attention.

Result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance, surpassing all 16 baselines on 5 public datasets across 21 experimental settings. Mechanistic analysis shows HELIX aligns learned feature identities and dependencies with latent physical and semantic structure progressively across layers.

Conclusion: HELIX effectively translates cross-feature structure into imputation accuracy by maintaining consistent feature representations through learnable identities, naturally handling datasets with mixed spatial and semantic features while outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Time series imputation benefits from leveraging cross-feature correlations, yet existing attention-based methods re-discover feature relationships at each layer, lacking persistent anchors to maintain consistent representations. To address this, we propose HELIX, which assigns each feature a learnable feature identity, a persistent embedding that captures intrinsic semantic properties throughout the network. Unlike graph-based methods that rely on predefined topology and assume homogeneous spatial relationships, HELIX learns arbitrary feature dependencies end-to-end from temporal co-variation, naturally handling datasets where features mix spatial locations with semantic variables. Integrated with hybrid temporal-feature attention, HELIX achieves the state-of-the-art performance, surpassing all 16 baselines on 5 public datasets across 21 experimental settings in our evaluation. Furthermore, our mechanistic analysis reveals that HELIX aligns learned feature identities and dependencies with latent physical and semantic structure progressively across layers, demonstrating that it more effectively translates cross-feature structure into imputation accuracy.

</details>


### [287] [Variational Matrix-Learning Fourier Networks for Parametric Multiphysics Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02280)
*Xinyu Li,Jianhua Zhang,Liang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VMLFN is a physics-informed neural network method that uses variational matrix learning for efficient parametric multiphysics surrogate modeling, achieving accurate predictions with substantial speedup over conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multiphysics simulation is computationally expensive for parametric design exploration in chiplet-based design, requiring repeated finite-element solutions of PDE-governed problems. Conventional physics-informed neural networks have training challenges.

Method: Proposes variational matrix-learning Fourier network (VMLFN) with log-space sine neural representation, frequency-dependent decay regulation, embedded Dirichlet boundary conditions, and variational formulation to convert physics-informed training into a linear matrix-solving problem. Includes heuristic frequency-scanning algorithm to select problem-adaptive maximum frequency.

Result: Validated on heat conduction, solid mechanics, and Helmholtz wave propagation problems across five benchmark cases. VMLFN delivers accurate full-field predictions with substantial speedup over conventional physics-informed neural networks and repeated finite-element simulations.

Conclusion: VMLFN provides an efficient method for parametric multiphysics surrogate modeling that avoids computational challenges of traditional approaches while maintaining accuracy, making it suitable for system-technology co-optimization in chiplet-based design.

Abstract: Multiphysics simulation is critical for system-technology co-optimization (STCO) in chiplet-based design, but repeated finite-element solutions of PDE-governed problems are computationally expensive in parametric design exploration. This paper proposes a variational matrix-learning Fourier network (VMLFN) for efficient parametric multiphysics surrogate modeling. VMLFN constructs a log-space sine neural representation with randomly sampled spectral frequencies, frequency-dependent decay regulation, and embedded Dirichlet boundary conditions. With fixed hidden-layer parameters, the output-layer weights are determined by reformulating the governing PDEs into variational weak forms and enforcing the stationarity condition of the resulting energy functional. This converts physics-informed training into a linear matrix-solving problem, requiring only first-order derivatives and avoiding both high-order automatic differentiation and penalty-coefficient tuning. A heuristic frequency-scanning algorithm is further introduced to select a problem-adaptive maximum frequency that covers the dominant spectral range of the target problem. The proposed method is validated on heat conduction, solid mechanics, and Helmholtz wave propagation problems. Results from five benchmark cases demonstrate that VMLFN delivers accurate full-field predictions with substantial speedup over conventional physics-informed neural networks and repeated finite-element simulations.

</details>


### [288] [Graph Federated Unlearning for Privacy Preservation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02297)
*Ruotong Ma,Wentao Yu,Qizhou Wang,Jie Yang,Chen Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A graph federated learning approach with machine unlearning capability that effectively removes user information after withdrawal while maintaining overall model performance through orthogonal gradient updates and virtual clients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite GDPR/CCPA regulations allowing users to withdraw from learning systems, graph federated learning still faces privacy leakage where user information persists after quitting and can propagate to central servers and malicious clients. This withdrawal privacy issue has received little attention in GFL.

Method: Two key adjustments: 1) Unlearning updates designed to be orthogonal to gradients from other data to minimize overall performance degradation, 2) Introduction of virtual clients maintained by the central server to preserve graph topology and global embeddings without recovering removed entity information.

Result: Comprehensive experiments under user-withdrawal scenarios show the approach's effectiveness. A novel membership inference framework validates privacy preservation. The method outperforms seven state-of-the-art baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed GFL approach with machine unlearning effectively addresses privacy leakage during user withdrawal while maintaining model performance, providing a practical solution for regulatory compliance in decentralized graph learning systems.

Abstract: Graph federated learning (GFL) facilitates decentralized training on distributed graph data while keeping sensitive user information local, aligning with policies such as GDPR and CCPA that grant users the right to freely join or withdraw from learning systems. However, even decentralized, user information can persist after quitting, potentially propagating to central servers and then redistributing to malicious clients. This privacy leakage during user withdrawal, despite its importance, has received seldom attention in GFL. To fill the gap, we explore the potential of machine unlearning (MU) to thoroughly remove user information. However, classical MU methods are known to degrade overall performance, a problem that is exacerbated in GFL due to local message passing and global model collaboration. To this end, we make two adjustments to mitigate this challenge for GFL. First, we ensure unlearning updates that minimally affect overall performance, steering them in directions orthogonal to the gradients from learning other data. Second, we introduce virtual clients, maintained by the central server, to preserve graph topology and global embeddings without recovering information of removed entities. We conduct comprehensive experiments under a representative user-withdrawal scenario and propose a novel membership inference framework to rigorously evaluate and validate the reliability of our privacy preservation. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, which also surpasses the performance of seven state-of-the-art baseline methods.

</details>


### [289] [A Meta Reinforcement Learning Approach to Goals-Based Wealth Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02300)
*Sanjiv R. Das,Harshad Khadilkar,Sukrit Mittal,Daniel Ostrov,Deep Srivastav,Hungjen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A meta reinforcement learning approach (MetaRL) pre-trained on thousands of goals-based wealth management problems can produce near-optimal investment strategies in milliseconds, achieving 97.8% of optimal expected utility while being robust to market regime changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional goals-based wealth management (GBWM) problems require separate training and optimization for each new investor problem, which is computationally expensive and time-consuming. There's a need for a more efficient approach that can handle dynamic investment portfolio decisions and goal fulfillment across multiple-year scenarios.

Method: Developed a meta reinforcement learning approach (MetaRL) using concepts from zero-shot meta-learning and foundation model pre-training. The model is pre-trained on thousands of GBWM problems, where each problem involves multi-year scenarios with optimal portfolio allocation and goal fulfillment decisions to maximize expected investor utility.

Result: MetaRL produces near-optimal dynamic investment portfolio and goal-fulfilling strategies for new GBWM problems within hundredths of a second. It delivers expected utilities averaging 97.8% of optimal expected utilities determined via Dynamic Programming. The approach is remarkably robust to capital market regime changes, even when trained on only one regime, and can solve problems with larger state spaces where Dynamic Programming becomes infeasible.

Conclusion: MetaRL provides an efficient and effective solution for goals-based wealth management that eliminates the need for separate optimization for each new problem, offers near-optimal performance with minimal computational overhead, and demonstrates strong robustness to changing market conditions while enabling solution of more complex problems than traditional methods.

Abstract: Applying concepts related to zero-shot meta-learning and pre-training of foundation models, we develop a meta reinforcement learning approach (denoted MetaRL) that is pre-trained on thousands of goals-based wealth management (GBWM) problems. Each GBWM problem involves a multiple year scenario over which the investor looks to optimally choose an investment portfolio each year and choose to fulfill all, some, or none of the different financial goals that arise each year. These choices seek to maximize the expected total investor utility obtained from the fulfilled financial goals. By eliminating separate training and optimization for each new investor problem, the MetaRL model in inference mode produces near-optimal dynamic investment portfolio and goal-fulfilling strategies for a new GBWM problem within a few hundredths of a second. This delivers expected utilities that are, on average, 97.8% of the optimal expected utilities (determined via Dynamic Programming). These results are remarkably robust to capital market regime changes, even when training uses only one capital market regime. Further, the MetaRL approach can enable solving problems with larger state spaces where Dynamic Programming becomes computationally infeasible.

</details>


### [290] [Differentiable Kernel Ridge Regression for Deep Learning Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02313)
*Jean-Marc Mercier,Gabriele Santin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sparse Kernels (SKs) are introduced as differentiable, modular kernel ridge regression layers that can be integrated into deep learning pipelines, enabling training-free transfer, nonlinear probing, and hybrid kernel-neural models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kernel methods remain underexplored at scale compared to deep neural networks, despite being alternative function approximators. The authors aim to make kernel methods scalable and differentiable so they can be integrated with deep learning rather than treated as a separate paradigm.

Method: Introduce Sparse Kernels (SKs), a localized and lazy variant of kernel ridge regression that defers training to inference time and solves small local systems. They integrate SKs into PyTorch as modular layers with three parameter sets (feature representations, target values, and evaluation points) that can be fixed or learned independently.

Result: SK-based modules match trained neural readout performance with substantially less training in some settings, and augment existing models to improve performance in others. They work across convolutional networks, vision transformers, and reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: Kernel methods, once made scalable and differentiable through approaches like Sparse Kernels, can be readily integrated with deep learning rather than remaining a separate paradigm, broadening the design space available to practitioners.

Abstract: Deep neural networks dominate modern machine learning, while alternative function approximators remain comparatively underexplored at scale. In this work, we revisit kernel methods as drop-in components for standard deep learning pipelines. We introduce \emph{Sparse Kernels} (SKs), a differentiable, localized, and lazy variant of kernel ridge regression (KRR) that defers training to inference time and reduces to the solution of small local systems. We integrate SKs into PyTorch as modular layers that preserve end-to-end trainability, and we show that they expose three distinct sets of parameters -- feature representations, target values, and evaluation points -- each of which can be fixed or learned. This decomposition broadens the design space available to practitioners, enabling, in particular, training-free transfer, nonlinear probing, and hybrid kernel-neural models. Across convolutional networks, vision transformers, and reinforcement learning, SK-based modules serve two complementary roles: in some settings, they match the performance of trained neural readouts with substantially less training; in others, they augment existing models and improve their performance when used as additional components. Our results suggest that kernel methods, once made scalable and differentiable, can be readily integrated with deep learning rather than treated as a separate paradigm.

</details>


### [291] [When Attention Collapses: Residual Evidence Modeling for Compositional Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02323)
*Niklas Houba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Standard attention fails under additive superposition due to slot collapse; evidence depletion prevents this by tracking residual evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attention-based models struggle with additive superposition (multiple components contributing to every observation) where they suffer from slot collapse - multiple slots converge to the same dominant component while weaker ones remain unrepresented.

Method: Introduced residual evidence modeling via evidence depletion - a minimal modification combining multiplicative depletion with an attention bias that adds residual state to sequential attention.

Result: Evidence depletion reduces slot collapse by up to an order of magnitude in synthetic benchmarks and real-world audio mixtures (FUSS), and enables successful multi-source posterior estimation in gravitational-wave source inference for LISA mission where standard attention fails.

Conclusion: Under additive superposition, residual evidence tracking is essential for preventing slot collapse and enabling compositional inference; standard attention's memorylessness with respect to explained evidence is the fundamental limitation.

Abstract: Compositional inference - the decomposition of observations into an unknown number of latent components - is central to perception and scientific data analysis. Attention-based models perform well when components are approximately separable, as in object-centric vision. Under additive superposition, however - where multiple components contribute to every observation - we identify a structural failure mode we term slot collapse: multiple slots converge to the same dominant component while weaker ones remain unrepresented. We trace this to a general limitation: attention is memoryless with respect to explained evidence. All slots repeatedly operate on the same input without accounting for what has already been explained, so gradients are dominated by the strongest component, inducing shared fixed points across slots. As a result, attention fails to enforce non-redundant allocation under additive superposition. We address this by introducing residual evidence modeling, instantiated via evidence depletion - a minimal modification combining multiplicative depletion with an attention bias. Controlled ablations show that parallel attention, sequential processing alone, and loss-based regularization fail to resolve collapse; evidence depletion, which adds residual state to sequential attention, consistently succeeds. Across synthetic benchmarks and real-world audio mixtures (FUSS), evidence depletion reduces slot collapse by up to an order of magnitude, generalizing beyond synthetic settings. On gravitational-wave source inference for the ESA/NASA LISA mission, under identical architectures, data, and losses, standard attention fails while evidence depletion prevents collapse and enables multi-source posterior estimation. These results show that under additive superposition, residual evidence tracking is the operative ingredient for preventing collapse and enabling compositional inference.

</details>


### [292] [A Near-optimal SQ Lower Bound for Smoothed Agnostic Learning of Boolean Halfspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02350)
*Tim Sinen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Study shows L¹ polynomial regression achieves nearly optimal complexity for smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces under uniform distribution with random flips, matching recent continuous Gaussian results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the complexity of smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces in the discrete setting (uniform distribution on {±1}ⁿ) with input coordinate flipping, complementing recent work on continuous Gaussian settings.

Method: Analyzes L¹ polynomial regression algorithm and proves lower bounds using Statistical Query (SQ) complexity theory for the smoothed agnostic learning model with independent coordinate flips at probability σ.

Result: L¹ polynomial regression achieves complexity Õ(n^{O(log(1/ε)/σ)}), with nearly matching SQ lower bound of n^{Ω(log(1+σ/ε²)/σ)}, showing near-optimality and extending continuous Gaussian results to discrete setting.

Conclusion: The complexity bounds for smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces in the discrete setting under uniform distribution match the pattern seen in continuous Gaussian settings, establishing fundamental limits and algorithmic near-optimality for this learning problem.

Abstract: We study the complexity of smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces on $\{\pm 1\}^n$ under the uniform distribution in the model of \citet{KM25} where each input coordinate is independently flipped with probability $σ\in (0, {1}/{2})$. We show that $L^1$ polynomial regression achieves complexity $\tilde{O}(n^{O(\log(1/\varepsilon)/σ)})$, and prove a nearly matching Statistical Query complexity lower bound of $n^{Ω(\log(1+σ/\varepsilon ^2)/σ)}$. This complements the recent work of \citet{DK26}, which established analogous bounds in the continuous setting under Gaussian marginals.

</details>


### [293] [ZNO: Stable Rational Neural Operators in the Z-Domain for Discrete-Time Dynamic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02356)
*Xianli Zhu,Jia Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZNO is a causal neural operator using z-domain rational filters for discrete-time system identification, excelling at stable rational systems with lightly damped poles near the unit circle and long-memory dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing operator learning methods are primarily designed for continuous-time problems, while many system-identification problems are intrinsically discrete-time. There's a need for neural operators specifically tailored for discrete-time systems with stable rational dynamics.

Method: ZNO uses layers that are stable low-rank MIMO rational filters directly parameterized in the z-plane. The approach combines: 1) low-rank channel mixing, 2) smooth stable pole reparameterization, 3) causal recurrence, and 4) an optional short FIR branch in a single z-domain rational recurrent layer. Stability is enforced via unit-disk pole constraints.

Result: ZNO achieves lowest mean error across memory regimes (10-200 steps) in controlled experiments, particularly excelling with stable rational systems having lightly damped poles near the unit circle. On public benchmarks, it's competitive with neural operator and state-space baselines, achieving best performance on systems aligning with stable rational discrete-time filters.

Conclusion: ZNO is a strong model for stable rational discrete-time dynamics, especially in near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes, but not a universal replacement for specialized system-identification methods. It provides interpretable discrete-time poles and addresses the gap in discrete-time operator learning.

Abstract: We introduce the Z-Domain Neural Operator (ZNO), a causal neural operator whose layers are stable low-rank multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) rational filters parameterized directly in the $z$-plane. ZNO addresses a limitation of existing operator learning methods, many of which are primarily tailored for continuous-time problems, while a large class of system-identification problems is intrinsically discrete-time. The $z$-domain form expresses stability as a unit-disk pole constraint and makes learned discrete-time poles directly readable. The model combines low-rank channel mixing, smooth stable pole reparameterization, causal recurrence, and an optional short finite impulse response (FIR) branch in a single $z$-domain rational recurrent layer. Across controlled discrete system-identification experiments, ZNO's advantage is most evident when the target dynamics are stable rational systems with lightly damped poles near the unit circle. Under matched parameter budgets, ZNO is not uniformly dominant; however, with validation-selected configurations, the same architecture can achieve the lowest mean error across the controlled tasks. A five-bin difficulty sweep over near-unit-circle / long-memory dynamics shows that ZNO has the lowest mean error across memory regimes, from short (approximately 10 steps) to long (approximately 100-200 steps). On five public nonlinear system-identification benchmarks, ZNO is competitive with neural operator and state-space baselines, achieving the lowest mean error on benchmarks whose dynamics align with stable rational discrete-time filters, while classical or state-space baselines remain preferable on some systems. These results position ZNO as a strong model for stable rational discrete-time dynamics, especially in near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes, but not as a universal replacement for specialized system-identification methods.

</details>


### [294] [Predicting Post Virality with Temporal Cross-Attention over Trend Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02358)
*Sarvagya Somvanshi,Mohan Xu,Rakhi Chadalavada,Nathan Canera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A model that predicts Reddit post virality by combining internal platform features with external Wikipedia trend signals, showing consistent improvements over text-only approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current social media virality prediction models rely too heavily on static textual and structural features, ignoring the highly dynamic nature of real-world attention signals and trends that influence what becomes viral.

Method: Introduced ViralityNET, an architecture that fuses internal Reddit features (title embeddings, body embeddings, structural metadata, subreddit embeddings) with exogenous temporal signals from Wikipedia pageview spikes using a cross-attention mechanism that queries a daily sliding-window trends matrix of top Wikipedia terms.

Result: The model outperforms text-only baselines by +0.015 AUC-PR and achieves an overall AUC-ROC of 0.836, demonstrating that incorporating external attention signals yields consistent improvements in virality prediction accuracy.

Conclusion: External attention signals from real-world dynamics (like Wikipedia trends) provide measurable improvements over text-only approaches for predicting social media virality, highlighting the importance of considering temporal and exogenous signals in content virality prediction models.

Abstract: Current models for predicting social media virality rely heavily on static textual and structural features, effectively ignoring the highly dynamic nature of trend signals. We study whether real-world attention signals can improve the prediction of social-media virality beyond what post text alone reveals. We introduce \model{}, an architecture that predicts Reddit post virality by fusing internal platform representations with exogenous temporal signals derived from Wikipedia pageview spikes. We frame virality as a binary classification task that accounts for differences in subreddit scale, labeling posts as viral if they exceed the 90th percentile of per-subreddit engagement and a minimum absolute score threshold.
  We introduce ViralityNET combines four post-level streams: title embeddings, body embeddings, structural metadata, and learned subreddit embeddings with a cross-attention block that queries a daily sliding-window trends matrix encoding the top-512 Wikipedia spike terms from the preceding seven days. Empirical results suggest that incorporating external attention signals yields consistent gains, outperforming text-only baselines by +0.015 AUC-PR and achieving an overall AUC-ROC of 0.836. Overall, we provide evidence that incorporating external attention signals yields measurable improvements over text-only baselines, highlighting the importance of real-world dynamics in shaping online virality.

</details>


### [295] [Binary Rewards and Reinforcement Learning: Fundamental Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02375)
*Marc Dymetman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR training causes diversity collapse because binary rewards create infinite optimal solutions, and KL-control selects concentrated distributions over valid outputs rather than maintaining coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR improves reasoning but causes diversity collapse where multi-sample coverage degrades, sometimes below the base model. Need to understand why binary reward optimization leads to reduced diversity despite improved single-sample accuracy.

Method: Analyzes structural properties of binary rewards showing infinite degeneracy in policy gradient optimization. Examines how KL-control selects filtered model as β→0 but under misspecification drives concentration on few valid outputs. Uses theoretical analysis and toy autoregressive experiments to demonstrate mechanisms.

Result: Binary rewards create fundamental degeneracy with infinite optimal distributions. KL-control selects filtered model in theory but under misspecification pushes toward highly concentrated distributions over few valid outputs, causing diversity collapse. Alternative divergences targeting filtered model directly avoid this by rewarding coverage of its support.

Conclusion: Diversity collapse in RLVR stems from binary reward degeneracy and KL-control's behavior under model misspecification. Solutions require targeting the filtered model's support coverage rather than optimizing for concentrated high-validity outputs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard approach for improving reasoning in language models, yet models trained with RLVR often suffer from diversity collapse: while single-sample accuracy improves, multi-sample coverage degrades, sometimes falling below the base model. We provide a structural account of this phenomenon grounded in the properties of binary rewards. Binary rewards create a fundamental degeneracy for policy gradient methods: the set of distributions maximizing expected reward is infinite, with no distinguished element. KL-control resolves this degeneracy by selecting, in the limit $β\to 0$, the filtered model $p_*:=a(\cdot\mid\mathcal{Y}_1)$ -- the base model conditioned on validity -- which is the unique fully valid distribution closest to the base model in KL divergence. This selection operates through a nontrivial asymmetry: the tilted distribution $p_{[β]}\propto a(y)\,e^{v(y)/β}$ converges to $p_*$ in forward KL as $β\to 0$, yet $p_*$ cannot serve as a direct optimization target because $\mathrm{KL}(q\,\|\,p_*)$ is infinite for any full-support policy $q$. We develop explicit formulas relating the hyperparameter $β$ to the more interpretable target validity rate $μ$. Under model misspecification -- the typical practical regime -- the pressure to decrease $β$ drives the optimizer toward highly concentrated distributions over a small number of valid outputs, collapsing toward ever fewer as $β$ decreases, rather than toward the filtered model. We illustrate this mechanism on a toy autoregressive experiment and discuss how alternative divergences that target $p_*$ directly -- as pursued empirically by \citet{kruszewski_whatever_2026} -- avoid this failure mode by rewarding coverage of $p_*$'s support rather than concentration on high-validity outputs.

</details>


### [296] [Statistically-Lossless Quantization of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02404)
*Michael Helcig,Eldar Kurtic,Dan Alistarh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Statistically-lossless LLM quantization balancing compression, fidelity, and speed - achieving task-lossless at <4 bits, distribution-lossless at 5-6 bits with 1.7-3.6x speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing quantization methods involve trade-offs: lossy methods (GPTQ, AWQ) sacrifice fidelity for compression, while lossless methods preserve fidelity but don't accelerate inference. Need for middle ground of statistically-lossless compression that balances compression, fidelity, and speed.

Method: Proposes three notions of losslessness: 1) Task-lossless (preserves zero-shot accuracy), 2) Distribution-lossless (preserves next-token distributions measured via Expected Acceptance Rate metric), and 3) formalizes variance analysis showing asymmetry is needed for distribution-lossless. Uses SLQ method - layer-wise non-uniform quantization with asymmetric quantization and wide bitwidth search.

Result: Achieves task-lossless compression at well below 4 bits per parameter (as low as 3.3 bits), distribution-lossless compression at 5-6 bits per parameter, and inference speedups of 1.7-3.6x relative to FP16 with optimized kernels.

Conclusion: SLQ enables statistically-lossless compression that balances compression, fidelity, and speed, achieving practical quantization with guaranteed task accuracy and distribution fidelity while providing significant inference acceleration.

Abstract: Model quantization has become essential for efficient large language model deployment, yet existing approaches involve clear trade-offs: methods such as GPTQ and AWQ achieve practical compression but are lossy, while lossless techniques preserve fidelity but typically do not accelerate inference. This paper explores the middle ground of statistically-lossless compression through three complementary notions of losslessness for quantized LLMs. First, task-lossless compression preserves zero-shot benchmark accuracy within natural sampling variance and remains achievable at aggressive bitwidths. Second, we formalize the stricter notion of distribution-lossless compression, requiring the quantized model's next-token distribution to be practically indistinguishable from the original, and propose the Expected Acceptance Rate (EAR), the maximum token-agreement probability under optimal coupling, as a directly interpretable fidelity metric (for example, EAR >= 0.99 indicates 99% agreement). Third, we prove a gamma-squared variance law showing that symmetric quantization inflates noise variance by gamma squared relative to asymmetric quantization, making asymmetry necessary for distribution-lossless fidelity but not for task-level preservation. Using SLQ, a layer-wise non-uniform method with asymmetric quantization and wide bitwidth search, we achieve task-lossless compression at well below 4 bits per parameter (as low as 3.3 bits depending on the model), distribution-lossless compression at 5 to 6 bits per parameter on average, and inference speedups of 1.7 to 3.6x relative to FP16 with optimized kernels. Source code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/SLQ.

</details>


### [297] [Closed-Loop CO2 Storage Control With History-Based Reinforcement Learning and Latent Model-Based Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02405)
*Sofianos Panagiotis Fotias,Vassilis Gaganis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a deep reinforcement learning framework for closed-loop management of CO2 storage, comparing model-free and model-based controllers that adapt to reservoir uncertainties using only deployable well-level observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Closed-loop CO2 storage management needs control policies that can adapt to uncertain reservoir behavior while relying only on realistically available operational observations (well-level data), avoiding repeated expensive online history matching and re-optimization.

Method: Formulates CO2 injection/brine production as a partially observable sequential decision problem. Compares privileged-state, well-only, history-conditioned, masking-curriculum, and asymmetric teacher-student model-free policies. Then evaluates latent model-based adaptation that reuses nominal latent dynamics and retunes controllers under various abnormal scenarios.

Result: History-conditioned policies recover nearly all privileged-state performance using only deployable well-level information. Latent model-based retuning outperforms direct model-free retuning under the same scenario-specific simulator budget in abnormal operating cases (injector failure, leakage-induced dynamics, compartmentalized connectivity).

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a simulator-budget-aware alternative to repeated online history matching and re-optimization for closed-loop CO2 storage control, enabling effective adaptation to reservoir uncertainties using only practical observations.

Abstract: Closed-loop management of geological CO2 storage requires control policies that adapt to uncertain reservoir behavior while relying on observations that are realistically available during operation. This work formulates CO2 injection and brine-production control as a partially observable sequential decision problem and studies deployable deep reinforcement-learning controllers trained with high-fidelity reservoir simulation. We first compare privileged-state, well-only, history-conditioned, masking-curriculum, and asymmetric teacher-student model-free policies in order to quantify the value of temporal well-response information and training-time privileged simulator states. We then evaluate a latent model-based adaptation pipeline that reuses nominal latent dynamics and retunes controllers under known injector failure, leakage-induced dynamics and reward shift, and compartmentalized reservoir connectivity. The results show that history-conditioned policies recover nearly all of the privileged-state performance while using only deployable well-level information, and that latent model-based retuning outperforms direct model-free retuning under the same scenario-specific real-simulator budget in the abnormal operating cases. The proposed framework therefore provides a simulator-budget-aware alternative to repeated online history matching and re-optimization for closed-loop CO2 storage control.

</details>


### [298] [Inducing Permutation Invariant Priors in Bayesian Optimization for Carbon Capture and Storage Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02409)
*Sofianos Panagiotis Fotias,Vassilis Gaganis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel permutation-invariant Gaussian Process kernel (GP-Perm) is proposed for Bayesian Optimization to handle permutation symmetries in optimization problems like well placement in Carbon Capture and Storage projects, where standard GP kernels fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Gaussian Process kernels in Bayesian Optimization are inefficient for problems with permutation symmetries (unordered sets), which arise in applications like well placement where wells operate under group control with interchangeable positions within injector/producer groups.

Method: Two approaches: 1) A novel GP-Perm kernel that encodes permutation invariance by comparing sets through stable divergence between their induced empirical representations, combinable with standard kernels. 2) A Deep Kernel Learning model (DKL-DS) using Deep Sets architecture as a learned invariant baseline.

Result: The methodology was evaluated across 8 use cases - seven synthetic benchmarks and one realistic Carbon Capture and Storage case study (Johansen formation), though specific performance metrics aren't detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: The proposed permutation-invariant approaches address a significant limitation of standard Bayesian Optimization methods when dealing with problems involving unordered sets and permutation symmetries, particularly relevant for complex applications like Carbon Capture and Storage project optimization.

Abstract: Bayesian Optimization is an iterative method, tailored to optimizing expensive black box objective functions. Surrogate models like Gaussian Processes, which are the gold standard in Bayesian Optimization, can be inefficient for inputs with permutation symmetries, as the most common kernels employed are better suited for vector inputs rather than unordered sets of items. Motivated by this issue, we turn to permutation invariant Bayesian Optimization for well placement in Carbon Capture and Storage projects. The high fidelity black box simulator is instructed to operate wells under group control, giving rise to permutation symmetries within injector and producer groups that cannot be exploited with standard GP kernels. In this work, our main contribution is a novel Gaussian Process kernel (GP-Perm) that encodes permutation invariance by comparing sets through a stable divergence between their induced empirical representations, and can be combined with standard kernels for additional vector-valued inputs. As a learned invariant baseline, we also consider a Deep Kernel Learning model (DKL-DS) using the Deep Sets architecture to learn a permutation-invariant embedding. We evaluate the proposed methodology across 8 use cases, comprising seven synthetic benchmarks and one realistic CCS case study (Johansen formation)

</details>


### [299] [Generalized Distributional Alignment Games for Unbiased Answer-Level Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02435)
*Mehryar Mohri,Jon Schneider,Yutao Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses systematic bias in distributional alignment games for Answer-Level Fine-Tuning by developing provably optimal estimators that eliminate structural estimation bias and accelerate training convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard algorithms for distributional alignment games in Answer-Level Fine-Tuning suffer from systematic bias due to Jensen's inequality when estimating logarithmic rewards from small batches, which destabilizes training.

Method: 1) Generalize alignment games to arbitrary Bregman divergences, showing polynomial reward families allow exact unbiased estimators using U-statistics. 2) For KL divergence games, derive globally robust minimax polynomial estimator achieving optimal statistical error limit. 3) Synthesize approaches into Variance-Optimal Augmented Polynomial Optimization Program (AQP) Estimator that systematically reduces variance.

Result: Developed estimators achieve fundamental statistical error limit of Θ(1/K²) for KL divergence games (proved via Ditzian-Totik theorem). The AQP estimator achieves optimal bias, provably accelerated game convergence, more efficient/stable training with zero online computational overhead.

Conclusion: The paper systematically resolves structural estimation bias in distributional alignment games through provably optimal estimators, enabling more stable and efficient Answer-Level Fine-Tuning without introducing additional computational costs during training.

Abstract: The Distributional Alignment Game framework provides a powerful variational perspective on Answer-Level Fine-Tuning (ALFT). However, standard algorithms for these games rely on estimating logarithmic rewards from small batches, introducing a systematic bias due to Jensen's inequality that can destabilize training. In this paper, we systematically resolve this structural estimation bias. First, we generalize the alignment game to arbitrary Bregman divergences, showing that for a family of geometries inducing polynomial rewards, we can construct provably exact and unbiased estimators using U-statistics. Second, for the canonical KL divergence game where an exact solution is impossible, we derive a globally robust minimax polynomial estimator that is provably optimal, achieving the fundamental statistical error limit of $Θ(1/K^2)$, which we establish via the Ditzian-Totik theorem. Finally, we synthesize these two approaches to propose a novel Variance-Optimal Augmented Polynomial Optimization Program (AQP) Estimator, proving that by systematically reducing variance, our method achieves not only optimal bias but also provably accelerated game convergence, leading to more efficient and stable training with zero online computational overhead.

</details>


### [300] [Reference-Sampled Boltzmann Projection for KL-Regularized RLVR: Target-Matched Weighted SFT, Finite One-Shot Gaps, and Policy Mirror Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02469)
*Yao Shu,Chenxing Wei,Hongbin Lin,Shuang Qiu,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces BOLT, a method that reformulates online RLVR as static weighted SFT with Boltzmann-targeted weights that match the optimal KL-regularized policy, providing theoretical error decomposition and empirical evidence of efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online RLVR (reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards) has optimization bottlenecks due to keeping rollout generation, verifier scoring, and reference-policy evaluations on the optimization path. Static weighted SFT on precomputed rollouts seems promising but requires careful weight specification to match the target policy.

Method: The paper identifies the reference-sampled weighted-SFT objective whose induced policy equals the fixed-reference KL-regularized RLVR optimizer (Boltzmann target policy). This leads to BOLT (Boltzmann-Targeted SFT), which uses prompt-normalized Boltzmann weights exp(r(x,y)/β)/Z(x) as the unique solution in the reference-sampled subclass.

Result: Theoretical analysis provides error decomposition separating exact stored-support price from partition estimation, effective-sample-size variance, generalization, optimization, and approximation errors. Empirical experiments with Qwen show evidence for target-matched weights, one-shot saturation, refreshed-sampler gains, and optimization-time savings.

Conclusion: BOLT provides a principled way to convert online RLVR into efficient static weighted SFT with theoretical guarantees about matching the optimal policy, explaining why extra SFT epochs cannot fix coverage issues and revealing trade-offs in the temperature-coverage-variance frontier.

Abstract: Online reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) turns checkable outcomes into a scalable training signal, but it keeps rollout generation, verifier scoring, and reference-policy evaluations on the optimization path. Static weighted supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on precomputed rollouts seems to remove this bottleneck, yet a weighted likelihood is not specified by rewards alone: its sampler and weights induce the policy being fit. This paper identifies the reference-sampled weighted-SFT objective whose induced policy equals the fixed-reference KL-regularized RLVR optimizer. The optimizer is the standard Boltzmann target policy, obtained by exponentially tilting the reference policy by verifier reward. Matching a weighted-SFT induced policy to this target forces density-ratio weights; in the reference-sampled subclass, this reduces uniquely, up to prompt scaling, to the prompt-normalized Boltzmann weight $\exp(r(x,y)/β)/Z(x)$. BOLT, a Boltzmann-Targeted SFT procedure, is the empirical estimator of this projection. The finite one-shot analysis separates the exact stored-support price $β\log(1/π^*(S_N\mid x))$ from partition estimation, effective-sample-size variance, generalization, optimization, and approximation errors. This decomposition explains why extra SFT epochs cannot repair missing reference-policy coverage and exposes the temperature--coverage--variance frontier. When coverage needs adaptive sampling, refreshed Boltzmann projections become KL policy mirror descent; finite inner solves enter as additive drift from the exact mirror step. Single-run Qwen experiments provide projection evidence for the target-matched weight, one-shot saturation, refreshed-sampler gains, and optimization-time savings, within the stated single-run scope.

</details>


### [301] [Efficient Preference Poisoning Attack on Offline RLHF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02495)
*Chenye Yang,Weiyu Xu,Lifeng Lai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies label flip poisoning attacks on offline RLHF pipelines like DPO, develops two attack methods (BAL-A and BMP-A) that exploit the gradient structure, and provides theoretical guarantees and experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RLHF pipelines like DPO are vulnerable to preference poisoning attacks since they train on pre-collected preference datasets. The authors study label flip attacks specifically against log-linear DPO to understand and exploit this vulnerability.

Method: The authors discover that flipping one preference label induces a parameter-independent shift in DPO gradient. They convert the targeted poisoning problem into a structured binary sparse approximation problem. They develop two attack methods: Binary-Aware Lattice Attack (BAL-A) that uses lattice reduction techniques, and Binary Matching Pursuit Attack (BMP-A) that adapts matching pursuit to non-normalized gradient dictionaries.

Result: The methods provide theoretical guarantees: BAL-A gives sufficient conditions for recovering minimum-flip objectives, while BMP-A yields coherence-based recovery guarantees and robustness certificates for K-flip budgets. Experiments on synthetic dictionaries and Stanford Human Preferences dataset validate the theory and show how dictionary geometry governs attack success.

Conclusion: Label flip poisoning attacks on DPO are feasible and effective, with attack success governed by dictionary geometry. The theoretical framework provides both attack methods and robustness certificates, highlighting security vulnerabilities in offline RLHF pipelines.

Abstract: Offline Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) pipelines such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) train on a pre-collected preference dataset, which makes them vulnerable to preference poisoning attack. We study label flip attacks against log-linear DPO. We first illustrate that flipping one preference label induces a parameter-independent shift in the DPO gradient. Using this key property, we can then convert the targeted poisoning problem into a structured binary sparse approximation problem. To solve this problem, we develop two attack methods: Binary-Aware Lattice Attack (BAL-A) and Binary Matching Pursuit Attack (BMP-A). BAL-A embeds the binary flip selection problem into a binary-aware lattice and applies Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovász reduction and Babai's nearest plane algorithm; we provide sufficient conditions that enforce binary coefficients and recover the minimum-flip objective. BMP-A adapts binary matching pursuit to our non-normalized gradient dictionary and yields coherence-based recovery guarantees and robustness (impossibility) certificates for $K$-flip budgets. Experiments on synthetic dictionaries and the Stanford Human Preferences dataset validate the theory and highlight how dictionary geometry governs attack success.

</details>


### [302] [Pretraining on Sleep Data Improves non-Sleep Biosignal Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02500)
*William Lehn-Schiøler,Magnus Ruud Kjær,Phillip Hempel,Magnus Guldberg Pedersen,Rahul Thapa,Bryan He,Nicolai Spicher,Andreas Brink-Kjaer,Lars Kai Hansen,Emmanuel Mignot*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sleep multimodal pretraining transfers effectively to non-sleep EEG/ECG tasks, achieving competitive or state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether sleep biosignals can serve as an effective pretraining distribution for learning representations that transfer beyond sleep to adjacent domains like EEG and ECG.

Method: Sleep-only multimodal contrastive pretraining with a leave-one-out objective, followed by evaluation on non-sleep EEG and ECG tasks across eight downstream tasks spanning multiple datasets.

Result: Sleep pretraining consistently improves performance relative to training from scratch. On several tasks, achieves performance competitive with or surpassing prior specialized state-of-the-art and foundation models.

Conclusion: Sleep biosignals are an effective pretraining distribution that enables transfer learning to adjacent biosignal domains, demonstrating the broader utility of sleep foundation models beyond sleep-specific applications.

Abstract: Sleep foundation models have recently demonstrated strong performance on in-domain polysomnography tasks, including sleep staging, apnea detection, and disease risk prediction. In this work, we investigate whether sleep biosignals can serve as an effective pretraining distribution for learning representations that transfer beyond sleep to adjacent domains. Following sleep foundation models, we perform sleep-only multimodal contrastive pretraining (with a leave-one-out objective) and evaluate transfer to non-sleep EEG and ECG, two well-benchmarked biosignal modalities with heterogeneous datasets and clinically meaningful downstream tasks. Across eight downstream tasks spanning multiple EEG and ECG datasets, sleep pretraining consistently improves performance relative to training from scratch. Moreover, on several tasks, we achieve performance competitive with or surpassing prior specialized state-of-the-art and foundation models.

</details>


### [303] [A Novel Preprocessing-Driven Approach to Remaining Useful Life (RUL) Prediction Using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02507)
*Florent Imbert,Tosin Adewumi,Hui Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel preprocessing pipeline that enhances RUL prediction in aero-engines by improving data quality and temporal representation before model training, achieving superior results compared to state-of-the-art neural models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate RUL prediction is vital for predictive maintenance, reliability, and cost reduction in aero-engines. While deep learning shows promise, most existing methods focus on model architecture and treat input features uniformly, often neglecting the influence of data preprocessing, which limits performance.

Method: Proposes a novel preprocessing pipeline that leverages complete temporal sequences and generates RUL estimates at each timestep to capture fine-grained degradation dynamics. The approach enhances data quality and temporal representation before model training to enable continuous prognostic insights.

Result: Experiments on NASA C-MAPSS dataset show the proposed pipeline consistently achieves superior accuracy and robustness compared to state-of-the-art models including CNN, RNN, LSTM, DCNN, TCN, BiGRU-TSAM, AGCNN, and ATCN.

Conclusion: The results highlight the critical role of preprocessing in maximizing the effectiveness of neural prognostic models for aero-engine RUL prediction, demonstrating that data preprocessing is as important as model architecture design.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of Remaining Useful Life (RUL) in aero-engines is vital for predictive maintenance, improved operational reliability, and reduced lifecycle costs. While deep learning approaches have demonstrated strong potential in this area, most existing methods focus primarily on model architecture design and treat input features uniformly, often neglecting the influence of data preprocessing. In this work, we propose a novel preprocessing pipeline that enhances RUL prediction by improving data quality and temporal representation before model training. Our approach leverages complete temporal sequences and generates RUL estimates at each timestep, enabling the model to capture fine-grained degradation dynamics and deliver continuous prognostic insights throughout the engine's operational life. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed pipeline, we conduct experiments on the NASA C-MAPSS dataset. Comparative evaluations against a suite of state-of-the-art neural models including CNN, RNN, LSTM, DCNN, TCN, BiGRU-TSAM, AGCNN, and ATCN, demonstrate that our approach consistently achieves superior accuracy and robustness in aero-engine RUL prediction. These results highlight the critical role of preprocessing in maximizing the effectiveness of neural prognostic models.

</details>


### [304] [MPCS: Neuroplastic Continual Learning via Multi-Component Plasticity and Topology-Aware EWC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02509)
*Joern Hentsch*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MPCS is a continual learning system with 11 neuroplastic mechanisms that achieves state-of-the-art performance on MEP-BENCH by optimizing the stability-plasticity tradeoff, with Fourier encoding identified as most critical and EWC regularization found to be detrimental.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the fundamental tension in continual learning between plasticity (acquiring new knowledge) and stability (retaining prior knowledge) through a comprehensive neuroplastic architecture.

Method: MPCS integrates 11 complementary mechanisms: task-driven neurogenesis, Fourier-encoded inputs, EWC regularization, meta-replay, mixed consolidation, hybrid gating, synapse pruning/regeneration, Hebbian updates, task similarity routing, adaptive growth control, and continuous neuron importance tracking.

Result: MPCS achieves Normalized Efficiency Score of 94.2, placing it on Pareto frontier among 9 of 14 gate-passing systems. Fourier encoding is most critical component, EWC degrades performance, and Pareto analysis predicts optimal model compression (MPCS_EFFICIENT improves performance at 4.7x lower compute).

Conclusion: The Pareto frontier provides actionable guidance for model compression in continual learning, with careful component selection enabling efficient systems that maintain high performance while reducing computational cost.

Abstract: Continual learning systems face a fundamental tension between plasticity -- acquiring new knowledge  --  and stability  --  retaining prior knowledge. We introduce MPCS (Multi-Plasticity Continual System), a neuroplastic architecture that integrates eleven complementary mechanisms: task-driven neurogenesis, Fourier-encoded inputs, EWC regularization, meta-replay, mixed consolidation, hybrid gating, synapse pruning/regeneration, Hebbian updates, task similarity routing, adaptive growth control, and continuous neuron importance tracking. We evaluate MPCS on MEP-BENCH, a multi-track benchmark spanning 31 tasks across regression, classification, logic, and mixed domains, using a three-dimensional Pareto criterion over task performance (Perf), representation diversity (RD), and gradient conflict rate (GCR). Across 15 ablation configurations (3 seeds x 4 tracks x 2000 epochs), MPCS achieves a Normalized Efficiency Score of 94.2, placing it on the Pareto frontier among 9 of 14 gate-passing systems. Key findings: (i) Fourier encoding is the single most critical component (removal drops Perf by 30.7 pp and fails the MEP gate on 14% of tasks); (ii) global EWC degrades performance (NES = -4.2); topology-local EWC reduces this penalty (NES 90.5->91.8) but does not eliminate it; removing EWC entirely yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, the highest-Perf system -- establishing a monotone relationship in the high task-similarity regime (s_bar ~= 0.95): global EWC < topology EWC < no EWC; (iii) the Pareto status assessment is predictive: removing the two Pareto-dominated components (EWC + Hebbian) jointly yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, which improves Perf by 0.6 pp at 4.7x lower compute cost (127 vs. 602 min), validating the Pareto frontier as an actionable model-compression guide.

</details>


### [305] [Evaluating Tabular Representation Learning for Network Intrusion Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02519)
*Muhammad Usman Butt,Andreas Hotho,Daniel Schlör*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates tabular representation learning methods for NetFlow intrusion detection, finding strong dataset-model dependencies with no single approach dominating across all scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classic NIDS require manual feature engineering which contradicts modern ML principles of models learning representations directly from data. The paper investigates whether tabular representation learning can improve intrusion detection by automatically learning robust feature representations for NetFlow data.

Method: Systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art representation learning methods on benchmark NetFlow datasets, comparing against traditional autoencoders and end-to-end transformer baselines. They evaluate learned representations using both supervised classifiers and unsupervised anomaly detectors with comprehensive hyperparameter exploration.

Result: Strong dataset-model dependency - no single approach consistently dominates. For supervised classification, TabICL performs best on CIDDS, while autoencoders tie with transformers for best average rank. Supervised approaches substantially outperform unsupervised methods. Cross-dataset transfer experiments show representations can generalize across network environments but performance varies substantially depending on source-target dataset combinations.

Conclusion: Tabular representation learning shows promise for automating feature learning in network intrusion detection, but optimal method selection depends heavily on the specific dataset and use case, with strong dataset-model dependencies observed. Transfer learning is possible but sensitive to distributional differences between network environments.

Abstract: Classic Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) often rely on manual feature engineering to extract meaningful patterns from network traffic data. However, this approach requires domain expertise and runs counter to the widely adopted principle of modern machine learning and neural networks: that models themselves should learn meaningful representations directly from data. We investigate whether tabular representation learning techniques can improve intrusion detection performance by automatically learning robust feature representations for NetFlow data. This paper presents a systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art representation learning methods on benchmark NetFlow datasets, comparing against traditional autoencoders and end-to-end transformer baselines. We evaluate learned representations using both supervised classifiers and unsupervised anomaly detectors, with comprehensive hyperparameter exploration for each combination. Our results reveal strong dataset-model dependency, with no single approach consistently dominating across all scenarios. For supervised classification, TabICL achieves the best performance on CIDDS, while autoencoders follow closely and tie with end-to-end transformer models for the best average rank across datasets. Supervised approaches substantially outperform unsupervised anomaly detection methods, where no single combination consistently dominates as optimal choices depend on the dataset. Cross-dataset transfer experiments demonstrate that learned representations can generalize across network environments with appropriate method and classifier selection. However, transfer performance varies substantially depending on the source-target dataset combination, indicating sensitivity to distributional differences between network environments.

</details>


### [306] [Physics-Informed Neural Learning for State Reconstruction and Parameter Identification in Coupled Greenhouse Climate Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02524)
*Sani Biswas,Khursheed J. Ansari,Md. Nasim Akhtar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A coupled physics-informed neural network (PINN) approach for joint reconstruction of greenhouse temperature and humidity dynamics with simultaneous parameter identification, outperforming purely data-driven methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of reconstructing indoor temperature and humidity dynamics in greenhouse environments under sparse and noisy observations, where traditional methods struggle with limited data and physical consistency.

Method: Proposed a coupled PINN framework that integrates a reduced-order physically motivated model into neural network training, incorporating governing dynamical constraints for joint state reconstruction and parameter identification.

Result: The method achieves improved reconstruction accuracy compared to purely data-driven neural networks, with significant error reduction in temperature and humidity, particularly pronounced in humidity channel. Successfully recovers dominant physical parameters while maintaining high coefficients of determination.

Conclusion: Physics-informed learning shows strong potential for greenhouse climate modeling and data-scarce environmental systems by enabling interpretable representations beyond data interpolation, combining physical consistency with data-driven learning.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have recently emerged as a promising framework for integrating data-driven learning with physical knowledge. In this work, we propose a coupled PINN approach for the joint reconstruction of indoor temperature and humidity dynamics in greenhouse environments, together with simultaneous identification of key model parameters. The method incorporates a reduced-order physically motivated model into the learning process, enabling consistent estimation under sparse and noisy observations.
  The artificial intelligence contribution lies in the development of a coupled physics-informed neural learning framework that integrates governing dynamical constraints into neural network training, while the engineering application focuses on greenhouse climate state reconstruction and parameter identification.
  The proposed framework is evaluated on a controlled synthetic benchmark that mimics diurnal forcing conditions. Compared with a purely data-driven neural network baseline, the coupled PINN achieves improved reconstruction accuracy, reducing temperature and humidity errors while maintaining high coefficients of determination. The improvement is particularly pronounced in the humidity channel, where latent moisture dynamics are more difficult to infer from limited measurements.
  In addition to accurate state reconstruction, the method successfully recovers the dominant physical parameters governing the system dynamics, demonstrating its ability to learn interpretable representations beyond data interpolation. These results highlight the potential of physics-informed learning for greenhouse climate modeling and, more broadly, for data-scarce environmental systems.

</details>


### [307] [Recurrent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Chemotherapy Control under Partial Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02552)
*Firas Mohamed Elamine Kiram,Imane Youkana,Rachida Saouli,Gian Antonio Susto,Laid Kahloul*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory-augmented recurrent reinforcement learning policies (LSTM-based TD3) outperform feed-forward methods for chemotherapy dose optimization under partial observability, providing better tumor suppression and reduced toxicity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chemotherapy dose optimization requires sequential decisions under uncertainty (balancing tumor suppression vs. toxicity), but most RL approaches assume full observability of patient state, which is unrealistic in clinical practice. The paper investigates whether memory-augmented policies can improve chemotherapy control when patient state information is incomplete or noisy.

Method: The researchers use recurrent TD3-based approach with separate LSTM actor-critic networks, evaluating on the AhnChemoEnv benchmark from DTR-Bench. They compare off-policy and on-policy recurrent architectures against feed-forward TD3 and Soft Actor-Critic baselines. Pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic variability is fixed to isolate hidden-state uncertainty and observation noise effects.

Result: Across ten random seeds: recurrence provides modest benefit under full observability, but substantially stronger and more stable performance under partial observability. Memory-augmented policies achieve more consistent tumor suppression and improved normal-cell preservation compared to feed-forward approaches.

Conclusion: Memory-based policies are particularly beneficial for chemotherapy dose optimization when clinically relevant state information is incomplete or noisy. The recurrent architecture enables better handling of partial observability in clinical settings where full patient state information is unavailable.

Abstract: Chemotherapy dose optimization can be formulated as a dynamic treatment regime, requiring sequential decisions under uncertainty that must balance tumor suppression against toxicity. However, most reinforcement learning approaches assume full observability of the patient state, a condition rarely met in clinical practice. We investigate whether memory-augmented policies can improve chemotherapy control under partial observability. To this end, we employ a recurrent TD3-based approach with separate LSTM actor-critic networks and evaluate it on the AhnChemoEnv benchmark from DTR-Bench, considering both off-policy and on-policy recurrent architectures against feed-forward TD3 and Soft Actor-Critic. Pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic variability are held fixed to isolate hidden-state uncertainty and observation noise and to avoid confounding effects from inter-patient variability. Across ten random seeds, recurrence yields modest benefit under full observability but substantially stronger and more stable performance under partial observability, with more consistent tumor suppression and improved normal-cell preservation. These findings indicate that memory-based policies are particularly beneficial when clinically relevant state information is incomplete or noisy.

</details>


### [308] [StreamIndex: Memory-Bounded Compressed Sparse Attention via Streaming Top-k](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02568)
*Jaber Jaber,Osama Jaber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StreamIndex is a Triton implementation of Compressed Sparse Attention that avoids OOM by using chunked partition-merge top-k without materializing large intermediate tensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CSA implementations materialize a large FP32 score tensor that exceeds single-GPU HBM memory limits at long sequence lengths (256 GB at S=65,536), preventing scaling to very long sequences.

Method: StreamIndex implements a chunked partition-merge top-k driver in Triton that processes the CSA pipeline in chunks without materializing the full intermediate score tensor, enabling memory-efficient operation.

Result: StreamIndex extends sequence length capability by 32x (up to S=1,048,576 vs OOM at S=65,536), uses only 6.21 GB peak HBM, maintains high recall (≥0.9980, mean 1.0000), and enables running previously OOM workloads in 1.97s at 18.56 GB peak.

Conclusion: StreamIndex successfully addresses the memory bottleneck in CSA indexer step through chunked processing, enabling scaling to much longer sequences without compromising accuracy.

Abstract: DeepSeek-V3.2 and V4 introduce Compressed Sparse Attention (CSA): a lightning indexer (a learned scoring projection over compressed keys) scores them, the top-k are selected per query, and a sparse attention kernel reads only those. Public CSA implementations materialize a [B, S, H_I, T] FP32 score tensor before the top-k reduction. With H_I=64 indexer heads and the V4-Flash compression ratio m=4, that intermediate is 256 GB at sequence length S=65,536, exceeding any single-GPU high-bandwidth-memory (HBM) budget. We present StreamIndex, a Triton implementation of the CSA pipeline whose central component is a chunked partition-merge top-k driver that never materializes the full intermediate. On synthetic-but-realistic V4-shaped inputs at the indexer-step (layer) level on a single NVIDIA H200, the materialize path runs out of memory (OOMs) at S=65,536 with V4-Flash dimensions; StreamIndex runs the same indexer to S=1,048,576 with 6.21 GB peak HBM, a 32x regime extension. Set-overlap recall against the materialize ground truth is bit-exact at small S where both fit; across three 5-point design-space sweeps (chunk size, key-tile size, top-k), mean recall rounds to 1.0000 with min recall at least 0.9980 in every cell. The chunked driver composes with TileLang's pipelined attention kernel: at S=262,144 with V4-Flash dimensions, the materialize indexer paired with TileLang attention OOMs while the chunked indexer paired with the same attention runs in 1.97 s at 18.56 GB peak. Our contribution targets the indexer step; we make no claim of a faster attention kernel or of real-checkpoint end-to-end behavior. Code: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/StreamIndex.

</details>


### [309] [Gradient Boosted Risk Scores](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02593)
*Costa Georgantas,Jonas Richiardi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A gradient boosting-based algorithm for creating compact, predictive risk scores that models nonlinear effects and achieves competitive performance with significantly fewer rules than regression-based alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Risk scores are important interpretable ML models used in medicine, insurance, and risk management, but current regression-based approaches have limitations in modeling nonlinear effects and often produce overly complex scores.

Method: The paper proposes an algorithm based on gradient boosting with C++ implementation and Python/R bindings that can model nonlinear effects while creating compact risk scores computed by attributing points based on limited criteria.

Result: The method achieves competitive predictive performance across 12 tabular datasets (regression, classification, time-to-event tasks) while producing substantially more compact scores: 60% fewer rules for classification and 16% fewer rules for time-to-event tasks compared to AutoScore on average.

Conclusion: The proposed gradient boosting approach provides a simple and effective alternative to regression-based risk score generation, offering better modeling of nonlinear effects while maintaining interpretability through more compact scores.

Abstract: Risk scores are an interpretable and actionable class of machine learning models with applications in medicine, insurance, and risk management. Unlike most computational methods, risk scores are designed to be computed by a human by attributing points to a data sample based on a limited set of criteria. The most common approaches for generating risk scores use linear regressions to estimate the effect of selected variables. We propose a simple and effective approach towards building compact and predictive risk scores. We provide an algorithm based on gradient boosting that is capable of modeling nonlinear effects, along with a C++ implementation with Python and R bindings. Through extensive empirical evaluation on twelve tabular datasets spanning regression, classification, and time-to-event tasks, we show that our method achieves competitive predictive performance while producing substantially more compact scores than regression-based alternatives, with 60% fewer rules for classification tasks and 16% fewer rules for time-to-event tasks on average, compared to AutoScore.

</details>


### [310] [HARMES: A Multi-Modal Dataset for Wearable Human Activity Recognition with Motion, Environmental Sensing and Sound](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02596)
*Robin Burchard,Pascal-André Brückner,Marius Bock,Juergen Gall,Kristof Van Laerhoven*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HARMES introduces a multi-modal wearable dataset combining wrist-recorded IMU motion, environmental sensors (humidity/temperature/pressure), and audio for human activity recognition of daily living activities, collected from 20 participants in home environments with 80+ hours of data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Individual sensing modalities have inherent strengths and limitations, creating ambiguity in recognizing similar or complex Activities of Daily Living (ADLs). Multi-modal approaches are needed where single modalities often produce ambiguous signals, particularly for household activities.

Method: Created HARMES dataset by collecting data from 20 participants performing household activities in their own homes, recording three wrist-worn modalities: IMU for motion, atmospheric environmental sensors (humidity, temperature, pressure), and audio. The dataset includes approximately 3 hours of labeled activity data per participant across 15 ADL classes, totaling over 80 hours of recorded data.

Result: HARMES is the first dataset combining this specific sensor trio (IMU + environmental sensors + audio) and is nearly 6 times larger than the previously largest wrist-inertial-acoustic HAR dataset. Benchmarks show modality contributions are activity-dependent and provide complementary value, especially for activities ambiguous from motion data alone.

Conclusion: HARMES provides a valuable multi-modal resource for wearable HAR research, demonstrating that combining motion, environmental, and audio sensors can improve activity recognition, particularly for ambiguous activities. The dataset is publicly available with supporting code for future research.

Abstract: With each sensing modality exhibiting inherent strengths and limitations, multi-modal approaches for wearable Human Activity Recognition (HAR) are becoming increasingly relevant -- particularly for recognizing Activities of Daily Living (ADLs), where individual modalities often produce ambiguous signals for similar or complex activities.
