This work introduces HARMES, a multi-modal wearable dataset combining three wrist-recorded modalities: motion sensing via an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), atmospheric environmental sensors (humidity, temperature, and pressure), and audio. Collected from 20 participants performing household activities in their own homes, HARMES totals over 80 hours of recorded data, with approximately three hours of labeled activity data per participant across 15 ADL classes. To the best of our knowledge, HARMES is the first dataset to combine this particular sensor trio, and it is nearly six times larger than the previously largest wrist-inertial-acoustic HAR dataset. In an extensive benchmark, we evaluate cross-subject generalization and conduct an ablation study revealing that modality contributions are activity-dependent and can provide complementary value, particularly for activities that are ambiguous from motion data alone. HARMES is freely available at Zenodo, alongside example code for loading the dataset and training models on GitHub.

</details>


### [311] [Isotropic Fourier Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02597)
*Michael F. Staddon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Isotropic Fourier Neural Operator: A modified FNO architecture that enforces spatial symmetry constraints to improve performance and reduce parameters by up to 16x in 2D and 96x in 3D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fourier Neural Operators can solve PDEs faster than traditional methods, but their linear transformations in Fourier layers don't respect the isotropic symmetries present in most physical systems. This lack of symmetry preservation limits performance and results in unnecessary parameter redundancy.

Method: The authors propose modifying the linear transformations in Fourier layers to enforce spatial symmetries, creating an Isotropic Fourier Neural Operator. This involves constraining the parameterization so that transformations respect coordinate system invariance, making the model inherently isotropic.

Result: The isotropic modification both improves model performance and dramatically reduces parameter counts by up to 16 times in 2D and 96 times in 3D compared to standard FNO architectures.

Conclusion: Enforcing physical symmetries in neural operators through architectural constraints not only aligns the model better with underlying physical principles but also leads to more efficient and effective models with significantly reduced parameter complexity.

Abstract: Fourier Neural Operators are deep learning models that learn mappings between function spaces and can be used to learn and solve partial differential equations (PDEs), in some cases significantly faster than traditional PDE solvers. Within the model are Fourier layers, which apply linear transformations directly to the Fourier modes, with parameters depending on the wave numbers. However, most physical systems are isotropic, with the results being independent of the coordinate system chosen, but the linear transformations do not necessarily respect these symmetries. We propose a modification to the linear transformations that ensures spatial symmetries are respected, called the Isotropic Fourier Neural Operator, which both improves model performance and reduces the number of parameters by up to a factor of 16 in 2D and 96 in 3D.

</details>


### [312] [Gradient-Discrepancy Acquisition for Pool-Based Active Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02609)
*Mohamadsadegh Khosravani,Sandra Zilles*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel gradient-based acquisition criterion for active learning that can replace uncertainty measures or be integrated with diversity-based methods, with theoretical justification and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The effectiveness of active learning critically depends on the acquisition criterion used to select informative data points for labeling. Current methods rely heavily on uncertainty measures or diversity considerations, and there is a need for theoretically-grounded alternatives that can enhance or replace existing approaches.

Method: The paper proposes a novel gradient-based acquisition criterion derived from a generalization bound introduced by Luo et al. (2022). This criterion can be applied in two ways: 1) as a replacement for uncertainty measures in uncertainty sampling, or 2) incorporated into diversity-based methods that consider both the spread of sampled points and label uncertainty.

Result: The authors provide theoretical justification for the proposed acquisition criterion and demonstrate its effectiveness through empirical evaluation, showing it performs well compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The gradient-based acquisition criterion offers a theoretically sound alternative to traditional active learning methods, with flexibility to replace uncertainty measures or enhance diversity-based approaches, and shows practical effectiveness in experimental evaluations.

Abstract: The effectiveness of active learning hinges on the choice of the acquisition criterion by which a learning algorithm selects potentially informative data points whose label is subsequently queried. This paper proposes a novel gradient-based acquisition criterion, derived from a generalization bound introduced by Luo et al. (2022). This criterion can be applied in lieu of uncertainty measures in uncertainty sampling, or incorporated into diversity-based methods that consider the spread of sampled points in addition to the uncertainty of their labels. We provide a theoretical justification of the proposed acquisition criterion, and demonstrate its effectiveness in an empirical evaluation.

</details>


### [313] [Selective Prediction from Agreement: A Lipschitz-Consistent Version Space Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02611)
*Mohamadsadegh Khosravani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a selective classification approach with abstention in fixed-pool setting, using Lipschitz margin constraints and version space analysis to ensure predictions only when all consistent classification heads agree.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selective classification with abstention is important when labels are costly to obtain (fixed labeling budget) and we need to make predictions only when confident, but existing approaches need to handle the transductive setting where the unlabeled pool is given beforehand.

Method: View selective prediction through agreement: given queried labels and Lipschitz margin constraints in embedding space, define version space of Lipschitz-consistent classification heads. Derive upper/lower Lipschitz margin bounds that define certified valid labels for each pool point. Predict only when label is forced (all consistent heads agree), abstain otherwise. Also propose monotone submodular geometric proxy for budgeted querying with greedy algorithm.

Result: Theoretical framework for selective classification with abstention in fixed-pool setting, providing certified guarantees that predictions are made only when all Lipschitz-consistent heads agree on the label. Greedy algorithm for budgeted querying retains standard approximation factor.

Conclusion: The approach provides a principled way to do selective classification with abstention in transductive settings, ensuring predictions are reliable through agreement of all consistent classification heads while maintaining theoretical guarantees for query selection.

Abstract: We consider selective classification with abstention in the fixed-pool (or transductive) setting, where the unlabeled pool is given beforehand and only a subset of points can be queried for labels. Our main insight is to view selective prediction through agreement: given queried labels and Lipschitz margin constraints in an embedding space, the version space of Lipschitz-consistent classification heads is well defined. We obtain upper and lower Lipschitz margin bounds that define, for each pool point, a set of certified valid labels containing the prediction of every head in the version space. The model therefore predicts only when the label is forced (i.e., all consistent heads agree), and abstains otherwise. We also propose a monotone submodular geometric proxy for budgeted querying, and show that a greedy algorithm retains the standard approximation factor.

</details>


### [314] [Gradient-Gated DPO: Stabilizing Preference Optimization in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02626)
*Inoussa Mouiche*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gate-DPO addresses the "squeezing effect" in DPO by modulating rejected gradients based on probability geometry, preventing systematic probability collapse while maintaining DPO's simplicity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DPO's simplicity in preference optimization comes with a pathology: it exhibits a "squeezing effect" where negative gradients on rejected responses concentrate probability mass on high-confidence predictions while suppressing alternative responses, leading to systematic probability collapse even in simple models.

Method: Gate-DPO (Gradient-Gated Preference Optimization) stabilizes DPO training by modulating rejected gradients according to the model's probability geometry. When updates target extremely low-probability responses, the gate attenuates harmful gradients while preserving standard optimization behavior. This approach works without modifying the underlying preference objective and is complementary to existing methods like SFT, IPO, and Cal-DPO.

Result: Experiments across multiple architectures and preference datasets show that Gate-DPO consistently reduces squeezing and improves chosen-response likelihood. Mass-dynamics analysis reveals healthier optimization behavior with improved preferred responses and reduced suppression of the overall distribution. Smaller gated models can exhibit stronger chosen-response improvements than larger ungated models.

Conclusion: Controlling gradient dynamics through gating mechanisms, rather than simply scaling model size, is key to stable and efficient alignment in preference optimization. Gate-DPO addresses DPO's optimization pathology while maintaining its simplicity.

Abstract: Preference optimization has become a central paradigm for aligning large language models with human feedback. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) simplifies reinforcement learning from human feedback by directly optimizing pairwise preferences, removing the need for reward modeling and policy optimization. However, recent work shows that DPO exhibits a squeezing effect, where negative gradients applied to rejected responses concentrate probability mass on high-confidence predictions while suppressing alternative responses. This phenomenon arises even in simple softmax models and can lead to systematic probability collapse during training. We introduce Gradient-Gated Preference Optimization (Gate-DPO), a method that stabilizes training by modulating rejected gradients according to the model's probability geometry. When updates target extremely low-probability responses, the gate attenuates harmful gradients while preserving standard optimization behavior. Gate-DPO addresses this optimization pathology without modifying the underlying preference objective and is complementary to existing methods such as extended SFT, IPO, and Cal-DPO. Experiments across multiple architectures and preference datasets show that Gate-DPO consistently reduces squeezing and improves chosen-response likelihood. Mass-dynamics analysis further reveals healthier optimization behavior, with improved preferred responses and reduced suppression of the overall distribution. Notably, smaller gated models can exhibit stronger chosen-response improvements than larger ungated models, suggesting that controlling gradient dynamics, rather than scale alone, is key to stable and efficient alignment.

</details>


### [315] [CNNs for Vis-NIR Chemometrics: From Contradiction to Conditional Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02636)
*Dário Passos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This review paper identifies the root causes of inconsistent CNN architecture recommendations in NIR spectroscopy DL chemometrics and proposes a conditional design framework linking architecture choices to spectral physics, dataset characteristics, and deployment scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a practical impasse in NIR deep-learning chemometrics where the same CNN architectures appear superior in one study and inferior in another, creating confusion for practitioners. The authors aim to resolve these contradictory recommendations by identifying underlying structural causes rather than treating them as irreconcilable method differences.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic review tracing recurring disagreements to three main sources: 1) indirect nature of Vis-NIR measurements in water-dominated matrices, 2) mismatch between effective receptive field (ERF) and informative spectral structure width, and 3) validation design acting as a hidden hyperparameter. They build on evidence from published chemometrics and spectroscopy studies.

Result: The review identifies that apparent contradictions in CNN design recommendations (kernel size, depth, preprocessing, transfer learning) are structurally expected consequences of uncontrolled moderating variables rather than true methodological conflicts. The authors propose a conditional design framework linking architecture and preprocessing choices to spectral physics, dataset regime, and intended deployment scenario.

Conclusion: The proposed perspective shifts DL chemometrics from template-driven architecture selection toward reproducible, physics-aware, and deployment-aligned model comparison. By understanding the conditional nature of architecture preferences based on dataset characteristics and measurement physics, practitioners can make more informed and reproducible design choices.

Abstract: Near-infrared (NIR; a.k.a.\ NIRS) deep-learning studies in chemometrics increasingly report mutually inconsistent conclusions regarding convolutional neural network (CNN) design, including small versus large kernels, shallow versus deep architectures, raw spectra versus preprocessing, and single-domain training versus transfer learning. As a result, the same architecture can appear superior in one study and inferior in another, creating a practical impasse for chemometric practitioners. In this review, we argue that these contradictions are not evidence of irreconcilable methods but a structurally expected consequence of uncontrolled moderating variables. Specifically, we trace recurring disagreements to (i) the indirect nature of Vis--NIR measurement in water-dominated matrices, (ii) mismatch between effective receptive field (ERF) and the width of informative spectral structure, and (iii) validation design (including split strategy, hyperparameter tuning budget, and exposure to deployment-like shifts) acting as a hidden hyperparameter that can dominate model ranking. Building on evidence from published chemometrics and spectroscopy studies, we propose a conditional design framework that links architecture and preprocessing choices to spectral physics, dataset regime, and intended deployment scenario. Overall, the proposed perspective moves DL Chemometrics from template-driven architecture selection toward reproducible, physics-aware, and deployment-aligned model comparison.

</details>


### [316] [CARD: Coarse-to-fine Autoregressive Modeling with Radix-based Decomposition for Transferable Free Energy Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02657)
*Ziyang Yu,Yi He,Wenbing Huang,Wen Yan,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CARD is a generative framework that uses radix-based decomposition to convert 3D coordinates into sequences, enabling efficient autoregressive modeling for free energy computation without molecular dynamics simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for estimating free energy differences face limitations: classical computational approaches require expensive molecular dynamics simulations, while deep learning methods have limited expressiveness or poor generalization across systems.

Method: Proposes CARD, a generative framework using novel radix-based decomposition to bijectively convert 3D coordinates into mixed discrete-continuous sequences, enabling coarse-to-fine autoregressive modeling. The model corresponds to a distribution with zero free energy, serving as a proposal for absolute free energy computation without alchemical pathways.

Result: Experiments show CARD matches classical computational methods' accuracy on unseen systems with diverse topologies while achieving approximately 40-fold speedup in inference.

Conclusion: CARD addresses key limitations in free energy estimation by providing an expressive, generalizable framework that combines the accuracy of classical methods with dramatically improved computational efficiency.

Abstract: Estimating free energy differences quantifies thermodynamic preferences in molecular interactions, which is central to chemistry and drug discovery. Despite fruitful progress, existing methods still face key limitations: classical computational approaches remain prohibitively expensive due to their reliance on extensive molecular dynamics simulations, while deep learning-based methods are constrained by either less-expressive generative models or input dimensions tied to a specific system, resulting in negligible generalization. To address these challenges, we propose CARD, a generative framework that employs a novel radix-based decomposition to bijectively convert 3D coordinates into mixed discrete-continuous sequences, enabling coarse-to-fine autoregressive modeling with enhanced expressiveness. Notably, the model corresponds to a distribution with zero free energy, serving as a proposal for absolute free energy computation of arbitrary systems without relying on alchemical pathways. Experiments across diverse tasks demonstrate that CARD matches the accuracy of classical computational methods on unseen systems with diverse topologies, while achieving an approximately 40-fold speedup in inference.

</details>


### [317] [Bolek: A Multimodal Language Model for Molecular Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02745)
*Frederic Grabowski,Jacek Szczerbiński,Maciej Jaśkowski,Kalina Jasińska-Kobus,Paweł Dąbrowski-Tumański,Tomasz Jetka,Bartosz Topolski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bolek is a compact multimodal language model that combines molecular fingerprints with text to create auditable molecular property predictions with grounded natural-language explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current molecular property models lack auditability - traditional predictors give scores without rationale, while language models produce explanations weakly grounded in actual molecular structure. There's a need for models that provide both accurate predictions and verifiable reasoning.

Method: Inject Morgan fingerprint embeddings into an instruction-tuned text decoder (Qwen3-4B-Instruct base). Fine-tune on molecular alignment tasks (molecule description, RDKit descriptor prediction, substructure detection) and downstream reasoning on 15 TDC binary classification tasks using synthetic chains-of-thought anchored in concrete molecular features.

Result: Bolek outperforms its base model on all endpoints in yes/no mode and 13/15 in chain-of-thought mode, improving mean ROC/PR AUC from 0.55 to 0.76. It beats TxGemma-9B-Chat on 13/15 tasks despite being less than half its size. Explanations are more grounded - cites numerical descriptors 10-100x more often, with strong correlation to RDKit values (Spearman rho = 0.87-0.91). Generalizes well to unseen tasks.

Conclusion: Targeted modality injection and reasoning supervision tied to verifiable molecular features can produce compact, auditable molecular reasoning models that provide both accurate predictions and well-grounded explanations.

Abstract: Molecular property models increasingly support high-stakes drug-discovery decisions, but their outputs are often difficult to audit: classical predictors return scores without rationale, while language models can produce fluent explanations weakly grounded in the input molecule.
  We introduce Bolek, a compact multimodal language model that grounds natural-language reasoning in molecular structure by injecting a Morgan fingerprint embedding into an instruction-tuned text decoder. Bolek is fine-tuned on molecular alignment tasks, including molecule description, RDKit descriptor prediction, and substructure detection, and on downstream reasoning over 15 TDC binary classification tasks using synthetic chains-of-thought anchored in concrete molecular features.
  Across these tasks, Bolek outperforms its Qwen3-4B-Instruct base on all endpoints in yes/no mode and on 13 of 15 in chain-of-thought mode, raising mean ROC/PR AUC from 0.55 to 0.76. It also outperforms TxGemma-9B-Chat on 13 of 15 binary classification tasks despite being less than half its size. Bolek's explanations are more grounded than those of the baseline LLMs: it cites numerical descriptors 10-100x more often per chain-of-thought, and the cited values agree strongly with RDKit for key descriptors such as TPSA, MolLogP, and MolWt (Spearman rho = 0.87-0.91). Generalisation extends beyond the training panel: on 15 unseen TDC classification endpoints, Bolek matches TxGemma on five, and it produces non-trivial rank correlations on three held-out regression endpoints despite never seeing downstream regression during training.
  These results suggest that targeted modality injection and reasoning supervision tied to verifiable molecular features can yield compact, auditable molecular reasoning models.

</details>


### [318] [Spectral Model eXplainer: a chemically-grounded explainability framework for spectral-based machine learning models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02684)
*Jose Vinicius Ribeiro,Rafael Figueira Goncalves,Fabio Luiz Melquiades,Sylvio Barbon Junior*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMX is a novel XAI framework that explains spectral classifiers through expert-informed spectral zones instead of isolated variables, using PCA summarization, quantile predicates, and graph-based ranking aggregation, with threshold spectrum reconstruction for visual interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current XAI methods (SHAP, PFI, VIP) were not designed for spectral data's physical continuity and high collinearity. They assign relevance to isolated spectral variables rather than chemically meaningful spectral zones, requiring post-hoc aggregation and lacking direct visual interpretability for domain experts.

Method: SMX is a post-hoc, global, model-agnostic framework that: 1) summarizes each expert-defined spectral zone via PCA, 2) defines quantile-based logical predicates for zones, 3) estimates predicate relevance with perturbation in stochastic subsamples, 4) aggregates bag-wise rankings in a directed weighted graph summarized by Local Reaching Centrality, and 5) reconstructs threshold spectra by back-projecting predicate boundaries to original spectral units for visual comparison.

Result: The method was evaluated on eight real spectral datasets (six XRF and two Gamma-ray Spectrometry) and one synthetic benchmark with known ground truth, demonstrating its effectiveness in explaining spectral classifiers while addressing the unique challenges of spectral data.

Conclusion: SMX provides a specialized XAI framework for spectral data that goes beyond generic methods by directly addressing spectral continuity and collinearity, offering chemically meaningful zone-level explanations with visual interpretability in natural measurement units for domain experts.

Abstract: Spectral-based machine learning models have been increasingly deployed in chemometrics and spectroscopy, where predictive accuracy is as important as explainability. Current employed eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods are largely adapted from tabular or generic multivariate domains, assigning relevance to isolated spectral variables rather than to the chemically meaningful spectral zones. Widely adopted tools such as SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), Permutation Feature Importance (PFI), and Variable Importance in Projection scores (VIP) were not designed for the physical continuity and high collinearity of spectral data, and their variable-level outputs require post-hoc aggregation to recover zone-level information. This study introduces the Spectral Model eXplainer (SMX), a post-hoc, global, model-agnostic XAI framework that explains spectral classifiers through expert-informed spectral zones. SMX summarizes each zone via PCA, defines quantile-based logical predicates, estimates predicate relevance with perturbation in stochastic subsamples, and aggregates bag-wise rankings in a directed weighted graph summarized by Local Reaching Centrality. A key component is threshold spectrum reconstruction, which back-projects predicate boundaries to the original spectral domain in natural measurement units, enabling direct visual comparison with measured spectra. The method was evaluated on eight real spectral datasets (six based on X-ray Fluorescence--XRF and two based on Gamma-ray Spectrometry) and one synthetic benchmark with known gr

</details>


### [319] [A decoupled diffusion planner that adapts to changing cost limits by using cost-conditioned generation for safety and reward gradients for performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02777)
*Rufeng Chen,Zhaofan Zhang,Zhejiang Yang,Hechang Chen,Sihong Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDGD is a diffusion-based method for offline safe RL that separates constraint satisfaction and reward optimization through classifier-free guidance and trajectory relabeling, achieving strong safety compliance while maintaining high reward.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline safe RL requires policies to adapt to varying safety budgets during deployment. Existing diffusion-based planners treat reward improvement and constraint satisfaction as competing objectives, leading to unreliable safety compliance under changing cost limits.

Method: 1) Safe Decoupled Guidance Diffusion (SDGD): Uses classifier-free guidance conditioned on cost limit to bias sampling toward safe trajectories, while using reward-gradient guidance to optimize return. 2) Feasible Trajectory Relabeling (FTR): Reshapes reward targets to discourage reward-induced cost drift.

Result: On DSRL benchmark: 94.7% constraint satisfaction rate (36/38 tasks), highest safety compliance among baselines, and highest reward among safe methods on 21 tasks.

Conclusion: By decoupling safety and reward guidance and introducing trajectory relabeling, SDGD provides reliable adaptive safety compliance while maintaining competitive reward performance in offline safe RL.

Abstract: Offline safe reinforcement learning often requires policies to adapt at deployment time to safety budgets that vary across episodes or change within a single episode. While diffusion-based planners enable flexible trajectory generation, existing guidance schemes often treat reward improvement and constraint satisfaction as competing gradient objectives, which can lead to unreliable safety compliance under cost limits. We reinterpret adaptive safe trajectory generation as sampling from a constrained trajectory distribution, where the budget restricts the trajectory region, and reward shapes preferences within that region. This perspective motivates Safe Decoupled Guidance Diffusion (SDGD), which conditions classifier-free guidance on the cost limit to bias sampling toward trajectories satisfying the specified limit, while using reward-gradient guidance to refine trajectories for higher return. Because direct reward guidance can increase return while also steering samples toward trajectories with higher cumulative cost, we introduce Feasible Trajectory Relabeling (FTR) to reshape reward targets and discourage such directions. We further provide a first-order sampling-time analysis showing that FTR suppresses reward-induced cost drift under a prefix-restorative alignment condition. Extensive evaluations on the DSRL benchmark show that SDGD achieves the strongest safety compliance among baselines, satisfying the constraint on 94.7% of tasks (36/38), while obtaining the highest reward among safe methods on 21 tasks.

</details>


### [320] [MSMixer: Learned Multi-Scale Temporal Mixing with Complementary Linear Shortcut for Long-Term Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02689)
*Ahmed Cherif*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MSMixer: A lightweight multi-scale MLP architecture for long-term time series forecasting that captures patterns at multiple temporal resolutions through parallel scale branches, achieving state-of-the-art performance with minimal parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing lightweight MLP-based models typically operate on a single temporal resolution, limiting their ability to explicitly model patterns at multiple scales needed for long-term time series forecasting (rapid oscillations, medium-range periodicities, and slowly evolving macro-trends).

Method: MSMixer uses three parallel scale branches with down-sample factors {1x, 4x, 16x} and independent MLP blocks, a learnable softmax gate that dynamically weighs branch outputs, and a DLinear complementary shortcut that provides full-window trend and seasonality context.

Result: MSMixer achieves the lowest average MSE (0.357) among lightweight models on ETT benchmarks, outperforming DLinear (0.386, -7.4%) and NLinear (0.365, -2.1%), winning 12 of 16 configurations. It matches or beats Transformer-based baselines while using 5x fewer parameters than PatchTST.

Conclusion: The multi-scale MLP architecture effectively captures temporal patterns at different resolutions, demonstrating that lightweight models can achieve state-of-the-art performance in long-term time series forecasting through complementary multi-scale modeling and trend-seasonality shortcuts.

Abstract: Long-term time series forecasting requires models that simultaneously capture rapid oscillations, medium-range periodicities, and slowly evolving macro-trends from a fixed look-back window. Existing lightweight MLP-based models typically operate on a single temporal resolution, limiting their ability to explicitly model patterns at multiple scales. We propose MSMixer, a channel-independent multi-scale MLP architecture that addresses this limitation through three complementary innovations: (i) three parallel scale branches at down-sample factors {1x, 4x, 16x} with independent MLP blocks, (ii) a learnable softmax gate that dynamically weighs branch outputs, and (iii) a DLinear complementary shortcut that provides full-window trend and seasonality context. MSMixer contains only 112K parameters at H=96 and runs at O(T) complexity. Evaluated on four ETT benchmarks with standard chronological splits and three random seeds, MSMixer achieves the lowest average MSE (0.357) among lightweight models, outperforming DLinear (0.386, -7.4%) and NLinear (0.365, -2.1%), winning 12 of 16 configurations. Against five Transformer-based baselines from the literature, MSMixer achieves best or second-best MSE in 9 of 16 configurations while using 5x fewer parameters than PatchTST. Ablation and sensitivity analyses confirm the complementary contributions of the multi-scale branches and the DLinear shortcut.

</details>


### [321] [Enhancing RL Generalizability in Robotics through SHAP Analysis of Algorithms and Hyperparameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02867)
*Lingxiao Kong,Cong Yang,Oya Deniz Beyan,Zeyd Boukhers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An explainable RL framework using SHAP values to quantify and decompose configuration impacts on generalization, enabling data-driven configuration selection for improved RL performance across environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL performance is highly sensitive to algorithm and hyperparameter configurations, while generalization gaps across environments complicate real-world deployment. Existing work hasn't quantitatively decomposed configuration contributions to generalization gaps or systematically leveraged this for configuration selection.

Method: Propose an explainable framework using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to quantify configuration impacts on RL performance across robotic environments. Establish theoretical foundation connecting Shapley values to generalizability, empirically analyze configuration impact patterns, and introduce SHAP-guided configuration selection.

Result: Reveal distinct patterns across algorithms and hyperparameters, with consistent configuration impacts across diverse tasks and environments. By applying SHAP-guided configuration selection, achieve improved RL generalizability.

Conclusion: The framework provides quantitative decomposition of configuration impacts on RL generalization, enabling data-driven configuration selection that improves generalizability and offers actionable guidance for practitioners.

Abstract: Despite significant advances in Reinforcement Learning (RL), model performance remains highly sensitive to algorithm and hyperparameter configurations, while generalization gaps across environments complicate real-world deployment. Although prior work has studied RL generalization, the relative contribution of specific configurations to the generalization gap has not been quantitatively decomposed and systematically leveraged for configuration selection. To address this limitation, we propose an explainable framework that evaluates RL performance across robotic environments using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to quantify configuration impacts. We establish a theoretical foundation connecting Shapley values to generalizability, empirically analyze configuration impact patterns, and introduce SHAP-guided configuration selection to enhance generalization. Our results reveal distinct patterns across algorithms and hyperparameters, with consistent configuration impacts across diverse tasks and environments. By applying these insights to configuration selection, we achieve improved RL generalizability and provide actionable guidance for practitioners.

</details>


### [322] [Federated Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Mobile Crowdsensing under Incomplete Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02705)
*Sumedh J. Dongare,Patrick Weber,Andrea Ortiz,Walid Saad,Oliver Hinz,Anja Klein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FDRL-PPO: A novel fully decentralized federated deep reinforcement learning algorithm for mobile crowdsensing, enabling mobile units to learn optimal task participation strategies without perfect system information through collaborative model training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile crowdsensing systems face dynamic challenges including changing task requirements, mobile units' availability/resources, and fragmented learning experiences. The main challenge is finding efficient task participation strategies for mobile units under incomplete information, as optimal strategies require perfect non-causal information unavailable in realistic scenarios.

Method: Proposed FDRL-PPO (Federated Deep Reinforcement Learning - Proximal Policy Optimization), a fully decentralized algorithm where each mobile unit learns its own task participation strategy based on experiences, available resources, and preferences. Leverages federated learning to enable collaborative model improvement without sharing private raw data, exchanging only learned models to compensate for individual limitations.

Result: Comprehensive evaluations on synthetic and real-world datasets show FDRL-PPO consistently outperforms benchmark algorithms in terms of: 1) Task completion ratio, 2) Fairness in task completion, 3) Energy consumption, and 4) Number of conflicting proposals.

Conclusion: FDRL-PPO provides a scalable, robust, and efficient solution for mobile crowdsensing systems, addressing the challenges of incomplete information and dynamic environments through federated reinforcement learning that enables mobile units to collaboratively improve strategies while preserving privacy.

Abstract: Mobile crowdsensing (MCS) is a distributed sensing architecture that utilizes existing sensors on mobile units (MUs) to perform sensing tasks. A mobile crowdsensing platform (MCSP) publishes the sensing tasks and the MUs decide whether to participate in exchange for money. The MCS system is dynamic: the task requirements, the MUs' availability, and their available resources change over time. The MUs aim to find an efficient task participation strategy to maximize their income while the MCSP focuses on maximizing the number of completed tasks. As optimal strategies require perfect non-causal information about the MCS system, which is unavailable in realistic scenarios, the main challenge is to find an efficient task participation strategy for the MUs under incomplete information. To this end, a novel fully decentralized federated deep reinforcement learning algorithm, FDRL-PPO, is proposed. FDRL-PPO enables every MU to learn its own task participation strategy based on its experiences, available resources, and preferences, without relying on perfect non-causal information about the MCS system. To replenish their batteries, the MUs rely on energy harvesting. As a result, their available energy varies over time, leading to varying availability and fragmented learning experiences. To mitigate these challenges, the proposed approach leverages federated learning, enabling MUs to collaboratively improve their models without sharing private raw data like their own experiences. By exchanging only learned models, MUs collectively compensate for individual limitations, and find more scalable, robust, and efficient task participation strategies. Comprehensive evaluations on both synthetic and real-world datasets show that FDRL-PPO consistently outperforms benchmark algorithms in terms of task completion ratio, fairness in task completion, energy consumption, and number of conflicting proposals.

</details>


### [323] [SpecKV: Adaptive Speculative Decoding with Compression-Aware Gamma Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02888)
*Shikhar Shukla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SpecKV is an adaptive controller that dynamically selects speculation length in speculative decoding using draft model confidence and entropy signals, achieving 56% improvement over fixed-length baselines with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current speculative decoding systems use fixed speculation lengths, but empirical evidence shows optimal lengths vary across task types and target model compression levels, suggesting need for adaptive approach.

Method: Profiled speculative decoding across multiple tasks, lengths, and compression levels; trained lightweight MLP controller using draft model confidence and entropy signals to predict acceptance rates and maximize tokens per step.

Result: Achieved 56.0% improvement over fixed γ=4 baseline with only 0.34ms overhead per decision (<0.5% of step time), statistically significant (p < 0.001); draft confidence/entropy strongly predict acceptance rates (correlation ≈0.56).

Conclusion: SpecKV demonstrates that adaptive speculation length selection based on draft model signals significantly improves speculative decoding performance across different compression levels, with the approach and data made available as open-source artifacts.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a small draft model to propose candidate tokens that a larger target model verifies. A critical hyperparameter in this process is the speculation length~$γ$, which determines how many tokens the draft model proposes per step. Nearly all existing systems use a fixed~$γ$ (typically~4), yet empirical evidence suggests that the optimal value varies across task types and, crucially, depends on the compression level applied to the target model. In this paper, we present \textbf{SpecKV}, a lightweight adaptive controller that selects~$γ$ per speculation step using signals extracted from the draft model itself. We profile speculative decoding across 4~task categories, 4~speculation lengths, and 3~compression levels (FP16, INT8, NF4), collecting 5,112 step-level records with per-step acceptance rates, draft entropy, and draft confidence. We demonstrate that the optimal~$γ$ shifts across compression regimes and that draft model confidence and entropy are strong predictors of acceptance rate (correlation~$\approx 0.56$). SpecKV uses a small MLP trained on these signals to maximize expected tokens per speculation step, achieving a 56.0\% improvement over the fixed-$γ$=4 baseline with only 0.34\,ms overhead per decision ($<$0.5\% of step time). The improvement is statistically significant ($p < 0.001$, paired bootstrap test). We release all profiling data, trained models, and notebooks as open-source artifacts.

</details>


### [324] [Visual Latents Know More Than They Say: Unsilencing Latent Reasoning in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02735)
*Xin Zhang,Qiqi Tao,Jiawei Du,Moyun Liu,Joey Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Inference-time latent optimization resolves the "Silenced Visual Latents" problem where visual reasoning tokens get suppressed despite semantic enrichment, by disentangling conflicting objectives through contrastive alignment and confidence-progression rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing latent visual reasoning methods suffer from an optimization pathology: visual latents become semantically enriched during training but their contribution to final answer prediction is systematically suppressed. Autoregressive objectives favor shortcuts using direct visual input, driving latent tokens toward transition-like states rather than informative reasoning content.

Method: Two-stage inference-time optimization with frozen backbone parameters: Stage I uses query-guided contrastive latent-visual alignment to warm up visual latents and prevent collapse. Stage II optimizes latent reasoning via confidence-progression reward that incentivizes predicted token distributions along the latent span to become progressively more concentrated.

Result: Experiments across eight benchmarks and four model backbones show that inference-time latent optimization (without parameter updates) effectively unleashes the suppressed reasoning capacity of visual latents.

Conclusion: Inference-time latent optimization addresses the Silenced Visual Latents problem by disentangling conflicting objectives, enabling visual latents to realize their potential for reasoning without architectural changes or training parameter updates.

Abstract: Continuous latent-space reasoning offers a compact alternative to textual chain-of-thought for multimodal models, enabling high-dimensional visual evidence to be integrated without explicit reasoning tokens. However, we identify a previously overlooked optimization pathology in existing latent visual reasoning methods: although visual latents become semantically enriched during training, their contribution to final answer prediction is systematically suppressed. Within the shared parameter space, the autoregressive objective favors shortcut reliance on direct visual input, driving latent tokens toward transition-like states rather than informative reasoning content. We term this phenomenon Silenced Visual Latents. To address it, we disentangle the two conflicting objectives by directly optimizing the latent reasoning at inference time, keeping backbone parameters frozen. In Stage I, visual latents are warmed up via query-guided contrastive latent--visual alignment, improving semantic quality while preventing latent collapse. In Stage II, the latent reasoning is further optimized via a confidence-progression reward, which incentivizes predicted token distributions along the latent span to become progressively more concentrated, routing predictions through the latent reasoning rather than bypassing it. Experiments across eight benchmarks and four model backbones show that inference-time latent optimization, without any parameter updates, effectively unleashes the suppressed reasoning capacity of visual latents.

</details>


### [325] [A Closed-Form Persistence-Landmark Pipeline for Certified Point-Cloud and Graph Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02836)
*Sushovan Majhi,Atish Mitra,Žiga Virk,Pramita Bagchi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PLACE is a closed-form persistent homology pipeline for classifying point clouds/graphs with three theoretical guarantees: margin-based risk bound, descriptor selection rule, and per-prediction certificates - all derived from training labels without learned weights or calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for interpretable topological data analysis methods with theoretical guarantees that don't require learned weights or held-out calibration, providing reliable classification of point clouds and graphs using persistent homology signatures.

Method: Closed-form pipeline summing Mitra-Virk single-point coordinate functions over sparse landmark grid; uses structural distortion constant λ(ν) optimization; Mahalanobis margin with Ledoit-Wolf-shrunk covariance for descriptor selection; provides certificates in Pinelis/Gaussian forms.

Result: Strongest diagram-based method on Orbit5k; matches strongest topology-based baseline on MUTAG/COX2; O(kR/(Δ√m_min)) margin bound with matching minimax lower bound; Mahalanobis margin best descriptor selector (mean ρ≈+0.54); constructive but not yet operational certificates.

Conclusion: PLACE provides theoretically-grounded topological classification with three quantitative guarantees from training labels alone, achieving competitive performance while identifying remaining gaps in descriptor blindness and pool-coverage limits.

Abstract: We introduce PLACE (Persistence-Landmark Analytic Classification Engine), a closed-form pipeline for classifying point clouds and graphs through their persistent-homology signatures. Three quantitative guarantees -- a margin-based excess-risk rate, a closed-form descriptor-selection rule, and a per-prediction certificate -- are derived from training labels alone, with no learned weights or held-out calibration. The embedding sums Mitra-Virk single-point coordinate functions over a sparse landmark grid; closed-form weights maximize a structural distortion constant $λ(ν)$ (a Lipschitz lower bound on $\mathcal{D}_n$ under non-interference). (i) An $O(kR/(Δ\sqrt{m_{\min}}))$ margin bound, driven by class-mean separation $Δ$ and embedding radius $R$, matched by a sample-starved minimax lower bound. (ii) The Mahalanobis margin under Ledoit-Wolf-shrunk covariance is the strongest closed-form descriptor selector on a heterogeneous 64-descriptor chemical-graph pool (mean Spearman $ρ\approx +0.54$ across 10 benchmarks, positive on 9 of 10); the isotropic surrogate $Δ/\sqrt\ell$ admits a closed-form selection-consistency rate on homogeneous (14-15 descriptor) protein/social pools. (iii) A training-time-decided certificate with no per-prediction overhead, in non-asymptotic Pinelis and asymptotic Gaussian plug-in forms. Empirically, PLACE is the strongest diagram-based method on Orbit5k and matches the strongest topology-based baseline within statistical noise on MUTAG and COX2. The remaining gaps fall into two diagnosable regimes: descriptor blindness on NCI1/NCI109, and pool-coverage limits elsewhere. Both radii exceed the firing threshold $\hatΔ/2$ on every benchmark at our training-set sizes, dominated by the $\sqrt\ell$ scaling of the multivariate-norm bound; the per-prediction certificate is constructive but not yet operational at these sizes.

</details>


### [326] [Trust, but Verify: Peeling Low-Bit Transformer Networks for Training Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02853)
*Arian Eamaz,Farhang Yeganegi,Mojtaba Soltanalian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A layer-wise peeling framework for monitoring transformer training dynamics by constructing achievable reference bounds that expose optimization inefficiencies hidden in aggregate loss curves.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural network optimization is challenging due to nonconvex landscapes, especially for transformer language models where expensive training, frozen model reuse, and poorly optimized layers can silently degrade performance. Standard metrics provide limited visibility into layer-wise learning quality.

Method: Propose a layer-wise peeling framework where each transformer layer is locally optimized against intermediate representations of the trained model. Construct lightweight, layer-specific reference solutions and project layers onto multiple intermediate outputs via different permutations to obtain achievable baselines for fine-grained diagnosis.

Result: Experiments on decoder-only transformers show layer-wise reference bounds can match or surpass trained models at various training stages, exposing inefficiencies hidden in aggregate loss curves. The analysis remains effective under binarization and quantized settings where training dynamics are fragile. Bounds consistently separate apparent convergence from effective optimality.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables fine-grained diagnosis of under-optimized transformer layers, revealing optimization opportunities invisible when relying solely on training loss. This provides better monitoring of training dynamics and identification of layers needing further optimization.

Abstract: Understanding whether deep neural networks are effectively optimized remains challenging, as training occurs in highly nonconvex landscapes and standard metrics provide limited visibility into layer-wise learning quality. This challenge is particularly acute for transformer-based language models, where training is expensive, models are often reused in frozen form, and poorly optimized layers can silently degrade performance. We propose a layer-wise peeling framework for monitoring training dynamics, in which each transformer layer is locally optimized against intermediate representations of the trained model. By constructing lightweight, layer-specific reference solutions and projecting layers onto multiple intermediate outputs via different permutations, we obtain achievable baselines that enable fine-grained diagnosis of under-optimized layers. Experiments on decoder-only transformer models show that these layer-wise reference bounds can match or even surpass the trained model at various stages of training, exposing inefficiencies that remain hidden in aggregate loss curves. We further demonstrate that this analysis remains effective under binarization and quantized settings, where training dynamics are particularly fragile. Across all numerical results, the proposed bounds consistently separate apparent convergence from effective optimality, highlighting optimization opportunities that are invisible when relying on training loss alone.

</details>


### [327] [Unsupervised Machine Learning for Detecting Structural Anomalies in European Regional Statistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02884)
*Bogdan Oancea*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An unsupervised machine learning framework using multiple anomaly detection methods to identify structurally atypical European regions based on key socio-economic indicators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional statistical validation tools are inadequate for detecting unusual combinations of indicators in high-dimensional settings, creating a need for more sophisticated approaches to identify structurally atypical regional socio-economic profiles.

Method: Constructed cross-sectional dataset of NUTS2 regions using Eurostat data with four key indicators (GDP per capita, unemployment rate, tertiary education, population density). Applied and compared five anomaly detection techniques: univariate z-scores, Mahalanobis distance, Isolation Forest, Local Outlier Factor, and One-Class SVM. Regions flagged by at least three methods were classified as structural anomalies.

Result: Machine learning methods consistently identified regions with multivariate profiles diverging from EU-wide patterns, including highly developed metropolitan economies (Brussels, Vienna, Berlin, Prague), regions with persistent disadvantages (Central/Western Slovakia, Northern Hungary, Castilla-La Mancha, Extremadura), and Istanbul. These anomalies represent meaningful structural divergence rather than data quality issues.

Conclusion: The proposed framework is reproducible, scalable, and compatible with existing validation workflows, offering a flexible tool for early detection of unusual regional configurations within the European Statistical System.

Abstract: Ensuring the coherence of regional socio-economic statistics is a central task for national statistical institutes. Traditional validation tools, such as range edits, ratio checks, or univariate outlier detection, are effective for identifying extreme values in individual series but are less suited for detecting unusual combinations of indicators in high-dimensional settings. This paper proposes an unsupervised machine learning framework for identifying structurally atypical regional profiles within Europe using publicly available Eurostat data. We construct a cross-sectional dataset of NUTS2 regions (2022) covering four key indicators: GDP per capita in PPS, unemployment rate, tertiary educational attainment, and population density. We apply and compare five anomaly detection techniques, univariate z-scores, Mahalanobis distance, Isolation Forest, Local Outlier Factor, and One-Class SVM, and classify a region as a structural anomaly if it is flagged by at least three of the five methods. The findings show that machine learning methods identify a consistent set of regions whose multivariate profiles diverge substantially from the EU-wide pattern. These include both highly developed metropolitan economies (Brussels, Vienna, Berlin, Prague) and regions with persistent socio-economic disadvantages (Central and Western Slovakia, Northern Hungary, Castilla-La Mancha, Extremadura), as well as Istanbul, whose profile differs markedly from EU capital regions. Importantly, these anomalies do not necessarily signal data quality issues; rather, they reflect meaningful structural divergence that warrants analytical or policy attention. The proposed framework is fully reproducible, scalable, and compatible with existing validation workflows, offering a flexible tool for early detection of unusual regional configurations within the European Statistical System.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [328] [The Cost of Consensus: Isolated Self-Correction Prevails Over Unguided Homogeneous Multi-Agent Debate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00914)
*Blaž Bertalanič,Carolina Fortuna*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Homogeneous multi-agent debate among LLMs (7-8B parameters) can actually reduce performance through sycophantic conformity, contextual fragility, and consensus collapse, with self-correction proving more efficient and effective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent debate is widely deployed with the assumption that peer review filters hallucinations, but the failure dynamics of homogeneous debate remain poorly understood, particularly whether peer exchange truly improves performance over simpler approaches.

Method: Controlled empirical study with homogeneous agents (Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, Ministral-3-8B) across N=10 agents and R=3 debate rounds on GSM-Hard and MMLU-Hard benchmarks, comparing peer debate against isolated self-correction and stochastic noise control. Analyzed communication density (K ∈ {2,4,9}) and sampling temperature (T ∈ {0.4, 0.7}) ablations.

Result: Three model-dependent failure pathways identified: sycophantic conformity (up to 85.5% modal adoption), contextual fragility (up to 70.0% vulnerability), and consensus collapse (up to 32.3 percentage point oracle gap). Debate consumes 2.1-3.4× more tokens than self-correction for equal or lower accuracy, with conformity reaching high levels at minimal peer exposure.

Conclusion: Within the 7-8B parameter class, homogeneous teams without structured roles do not benefit from unguided peer exchange; isolated self-correction consistently offers a more favorable cost-accuracy tradeoff, challenging the assumption that peer debate inherently improves performance.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate, where teams of LLMs iteratively exchange rationales and vote on answers, is widely deployed under the assumption that peer review filters hallucinations. Yet the failure dynamics of homogeneous debate remain poorly understood, therefore we report findings from a controlled empirical study of teams of $N{=}10$ homogeneous agents (Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, Ministral-3-8B) across $R{=}3$ debate rounds on two high-difficulty benchmarks (GSM-Hard and MMLU-Hard). We compare peer debate against isolated self-correction and a stochastic noise control that injects rationales from unrelated problems. We decompose debate failure into three model-dependent pathways: sycophantic conformity, where agents uncritically adopt majority answers (modal adoption up to 85.5%); contextual fragility, where peer rationales destabilize previously correct reasoning (vulnerability rate up to 70.0%); and consensus collapse, where plurality voting discards correct answers already present in the generation pool (oracle gap up to 32.3 percentage points). Ablations over communication density ($K \in \{2,4,9\}$) and sampling temperature ($T \in \{0.4, 0.7\}$) show that conformity reaches high levels at minimal peer exposure ($K{=}2$) and intensifies with greater initial diversity. Across all configurations, debate consumes 2.1-3.4$\times$ more tokens (up to 28,631 tokens per problem) than self-correction for equal or lower accuracy. Our results indicate that, within the 7-8B parameter class, homogeneous teams without structured roles do not benefit from unguided peer exchange, and that isolated self-correction consistently offers a more favorable cost-accuracy tradeoff.

</details>


### [329] [Breaking the Communication-Accuracy Trade-off: A Sparsified Information Diffusion Framework for Multi-Agent Collaborative Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00946)
*Jirong Zha,Chenyu Zhao,Nan Zhou,Zhenyu Liu,Tao Sun,Bin Zhang,Xiaochun Zhang,Xinlei Chen*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Proposes an event-triggered diffusion cubature information filter (EDC-CIF) for multi-agent target tracking that improves accuracy, reduces communication, and accelerates convergence through error-minimized local estimation and correlation-aware global fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent systems need communication-efficient filters for collaborative perception to reduce communication burden. Existing event-triggered filters face a trade-off between estimation accuracy and communication cost, creating a need for a solution that maintains tracking performance while minimizing data transmission.

Method: Combines error-minimized event-triggered cubature information filter (CIF) for local estimation with correlation-aware diffusion strategy for global fusion. The ET mechanism reduces unnecessary transmissions, while the diffusion approach efficiently shares information across agents.

Result: Experimental results show the EDC-CIF algorithm simultaneously reduces estimation error and computation time while significantly enhancing communication efficiency. The approach demonstrates scalability and efficacy in real-time multi-agent collaborative target tracking.

Conclusion: The proposed EDC-CIF filter successfully addresses the accuracy-communication trade-off in event-triggered filters, providing a fast and accurate solution for communication-efficient collaborative target tracking in multi-agent systems with improved tracking accuracy, reduced data transmission, and accelerated convergence.

Abstract: The growing relevance of multi-agent systems has drawn increasing focus on communication-efficient filters for collaborative perception to alleviate the system's communication burden. While the event-triggered (ET) mechanism can improve communication efficiency in collaborative state estimation, an inevitable trade-off exists between estimation accuracy and communication cost in ET filters. This paper proposes a fast and accurate ET diffusion-based filter for real-time multi-agent collaborative target tracking, aiming to reduce the system's data transmission without compromise in tracking performance. The proposed filter achieves improved tracking accuracy, reduced data transmission, and accelerated convergence using an error-minimized ET cubature information filter (CIF) for local estimation, and a correlation-aware diffusion strategy for global fusion. The experimental results confirm the scalability of the proposed EDC-CIF algorithm and demonstrate its efficacy in simultaneously reducing estimation error and computation time while significantly enhancing communication efficiency.

</details>


### [330] [Separation Assurance between Heterogeneous Fleets of Small Unmanned Aerial Systems via Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01041)
*Iman Sharifi,Hyeong Tae Kim,Maheed Hatem Ahmed,Mahsa Ghasemi,Peng Wei*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether heterogeneous fleets of drones with different configurations can reach equilibrium in tactical deconfliction, using attention-enhanced PPOA2C reinforcement learning, finding that equilibria often favor fleets with stronger configurations, highlighting fairness concerns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In future dense urban airspace, multiple companies will operate heterogeneous fleets of small unmanned aerial systems with different configurations (equipage, sensing, communication ranges), making tactical deconfliction complex. The paper addresses whether these systems can reach conflict-free equilibria and whether converged policies discriminate against weaker configurations.

Method: Uses a multi-agent reinforcement learning paradigm with attention-enhanced Proximal Policy Optimization-based Advantage Actor-Critic (PPOA2C) framework. Each fleet independently trains its own policy while preserving privacy, resolving intra- and inter-fleet conflicts for package delivery missions over Dallas, Texas.

Result: Fleets with distinct PPOA2C policies can reach equilibrium to maintain safe separation. PPOA2C policies outperform rule-based baselines, and a PPOA2C policy interacts safely with a rule-based policy. However, equilibria between similar policy types tend to favor fleets with stronger configurations, and even with similar configurations but different policy types, equilibrium favors one policy over another.

Conclusion: While reinforcement learning approaches like PPOA2C can achieve safe conflict resolution in heterogeneous drone fleets, there are fairness concerns as equilibria often discriminate against weaker configurations or different policy types, highlighting the need for fairness-aware conflict management systems.

Abstract: In the envisioned future dense urban airspace, multiple companies will operate heterogeneous fleets of small unmanned aerial systems (sUASs), where each fleet includes several homogeneous aircraft with identical policies and configurations, e.g., equipage, sensing, and communication ranges, making tactical deconfliction highly complex for the aircraft. This paper aims to address two core questions: (1) Can tactical deconfliction policies converge or reach an equilibrium to ensure a conflict-free airspace when companies operate heterogeneous fleets of homogeneous aircraft? (2) If so, will the converged policies discriminate against companies operating sUASs with weaker configurations? We investigate a multi-agent reinforcement learning paradigm in which homogeneous aircraft within heterogeneous fleets operate concurrently to perform package delivery missions over Dallas, Texas, USA. An attention-enhanced Proximal Policy Optimization-based Advantage Actor-Critic (PPOA2C) framework is employed to resolve intra- and inter-fleet conflicts, with each fleet independently training its own policy while preserving privacy. Experimental results show that two fleets with distinct, shared PPOA2C policies can reach an equilibrium to maintain safe separation. While two PPOA2C policies outperform two strong rule-based baselines in terms of conflict resolution, a PPOA2C policy exhibits safer interaction with a rule-based policy, indicating adaptive capabilities of PPOA2C policies. Furthermore, we conducted extensive policy-configuration evaluations, which reveal that equilibria between similar policy types tend to favor fleets with stronger configurations. Even under similar configurations but different policy types, the equilibrium favors one of the heterogeneous policies, underscoring the need for fairness-aware conflict management in heterogeneous sUAS operations.

</details>


### [331] [rAIson: Developing Reliable Decision-Making Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01351)
*Pavlos Moraitis,Nikolaos Spanoudakis,Antonis Kakas*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The rAIson platform enables development of automated, reliable, and explainable decision-making agents without coding through a mature high-level technological environment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to provide a platform that allows development of complex real-life AI decision-making applications without requiring programming expertise, addressing the need for automated, reliable, and explainable AI systems that can be deployed in practical scenarios.

Method: The method involves creating a high-level technological platform called rAIson that has reached a mature development stage. The platform provides an environment where users can develop decision-making agents through configuration and tooling rather than traditional programming.

Result: The platform has been successfully developed to the point where it can be used to create complex real-life applications entirely without writing code, making AI decision-making systems accessible to non-programmers while maintaining reliability and explainability.

Conclusion: The rAIson platform represents a significant advancement in AI application development, democratizing access to sophisticated decision-making systems by eliminating the coding barrier while ensuring the systems remain automated, reliable, and explainable for real-world deployment.

Abstract: This paper presents the rAIson platform, a high-level technological environment for the development of automated, reliable and explainable decision-making agents. The research underlying the platform and its technological progress has now reached a mature stage that allows the platform to be used for the development of complex real-life applications without writing a single line of code.

</details>


### [332] [Talk is Cheap, Communication is Hard: Dynamic Grounding Failures and Repair in Multi-Agent Negotiation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01750)
*Yiheng Yao,Chelsea Zou,Robert D. Hawkins*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Multi-agent LLMs fail at dynamic grounding - the iterative negotiation process needed to establish shared meaning - despite being able to identify optimal solutions individually. The paper introduces a multi-turn resource allocation game that reveals specific breakdown patterns in collaborative grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-agent LLM benchmarks focus on static, one-shot tasks that overlook the ability to repair grounding breakdowns across turns. There's a lack of understanding about how LLMs perform in dynamic grounding - the joint activity where meaning is negotiated through interaction rather than just mapped to observable context.

Method: Introduced an iterated, multi-turn negotiation game where two agents allocate shared resources toward private projects with verifiable jointly optimal outcomes. Used this framework to measure coordination gaps through three baselines: oracle (individual reasoning), no-talk (communication necessity), and full-transparency (information exchange sufficiency).

Result: Agent dyads consistently failed to reach Pareto-optimal allocations despite individual ability to identify them. Identified four failure modes: 1) coordination degradation without shared history, 2) stubborn anchoring to initial proposals, 3) preference for perfunctory fairness over reward maximization, and 4) failures in referential binding across turns.

Conclusion: Dynamic grounding is a critical and understudied axis of multi-agent coordination. The bottleneck lies not in individual reasoning or information exchange, but in the interactive processes of joint plan formation, commitment, and execution that constitute dynamic grounding. Communication alone is insufficient without effective grounding mechanisms.

Abstract: Grounding is the collaborative process of establishing mutual belief sufficient for the current communicative purpose. While static grounding maps language to a shared, externally observable context, dynamic grounding is a joint activity where meaning is negotiated through interaction. Current multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) benchmarks focus on static, one-shot tasks, overlooking the ability to repair grounding breakdowns across turns. We introduce an iterated, multi-turn negotiation game in which two agents allocate shared resources toward private projects with verifiable jointly optimal outcomes. While individual agents can identify Pareto-optimal allocations in isolation, agent dyads consistently fail to reach them across open- and closed-source models. Our investigation reveals four failure modes: (1) coordination degrades when shared interaction history is absent; (2) yet accumulated context can itself become a liability through stubborn anchoring, where initial proposals are treated as axiomatic rather than negotiable; (3) a reliance on perfunctory fairness (equal resource splits) over reward-maximizing coordination; and (4) failures in referential binding, where agents lose track of commitments across turns. These results highlight dynamic grounding as a critical and understudied axis of multi-agent coordination. Our framework decomposes the coordination gap into measurable components: the oracle baseline establishes that the gap is not attributable to individual reasoning limitations; the no-talk baseline establishes that communication is necessary; and a full-transparency intervention establishes that information exchange alone is insufficient: the bottleneck lies in the interactive processes of joint plan formation, commitment, and execution that constitute dynamic grounding.

</details>


### [333] [Koopman Representations for Early Outbreak Warning and Minimal Counterfactual Intervention in Multi-Agent Epidemic Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01803)
*Florin Leon*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A Koopman operator framework for early outbreak detection and intervention selection in multi-agent epidemic simulations, using low-dimensional latent representations for forecasting and random forest classification to predict major outbreaks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to study near-critical epidemic regimes where small changes in exposure or timing can dramatically alter final outcomes, requiring methods for early warning and effective intervention in complex multi-agent systems with heterogeneous agents.

Method: Uses a Koopman-based framework that encodes aggregate daily observables from early trajectory windows into a low-dimensional latent space with approximately linear evolution. This supports short-horizon forecasting and outbreak risk estimation. Combines these representations with a random forest classifier trained to predict whether final attack rates exceed a major outbreak threshold.

Result: Experiments near system tipping points show strong early warning performance, with Koopman-derived features contributing to effective class separation. Counterfactual analysis demonstrates that minimal interventions (like keeping a single selected agent at home for one day) can reduce attack rates and often shift trajectories below the outbreak threshold.

Conclusion: The Koopman framework provides an effective approach for early outbreak detection and intervention optimization in complex epidemic simulations, particularly valuable in near-critical regimes where small, timely interventions can prevent major outbreaks.

Abstract: This paper presents a Koopman-based framework for early outbreak detection and intervention selection in a multi-agent epidemic simulation. Agents exhibit mobility patterns, heterogeneous susceptibility, immunity-dependent viral load progression, and local transmission through co-location. The goal of the simulation is to study near-critical epidemic regimes in which small changes in exposure or timing can alter the final outcome. Aggregate daily observables from early trajectory windows are encoded into a low-dimensional Koopman latent space whose approximately linear evolution supports short-horizon forecasting and outbreak risk estimation. These representations are combined with a random forest classifier trained to predict whether the final attack rate exceeds a major outbreak threshold. Experiments near the system tipping points show strong early warning performance, with Koopman-derived features contributing to class separation. Counterfactual analysis further shows that minimal interventions, such as keeping a single selected agent at home for one day, can reduce attack rates and, often, shift the trajectory below the outbreak threshold.

</details>


### [334] [MAGIC: Multi-Step Advantage-Gated Causal Influence for Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01805)
*Haohan Yu,Jinmiao Cong,Shengzhi Wang,Lu Wang,Chanjuan Liu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: MAGIC is a MARL framework that uses multi-step causal influence quantification and advantage-based gating to create effective intrinsic rewards for multi-agent coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The key challenge in MARL is designing learning signals that effectively promote coordination among agents, which requires quantifying the true, long-term causal influence between agents.

Method: MAGIC extracts multi-step causal influences between agents using causal intervention with conditional mutual information, then selectively converts them into intrinsic rewards through an advantage-based gating mechanism that directs exploration toward beneficial, goal-aligned behaviors.

Result: Experiments across MARL benchmarks including MPE and SMAC/SMACv2 show MAGIC outperforms state-of-the-art methods by at least 10.1% improvement in the main evaluation metric.

Conclusion: MAGIC successfully addresses the challenge of promoting coordination in MARL by quantifying long-term causal influence and converting it into effective intrinsic rewards, demonstrating significant performance improvements over existing methods.

Abstract: A key challenge in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) lies in designing learning signals that effectively promote coordination among agents. Designing such signals necessitates the ability to quantify the true, long-term causal influence between agents. To address this, we introduce Multi-step Advantage-Gated Interventional Causal MARL (MAGIC), a framework that extracts multi-step causal influences between agents and selectively converts them into intrinsic rewards. MAGIC uses causal intervention with conditional mutual information to quantify long-horizon agent influence, and introduces an advantage-based gating mechanism to ensure exploration is directed toward beneficial, goal-aligned behaviors. Experiments across multiple standard MARL benchmarks and task families, including MPE and SMAC/SMACv2, demonstrate that MAGIC outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin, achieving an improvement of at least 10.1% in the main evaluation metric.

</details>


### [335] [Quality-Aware Exploration Budget Allocation for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01865)
*Dahyun Oh,Minhyuk Yoon,H. Jin Kim*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A new method adapts exploration intensity in multi-agent RL using return-conditioned scheduling and reward signal quality metrics to optimize coordination while avoiding collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cooperative MARL faces challenges: effective coordination strategies are rare in large state-action spaces. Intrinsic motivation helps exploration but requires careful tuning of exploration intensity β. Too high β overwhelms task rewards causing coordination collapse; too low β prevents discovery of rare strategies. Also, exploration budget needs allocation across agents with varying intrinsic reward reliability.

Method: Combines two components: 1) Return-conditioned sigmoid schedule (RCB) for global β adaptation over training, 2) Per-agent Reward Signal Quality (RSQ) metric to allocate exploration budget to agents with more reliable intrinsic reward signals. Uses signal-to-noise statistics to determine allocation. Built on Successor Distance (SD) intrinsic reward which produces distinguishable per-agent signal quality. The method has convergence and ordering preservation guarantees.

Result: Achieves top-tier returns across all seven cooperative benchmarks tested (MPE, SMAX, MABrax environments). The method effectively balances exploration and coordination while avoiding collapse.

Conclusion: The framework successfully addresses the dual challenges of global exploration intensity adaptation and per-agent exploration budget allocation in cooperative MARL, enabling discovery of rare coordination strategies while maintaining stable task performance.

Abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) requires agents to discover joint strategies in a combinatorially large state-action space, yet effective coordination configurations are exceedingly rare. Intrinsic motivation, which augments task rewards with novelty bonuses, is a popular approach for driving exploration, but its effectiveness hinges on the exploration intensity $β$, where too large a value overwhelms the task signal and causes coordination collapse, while too small a value prevents discovery of rare strategies. We address two complementary challenges: adapting $β$ globally over training, and allocating the exploration budget across agents whose intrinsic reward signals vary in reliability. Our framework combines a return-conditioned sigmoid schedule (RCB) for global intensity control with a per-agent Reward Signal Quality (RSQ) metric that concentrates the exploration budget on agents with reliable signals. The core insight is that agents receiving noisy intrinsic rewards should explore less aggressively, and this allocation can be determined automatically from signal-to-noise statistics.
