Successor Distance (SD), a quasimetric intrinsic reward, naturally produces distinguishable per-agent signal quality, completing the framework with convergence and ordering preservation guarantees. On seven cooperative benchmarks (MPE, SMAX, MABrax), our method achieves top-tier returns across all environments.

</details>


### [336] [Coopetition-Gym v1: A Formally Grounded Platform for Mixed-Motive Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning under Strategic Coopetition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02063)
*Vik Pant,Eric Yu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Coopetition-Gym v1 is a benchmark platform for mixed-motive multi-agent reinforcement learning with 20 environments across 4 mechanism classes, featuring parameterized rewards, calibrated interdependence matrices, and validated against historical coopetitive cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for a comprehensive benchmark platform to study strategic coopetition (cooperation + competition) in multi-agent reinforcement learning with mixed-motive environments that can capture complex interdependent relationships.

Method: The platform provides 20 environments organized into 4 mechanism classes derived from foundational technical reports, each with closed-form payoff structures and calibrated interdependence matrices. It features parameterized reward layers (private, integrated, cooperative) for reward-type ablation studies, and includes 126 reference algorithms.

Result: The platform successfully calibrates 4 environments against historically documented coopetitive relationships with validation accuracies of 98.3%, 81.7%, 86.7%, and 87.3%. A comprehensive experimental study generated a 25,708-run training corpus and 1,116-run behavioral audit corpus.

Conclusion: Coopetition-Gym v1 is the first platform to combine continuous-action mixed-motive environments, parameterized reward mutuality, calibrated interdependence coefficients, game-theoretic oracle baselines, and validated case studies, providing a comprehensive benchmark for coopetition research.

Abstract: We present Coopetition-Gym v1, a benchmark platform for mixed-motive multi-agent reinforcement learning under strategic coopetition. The platform comprises twenty environments organized into four mechanism classes that correspond to four foundational technical reports: interdependence and complementarity (arXiv:2510.18802), trust and reputation dynamics (arXiv:2510.24909), collective action and loyalty (arXiv:2601.16237), and sequential interaction and reciprocity (arXiv:2604.01240). Each environment carries a closed-form payoff structure and a calibrated interdependence matrix derived from the corresponding report.
  Every environment exposes a parameterized reward layer configurable across three structurally distinct modes (private, integrated, cooperative). This separation of payoff from reward enables reward-type ablation, the platform's principal methodological apparatus. Four of the twenty environments are calibrated against historically documented coopetitive relationships and reproduce their outcomes at 98.3, 81.7, 86.7, and 87.3 percent on the validation rubric (Samsung-Sony LCD, Renault-Nissan Alliance, Apache HTTP Server, Apple iOS App Store).
  The platform exposes Gymnasium, PettingZoo Parallel, and PettingZoo AEC interfaces and ships 126 reference algorithms: 16 learning algorithms, 7 game-theoretic oracles, 2 heuristic baselines, and 101 constant-action policies. A reference experimental study trained the 16 learning algorithms on every environment under every reward configuration with seven random seeds, producing a 25,708-run training corpus and a 1,116-run behavioral audit corpus, both released under CC-BY-4.0 with Croissant 1.0 metadata. Coopetition-Gym v1 is the first platform to combine continuous-action mixed-motive environments, parameterized reward mutuality, calibrated interdependence coefficients, game-theoretic oracle baselines, and validated case studies.

</details>


### [337] [SOTOPIA-TOM: Evaluating Information Management in Multi-Agent Interaction with Theory of Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02307)
*Yashwanth YS,Ruichen Wang,Shihua Zeng,Xuhui Zhou,Koichi Onoue,Vasudha Varadarajan,Maarten Sap*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: SOTOPIA-TOM is a benchmark framework that evaluates LLM agents' ability to handle information asymmetry in multi-party settings, revealing significant deficiencies even in state-of-the-art models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents increasingly operate in multi-party settings requiring them to handle information asymmetry properly, but existing benchmarks fail to measure this ability in realistic scenarios. There's a need to evaluate agents' capacity to know when and to whom to disclose information appropriately in complex, privacy-sensitive interactions.

Method: Created a multi-agent interaction environment supporting both public (broadcast) and private (direct message) communication. Developed 160 human-reviewed scenarios across 8 industry sectors involving 3-5 agents with partitioned private knowledge and channel-dependent sharing policies. Established a multi-dimensional evaluation framework assessing information sharing, seeking, coordination efficiency, and privacy protection, combined into a composite INFOMGMT metric. Tested 6 LLM backbones with three prompting strategies: vanilla, CoT-privacy, and theory-of-mind (ToM) based interventions.

Result: Even the best model (GPT-5) achieved only 62% INFOMGMT score, showing persistent deficiencies in information seeking and privacy-aware decision-making. ToM-based interventions improved coordination-privacy balance significantly, with ToM-Coach reducing critical privacy violations on GPT-4o from 9.9% to 2.2% and increasing composite InfoMgmt score from 15% to 40%. The benchmark reveals current LLM agents' limitations in complex, information-asymmetric coordination.

Conclusion: SOTOPIA-TOM exposes significant limitations of current LLM agents in handling information asymmetry and privacy in multi-party interactions. The framework provides an extensible testbed for developing more privacy-aware, theory-of-mind capable multi-agent systems, highlighting the need for improved reasoning about information disclosure and coordination in complex social settings.

Abstract: As LLM-based agents are increasingly interacting in multi-party settings, they need to properly handle information asymmetry, i.e., knowing when and to whom to disclose information is appropriate. Yet, existing benchmarks fail to measure this ability in realistic multi-party settings. Thus, we introduce SOTOPIA-TOM, a multi-dimensional benchmarking framework to evaluate LLM agents' ability to successfully navigate information asymmetric and privacy sensitive multi-party interactions. We create an interaction environment which enables both public (broadcast) and private (direct message) communication, and craft 160 human-reviewed scenarios across eight industry sectors, each involving 3 to 5 agents with partitioned private knowledge and channel-dependent sharing policies. To measure interaction abilities, we create a multi-dimensional evaluation framework to assess how well agents share useful information, seek missing details, coordinate efficiently, and protect privacy, which we also combine into a composite INFOMGMT metric. Results show that, across 6 LLM backbones and prompting strategies (vanilla, CoT-privacy, and ToM-based interventions), even the largest high-reasoning model (GPT-5) reaches only a 62% INFOMGMT score, which indicates persistent deficiencies in information seeking and privacy-aware decision-making. Additionally, ToM-based interventions more consistently improve the overall coordination-privacy balance (for example, relative to the vanilla baseline, ToM-Coach reduces critical privacy violations on GPT-4o from 9.9% to 2.2% while increasing the composite InfoMgmt score more than 2.5x from 15% to 40%). Overall, SOTOPIA-TOM exposes persistent limitations of current LLM agents in complex, information-asymmetric coordination and provides an extensible testbed for developing more privacy-aware, theory-of-mind capable multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [338] [LLM-enabled Social Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02335)
*Önder Gürcan,Moharram Challenger*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: LLM agents need persona-based role definitions to turn language competence into socially intelligible behavior, moving beyond fluent language to meaningful social participation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-enabled agents have fluent language capabilities but lack social grounding in roles, norms, intentions, and contextual constraints, limiting their ability to participate meaningfully in social environments.

Method: Develops a conceptual framework arguing that LLM agents should be grounded in role definitions operationalized through persona descriptions, outlining research directions for representation, hybrid control, and evaluation.

Result: Establishes persona-based role definitions as a necessary foundation for transforming language competence into social behavior in LLM-enabled agents.

Conclusion: Persona-based role definitions are essential for grounding LLM agents in social contexts, enabling them to move beyond mere language fluency to socially intelligible behavior and meaningful participation in agent-agent and human-agent interactions.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed agent-agent and human-agent interaction by enabling software, physical, and simulation agents to communicate and deliberate through natural language. Yet fluent language use does not by itself yield socially intelligible behaviour. Most current systems remain weakly grounded in roles, norms, intentions, and contextual constraints, limiting their capacity for meaningful participation in social environments. This paper develops a conceptual baseline for LLM-enabled social agents by arguing that they should be grounded in role definitions operationalized through persona descriptions. On this basis, we outline research directions for representation, hybrid control, and evaluation. The paper concludes that persona-based role definitions are a necessary foundation for turning language competence into social behaviour.

</details>


### [339] [When Stress Becomes Signal: Detecting Antifragility-Compatible Regimes in Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02463)
*Jose Manuel de la Chica,Juan Manuel Vera,Jairo Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: CAFE is a statistical framework for detecting antifragility-compatible regimes in multi-agent LLM architectures by analyzing structured variation under semantic stress, rather than measuring immediate performance changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-agent LLM systems are evaluated for robustness (performance preservation under perturbation), but the paper studies whether semantic stress exposes structured variation that could support future antifragile learning, moving beyond simple robustness assessment.

Method: CAFE models controlled expected distribution of semantic stressors, reconstructs architecture-specific observed effective stress distribution from multi-dimensional judge signals, and compares both distributions using a distributional Jensen Gap under a convex stress potential to detect convex-expansive deformation patterns.

Result: Across five multi-agent architectures (flat, hierarchical, debate, meta-adaptive, ensemble), semantic stress reduces average judged quality by ~33%, yet all architectures exhibit positive distributional Jensen Gaps with bootstrap confidence intervals above zero, showing quality degradation coexists with detectable antifragility-compatible stress geometry.

Conclusion: CAFE is not an antifragile learner itself, but a measurement layer for identifying when and where antifragility learning may be worth applying, demonstrating that immediate quality degradation can coexist with statistically detectable antifragility-compatible stress structure in multi-agent architectures.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems are increasingly used to solve complex tasks through decomposition, debate, specialization, and ensemble reasoning. However, these systems are usually evaluated in terms of robustness: whether performance is preserved under perturbation. This paper studies a different question: whether semantic stress exposes structured variation that could support future antifragile learning. We introduce CAFE, a statistical framework for detecting antifragility-compatible regimes in multi-agent architectures. CAFE models a controlled expected distribution of semantic stressors, reconstructs an architecture-specific observed effective stress distribution from multi-dimensional judge signals, and compares both distributions using a distributional Jensen Gap under a convex stress potential. A positive gap does not imply immediate performance improvement; instead, it indicates a convex-expansive deformation of the observed stress distribution, suggesting that the architecture exposes learnable stress structure. We evaluate CAFE on a banking-risk analysis benchmark with five multi-agent architectures: flat, hierarchical, debate, meta-adaptive, and ensemble. Across all architectures, semantic stress reduces average judged quality by roughly one third. Yet all architectures exhibit positive distributional Jensen Gaps with bootstrap confidence intervals above zero. These results show that immediate quality degradation can coexist with statistically detectable antifragility-compatible stress geometry. CAFE is therefore not an antifragile learner itself, but a measurement layer for identifying when and where antifragility learning may be worth applying.

</details>
